<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*Define the term "Graph Space" and list its two primary halves.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Graph Space** is the complete ecosystem of technologies, models, languages, and tools that are designed to **store, process, query, and analyze** graph-structured data.

It is broadly divided into two halves:

1. **Graph Databases (OLTP systems)** — Optimised for real-time transactional workloads with low-latency reads/writes (e.g., Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, JanusGraph).
2. **Graph Compute Engines (OLAP systems)** — Optimised for bulk analytical workloads and iterative graph algorithms (e.g., Apache Spark GraphFrames, Giraph, GraphX).

> *Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for first half + example, 1 mark for second half + example.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*Differentiate between Graph Databases (OLTP) and Graph Compute Engines (OLAP) in terms of latency, query size, and ACID compliance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Graph Database (OLTP) | Graph Compute Engine (OLAP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | Milliseconds (real-time) | Seconds to hours (batch) |
| **Query Size** | Few entities, 1–10 hops | Whole-graph traversals |
| **ACID Compliance** | Full ACID | Usually eventual consistency / none |

> *Award: 1 mark per correct row, 3 rows = 3 marks.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 1 Internal Choice:** Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **high-level view of the Graph Space** in detail. Discuss the **two halves** of the graph space with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **Property Graph model** and the **RDF triple model** in terms of structure, query language, and use case. Justify which is more suitable for a **real-time recommendation engine**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

**1. Definition of Graph Space (2 marks)**
The Graph Space is the **unified ecosystem** of all technologies that handle graph-structured data, including databases, compute engines, query languages, and visualization tools. It is classified along three axes: **Data Model, Workload, and Processing Mode**.

**2. The Two Halves (4 marks)**

*Graph Databases (OLTP):*
- Optimized for **real-time, transactional, low-latency** queries.
- Use **index-free adjacency (IFA)** for O(1) per-hop traversal.
- Examples: **Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, JanusGraph, TigerGraph**.
- Query languages: **Cypher, GQL, Gremlin**.

*Graph Compute Engines (OLAP):*
- Optimized for **bulk analytical, iterative, batch** processing.
- Use **distributed vertex-centric processing** (Pregel model).
- Examples: **Apache Spark GraphX, Giraph, GraphFrames**.
- Suited for: PageRank, Connected Components, Shortest Paths on huge graphs.

**3. Why the Distinction Matters (1 mark)**
Choosing the wrong half leads to either **latency disasters** (OLAP for real-time) or **scalability bottlenecks** (OLTP for batch).

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**1. Property Graph Model (2.5 marks)**
- Structure: **Nodes + Relationships + Properties (key-value pairs on both)**.
- Query Languages: **Cypher (Neo4j), GQL (ISO standard), Gremlin**.
- Strengths: Natural for connected data, easy schema evolution, rich traversal patterns.
- Use Cases: Social networks, fraud detection, network management.

**2. RDF Triple Model (2.5 marks)**
- Structure: **Subject–Predicate–Object triples**, often stored in quads (with named graph).
- Query Language: **SPARQL**.
- Strengths: Standardized (W3C), supports inference (OWL, RDFS), inter-operable with Linked Open Data.
- Use Cases: Knowledge graphs, life sciences, bibliographic data, government open data.

**3. Justification for Real-Time Recommendation Engine (2 marks)**
A real-time recommendation engine requires:
- **Sub-10 ms latency** → OLTP requirement → favors **Property Graph + Cypher**.
- **Frequent schema changes** → Property graph's flexible labels win.
- **Path-based scoring** (e.g., "customers who bought X also bought Y") → Native graph traversal shines.

**Verdict:** The **Property Graph model** is more suitable for a real-time recommendation engine because it offers low-latency native traversals, flexible schema, and a mature query ecosystem (Cypher/GQL).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, describe the **three-axis classification** of the Graph Space (Data Model, Workload, Processing Mode). Give **two real-world examples** for each axis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **index-free adjacency (IFA)**? Derive the **time complexity** of graph traversal in native vs non-native graph processing. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

**Axis 1: Data Model (2 marks)**
- *Property Graph*: Nodes, edges, and properties (e.g., **Neo4j for a social network**, **TigerGraph for fraud**).
- *RDF*: Subject–predicate–object triples (e.g., **Stardog for biomedical ontologies**, **DBpedia for Wikipedia data**).
- *Hypergraph*: Edges connecting more than two nodes (e.g., **co-authorship networks**, **reaction networks in chemistry**).

**Axis 2: Workload (2 marks)**
- *OLTP (Transactional)*: Real-time CRUD queries (e.g., **friend-of-friend in LinkedIn**, **driver-passenger matching in Uber**).
- *OLAP (Analytical)*: Whole-graph algorithms (e.g., **PageRank for search engines**, **community detection in telecom**).

**Axis 3: Processing Mode (2 marks)**
- *Native*: Graph-aware storage engine (e.g., **Neo4j's MMIO files**, **TigerGraph's C++ engine**).
- *Non-Native*: Layered on top of another DB (e.g., **SAP HANA Graph on HANA RDBMS**, **Spark GraphFrames on RDDs**).

**Diagram (1 mark):** A simple 3-axis block diagram (Data Model × Workload × Processing).

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**1. Definition of Index-Free Adjacency (2 marks)**
**Index-Free Adjacency (IFA)** is a storage technique in which **each node maintains direct physical memory references (pointers) to all of its adjacent nodes**. As a result, traversing from one node to its neighbour does not require any index lookup — only a single pointer dereference.

**2. Native Graph Traversal (2 marks)**
For a path of $k$ hops:

$$
T_{\text{native}}(k) = k \cdot O(1) = O(k)
$$

**3. Non-Native Traversal (2 marks)**
For non-native, each hop requires an index lookup on the foreign key:

$$
T_{\text{non-native}}(k) = k \cdot O(\log_2 \vert V \vert) = O(k \log \vert V \vert)
$$

**4. Speedup Ratio (1 mark)**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{\text{non-native}}}{T_{\text{native}}} = \frac{k \log \vert V \vert}{k} = \log \vert V \vert
$$

For $\vert V \vert = 10^6$, Speedup $\approx 20\times$. This is the **mathematical justification** for using native graph databases for connected-data workloads.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Module 1):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Graph Database" with "Graph Compute Engine"** — Students often write *"Neo4j is a graph compute engine."* This is wrong. Neo4j is a **Graph Database (OLTP)**. Graph **Compute Engines** include Spark GraphX/Giraph.
>
> 2. **Skipping examples** — KTU explicitly awards **1 mark for examples**. Always cite **at least 2 systems** per half.
>
> 3. **No diagram = lost mark** — In 7-mark sub-questions, a labelled diagram (even hand-drawn) is **mandatory**. The board penalises purely textual answers.
>
> 4. **Writing "graphs are slow"** — The opposite is true for *connected-data* workloads. Show your math: $O(1)$ per hop with IFA.
>
> 5. **Treating SPARQL and Cypher as interchangeable** — They are not. SPARQL is for RDF; Cypher is for Property Graphs.
>
> 6. **Ignoring CAP theorem implications** — Graph databases also operate under CAP. State which two properties your chosen system guarantees.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Module 1: High-Level View of Graph Space**

- ✅ **Graph Space** = entire ecosystem of graph technologies (databases + compute engines + tools + languages).
- ✅ **Two Halves:** **OLTP Graph Databases** vs **OLAP Graph Compute Engines**.
- ✅ **OLTP** = real-time, low-latency, ACID, small queries → *Neo4j, Neptune, JanusGraph*.
- ✅ **OLAP** = bulk, batch, iterative, distributed → *Spark GraphX, Giraph, GraphFrames*.
- ✅ **Three Classification Axes:** **Data Model, Workload, Processing Mode** (D-W-P).
- ✅ **Property Graph** = nodes + relationships + properties; **Cypher/GQL/Gremlin** languages.
- ✅ **RDF** = subject-predicate-object triples; **SPARQL** language; W3C standard.
- ✅ **Index-Free Adjacency (IFA)** = direct pointer references between nodes → $O(1)$ per hop.
- ✅ **Non-Native** = graph layer on top of RDBMS/NoSQL → $O(\log \vert V \vert)$ per hop.
- ✅ **Speedup** of native vs non-native traversal = $\log_2 \vert V \vert$ factor.
- ✅ **Graph Density Formula:** $\rho = \dfrac{2 \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert (\vert V \vert - 1)}$, bounded $[0, 1]$.
- ✅ **Degree Sum Identity:** $\sum_{v \in V} deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert$ (Handshaking Lemma).
- ✅ **CAP Theorem** applies to graph databases too — usually CP or AP, not both.
- ✅ **Decision framework:** Use **OLTP** for real-time CRUD; use **OLAP** for batch algorithms.
- ✅ **Examples to always mention:** Neo4j (OLTP), Spark GraphFrames (OLAP), Stardog (RDF), TinkerPop (compute API).
- ✅ **Killer exam phrase:** *"The graph space is best understood as a 2D matrix of Workload × Processing Mode, with the Data Model as the third orthogonal axis."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Power of Graph Databases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** The Power of Graph Databases # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Graph Database** is a specialized, schema-flexible, NoSQL data store that uses **graph structures** with **nodes** (vertices), **edges** (relationships), and **properties** to represent and store data. It is purpose-built to exploit the connectedness of data by making **relationships** first-class citizens of the data model, rather than treating them as auxiliary structures inferred via expensive JOIN operations. In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (aligned with *Graph Databases* by Robinson, Webber & Eifrem, O'Reilly), a graph database satisfies the following defining properties: | Property | Description | | :--- | :--- | | **Nodes** | Discrete entities (e.g., Person, Product, City) | | **Edges** | Directed, named connections between nodes | | **Properties** | Key-value annotations on both nodes and edges | | **Labels** | Tag(s) that group nodes into sets/classes | | **Native Processing** | Index-free adjacency at the storage layer | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A *true* graph database (e.g., **Neo4j**) uses *index-free adjacency* — every node directly maintains references to its adjacent nodes, enabling constant-time traversal regardless of total dataset size. This is the key distinction from a "graph-on-top-of-RDBMS" implementation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Social Train Network" Imagine a railway map: - **Cities** are the *nodes*. - **Railway tracks** between cities are the *edges* (relationships). - **Distance, ticket price, train type** are the *properties* of each track. - **"Metropolitan", "Tourist", "Heritage"** labels group cities. When a passenger asks: *"What is the cheapest route from Kochi to Delhi visiting at most 2 intermediate cities?"* — a **relational database** would have to: 1. Build a Cartesian product across multiple `JOIN`ed tables. 2. Re-evaluate `JOIN` paths for every hop. A **graph database**, however, simply *walks the tracks* — because the tracks are real, persistent pointers (not recomputed at query time). The answer is delivered in the same number of milliseconds whether the network has 100 cities or 100 million. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition:** A relational database is like asking for directions by re-shuffling a giant address-book repeatedly. A graph database is like *following signboards already placed at every junction*. ## 1.3 The "Power" — Why It Matters The "power" of graph databases arises from three engineered advantages: 1. **Performance at Depth** — Traversal time is proportional to the *path length*, **not** to the *total dataset size**. 2. **Semantic Clarity** — Relationships are named and directional, expressing business meaning directly (e.g., `(:Employee)-[:MANAGES]->(:Department)`). 3. **Agile Schema Evolution** — Adding a new relationship type does not require a schema migration, table alters, or downtime. A standard industry metric is the **Connected Data Threshold** — empirically, when the average node has more than **5 relationships** in a query workload, graph databases begin to outperform RDBMS by **1 to 3 orders of magnitude**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Constant (Board-Favorite):** For a join across **N** tables on a dataset of size **M**, RDBMS complexity is typically $O(M \cdot \log M)$ per JOIN, while graph traversal is $O(k)$ where **k** = number of hops. This is the *raison d'être* of graph databases. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Graph Database "Power" The "power" can be decomposed into three orthogonal design pillars that the KTU board expects a student to articulate: ### Pillar I — Native Graph Storage (Index-Free Adjacency) - Each node stores **direct physical pointers** (memory/disk addresses) to its neighboring nodes. - Traversal is *pointer-chasing*, not *index lookup*. - Eliminates the overhead of B-Tree index descent at every hop. ### Pillar II — Declarative Graph Query Languages - Languages like **Cypher** (Neo4j), **GQL** (ISO/IEC 39075), and **Gremlin** enable *pattern matching* on the graph topology. - Example Cypher pattern (forbidden in this exam, but illustrates syntax): ```cypher MATCH (a:Person {name:'Kavya'})-[:FRIEND_OF]->(b:Person) -[:PURCHASED]->(p:Product) RETURN p.name, count(*) AS popularity ORDER BY popularity DESC ``` ### Pillar III — ACID + High Availability - Modern native graph DBs offer **full ACID transactions** (Neo4j is ACID-compliant since 2.0+). - Supports **Causal Consistency** in distributed deployments (Neo4j Enterprise, Cassandra-based JanusGraph). ## 2.2 Relational vs Graph — The Cost of JOINs The most important KTU 14-mark question pattern is the *complexity comparison*. Let us derive it formally. **Step A — Relational JOIN Cost:** For a query joining $k$ tables, each filtered on indexed attribute, the cost is dominated by the nested-loop or hash-join: $$ C_{RDBMS} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert R_i \vert \cdot \log \vert R_i \vert $$ **Step B — Graph Traversal Cost:** For a graph of $N$ nodes and $E$ edges, traversing $h$ hops from a source node: $$ C_{Graph} = O(h \cdot d_{avg}) $$ where $d_{avg}$ is the **average node degree**. **Step C — The Cross-Over Point:** Graphs become strictly superior when: $$ h \cdot d_{avg} \;\ll\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert R_i \vert \cdot \log \vert R_i \vert $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This is why social networks, fraud detection, recommendation engines, and knowledge graphs (Google Knowledge Graph contains **> 500 billion facts**) are predominantly modeled as graphs. Connectedness is the *primary* feature, not the exception. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Typical Value | Use Case | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Traversal Complexity | $O(h \cdot d_{avg})$ | $h \le 6$ in most OLTP | Social networks | | RDBMS Join Cost | $O(\vert R \vert \log \vert R \vert)$ | Grows with data | Tabular data | | **Property Graph** | $G = (V, E, L, P)$ | $P = KV$ pairs | Neo4j model | | Average Degree | $d_{avg} = 2E / N$ | $5$–$50$ typical | Network analysis | | Triangle Count | $T = \tfrac{1}{6} \sum d_i (d_i-1)$ | Density metric | Fraud rings | | Clustering Coeff. | $C_i = 2T_i / (k_i(k_i-1))$ | $0 \le C_i \le 1$ | Community detect | | Connected Threshold | $d_{avg} \ge 5$ | Empirical | RDB→Graph migrate | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe Symbol Rule:** All absolute-value / set-cardinality notations in the table above use `\vert` or `\mid`. Never write raw `\vert R \vert` inside a markdown table cell — it breaks parsing. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Banking (Fraud Detection):** PayPal reduced fraud-analysis query time from **> 10 minutes** (Oracle JOINs) to **< 30 seconds** (Neo4j graph), a 1200x improvement. - **Telecom (Networkx):** Modelling call-detail records to detect *wangiri* fraud rings. - **Healthcare:** Knowledge graphs linking symptoms → diseases → drugs → trials. - **Transportation:** Google Maps, Uber routing on road graphs. - **Recommendation Engines:** Neo4j-powered *real-time* "people-you-may-know" on social platforms. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why Graph Traversal is Constant in Path Length Let us rigorously derive the asymptotic advantage of graph databases over RDBMS for *multi-hop* queries. ### Step 1 — Define the Query We want all nodes reachable from a source node $v_0$ in exactly $h$ hops, traversing only edges of type $T$. ### Step 2 — RDBMS Execution Path A relational engine must re-evaluate a $k$-way self-JOIN on the *edges* (or *adjacency*) table for every hop: $$ Q_{RDBMS} = E \bowtie_{e_1.v = e_2.u} E \bowtie_{e_2.v = e_3.u} E \; \cdots \;\text{(h times)} $$ The intermediate result set at level $i$ is bounded by $\vert V \vert^{i}$. With B-Tree index lookup $\log \vert E \vert$ per probe: $$ C_{RDBMS}(h) = \sum_{i=1}^{h} \vert V \vert^{i} \cdot \log \vert E \vert $$ $$ C_{RDBMS}(h) = \log \vert E \vert \cdot \vert V \vert \cdot \frac{\vert V \vert^{h} - 1}{\vert V \vert - 1} $$ ### Step 3 — Graph Execution Path A native graph engine uses **index-free adjacency** — each node stores physical disk/memory pointers to neighbors. Each hop is a *single pointer dereference*: $$ C_{Graph}(h) = h \cdot d_{avg} $$ ### Step 4 — Compare the Two Taking the ratio: $$ R(h) = \frac{C_{RDBMS}(h)}{C_{Graph}(h)} = \frac{\log \vert E \vert \cdot \vert V \vert}{\,h \cdot d_{avg}\,} \cdot \frac{\vert V \vert^{h} - 1}{\vert V \vert - 1} $$ $$ R(h) \;\approx\; \frac{\log \vert E \vert \cdot \vert V \vert^{h}}{h \cdot d_{avg}} $$ This is an **exponential** ratio in $h$. For a social graph with $\vert V \vert = 10^{8}$ users and $h = 4$ hops: $$ R(4) \approx \frac{27 \cdot 10^{32}}{4 \cdot 50} \;\approx\; 1.35 \times 10^{31} $$ This is the **theoretical proof** of the "power" of graph databases. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What $$ T_{decision} = \arg\min_{DB \in \{RDBMS, Graph\}} \Big( C_{DB}(Q) \Big) $$ The KTU board loves this **decision matrix**: | Query Characteristic | RDBMS Wins | Graph Wins | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | Tabular, normalized (HR, Billing) | $\checkmark$ | — | | Highly connected (Social, Fraud) | — | $\checkmark$ | | Aggregations over millions of rows | $\checkmark$ | — | | Variable hop-depth traversals | — | $\checkmark$ | | Schema is rigid and stable | $\checkmark$ | — | | Schema evolves frequently | — | $\checkmark$ | | Reporting / OLAP cubes | $\checkmark$ | — | | Real-time path / recommendation | — | $\checkmark$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Aid for Exams:** "Use **G**raph for **G**rowing, **G**lobal, **G**lued data — Use **R**DBMS for **R**ows, **R**eports, **R**eliable schemas." ## 3.3 The CAP Triangle in Graph Context Although traditionally stated for distributed NoSQL, the **CAP Theorem** still applies: $$ C + A + P \le 2 \quad \text{(Pick any two)} $$ - **Neo4j** (Causal Cluster) → Prioritizes **C**onsistency and **P**artition tolerance. - **JanusGraph** (on Cassandra) → Prioritizes **A**vailability and **P**artition tolerance. - **Amazon Neptune** → Tunable; defaults to eventual consistency with read-replicas. KTU students must state CAP for the specific graph engine being discussed. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Property Graph Model — Visual Anatomy ```mermaid graph LR classDef node fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef edge fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff p1["Person: Kavya
age: 21
city: Kochi"] p2["Person: Arun
age: 22
city: Trivandrum"] c1["Company: Neo4j
HQ: San Mateo"] pr1["Product: GraphDB
price: 499"] p1 -- "FRIEND_OF since 2018" --> p2 p2 -- "WORKS_AT role Engineer" --> c1 c1 -- "MAKES" --> pr1 p1 -- "BOUGHT rating 5" --> pr1 class p1,p2,c1,pr1 node ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Native Graph DB ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef layer fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef engine fill:#d62728,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef store fill:#9467bd,stroke:#fff,color:#fff L1["User Application Layer
Cypher / GQL / Gremlin"]:::layer L2["Parser and Planner Layer
AST Builder + Cost Optimizer"]:::layer L3["Execution Engine
Iterator Pipeline + Traversal"]:::engine L4["Transactional Layer
ACID + WAL + Lock Manager"]:::engine L5["Native Graph Storage
Node Store + Relationship Store + Property Store"]:::store L6["Physical Disk
B Trees and Fixed Size Records"]:::store L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 L6 -. page cache .-> L3 ``` ## 4.3 RDBMS vs Graph DB — Query Flow Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef rdb fill:#e377c2,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef grp fill:#17becf,stroke:#fff,color:#fff subgraph RDBMS_Path direction TB R1["Parse SQL"]:::rdb --> R2["Resolve JOINs"]:::rdb R2 --> R3["Build Cartesian
then filter"]:::rdb R3 --> R4["Hash or Nested Loop
JOIN per hop"]:::rdb R4 --> R5["Materialize Result"]:::rdb end subgraph Graph_Path direction TB G1["Parse Cypher"]:::grp --> G2["Build Pattern AST"]:::grp G2 --> G3["Start at Match Node
Index Probe if needed"]:::grp G3 --> G4["Pointer Hop
Index Free Adjacency"]:::grp G4 --> G5["Stream Records"]:::grp end ``` ## 4.4 The "Power Triangle" — Capabilities of Graph Databases ```mermaid graph TD classDef cap fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#fff,color:#000 PERF["Performance
Constant time hops"]:::cap SEM["Semantic Expressiveness
Typed relationships"]:::cap AGI["Agility
Schema free evolution"]:::cap POWER["The Power of
Graph Databases"] PERF --> POWER SEM --> POWER AGI --> POWER ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define a graph database. List its four core building blocks. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (10 lines):** A **graph database** is a NoSQL data store that uses *graph structures* — consisting of **nodes**, **edges**, and **properties** — to represent and store data, with relationships treated as first-class citizens. The four core building blocks are: 1. **Nodes** — Discrete real-world entities (e.g., Person, Product). 2. **Edges** — Named, directed connections between nodes (e.g., `FRIEND_OF`, `PURCHASED`). 3. **Properties** — Key-value pairs annotating both nodes and edges. 4. **Labels** — Categorical tags that group nodes into sets, enabling efficient lookup. A graph database is *native* if it uses **index-free adjacency** at the storage layer, ensuring traversals remain proportional to path length rather than total dataset size. *(3 marks)* --- ### Q2. Differentiate between a relational database and a graph database in terms of relationship handling. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (8–10 lines):** | Aspect | RDBMS | Graph Database | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Relationship Storage | Foreign keys + JOIN tables | First-class edges with pointers | | Query Execution | JOIN recomputed at query time | Pointer traversal (index-free adjacency) | | Cost | $O(\vert R \vert \log \vert R \vert)$ per JOIN | $O(h \cdot d_{avg})$ per hop | | Schema | Rigid, predefined | Schema-optional, agile | | Performance Degradation | Exponential with JOIN depth | Constant w.r.t. dataset size | In RDBMS, relationships are *computed* via expensive multi-way JOINs at query time. In graph databases, relationships are *pre-materialized* as physical pointers between nodes, enabling direct traversal with predictable, low latency. *(3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Two internal-choice sub-parts, each 7 marks. Comprehensive model solutions are mandatory. ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q.A (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Property Graph Model**. Define each of its components with an example. *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** The **Property Graph Model** is the canonical data model used by graph databases such as Neo4j. It consists of five components: - **Vertices (Nodes):** Represent entities. - **Edges (Relationships):** Connect two nodes; always have a *type*, a *direction*, and may have a *name*. - **Properties:** Arbitrary key-value pairs on both nodes and edges. - **Labels:** Tag nodes into categories. - **Paths:** Sequences of alternating nodes and edges. **Example Schema (illustrative, not for coding-marking):** - Node `(:Person {name: "Kavya", age: 21})` — labeled `Person`. - Node `(:Company {name: "Neo4j"})` — labeled `Company`. - Edge `(:Person)-[:WORKS_AT {since: 2022}]->(:Company)` — directed, typed, with property. **Neat Diagram (Property Graph for a Mini Social Network):** ```mermaid graph LR A["Person: Kavya"] -- "FRIEND_OF" --> B["Person: Arun"] B -- "WORKS_AT" --> C["Company: Neo4j"] C -- "SOLD" --> D["Product: GraphDB"] A -- "BOUGHT" --> D ``` **Key Properties of the Model:** - Both nodes and edges can carry properties. - Relationships are *typed* and *directed*, encoding business meaning. - Labels enable *efficient subset queries* (similar to indexed class lookups in OOP). - Multiple labels per node are allowed (multi-classification). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Tip:** Always draw the diagram first, *then* define the components. Examiners award 2 marks for the diagram alone. *(7 marks)* --- **Q.A (b)** Compare the **performance of relational and graph databases** for multi-hop queries. Derive the asymptotic complexity of both and state the cross-over condition. *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Relational Cost (per hop):** For an $h$-hop query on a relational representation of a graph with $\vert V \vert$ nodes and $\vert E \vert$ edges, the cost is dominated by $h$ self-JOINs on the *edges* table: $$ C_{RDBMS}(h) = \log \vert E \vert \cdot \vert V \vert \cdot \frac{\vert V \vert^{h} - 1}{\vert V \vert - 1} $$ **Graph Cost (per hop):** For a native graph store with average degree $d_{avg}$: $$ C_{Graph}(h) = h \cdot d_{avg} $$ **Cross-Over Condition:** Graph DB strictly outperforms RDBMS when: $$ h \cdot d_{avg} \;\ll\; \log \vert E \vert \cdot \vert V \vert^{h} $$ **Numerical Illustration:** For a social network with $\vert V \vert = 10^{7}$, $\vert E \vert = 10^{9}$, $d_{avg} = 50$, $h = 4$: - RDBMS: $C \approx 30 \cdot 10^{7} \cdot 10^{28} \approx 3 \times 10^{36}$ operations. - Graph: $C = 4 \cdot 50 = 200$ pointer dereferences. The ratio is approximately $10^{34}$ — the *theoretical* justification for the "power" of graph databases. **Real-world Validation:** PayPal reported a 1200x speedup for fraud-graph queries when migrating from Oracle to Neo4j. Google Knowledge Graph serves queries over **> 500 billion facts** using graph storage. *(7 marks)* --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q.B (a)** Explain the concept of **index-free adjacency** with a diagram. Why is it considered the defining feature of a *native* graph database? *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Definition:** **Index-free adjacency** is a physical storage strategy in which every node maintains *direct disk/memory references* to each of its adjacent nodes and edges. Unlike a B-Tree index, no global lookup is needed at query time — the node itself *knows* where its neighbors live. **Diagram (Pointer Network):** ```mermaid graph TB classDef ptr fill:#8c564b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff A["Node: Kavya
next: 0x7F3A"]:::ptr B["Node: Arun
next: 0x7F3B"]:::ptr C["Node: Maya
next: 0x7F3C"]:::ptr A -- "ptr 0x7F3B" --> B A -- "ptr 0x7F3C" --> C B -- "ptr 0x7F3C" --> C ``` **Why It Is the Defining Feature:** 1. **Constant-Time Hops:** Each traversal step is one memory dereference, independent of total dataset size. 2. **Predictable Performance:** Query latency scales with *path length*, not *graph size*. 3. **No Index Maintenance Overhead:** Adding/removing edges updates only the local pointer, not a global index. 4. **Locality of Reference:** Sequential traversal benefits from CPU cache lines. A graph database *without* index-free adjacency (e.g., one that uses B-Tree indices to find neighbors) suffers the same exponential degradation as RDBMS and cannot legitimately be called "native." *(7 marks)* --- **Q.B (b)** Discuss **five real-world application domains** where graph databases provide a clear advantage over relational databases. Justify each with one specific use case. *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** | # | Domain | Use Case | Why Graph Wins | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Social Networks** | Friend-of-friend recommendations | 2nd/3rd-degree connections require 2–3 multi-hop JOINs — exponential cost in RDBMS | | 2 | **Fraud Detection** | Money-laundering ring detection | Path queries across 5–10 hops become trivial; PayPal uses Neo4j for real-time scoring | | 3 | **Knowledge Graphs** | Google's Knowledge Graph (500B+ facts) | Entity-relationship queries are core; semantic triples map directly to nodes/edges | | 4 | **Recommendation Engines** | "Customers who bought X also bought Y" | Path patterns express complex co-purchase relationships in single queries | | 5 | **Network/IT Operations** | Telco call routing, dependency graphs | Real-time shortest-path on infrastructure graphs (millions of devices) | | 6 *(bonus)* | **Bioinformatics** | Protein-protein interaction networks | Pathway analysis is inherently a graph problem with weighted edges | **Justification Sketch (for point 2):** In a money-laundering ring, the fraudster's account (A) sends small amounts to 10 "mule" accounts (B1…B10), which forward to 5 aggregator accounts (C1…C5), which finally deposit to a master account (D). Detecting this requires a 4-hop traversal: $A \to B \to C \to D$. An RDBMS would need 4 self-JOINs on the *transactions* table — a 100M-row cartesian. Neo4j performs this in milliseconds. *(7 marks)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing "graph database" with "graph visualization."** A chart library is *not* a graph DB. The board deducts up to 2 marks for this conflation. > 2. **Forgetting to state index-free adjacency.** Without this phrase, the answer to "what makes a graph database native" is considered *incomplete*. > 3. **Writing Python/Cypher code in the exam.** For 3-mark theory questions, pseudocode is acceptable but executable code is *not* rewarded extra and may be marked down if syntactically wrong. > 4. **Skipping the diagram.** 14-mark questions almost always allocate 2–3 marks to a *neat, labeled* diagram. Always include one. > 5. **Not stating the cross-over condition numerically.** Saying "graph is faster" without the $O(h \cdot d_{avg})$ vs $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ comparison loses 3–4 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - [x] **Graph Database** = nodes + edges + properties + labels, with *relationships as first-class citizens*. - [x] **Native Graph DB** = uses **index-free adjacency** at the storage layer. - [x] **Property Graph Model** is the de-facto model (Neo4j, JanusGraph, Neptune). - [x] **Traversal cost:** $O(h \cdot d_{avg})$ — independent of total graph size. - [x] **RDBMS JOIN cost:** $O(\vert R \vert \log \vert R \vert)$ per JOIN — exponential in hop count. - [x] **Connected Data Threshold:** $d_{avg} \ge 5$ triggers graph-DB dominance. - [x] **ACID:** Modern native graph DBs (Neo4j 2.0+) are fully ACID-compliant. - [x] **CAP positioning:** Neo4j → CP; JanusGraph (Cassandra backend) → AP. - [x] **Query Languages:** Cypher (Neo4j), Gremlin (TinkerPop), GQL (ISO standard). - [x] **Key Real-World Wins:** PayPal (1200x fraud), Google KG (500B facts), LinkedIn, Facebook TAO. - [x] **Decision Rule:** *Tabular/Reporting* → RDBMS; *Connected/Variable-hop* → Graph. - [x] **Industry Standard:** Neo4j is the most-deployed native graph DB as of the 2024 syllabus reference.

topics.title: Options for Storing Connected Data: Relational Database Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Options for Storing Connected Data: Relational Database Limitations # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is "Connected Data"? **Connected Data** is a class of data in which the *relationships* (associations, links, edges) between data entities carry first-class semantic meaning — often as much meaning as the entities (nodes, vertices) themselves. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim-aligned):** > *Connected data is information whose primary value is derived not from the individual data points, but from the way those data points are linked together into a network of relationships.* Formally, a connected dataset can be modelled as a graph: $$G = (V, E)$$ where: - $V$ = finite set of vertices (entities, nodes) - $E \subseteq V \times V$ = set of edges (relationships, links) Each edge $e \in E$ can be written as $e = (u, v)$ connecting vertices $u, v \in V$. A path of length $d$ between $u$ and $v$ is a sequence: $$P(u,v) = \langle v_0, v_1, v_2, \dots, v_d \rangle \quad \text{where} \quad v_0 = u, \; v_d = v, \; (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$$ ## 1.2 The Five Major Options for Storing Connected Data | # | Option | Native Relationship Support | Best Suited For | |---|--------|----------------------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Relational Databases (RDBMS)** | Foreign keys + JOINs (synthetic) | Structured, tabular data | | 2 | **Document Stores (NoSQL)** | Embedded sub-documents (limited) | Hierarchical, semi-structured data | | 3 | **Key-Value Stores** | None (flat lookup only) | Caching, session data | | 4 | **Column-Family Stores** | None (column grouping) | Analytical, time-series data | | 5 | **Graph Databases (Native)** | First-class edges, traversable | **Connected data (network, social, fraud)** | ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Phone Book vs. The Social Network" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy: Phone Book vs. Friendship Graph** > > Imagine you have two information systems in front of you: > > 1. **A Phone Book (RDBMS approach)** — Every person is a *row* in a giant ledger. To know "who knows whom", you must repeatedly flip between the *People table* and the *Relationships table*, performing **manual JOINs** in your head. To find a friend of a friend of a friend (3-hop query), you must flip through the book **three times** — and the work grows **exponentially** with depth. > > 2. **A Friendship Graph (Graph DB approach)** — Every person is a *node*, and every friendship is an *arrow* drawn on the map. To find a friend-of-a-friend, you simply **walk the arrows** from any starting node. The map size doesn't explode — you only traverse the *paths that actually exist*. > > **Takeaway:** A relational database *forces* the database engine to reconstruct the graph at query time using expensive JOINs. A native graph database *stores* the graph ready-made for traversal. ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU Syllabus Module 1 of *PECDT751 – Graph Databases and Analysis* establishes the **motivation** for the entire course. Before learning Cypher/GQL, Neo4j, or graph algorithms, students must internalise *why* a dedicated graph storage model exists when relational databases have dominated the industry for 40+ years. The relational limitations covered here are the **engineering rationale** for graph databases — they are the most frequently asked opening questions in KTU university exams. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Seven Major Limitations of Relational Databases for Connected Data ### Limitation 1 — The JOIN Performance Problem (Most Critical) Relational databases do not store relationships; they *infer* them at query time via the `JOIN` operation. For a graph of depth $d$ and average branching factor $k$, the result set of a recursive traversal grows as: $$R_d = N \cdot k^d$$ where: - $N$ = number of starting rows - $k$ = average out-degree (friends per person, connections per device) - $d$ = traversal depth (hops) **Example:** A social network with $k = 50$ friends per user: - Depth 2 (friend of friend): $50^2 = \mathbf{2{,}500}$ rows - Depth 3: $50^3 = \mathbf{125{,}000}$ rows - Depth 4: $50^4 = \mathbf{6{,}250{,}000}$ rows > [!NOTE] > The result set grows **exponentially** with depth, making deep traversals practically impossible in production RDBMS systems beyond $d = 3$ or $d = 4$. ### Limitation 2 — Schema Rigidity Relational schemas require a *fixed* set of relationship types at design time. Adding a new relationship type (e.g., "is_colleague_of", "has_visited") requires: 1. `ALTER TABLE` operations 2. Migration scripts 3. Downtime planning 4. Application code updates Connected data in the real world is **schema-on-read**, not schema-on-write. ### Limitation 3 — Impedance Mismatch (Object-Relational Impedance) Application code uses **objects/pointers** (rich, cyclic, free-form). Relational tables use **flat tuples** (rigid, acyclic, normalized). Mapping between them requires an **ORM (Object-Relational Mapper)**, which: - Loses relationship semantics - Forces artificial primary/foreign key plumbing - Hides traversals behind complex query construction ### Limitation 4 — Recursive Query Complexity (CTEs) To traverse arbitrary depth, RDBMS requires **Recursive Common Table Expressions (CTEs)** — a feature added late to the SQL standard (SQL:1999) and still inconsistently supported. ```sql -- A 3-level recursive traversal in SQL WITH RECURSIVE friends_chain AS ( SELECT friend_id, 1 AS depth FROM friends WHERE person_id = 42 UNION ALL SELECT f.friend_id, fc.depth + 1 FROM friends f JOIN friends_chain fc ON f.person_id = fc.friend_id WHERE fc.depth < 3 ) SELECT * FROM friends_chain; ``` Such queries: - Are **declaratively opaque** to optimise - Stack up **multiple JOIN operations** internally - Are bounded by `MAXRECURSION` (e.g., 100 in SQL Server, 32767 in PostgreSQL) - Often **under-perform** even with materialised path columns ### Limitation 5 — No Native Path / Variable-Length Semantics SQL has no first-class concept of "any path of any length between two nodes". Workarounds use: - **Adjacency lists** (deep JOINs) - **Nested sets** (rewrites on every insert) - **Materialised paths** (string manipulation, fragile) - **Closure tables** (extra $O(N^2)$ storage) > [!IMPORTANT] > A **native graph database** supports a single, optimised operator — typically `MATCH p = (a)-[*1..N]-(b)` (Neo4j Cypher) — that returns all paths of length 1 to N in **one operation**. ### Limitation 6 — Lack of Relationship Semantics & Properties In RDBMS, a relationship is *implied* by matching keys in two tables. A relationship **cannot have its own properties** (e.g., "since 2018", "weight: 0.8", "role: manager"). To add relationship properties, you must create a **junction/bridge table**: ```sql CREATE TABLE person_knows_person ( person_id_1 BIGINT, person_id_2 BIGINT, since DATE, strength DECIMAL(3,2), relationship VARCHAR(20), PRIMARY KEY (person_id_1, person_id_2) ); ``` This adds *another* JOIN to every traversal, multiplying the cost. ### Limitation 7 — Referential Integrity & Maintenance Cost Enforcing relationship validity (no orphan rows) requires: - `FOREIGN KEY` constraints - `ON DELETE CASCADE` policies - Trigger logic - Application-layer validation The **maintenance cost is proportional to the number of relationships**, not the data size — directly penalising dense, evolving graphs. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Complexity Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it appears in nearly every KTU Module-1 exam.** | # | Concept | Relational DB Cost | Graph DB Cost | Notes | |---|---------|--------------------|---------------|-------| | 1 | Single-record lookup by primary key | $O(\log N)$ | $O(1)$ | B-tree vs. hash direct | | 2 | 1-hop traversal (neighbour) | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(k)$ | RDBMS must scan FK column | | 3 | $d$-hop traversal (path of length $d$) | $O(N \cdot k^d)$ | $O(k^d)$ | Exponential blow-up in RDBMS | | 4 | Shortest path (BFS) | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert + R \cdot k^d)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Native index-free adjacency | | 5 | All paths of length $\leq d$ | $O(k^d)$ (per pair) | $O(k^d)$ (lazy) | RDBMS materialises all; GDB streams | | 6 | Variable-length pattern match | Not natively supported | $O(k^d)$ (bounded) | Cypher `[*1..N]` | | 7 | Subgraph isomorphism | $O(\vert V \vert^{\vert V' \vert})$ NP-hard | Heuristic-optimised | Hard in both; GDB has tooling | | 8 | Recursive CTE depth bound | $\leq 32{,}767$ (Postgres) | Unlimited (bounded by memory) | RDBMS hard limit | > **Notation key:** $N$ = rows, $k$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth, $\vert V \vert$ = vertices, $\vert E \vert$ = edges, $R$ = result set size. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility These limitations are not academic — they hit production systems every day: 1. **Social Networks (Facebook, LinkedIn):** Friend-of-friend recommendations. RDBMS cannot serve in real time; Facebook built **TAO** (a custom graph-on-top-of-MySQL layer). 2. **Fraud Detection (PayPal, banks):** "Find all accounts reachable from this suspect in 6 hops." RDBMS query times blow up; graph DBs answer in milliseconds. 3. **Network & IT Operations:** Cisco, Deutsche Telekom — device-dependency graphs of millions of nodes. 4. **Knowledge Graphs (Google, Wikidata):** Billions of typed edges; RDBMS would require hundreds of JOINs per query. 5. **Bioinformatics & Drug Discovery:** Protein–protein interaction networks, pathway analysis. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Result-Set Explosion in Recursive JOINs **Problem Setup:** We have a `person` table with $N$ rows and a `knows` (foreign-key join) table. Each person knows an average of $k$ other people. We wish to find *all friends of friends* of person $P_0$ (depth = 2). ### Step 1 — Define the data - $N$ total persons. - Each person has on average $k$ outgoing `knows` edges. - $P_0$ is the starting vertex. ### Step 2 — Depth-1 traversal From $P_0$, we JOIN once. The result set has $k$ rows (the direct friends): $$R_1 = k$$ ### Step 3 — Depth-2 traversal For each of the $k$ friends, we JOIN the `knows` table again. Each friend has $k$ friends, but we must count with overlap. **Upper bound** (worst case, no overlap): $$R_2 = k \cdot k = k^2$$ ### Step 4 — Depth-$d$ generalisation By induction, the result-set size at depth $d$ is: $$R_d = k^d$$ **Including the source row** (some textbooks count it): $$R_d^{\text{incl. source}} = 1 + k + k^2 + \dots + k^d = \frac{k^{d+1} - 1}{k - 1}, \quad k \neq 1$$ ### Step 5 — Big-O complexity $$\boxed{\,R_d = O(k^d)\,}$$ This is **exponential** in $d$, with base $k$. ### Step 6 — Numerical example (KTU favourite) Assume $k = 30$ (typical Facebook user): $$\begin{aligned} R_1 &= 30 \\ R_2 &= 30^2 = 900 \\ R_3 &= 30^3 = 27{,}000 \\ R_4 &= 30^4 = 810{,}000 \\ R_5 &= 30^5 = 24{,}300{,}000 \end{aligned}$$ By depth 5, we have 24 million rows — and we have not even added attribute filters. This is the **JOIN explosion** that motivates graph databases. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — JOIN Cost with B-tree Indexing **Setup:** The `knows` table is indexed on `person_id` using a B-tree. We perform a hash-merge or nested-loop JOIN. ### Step 1 — Cost of a single B-tree lookup For a table with $M$ rows and B-tree of order $b$: $$C_{\text{lookup}} = O(\log_b M)$$ ### Step 2 — Cost of one JOIN step (depth 1) We look up $k$ rows in the `knows` table: $$C_1 = k \cdot \log_b M$$ ### Step 3 — Cost of depth $d$ We perform the lookup $k^d$ times (once per accumulated row), so: $$C_d = k^d \cdot \log_b M$$ ### Step 4 — Full big-O statement $$\boxed{\,C_d = O(k^d \log_b M)\,}$$ The logarithmic factor $\log_b M$ is the **only** relief. The exponential $k^d$ dominates. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Why Index-Free Adjacency Wins In a **native graph database** (Neo4j-style), each vertex stores direct pointers to its neighbours — the relationship is a first-class object with a physical pointer. ### Cost of 1-hop traversal - **Graph DB:** $O(1)$ — follow the pointer directly. - **RDBMS:** $O(\log_b M)$ — B-tree lookup on foreign-key index. ### Cost of $d$-hop traversal - **Graph DB:** $O(k^d)$ — but **no index lookup per hop**, just pointer chasing. Cache-friendly. - **RDBMS:** $O(k^d \log_b M)$ — and the index lookups are **random I/O**, cache-hostile. ### Speed-up factor $$\boxed{\,S_d = \frac{C_d^{\text{RDBMS}}}{C_d^{\text{Graph}}} = \log_b M\,}$$ In practice, with $M = 10^7$ rows and $b = 100$: $$S_d = \log_{100} 10^7 \approx 3.5$$ But the *real* speed-up comes from **cache locality** of pointer chasing vs. **disk I/O** of B-tree lookups, often yielding 100×–1000× in production benchmarks (per Neo4j's published TPC-H-like benchmarks). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Performance Matrix — Depth vs. Storage Option > [!NOTE] > **Read this matrix column-by-column. It is the heart of Module 1.** | Traversal Depth $d$ | RDBMS Rows Generated | RDBMS Query Time (s, indicative) | Graph DB Time (s) | Recommendation | |:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | 1 | $30$ | $0.001$ | $0.0001$ | RDBMS fine | | 2 | $900$ | $0.01$ | $0.0005$ | RDBMS fine | | 3 | $27{,}000$ | $0.3$ | $0.002$ | RDBMS borderline | | 4 | $810{,}000$ | $9$ | $0.01$ | **Graph DB wins** | | 5 | $24{,}300{,}000$ | $270$ | $0.05$ | **Graph DB required** | | 6 | $729{,}000{,}000$ | hours | $0.3$ | **Graph DB only** | **Generalised rule of thumb** (often cited in KTU viva): > A relational database **degrades below interactive responsiveness somewhere between $d = 3$ and $d = 4$** for typical real-world branching factors. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Storage Cost of Closure Table Workaround A common RDBMS workaround for graph queries is the **closure table**: store *every* reachable pair $(u, v)$ with its path length. ### Step 1 — Number of reachable pairs For an undirected graph with $N$ vertices and average degree $k$, the number of reachable pairs up to distance $D$ is: $$P_D = \sum_{d=1}^{D} N \cdot k^d = N \cdot \frac{k^{D+1} - k}{k - 1}$$ ### Step 2 — For $N = 10^5$, $k = 30$, $D = 5$ $$\begin{aligned} P_5 &= 10^5 \cdot \frac{30^6 - 30}{29} \\ &= 10^5 \cdot \frac{729{,}000{,}000 - 30}{29} \\ &\approx 2.51 \times 10^{12} \text{ rows} \end{aligned}$$ This is **2.5 trillion rows** to support a 5-hop query — clearly infeasible. $$\boxed{\,P_D = O(N \cdot k^D)\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** No RDBMS workaround (closure table, nested sets, materialised path) escapes the **exponential storage cost** of the connected-data problem. Only a *native* graph storage model — with **index-free adjacency** — solves it at scale. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Relational Storage of a Social Graph (JOIN Explosion) The following Mermaid diagram shows how a *friends-of-friends* query is decomposed into 3 sequential JOINs in an RDBMS, producing exponential row counts at each step. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["PERSON P0 : 1 row"] --> B["JOIN knows : k = 30 rows"] B --> C["JOIN knows again : k squared = 900 rows"] C --> D["JOIN knows third time : k cubed = 27,000 rows"] D --> E["Materialised result set : 27K tuples transferred"] A:::src B:::hop1 C:::hop2 D:::hop3 E:::out classDef src fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#a0522d,stroke-width:2px classDef hop1 fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#daa520,stroke-width:2px classDef hop2 fill:#fffacd,stroke:#eee8aa,stroke-width:2px classDef hop3 fill:#fafad2,stroke:#bdb76b,stroke-width:2px classDef out fill:#ff6347,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` **Read this diagram as:** Each `JOIN` box is a separate SQL operation that the RDBMS optimiser plans and executes. The output of one JOIN becomes the input of the next. The total work compounds. --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Relational Table Layout (Person + Junction Table) This diagram shows the **physical schema** of storing a friendship graph in a relational database. Note the extra `junction` table and the extra `JOIN` it forces. ```mermaid flowchart LR P["PERSON_TABLE
person_id : PK
name
dob"]:::tab1 J["PERSON_KNOWS_PERSON
person_id_1 : FK
person_id_2 : FK
since : DATE
strength : DECIMAL"]:::tab2 Q["QUERY: P0 friends-of-friends
SELECT * FROM PERSON
JOIN PERSON_KNOWS_PERSON k1
JOIN PERSON_KNOWS_PERSON k2
JOIN PERSON p2 ON k2.person_id_2 = p2.person_id"]:::q P -->|person_id_1| J P -->|person_id_2| J J --> Q P --> Q classDef tab1 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:2px classDef tab2 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px classDef q fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px ``` **Key observation:** The junction table `PERSON_KNOWS_PERSON` is required to model a *single* relationship type. If we add 10 more relationship types (works_with, visited, etc.), we add 10 more junction tables — and 10 more JOINs per query. --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block Architecture: RDBMS Query Processing Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD Q["SQL Recursive CTE Query"]:::in P["Parser / Lexer"]:::blk O["Query Optimiser
(Cost-Based)"]:::blk E["Execution Engine
Nested Loop / Hash Join"]:::blk J1["JOIN pass 1 : depth 1"]:::j J2["JOIN pass 2 : depth 2"]:::j J3["JOIN pass 3 : depth 3"]:::j RS["Materialised Result Set : N x k cubed rows"]:::out D["Disk I/O : B-tree index seeks"]:::io Q --> P --> O --> E E --> J1 --> J2 --> J3 --> RS E -.->|random I/O| D J1 -.-> D J2 -.-> D J3 -.-> D classDef in fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#4b0082,stroke-width:2px classDef blk fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px classDef j fill:#ffdab9,stroke:#d2691e,stroke-width:2px classDef out fill:#ff6347,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef io fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px ``` **Architecture insight:** The execution engine is **stateless with respect to relationships**. Every traversal requires re-reading the B-tree from disk, since no traversal memory is preserved across operations. This is the fundamental architectural difference from a graph database. --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Conceptual Comparison: RDBMS vs. Graph DB Storage Model ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RDB["RDBMS Storage Model"] R1["Table: PERSON"]:::r R2["Table: PERSON_KNOWS_PERSON
(junction, 3rd normal form)"]:::r R3["Query: reconstruct graph via JOINs"]:::r end subgraph GDB["Graph DB Storage Model"] G1["Node: Person (with labels and properties)"]:::g G2["Edge: KNOWS (first-class, with properties)"]:::g G3["Traversal: follow pointers directly"]:::g end R1 --- R2 --- R3 G1 --- G2 --- G3 R3 -.->|exponential cost| X["Slow at depth > 3"]:::bad G3 -.->|linear in result| Y["Fast at any depth"]:::good classDef r fill:#ffe4e1,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px classDef g fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px classDef bad fill:#ff6347,stroke:#8b0000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef good fill:#32cd32,stroke:#006400,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px ``` **Take-home message:** The same logical query becomes a *reconstruction problem* in RDBMS but a *direct navigation* in a graph database. This is the architectural foundation of the entire PECDT751 course. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **"List and briefly explain any three limitations of relational databases when used for storing connected data."** **Model Answer (3 marks, ~80–100 words):** > 1. **JOIN Performance Degradation:** Recursive traversals require multiple JOINs whose result sets grow exponentially — $O(k^d)$ — making queries beyond 3 or 4 hops impractical in production. > 2. **Schema Rigidity:** Relationship types must be defined at design time. Adding a new relationship (e.g., "supervised_by") requires `ALTER TABLE`, migration, and application changes — unsuited to evolving graph data. > 3. **No Native Relationship Semantics:** A relationship is *implied* via foreign-key matching and cannot carry properties (e.g., "since 2018", "strength 0.8") without a junction table — adding yet another JOIN to every traversal. *(Award 1 mark per correctly explained limitation; ½ mark for partial explanation.)* --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **"What is meant by the 'impedance mismatch' between object-oriented applications and relational databases? How does it affect connected data storage?"** **Model Answer (3 marks, ~80–100 words):** > The **object-relational impedance mismatch** is the conceptual gap between the way applications model data (rich, cyclic, pointer-based objects) and the way RDBMS stores it (flat, acyclic, normalised tuples). Bridging this gap requires an ORM layer, which: > > - Hides relationship traversal behind complex query construction. > - Forces every link to be expressed as a primary/foreign-key pair. > - Loses native semantics such as direction, weight, or type of relationship. > > For connected data — where relationships *are* the primary information — this mismatch is severe: the RDBMS must repeatedly materialise the graph from tables at query time, paying a heavy performance and code-complexity cost. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout** > > - Students who write *only* definitions without **numerical examples** of the JOIN explosion **lose 2–3 marks** consistently. Always illustrate with a worked numerical example (e.g., $k=30$, compute $R_3, R_4$). > - Students who list limitations **without ordering them by severity** (JOIN performance first) are penalised. Always start with the JOIN problem. > - Avoid stating that "RDBMS cannot store connected data" — it **can**, just inefficiently. Frame it as *performance, scalability, and semantic* limitations. > - In 14-mark answers, **a diagram is mandatory**. KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for a clear schematic (Mermaid-style or hand-drawn equivalent). --- ### PART B — Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** With a suitable example, explain the **JOIN performance problem** of relational databases when used to store highly connected data. Derive the expression for the size of the result set at depth $d$ and comment on its complexity. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **storage and query approaches** of a relational database and a native graph database for a social network with one million users and average degree 30. Discuss the cost of finding friends-of-friends up to depth 5 in both systems. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A(a) (7 marks) **Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):** Consider a social network stored relationally with: - A `PERSON` table (one row per user). - A `KNOWS` junction table linking `person_id_1` to `person_id_2`. - Average out-degree $k = 30$ (each person knows 30 others on average). To find *all people within 2 hops* of person $P_0$, we write a recursive CTE that performs **two successive JOINs** on the `KNOWS` table. **Step 2 — Derivation of result-set size (3 marks):** Let $R_d$ be the result-set size at depth $d$. $$\begin{aligned} R_1 &= k \quad \text{(direct friends)} \\ R_2 &= k \cdot k = k^2 \quad \text{(friends of friends)} \\ R_d &= k^d \quad \text{(by induction on } d \text{)} \end{aligned}$$ Including the source row and all intermediate depths: $$R_d^{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=0}^{d} k^i = \frac{k^{d+1} - 1}{k - 1}$$ **Step 3 — Numerical illustration (2 marks):** With $k = 30$: $$\begin{aligned} R_1 &= 30 \\ R_2 &= 900 \\ R_3 &= 27{,}000 \\ R_4 &= 810{,}000 \\ R_5 &= 24{,}300{,}000 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Complexity comment (1 mark):** The complexity is $O(k^d)$ — **exponential** in traversal depth $d$. Beyond $d = 3$ or $4$, the result set exceeds practical limits of memory and disk I/O, making the query non-interactive. This is the core motivation for graph databases. --- #### Model Solution — Question A(b) (7 marks) **Step 1 — RDBMS storage & query (2 marks):** - **Storage:** $N = 10^6$ rows in `PERSON`, and roughly $N \cdot k = 30 \times 10^6$ rows in `KNOWS` (since each of $10^6$ persons has 30 outgoing edges). - **Query:** A recursive CTE on `KNOWS` joined 4 times for depth 4. - **Cost (using the derived formula):** $R_4 = k^4 = 30^4 = 810{,}000$ rows materialised. - **Cost for depth 5:** $R_5 = 30^5 = 24{,}300{,}000$ rows. - **Time:** Each row requires a B-tree index lookup ($\log_b M$ with $M = 3 \times 10^7$); with disk I/O, the query takes **seconds to minutes** in practice. **Step 2 — Graph DB storage & query (2 marks):** - **Storage:** $N$ nodes + $N \cdot k$ edges, each with a *direct pointer* to neighbour (index-free adjacency). - **Query:** A single `MATCH p = (a)-[*1..5]-(b) WHERE id(a) = 42 RETURN p` (Cypher-equivalent). - **Cost:** $O(k^d)$ in pointer-chasing operations, all in memory; no intermediate materialisation. - **Time:** Typically **milliseconds**, even at depth 5 or 6. **Step 3 — Comparative analysis (2 marks):** | Aspect | RDBMS | Graph DB | |---|---|---| | Storage rows/edges | $\sim 31$ million | $\sim 31$ million (similar) | | Depth-5 query latency | Seconds–minutes | Milliseconds | | Schema change for new relation | `ALTER TABLE` | Add label/type — no migration | | Relationship properties | Junction table (extra JOIN) | First-class edge property | | Best fit | Depth $\leq 3$ | Any depth | **Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):** For a social network with 1 million users and 5-hop queries, a graph database provides orders-of-magnitude better performance. RDBMS remains preferable only for shallow, highly structured, transactional workloads. --- ### PART B — Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]` **(a)** Explain **schema rigidity** and the **lack of native relationship semantics** as two key limitations of relational databases in the context of graph data. Provide one example for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **recursive CTE mechanism** in SQL. With an example, show how a 3-hop traversal is expressed and discuss the practical limitations (depth bounds, performance, maintainability). Compare it with the **variable-length pattern** in Cypher. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B(a) (7 marks) **Schema Rigidity (3.5 marks):** > [!NOTE] > A relational schema requires the full set of relationship types to be known *at design time*. Each relationship is encoded as either a foreign-key column or a junction table. **Example (1.5 marks):** Consider a knowledge graph for a university. Initially, we model `Person` and `Course` with `Person ENROLLED_IN Course`. Six months later, the university wants to add a new relation `Person CO_SUPERVISES Person` (PhD co-supervision). In an RDBMS this requires: - A new junction table `PERSON_COSUPERVISES_PERSON`. - An `ALTER TABLE` migration on a live production database. - Updating ORM mappings and application code. - Re-deploying services. In a graph database, we simply add a new edge type — no schema change, no downtime. **Lack of Native Relationship Semantics (3.5 marks):** A relationship in an RDBMS is *implied* by matching foreign keys across two rows; it has no independent identity, no direction, no properties. **Example (1.5 marks):** To represent that "Alice knows Bob since 2018 with strength 0.9", an RDBMS requires the junction table `PERSON_KNOWS_PERSON` with columns `(person_id_1, person_id_2, since, strength)`. Every query that traverses this relationship must perform an *additional* JOIN against this junction table. The relationship itself is not a first-class object. In contrast, in a graph database (Neo4j), the edge `(Alice)-[:KNOWS {since: 2018, strength: 0.9}]->(Bob)` carries these properties *natively*, with no extra storage or join cost. --- #### Model Solution — Question B(b) (7 marks) **Step 1 — Recursive CTE explanation (2 marks):** A **Recursive Common Table Expression (CTE)** is a SQL construct (standardised in SQL:1999) that allows a query to reference its own output, enabling hierarchical or graph traversals. It has two parts: - **Anchor member** — the base case (starting rows). - **Recursive member** — the iteration, joined to the CTE itself, terminated by a `UNION ALL` and a depth/cycle guard. **Step 2 — Example: 3-hop traversal (2 marks):** ```sql WITH RECURSIVE network(person_id, depth) AS ( SELECT 42, 0 -- anchor: start at person 42 UNION ALL SELECT k.friend_id, n.depth + 1 FROM network n JOIN KNOWS k ON k.person_id = n.person_id WHERE n.depth < 3 -- up to 3 hops ) SELECT * FROM network; ``` This produces $1 + 30 + 900 + 27{,}000 = 27{,}931$ rows in our $k=30$ example. **Step 3 — Practical limitations (2 marks):** - **Depth bound:** Most RDBMS engines impose a hard `MAXRECURSION` (e.g., 100 in SQL Server, 32,767 in PostgreSQL) — large graphs cannot be fully traversed. - **Performance:** Each recursive step triggers a new JOIN with B-tree index seeks — random I/O grows with result-set size. - **Maintainability:** Recursive CTEs are declaratively complex; query optimisers often choose poor plans, forcing manual hints or materialised sub-tables. - **No path return:** CTEs return *vertices*, not *paths*. Recovering the actual sequence of edges requires additional self-joins. **Step 4 — Cypher comparison (1 mark):** Cypher (Neo4j) expresses the same query in one line: ```cypher MATCH p = (a:Person {id: 42})-[*1..3]-(b) RETURN p; ``` - Variable-length pattern `[*1..3]` is **native** to the language. - The full **path** `p` (sequence of nodes and edges) is returned, with all edge properties. - No recursion boilerplate; query planner optimises pointer traversal directly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic 1** - ✅ **Connected data** = entities *plus* the relationships between them; both carry semantic value. - ✅ The **graph model** $G = (V, E)$ is the natural representation; $E \subseteq V \times V$. - ✅ Storage options: **RDBMS, Document, Key-Value, Column-Family, Graph DB** — only the last has *first-class* relationships. - ✅ The **#1 limitation** of RDBMS for connected data is the **JOIN performance problem**: $R_d = O(k^d)$ — exponential in depth. - ✅ With $k = 30$, depth 3 yields **27,000 rows**; depth 5 yields **24.3 million rows**. - ✅ With B-tree indexing, the per-hop cost is $O(\log_b M)$, giving total cost $O(k^d \log_b M)$. - ✅ **Schema rigidity**: new relationship types require `ALTER TABLE` and migration. - ✅ **Impedance mismatch**: object graphs vs. flat tuples; ORM overhead and lost semantics. - ✅ **Recursive CTEs** are the SQL workaround but are bounded by `MAXRECURSION` (often 100–32,767) and degrade exponentially. - ✅ **No native relationship properties** — junction tables are needed, adding more JOINs. - ✅ **Closure-table workaround** has storage cost $O(N \cdot k^D)$ — also exponential, so no escape. - ✅ **Native graph DBs** use **index-free adjacency**: direct pointers from node to neighbour — $O(1)$ per hop. - ✅ The **threshold** beyond which RDBMS becomes impractical for traversal is **$d \approx 3$ to $4$** for typical $k$. - ✅ KTU exam must-show: **one worked numerical example** + **one diagram** + **ordered list of limitations** (JOIN first). - ✅ **Reference architectures** to cite: Facebook TAO, Google Knowledge Graph, PayPal fraud detection, Neo4j benchmarks.

topics.title: NoSQL Databases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** NoSQL Databases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 1 — NoSQL Databases

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **NoSQL (Not Only SQL)** is a class of database management systems that provide a mechanism for storage, retrieval, and management of data that is **designed for distributed data stores** where scale, flexibility, and high availability are prioritized over the strict relational, tabular, and ACID-compliant characteristics of traditional Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, NoSQL databases are **non-relational, schema-flexible, horizontally scalable** data stores that emerged to address the **"Three V" challenges of Big Data** — **Volume, Velocity, and Variety** — which traditional RDBMS struggle to handle efficiently.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🧠 The Library Analogy**
> Imagine a **traditional library (RDBMS)**: every book must be pre-catalogued with a fixed structure (title, author, ISBN, year, genre). Adding a new field like "movie adaptation" requires redesigning the entire catalogue system.
> Now imagine a **modern warehouse of mixed boxes (NoSQL)**: each box can have any shape, any label, any combination of items. You can throw in books, gadgets, documents, and art — all together. You don't need a librarian to reshelve everything when a new product arrives. This is the essence of NoSQL — **flexibility over rigidity, scale over structure, agility over uniformity**.

### 1.3 Why NoSQL? — Motivation & Origin

The early 2000s explosion of **web 2.0 platforms** (Google, Facebook, Amazon, Twitter) exposed the limitations of RDBMS for:
- **Petabyte-scale data** distributed across thousands of servers
- **Semi-structured and unstructured data** (JSON, XML, social graphs, IoT sensor streams)
- **High write-throughput** for millions of users simultaneously
- **Horizontal scaling** across commodity hardware

Google's **BigTable (2006)** and Amazon's **Dynamo (2007)** whitepapers were the foundational academic works that defined the NoSQL movement.

### 1.4 Key Characteristics of NoSQL Databases

| # | Characteristic | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Schema-less / Dynamic Schema** | No predefined table structure; fields can differ row to row |
| 2 | **Horizontally Scalable** | Scale by adding more servers (sharding), not by upgrading one (vertical) |
| 3 | **Eventually Consistent** | Trades strict ACID for the BASE model for higher availability |
| 4 | **Distributed by Design** | Built to run across clusters of commodity machines |
| 5 | **Open & Flexible Data Models** | Supports key-value, document, columnar, and graph models |
| 6 | **No (or minimal) JOINs** | Denormalization and embedding replace relational joins |
| 7 | **High Throughput / Low Latency** | Optimized for read/write-heavy workloads at massive scale |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight (2024 Scheme):** When answering exam questions, always emphasize that NoSQL stands for **"Not Only SQL"**, NOT "No SQL". This is a frequent MCQ trap.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 RDBMS vs NoSQL — Foundational Comparison

| Parameter | RDBMS | NoSQL |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Model** | Tabular (rows & columns) | Key-Value, Document, Column, Graph |
| **Schema** | Fixed / Rigid | Dynamic / Schema-less |
| **Scaling** | Vertical (scale-up) | Horizontal (scale-out) |
| **Transactions** | ACID-compliant | BASE model / Eventual Consistency |
| **Query Language** | SQL (standardized) | Varies (CQL, MQL, GQL, REST APIs) |
| **Joins** | First-class citizens | Typically avoided (denormalized) |
| **Consistency** | Strong | Tunable (eventual / causal / strong) |
| **Best For** | Structured, transactional OLTP | Big Data, semi/unstructured, real-time |
| **Examples** | MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle | MongoDB, Cassandra, Redis, Neo4j |

## 2.2 The ACID vs BASE Paradigm

### 2.2.1 ACID Properties (RDBMS — Traditional)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ACID** stands for **Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability** — the strict reliability model of RDBMS.

- **Atomicity** — A transaction is "all or nothing". Either every operation completes, or none do.
- **Consistency** — Every transaction takes the database from one valid state to another, preserving all defined rules (constraints, triggers, cascades).
- **Isolation** — Concurrent transactions do not interfere with each other; intermediate states are invisible.
- **Durability** — Once a transaction commits, the result is permanently persisted, even against power loss or crashes.

### 2.2.2 BASE Properties (NoSQL — Distributed)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BASE** stands for **Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency** — the relaxed reliability model of NoSQL.

- **Basically Available** — The system guarantees availability (it always responds), even if some nodes fail.
- **Soft State** — The state of the system may change over time, even without input, due to eventual consistency propagation.
- **Eventual Consistency** — Given enough time (and no new updates), all replicas of a data item will converge to the same value.

### 2.2.3 ACID vs BASE Comparison Table

| Property | ACID (RDBMS) | BASE (NoSQL) |
|---|---|---|
| **Consistency Model** | Strong, immediate | Eventual, convergent |
| **Availability Focus** | Lower (during partitions) | Higher (always responds) |
| **Transaction Scope** | Multi-row, multi-table | Single-document / single-key |
| **Suitable For** | Banking, ERP, Inventory | Social media, IoT, Analytics |
| **Latency** | Higher (locking overhead) | Lower (no locks) |

## 2.3 The CAP Theorem (Brewer's Theorem — 2000)

> [!NOTE]
> **CAP Theorem** states that in any distributed data store, you can only simultaneously guarantee **two out of the following three** properties: **Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance**.

- **Consistency (C):** Every read receives the most recent write or an error.
- **Availability (A):** Every request receives a (non-error) response, even if some nodes are down.
- **Partition Tolerance (P):** The system continues to operate despite arbitrary message loss (network splits) between nodes.

### 2.3.1 CAP Trade-offs

| Choice | Pair Promised | Sacrifice | Example Systems |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CA** | Consistency + Availability | Partition Tolerance (single-site, fragile) | Traditional RDBMS (PostgreSQL) |
| **CP** | Consistency + Partition Tolerance | Availability (may reject requests during partitions) | MongoDB (default), HBase, Redis |
| **AP** | Availability + Partition Tolerance | Consistency (returns stale data during partitions) | Cassandra, CouchDB, DynamoDB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** P (Partition Tolerance) is **mandatory** in modern distributed systems because network failures are inevitable. Hence, the real-world choice is between **CP** and **AP** — not CA.

## 2.4 The Four Major Types of NoSQL Databases

### 2.4.1 Key-Value Store

- **Data Model:** A giant hash table → `{key : value}` pairs
- **Value** is opaque (a blob, JSON, binary, image) — the database doesn't understand the structure
- **Operations:** `GET(key)`, `SET(key, value)`, `DELETE(key)`
- **Strengths:** O(1) read/write, blazing fast, ideal for caching and session storage
- **Weaknesses:** Cannot query on value contents; no secondary indexes
- **Examples:** Redis, Amazon DynamoDB, Riak, Memcached

### 2.4.2 Document Store

- **Data Model:** Documents (typically JSON, BSON, or XML) with nested fields
- **Schema-less:** Each document in a collection can have a different structure
- **Operations:** CRUD on documents, field-level queries, aggregation pipelines
- **Strengths:** Natural fit for object-oriented programming; rich querying; secondary indexes
- **Weaknesses:** No true joins (workaround via `$lookup` or embedding)
- **Examples:** MongoDB, CouchDB, Amazon DocumentDB, Firebase Firestore

**Sample Document (MongoDB BSON):**
```json
{
  "_id": "stu_1024",
  "name": "Ananya Pillai",
  "branch": "CSE",
  "skills": ["Python", "Neo4j", "MongoDB"],
  "address": { "city": "Kochi", "pin": 682001 },
  "cgpa": 9.12
}
```

### 2.4.3 Column-Family (Wide-Column) Store

- **Data Model:** Rows identified by a row key, each row contains a flexible set of column families, each family having many columns
- **Conceptual Layout:** Two-dimensional map → `row_key → {column_family : {column : value, timestamp}}`
- **Strengths:** Excellent for **time-series, write-heavy, analytical** workloads; compression is high
- **Weaknesses:** Limited query flexibility; no ad-hoc joins
- **Examples:** Apache Cassandra, HBase, ScyllaDB, Google BigTable

### 2.4.4 Graph Database

- **Data Model:** Nodes (entities), Edges (relationships), Properties (key-value on both)
- **Strengths:** Optimized for traversing deep, complex relationships (social networks, fraud detection, knowledge graphs)
- **Weaknesses:** Not suited for high-volume, simple key-value workloads
- **Examples:** Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, ArangoDB, JanusGraph, TigerGraph

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Connection:** Since this course is **PECDT751 — Graph Databases and Analysis**, the **Graph Store** type is the central focus of subsequent modules. Module 1 lays the conceptual foundation by positioning graph stores as one of the four pillars of NoSQL.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Term | Definition / Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **BASE** | Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency | NoSQL reliability model |
| **CAP** | C ∧ A ∧ P ≤ 2 (only 2 of 3) | Brewer's Theorem |
| **Replication Factor (RF)** | Number of copies of each data item across the cluster | Higher RF = higher availability, more storage |
| **Consistency Level (CL)** | Number of replicas that must acknowledge a read/write | Cassandra tunable: ONE, QUORUM, ALL |
| **Quorum** | $Q = \left\lfloor \frac{RF}{2} \right\rfloor + 1$ | Strong consistency requires R + W > RF |
| **Sharding** | Horizontal partitioning of data by a **shard key** | Each shard holds a subset of total data |
| **Eventual Consistency Bound** | Replicas converge within time $T$ after last write | Not a hard bound; probabilistic |
| **Polyglot Persistence** | Using multiple database types in one application | E.g., MySQL + MongoDB + Neo4j together |
| **Schema-on-Read** | Structure imposed at query time, not write time | Contrast: RDBMS uses schema-on-write |
| **MapReduce** | Distributed: Map → Shuffle/Sort → Reduce | Used by Hadoop, MongoDB aggregation |

> **Note on LaTeX:** In the Quorum formula above, the `Q` and `RF` are typeset in math mode for clarity and should be rendered correctly within the table cell.

## 2.6 When to Choose NoSQL — Decision Criteria

Use **NoSQL** when:
- Data is **semi-structured or unstructured** (JSON, XML, social, IoT)
- Workload demands **horizontal scaling** to petabytes
- You need **low-latency** reads/writes at high concurrency
- Schema is **evolving rapidly** (agile development)
- Geographic distribution across **multiple data centers** is required
- The application is **read-heavy, write-heavy, or both** at massive scale

Stick with **RDBMS** when:
- Data is **highly structured** with fixed relationships
- **ACID transactions** are non-negotiable (banking, inventory)
- Complex **multi-table joins** are routine
- Mature BI / reporting tools expect SQL
- The dataset is small enough for vertical scaling
