(ii) Find the shortest path between Anu and Karthik. (iii) Create a new person 'Meera' and a `:FRIEND` edge from Karthik to Meera, **only if Meera does not already exist**. (iv) Delete the person Karthik and all his relationships. (v) List all persons ordered by name descending. **Model Solution:** (i) Reachable within 3 hops: $$\begin{aligned} \text{MATCH (a:Person \{name: 'Anu'\})-[:FRIEND*1..3]->(p:Person)} \\ \text{RETURN DISTINCT p.name} \end{aligned}$$ (ii) Shortest path between Anu and Karthik: $$\begin{aligned} \text{MATCH p = shortestPath((a:Person \{name: 'Anu'\})-[:FRIEND*]-(k:Person \{name: 'Karthik'\}))} \\ \text{RETURN p, length(p) AS hopCount} \end{aligned}$$ (iii) Idempotent create of Meera and edge: $$\begin{aligned} \text{MERGE (k:Person \{name: 'Karthik'\})} \\ \text{MERGE (m:Person \{name: 'Meera'\})} \\ \text{MERGE (k)-[:FRIEND]->(m)} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > Using `MERGE` ensures that re-running the script does NOT create duplicate Meera nodes. (iv) Delete Karthik and all his edges: $$\begin{aligned} \text{MATCH (k:Person \{name: 'Karthik'\})} \\ \text{DETACH DELETE k} \end{aligned}$$ > [!WARNING] > Using plain `DELETE k` would throw a constraint error. **Always use `DETACH DELETE` for nodes with relationships.** (v) List all persons by name descending: $$\begin{aligned} \text{MATCH (p:Person)} \\ \text{RETURN p.name} \\ \text{ORDER BY p.name DESC} \end{aligned}$$ --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Cypher Exam Answers:** > 1. **Forgetting `DISTINCT`** — when returning a variable that can be reached by multiple paths, students lose 1 mark for duplicate rows. > 2. **Using `DELETE` instead of `DETACH DELETE`** — guaranteed 1-mark cut for any delete question. > 3. **Symmetric pairs in shared-neighbour queries** — must include `WHERE a.id < b.id` to avoid duplicates. > 4. **Confusing `MATCH ... CREATE` with `MERGE`** — `MATCH ... CREATE` is **not idempotent**; every run duplicates the edge. Examiners explicitly test this. > 5. **Forgetting that relationship direction is significant** — `(a)-[:KNOWS]->(b)` ≠ `(b)-[:KNOWS]->(a)`. Use `--` for undirected. > 6. **Mixing string case** — Cypher keywords are conventionally UPPERCASE. While the engine accepts lowercase, KTU evaluators may dock ½ mark for inconsistent style. > 7. **Writing `RETURN *`** — Cypher does NOT support `*` in RETURN. Always list variables explicitly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Cypher = ASCII-art declarative graph query language** for property graphs. - [x] **Nodes `()` and relationships `[]`** are the syntactic primitives. - [x] **Pattern syntax:** `(var:Label {prop: value})-[:TYPE]->(var:Label)`. - [x] **Variable-length paths:** `-[:TYPE*minHops..maxHops]-` for N-hop traversal. - [x] **Clause execution order (logical):** `MATCH → OPTIONAL MATCH → WITH → WHERE → ORDER BY → RETURN`. - [x] **`MATCH`** = pattern enumeration (INNER semantics). - [x] **`OPTIONAL MATCH`** = null-preserving (LEFT semantics). - [x] **`WHERE`** = filtering on already-matched rows. - [x] **`WITH`** = pipeline aggregator / variable-scope reset. - [x] **`RETURN`** = final projection to client. - [x] **`CREATE`** = always-insert; **`MERGE`** = idempotent insert-or-match. - [x] **`SET`** updates properties; **`REMOVE`** deletes properties. - [x] **`DELETE`** removes rels/isolated nodes; **`DETACH DELETE`** removes a node *and its incident relationships* — **always required** when deleting connected nodes. - [x] **`DISTINCT`** eliminates duplicate rows from multi-path results. - [x] **`shortestPath((a)-[*]-(b))`** finds the minimum-hop path between two nodes. - [x] **Cypher keywords are UPPERCASE by convention**; **labels and relationship types are case-sensitive**. - [x] **Index-free adjacency** makes Cypher N-hop queries dramatically faster than SQL recursive joins at depth > 2. - [x] **Always filter out symmetric duplicates** in pairwise queries: `WHERE a.id < b.id`. - [x] **Common applications:** social networks, fraud detection, knowledge graphs, recommendations, bioinformatics.

topics.title: Other Cypher Clauses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Other Cypher Clauses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the **Cypher Query Language** (the declarative graph query language used by Neo4j and the ISO/IEC proposed Graph Query Language standard), the term **"Other Cypher Clauses"** refers to the set of secondary (but essential) clauses that complement the core reading clauses `MATCH`, `WHERE`, `RETURN`, `CREATE`, and `DELETE`. These clauses govern the **flow of execution**, **result transformation**, **conditional pattern existence**, **set composition**, and **bulk data ingestion** in Cypher queries.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Mapping (PECDT751 – Module 2):** This topic extends the foundational Cypher constructs and prepares the learner for advanced graph querying, schema enforcement, and data manipulation patterns used in real-world graph applications.

The principal clauses covered in this section are:

| # | Clause | Functional Category |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MERGE` | Schema / Pattern Management |
| 2 | `OPTIONAL MATCH` | Null-Safe Pattern Matching |
| 3 | `WITH` | Query Chaining / Pipeline Control |
| 4 | `UNION` / `UNION ALL` | Set Composition |
| 5 | `UNWIND` | List Expansion |
| 6 | `FOREACH` | Iterative Side-Effects |
| 7 | `CALL` | Procedure Invocation |
| 8 | `LOAD CSV` | Bulk Data Import |
| 9 | `SKIP` / `LIMIT` | Result Paging |
| 10 | `ORDER BY` | Result Sorting |
| 11 | `DISTINCT` | Deduplication |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Train Conductor Analogy 🚂**
> Think of a Cypher query as a **train route** from a starting station to a final destination.
> - **`MATCH`** → the **tracks** (the path to walk)
> - **`WITH`** → a **junction station** where passengers (intermediate results) must change trains
> - **`MERGE`** → a **ticket counter that creates a new station if one doesn't exist** (idempotent creation)
> - **`OPTIONAL MATCH`** → a **scenic side-track**; if it doesn't exist, the train continues anyway (NULL instead of dropping the row)
> - **`UNWIND`** → a **box of chocolates** 🧆 that is opened so each chocolate can be inspected individually
> - **`CALL`** → a **phone call to a specialist** who can run a custom procedure for you
> - **`LOAD CSV`** → a **bulk cargo loader** that fills the train from an external spreadsheet

In essence, these clauses control **how data flows**, **transforms**, **merges**, and **is constrained** during a graph query.

## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Neo4j Cypher Reference Metrics (Bolded for Examiner Attention):**
> - The default **list index starts at `0`** (zero-based indexing) in Cypher.
> - The default **result row limit is unbounded** unless `LIMIT` is specified.
> - The `MERGE` clause is **idempotent** — running it twice does not duplicate the pattern.
> - The default **CSV import field terminator is a comma `,`**, with the optional header row.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The `MERGE` Clause

**Definition:** `MERGE` ensures that a **given pattern exists** in the graph. If the pattern does not exist, `MERGE` creates it; if it does, `MERGE` matches it. This is the **idempotent equivalent of a `MATCH` + conditional `CREATE`**.

```cypher
MERGE (p:Person {name: 'Alice'})
ON CREATE SET p.createdAt = timestamp()
ON MATCH SET p.lastSeen = timestamp()
RETURN p;
```

**Why does this matter?**
- It is the foundation for **upsert** semantics in graph databases.
- It enforces **uniqueness constraints** at the query layer.
- It is the building block for **pattern-based schema** definition.

## 2.2 The `OPTIONAL MATCH` Clause

**Definition:** `OPTIONAL MATCH` behaves like `MATCH`, but if no match is found, it returns **`NULL`** for that part of the pattern instead of eliminating the row.

```cypher
MATCH (p:Person {name: 'Alice'})
OPTIONAL MATCH (p)-[:FRIEND]->(friend)
RETURN p.name, friend.name;  -- friend.name can be NULL
```

This is the **graph equivalent of a SQL LEFT OUTER JOIN**.

## 2.3 The `WITH` Clause (Pipeline Controller)

**Definition:** `WITH` is the Cypher **chaining operator**. It transfers intermediate results to the next part of the query, allowing transformations (aggregation, filtering, aliasing) before the next pattern match.

```cypher
MATCH (p:Person)-[:KNOWS]->(friend)
WITH p, count(friend) AS friendCount
WHERE friendCount > 5
RETURN p.name, friendCount
ORDER BY friendCount DESC;
```

**Why is it crucial?**
- It is the **only** way to perform aggregation in the middle of a query.
- It enables **query composition** by allowing you to treat intermediate results as a new "table".

## 2.4 The `UNION` / `UNION ALL` Clause

**Definition:** `UNION` combines the results of **two or more queries** into a single result set, **removing duplicates** (like SQL `UNION`). `UNION ALL` retains all rows including duplicates.

```cypher
MATCH (p:Person) RETURN p.name AS name
UNION
MATCH (c:Company) RETURN c.name AS name;
```

**Rule:** All `RETURN` clauses combined with `UNION` must have the **same number of columns** with **compatible types**.

## 2.5 The `UNWIND` Clause

**Definition:** `UNWIND` **expands a list** into a sequence of rows, with one row per list element.

```cypher
UNWIND [1, 2, 3] AS x
RETURN x * 10 AS result;  -- returns 10, 20, 30
```

> [!TIP]
> `UNWIND` is the **primary mechanism for batch operations and `MERGE` loops** without writing explicit procedures.

## 2.6 The `FOREACH` Clause

**Definition:** `FOREACH` executes a **mutating clause** (e.g., `CREATE`, `MERGE`, `SET`, `DELETE`) for every element in a list. **It cannot be used with `RETURN`**.

```cypher
MATCH (p:Person {name: 'Alice'})
FOREACH (tag IN ['VIP', 'Premium', 'Active'] |
    MERGE (t:Tag {name: tag})
    MERGE (p)-[:TAGGED]->(t)
);
```

## 2.7 The `CALL` Clause

**Definition:** `CALL` invokes a **stored procedure** (built-in or user-defined). It is the **escape hatch** for functionality not natively supported by Cypher.

```cypher
CALL db.labels() YIELD label
RETURN label;
```

## 2.8 The `LOAD CSV` Clause

**Definition:** `LOAD CSV` reads data from a **CSV file** at a given URL, with optional headers. It is the standard bulk-import mechanism in Neo4j.

```cypher
LOAD CSV WITH HEADERS FROM 'file:///people.csv' AS row
MERGE (p:Person {id: row.id})
SET p.name = row.name, p.age = toInteger(row.age);
```

## 2.9 The `SKIP` / `LIMIT` / `ORDER BY` / `DISTINCT` Clauses

These are **terminal projection modifiers**:

```cypher
MATCH (p:Person)
RETURN DISTINCT p.name
ORDER BY p.name ASC
SKIP 10
LIMIT 5;
```

- **`SKIP n`** → skips the first `n` rows.
- **`LIMIT n`** → returns at most `n` rows.
- **`ORDER BY`** → sorts ascending (default `ASC`) or `DESC`.
- **`DISTINCT`** → removes duplicate rows.

## 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Clause | Syntax Skeleton | Idempotent? | Returns Data? | Common Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `MERGE` | `MERGE (n:Label {key:val})` | **Yes** | Optional | Upsert / Schema enforcement |
| `OPTIONAL MATCH` | `OPTIONAL MATCH (n)-->(m)` | No | Yes (with NULLs) | Left join semantics |
| `WITH` | `WITH x, count(y) AS c` | No | Passes data | Aggregation & chaining |
| `UNION` | `Q1 UNION Q2` | Dedups | Yes | Combine result sets |
| `UNION ALL` | `Q1 UNION ALL Q2` | Keeps dupes | Yes | Combine result sets |
| `UNWIND` | `UNWIND [a,b] AS x` | No | Yes | List-to-rows |
| `FOREACH` | `FOREACH (x IN list | ...)` | No | **No (side-effect only)** | Batch mutations |
| `CALL` | `CALL proc.name() YIELD x` | Depends | Yes | Run procedures |
| `LOAD CSV` | `LOAD CSV WITH HEADERS FROM 'url' AS row` | No | Iterates rows | Bulk import |
| `SKIP` / `LIMIT` | `... SKIP 5 LIMIT 10` | No | Yes | Pagination |
| `ORDER BY` | `... ORDER BY x ASC` | No | Yes | Sorting |
| `DISTINCT` | `RETURN DISTINCT x` | Dedups | Yes | Deduplication |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Idempotency Invariant of `MERGE`

**Claim (KTU High-Yield Theorem):** Running `MERGE (n:L {k:v})` *N* times produces **exactly one** node with the given label and key-value pair, regardless of prior state.

**Proof by cases:**

Let $S_0$ denote the initial graph state (possibly empty), and let $\phi = (n:L \mid k = v)$ denote the target pattern.

**Case 1 — Pattern does not exist in $S_0$:**
The matching sub-routine of `MERGE` returns the empty set $\emptyset$. The creation sub-routine then executes:
$$S_1 = S_0 \cup \{ n : L, k = v \}$$
After $N$ executions (with $N \geq 1$):
$$S_N = S_0 \cup \{ n : L, k = v \}$$
The cardinality of the matching set remains $\vert \{n : L, k = v\} \vert = 1$.

**Case 2 — Pattern already exists in $S_0$:**
The matching sub-routine returns $\{n_0\}$. The creation sub-routine is **skipped** (the `ON CREATE` branch is not taken). After $N$ executions:
$$S_N = S_0$$
The cardinality remains unchanged: $\vert \{n : L, k = v\} \vert = 1$.

$$\blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This invariant is what allows `MERGE` to be safely used in **concurrent ETL pipelines**, where multiple workers may attempt to insert the same entity. The database guarantees **at-most-one** creation due to uniqueness constraints (if defined) and the atomic check-and-create operation.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Clause Composition Rules

A KTU-favourite exam question is to **trace query execution** through multiple clauses. The following matrix is the **authoritative reference** for how each clause interacts.

| Composition | Valid? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `MATCH ... WITH ... MATCH ...` | ✅ | `WITH` resets the pattern scope. |
| `MATCH ... MERGE ... RETURN ...` | ✅ | Standard read-then-upsert flow. |
| `MERGE ... RETURN ...` | ✅ | Returns the matched/created pattern. |
| `UNION` of two `MATCH` queries | ✅ | Same column count required. |
| `UNION` of `MATCH` and `CREATE` | ❌ | `CREATE` returns no rows for `UNION` semantics. |
| `FOREACH` ... `RETURN` | ❌ | `FOREACH` does not pass data to `RETURN`. |
| `LOAD CSV` ... `RETURN` (in same subquery) | ❌ | `LOAD CSV` is itself an iterating clause. |
| `WITH` without aggregation, then `MATCH` | ✅ | `WITH` acts as a passthrough. |
| `OPTIONAL MATCH` before `MATCH` | ❌ (often) | Pattern variables from `OPTIONAL MATCH` may become NULL; subsequent `MATCH` filtering NULLs them out — this is the **most common KTU pitfall** (see Section 5). |

## 3.3 Worked Example: `WITH` + `UNWIND` + `MERGE` for Bulk Insert

**Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme style):** Given a list of employee dictionaries, create a `Person` node for each and connect them to a `Department`.

**Input list:** `[{name: 'A', dept: 'IT'}, {name: 'B', dept: 'HR'}]`

**Step 1 — Receive the list as a parameter:**
```cypher
WITH [{name:'A', dept:'IT'}, {name:'B', dept:'HR'}] AS employees
```
This defines the local variable `employees` of type `LIST<MAP>`.

**Step 2 — Expand the list with `UNWIND`:**
```cypher
UNWIND employees AS emp
```
This yields two virtual rows: `{name:'A', dept:'IT'}` and `{name:'B', dept:'HR'}`.

**Step 3 — For each row, ensure the `Department` exists (idempotent):**
```cypher
MERGE (d:Department {name: emp.dept})
```
Per the **idempotency invariant** (Section 3.1), even if `MERGE` runs on the same row twice, only one `Department` node per name is created.

**Step 4 — Create or match the `Person`:**
```cypher
MERGE (p:Person {name: emp.name})
```

**Step 5 — Create the relationship (non-idempotent, but we wrap in `MERGE` chain):**
```cypher
MERGE (p)-[:WORKS_IN]->(d)
```

**Final Consolidated Query:**
```cypher
WITH [{name:'A', dept:'IT'}, {name:'B', dept:'HR'}] AS employees
UNWIND employees AS emp
MERGE (d:Department {name: emp.dept})
MERGE (p:Person {name: emp.name})
MERGE (p)-[:WORKS_IN]->(d);
```

**Execution trace (two rows):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row}_1 &: \{ emp = \{name:'A', dept:'IT'\}\} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{MERGE}(d:Department\{name:'IT'\}) \rightarrow \text{creates node} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{MERGE}(p:Person\{name:'A'\}) \rightarrow \text{creates node} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{MERGE}(p)-[:WORKS\_IN]\rightarrow(d) \rightarrow \text{creates edge} \\
\text{Row}_2 &: \{ emp = \{name:'B', dept:'HR'\}\} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{MERGE}(d:Department\{name:'HR'\}) \rightarrow \text{creates node} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{MERGE}(p:Person\{name:'B'\}) \rightarrow \text{creates node} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{MERGE}(p)-[:WORKS\_IN]\rightarrow(d) \rightarrow \text{creates edge}
\end{aligned}
$$

The graph after execution contains **4 nodes** (2 Persons, 2 Departments) and **2 edges**, regardless of how many times the query runs.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: Clause Pipeline Architecture

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the typical control flow when multiple "other Cypher clauses" are composed within a single query.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Query Entry Point] --> B[OPTIONAL MATCH]
    B --> C{Does Pattern Exist?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Bind Pattern Variables]
    C -- No --> E[Bind NULL to Variables]
    D --> F[WITH: Pipeline Chaining]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Aggregation or Filtering]
    G --> H[UNWIND: Expand Lists]
    H --> I[MERGE: Idempotent Upsert]
    I --> J{ON CREATE?}
    J -- Yes --> K[ON CREATE SET]
    J -- No --> L[ON MATCH SET]
    K --> M[RETURN / UNION / CALL]
    L --> M
    M --> N[ORDER BY + SKIP + LIMIT]
    N --> O[END: Final Result Stream]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style O fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style I fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style M fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Diagram: MERGE Decision Tree (Idempotency Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Execute MERGE pattern P] --> Check{P exists in graph?}
    Check -- Yes --> MatchBranch[Bind existing pattern to variables]
    Check -- No --> CreateBranch[Create new pattern in graph]
    MatchBranch --> OnMatch{ON MATCH clause?}
    OnMatch -- Yes --> RunMatch[Execute ON MATCH SET logic]
    OnMatch -- No --> Continue1[Proceed to next clause]
    CreateBranch --> OnCreate{ON CREATE clause?}
    OnCreate -- Yes --> RunCreate[Execute ON CREATE SET logic]
    OnCreate -- No --> Continue2[Proceed to next clause]
    RunMatch --> Continue1
    RunCreate --> Continue2
    Continue1 --> EndReturn[Return to next query stage]
    Continue2 --> EndReturn

    style Start fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style Check fill:#8c564b,color:#fff
    style MatchBranch fill:#17becf,color:#000
    style CreateBranch fill:#bcbd22,color:#000
    style EndReturn fill:#e377c2,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Diagram: WITH as a Query Pipeline Junction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[MATCH subquery 1] --> J[WITH: Junction Station]
    J --> Agg[Aggregation: count, sum, collect]
    Agg --> Filter[WHERE filter on aggregates]
    Filter --> Q2[MATCH subquery 2: new pattern]
    Q2 --> R[RETURN final result]

    style Q1 fill:#4a90e2,color:#fff
    style J fill:#f5a623,color:#000
    style Agg fill:#7ed321,color:#000
    style Filter fill:#9013fe,color:#fff
    style Q2 fill:#4a90e2,color:#fff
    style R fill:#d0021b,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Diagram: UNION vs. UNION ALL (Set Composition)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Query A: RETURN rows] --> U{UNION or UNION ALL?}
    B[Query B: RETURN rows] --> U
    U -- UNION --> DEDUP[Remove duplicate rows]
    U -- UNION ALL --> KEEP[Keep all rows including dupes]
    DEDUP --> OUT[Combined Result Set]
    KEEP --> OUT

    style A fill:#1abc9c,color:#000
    style B fill:#3498db,color:#fff
    style U fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff
    style DEDUP fill:#f39c12,color:#000
    style KEEP fill:#9b59b6,color:#fff
    style OUT fill:#2ecc71,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Diagram: LOAD CSV Bulk Import Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CSV[(CSV file at URL)] --> Load[LOAD CSV WITH HEADERS]
    Load --> Iterate{For each row}
    Iterate --> Validate[Apply type conversion: toInteger, toFloat]
    Validate --> Merge[MERGE entity node idempotently]
    Merge --> SetProps[SET properties from CSV columns]
    SetProps --> Relate[CREATE / MERGE relationships]
    Relate --> Iterate
    Iterate -- Done --> Commit[Commit transaction]
    Commit --> Done[Graph populated]

    style CSV fill:#34495e,color:#fff
    style Load fill:#16a085,color:#fff
    style Merge fill:#f39c12,color:#000
    style Done fill:#27ae60,color:#fff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECDT751]
**Differentiate between `MATCH` and `MERGE` clauses in Cypher. When would you prefer `MERGE` over a `MATCH` followed by `CREATE`?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `MATCH` | `MERGE` |
|---|---|---|
| Existence check | No — returns nothing if pattern absent | Yes — guarantees pattern exists after execution |
| Side effect | Read-only (no writes) | Read-or-write (idempotent upsert) |
| Duplication risk | None (read) | None (idempotent) |
| Use case | Pure querying | ETL, schema enforcement, unique-entity creation |

`MERGE` is preferred over `MATCH` + conditional `CREATE` because it is **atomic** (single Cypher operation), **idempotent** (safe to re-run), and supports **`ON CREATE` / `ON MATCH`** hooks for differentiated logic — making it the canonical choice for **upserts in graph ETL pipelines**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECDT751]
**Explain the role of the `WITH` clause in Cypher with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

The `WITH` clause is the **pipeline chaining operator** in Cypher. It transfers intermediate results to the next query stage, enabling:
1. **Aggregation** in the middle of a query (e.g., `count()`, `collect()`).
2. **Aliasing** of intermediate variables.
3. **Filtering** on aggregate values.
4. **Limiting** rows before a subsequent expensive pattern match (optimization).

**Example:**
```cypher
MATCH (p:Person)-[:FRIEND]->(f)
WITH p, count(f) AS friendCount
WHERE friendCount > 3
RETURN p.name, friendCount
ORDER BY friendCount DESC;
```

Here, `WITH` acts as a junction that aggregates the number of friends per person **before** the filtering stage — something impossible in a single `MATCH ... WHERE` chain.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mixing `OPTIONAL MATCH` with subsequent `MATCH`**: A common 3-mark deduction occurs when students fail to realize that any non-optional `MATCH` following an `OPTIONAL MATCH` will silently filter out rows where the optional pattern was NULL. The correct pattern is to **move the subsequent `MATCH` into the `OPTIONAL MATCH` itself** or place it **before** the optional one.
> 2. **Forgetting `WITH` before `MERGE` after aggregation**: Aggregation consumes the variables; you cannot `MATCH` on aggregated aliases without re-binding them.
> 3. **`UNION` column-count mismatch**: Examiners will deduct **2 marks** if column counts differ or types are incompatible.
> 4. **`FOREACH` with `RETURN`**: This is **syntactically invalid** — examiners mark it zero for the sub-part.
> 5. **`LOAD CSV` without `WITH HEADERS`**: Forgetting this causes CSV column names to be ignored — common 2-mark loss.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Bulk Data Ingestion and Schema Enforcement**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECDT751, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

Consider the following CSV file `students.csv`:

```
id,name,age,course
101,Anu,21,CSE
102,Balu,22,ECE
103,Chitra,21,CSE
104,Divya,23,ME
```

**(a)** Write a Cypher query using `LOAD CSV` and `MERGE` to **create unique `Student` and `Course` nodes** and connect them via a `:ENROLLED_IN` relationship. Assign each student an `enrollmentDate` property set to the current timestamp **only on creation**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the idempotency guarantee of your query. What happens if the same CSV is loaded twice? Demonstrate with an execution trace. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Query Construction (7 marks):**

```cypher
LOAD CSV WITH HEADERS FROM 'file:///students.csv' AS row

MERGE (c:Course {name: row.course})

MERGE (s:Student {id: toInteger(row.id)})
ON CREATE SET s.enrollmentDate = timestamp(),
              s.name          = row.name,
              s.age           = toInteger(row.age)

MERGE (s)-[:ENROLLED_IN]->(c);
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. `LOAD CSV WITH HEADERS FROM 'file:///students.csv' AS row` — loads the CSV from the Neo4j `import` directory, treats the first row as headers, and binds each subsequent row to the variable `row` (a map of `column → value`).
2. `MERGE (c:Course {name: row.course})` — ensures each unique `Course` node exists.
3. `MERGE (s:Student {id: toInteger(row.id)})` — ensures each unique `Student` exists, with the CSV's `id` column cast to integer.
4. `ON CREATE SET ...` — fires **only** the first time a student is created, capturing the timestamp.
5. `MERGE (s)-[:ENROLLED_IN]->(c)` — idempotent relationship creation.

**(b) — Idempotency Guarantee and Execution Trace (7 marks):**

**Guarantee:** The `MERGE` clause guarantees that running the query $N$ times produces the **same final graph state** as running it once. Specifically:
- $\vert \{ \text{Student} \} \vert = 4$ (one per row, no duplicates)
- $\vert \{ \text{Course} \} \vert = 3$ (CSE, ECE, ME — duplicates removed by `MERGE`)
- $\vert \{ \text{:ENROLLED\_IN} \} \vert = 4$ (one per student)
- The `enrollmentDate` is set **only on the first run**; subsequent runs trigger `ON MATCH` (no clause defined here, so no change).

**Execution trace (second run):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row}_{101} &: c_{\text{CSE}} \in G \Rightarrow \text{MERGE matches, no create} \\
                  &: s_{101} \in G \Rightarrow \text{MERGE matches, } ON\ CREATE\ \text{NOT fired} \\
                  &: (s_{101})-[:ENROLLED\_IN]\rightarrow(c_{\text{CSE}}) \in G \Rightarrow \text{no create} \\
\text{Row}_{102} &: \text{analogous, no creates} \\
\text{Row}_{103} &: \text{analogous, no creates} \\
\text{Row}_{104} &: \text{analogous, no creates} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, **zero new nodes and zero new edges** are created on the second run — confirming idempotency.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Aggregation, Chaining, and Set Composition**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECDT751, May 2024 Pattern]**

A social graph has `Person` nodes connected by `:LIKES` and `:FRIEND` relationships.

**(a)** Write a Cypher query that returns the **top 3 most-liked persons** (by incoming `:LIKES` count) along with the **number of their friends**, but only consider persons with **more than 2 friends**. Use `WITH` chaining and `ORDER BY` + `LIMIT`. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Demonstrate the use of `UNION` to combine the names of all `Person` nodes and the names of all `City` nodes into a single column called `entityName`. Show how `UNION ALL` differs. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Query with `WITH`, `ORDER BY`, `LIMIT` (7 marks):**

```cypher
MATCH (p:Person)<-[:LIKES]-()
WITH p, count(*) AS likeCount

MATCH (p)-[:FRIEND]->(f)
WITH p, likeCount, count(f) AS friendCount
WHERE friendCount > 2

RETURN p.name AS person, likeCount, friendCount
ORDER BY likeCount DESC
LIMIT 3;
```

**Explanation:**

1. The first `MATCH ... WITH` block counts incoming `:LIKES` per person.
2. The second `MATCH` (after `WITH`) starts a new pattern match; `p` is rebound from the pipeline.
3. The second `WITH` aggregates the friend count and the existing `likeCount`.
4. `WHERE friendCount > 2` filters the aggregated set.
5. `ORDER BY likeCount DESC LIMIT 3` returns the top 3.

**Sample output table:**

| person | likeCount | friendCount |
|---|---|---|
| Anu    | 25        | 5           |
| Balu   | 18        | 3           |
| Chitra | 12        | 4           |

**(b) — `UNION` vs `UNION ALL` (7 marks):**

```cypher
MATCH (p:Person)  RETURN p.name AS entityName
UNION
MATCH (c:City)    RETURN c.name AS entityName;
```

This returns the **deduplicated** set of all person and city names. If 'Anu' appears as both a person and a city, it appears **once**.

```cypher
MATCH (p:Person)  RETURN p.name AS entityName
UNION ALL
MATCH (c:City)    RETURN c.name AS entityName;
```

This returns **all** names, including duplicates — twice in the case of 'Anu' being both.

**Constraint (often asked in KTU):** Both `RETURN` clauses must have:
- The **same number of columns** (here, 1).
- **Compatible types** (both strings, here).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE:**

- ✅ `MERGE` = idempotent `MATCH + conditional CREATE`; the cornerstone of **upsert** in Cypher.
- ✅ `ON CREATE SET` fires only when the pattern is **newly created**; `ON MATCH SET` fires only when the pattern **already exists**.
- ✅ `OPTIONAL MATCH` = graph-equivalent of SQL **LEFT OUTER JOIN**; returns `NULL` instead of dropping rows.
- ✅ `WITH` is the **only** way to perform aggregation mid-query; it also serves as a query-chaining junction.
- ✅ `UNION` deduplicates; `UNION ALL` retains duplicates. Both require **same column count and types**.
- ✅ `UNWIND` expands a list into rows — the **canonical pattern for batch `MERGE` loops**.
- ✅ `FOREACH` performs **side-effect mutations only**; it **cannot** be followed by `RETURN` in the same scope.
- ✅ `CALL` invokes stored procedures; the `YIELD` keyword specifies the returned columns.
- ✅ `LOAD CSV WITH HEADERS` is required for named-column access; `toInteger()`, `toFloat()` must be applied for numeric conversions.
- ✅ `ORDER BY` (default `ASC`), `SKIP`, `LIMIT` are **terminal** clauses that control the final result stream.
- ✅ `DISTINCT` removes duplicate rows in the result projection.
- ✅ **Examiner pitfall**: Never put a `MATCH` after an `OPTIONAL MATCH` expecting the NULL rows to survive — they will be filtered out.

> **End of Notes — Other Cypher Clauses (PECDT751, Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of Relational and Graph Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Comparison of Relational and Graph Modelling # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Relational Modelling** is a data representation paradigm in which information is stored in **tables (relations)** composed of **rows (tuples)** and **columns (attributes)**, where relationships between entities are expressed through **foreign keys**, **JOIN operations**, and rigid **schema constraints** (primary key, unique, NOT NULL, referential integrity). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Graph Modelling** is a data representation paradigm in which information is stored as a set of **nodes (vertices)** representing entities and **edges (relationships)** representing associations, where the structure, traversal semantics, and topology of connections are treated as **first-class citizens** of the data model. ### Key Terminology Crosswalk | Relational Term | Equivalent Graph Term | Function | |---|---|---| | Table | Label / Node Set | Container of like entities | | Row / Tuple | Node / Vertex | A single record | | Column / Attribute | Property (key-value) | A single data field | | Foreign Key | Edge (typed/labeled) | Inter-entity link | | JOIN | TRAVERSE / MATCH | Cross-entity query | | Schema Constraint | Property existence / Type | Data validity rule | | Index | Index (in Neo4j) / Label | Lookup accelerator | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of a city metro system.** > - **Relational Modelling** is like a **printed bus/train timetable**: every route is described in a separate table, stations are rows, schedules are columns. To know "How do I get from A to D with one transfer?", you must look up Route-1, then Route-7, then mentally *JOIN* them at a common station. The map exists only in your head after you assemble it. > - **Graph Modelling** is the **actual metro map on the wall**: stations are dots (nodes), lines between them are the connections (edges). You *see* the topology. Asking "How do I go from A to D with one transfer?" is a literal visual trace along the lines. The relationship *is* the data. ### Why the Distinction Matters In **relational systems**, data is **disassembled** into normalized tables, and meaning is **reassembled at query time** through JOINs. In **graph systems**, meaning is **pre-materialized** as edges, and queries simply **traverse** the existing structure. This single difference drives every downstream comparison — performance, scalability of relationships, schema flexibility, and developer ergonomics. > [!TIP] > **Standard Metric to Remember:** > - **Codd's 12 Rules (1985)** — **12 rules** defining a *Fully Relational* DBMS (most systems satisfy ≤ 8 in practice). > - **Property Graph Model components (Rodriguez & Neubauer, 2010)** — **3 primitives**: **Nodes**, **Edges**, **Properties**. --- ## 1.3 When to Choose Which — A Rule of Thumb > [!IMPORTANT] > - Choose **Relational** when the domain is **transaction-heavy, tabular, and relationships are simple, fixed, and shallow** (e.g., banking ledgers, inventory). > - Choose **Graph** when the domain is **relationship-heavy, hierarchical, networked, or has deeply recursive connections** (e.g., social networks, fraud rings, knowledge graphs, recommendation engines, IT networks). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Foundational Structural Differences The relational model was formally defined by **Edgar F. Codd (1970)** based on mathematical **set theory and first-order predicate logic**. The graph model — in its modern property-graph incarnation — traces its lineage through Euler's *Königsberg bridge problem (1736)*, evolving through RDF (W3C, 1999) and the property graph formalization used by Neo4j (2010+). ### 2.1.1 Data Structure Granularity - **Relational**: Data exists at the **tuple level**. A relationship between two entities is *not* a stored object — it is a **derived value** computed via a foreign-key equality match during a JOIN. - **Graph**: A relationship between two entities is a **physical, persisted edge object** with its own identity, direction, type, and properties. ### 2.1.2 Query Evaluation Strategy $$Q_{\text{relational}} = \sigma \rightarrow \pi \rightarrow \Join$$ $$Q_{\text{graph}} = \text{MATCH}(a) - [r:\text{KNOWS*1..3}] \rightarrow (b)$$ > [!NOTE] > In the relational algebra expression, $\sigma$ is **selection** (WHERE), $\pi$ is **projection** (SELECT columns), and $\Join$ is the **JOIN operator**. In the graph Cypher expression, `*1..3` is a **variable-length path** specifying 1 to 3 hops — a construct that has **no direct equivalent in relational algebra**. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is the single most examinable artifact in this module.** Memorize the *direction* of every comparison (which side "wins"). | Dimension | Relational Modelling | Graph Modelling | |---|---|---| | **Underlying Math** | Set theory, First-Order Predicate Logic | Graph Theory (G = V, E) | | **Storage Unit** | Table (Relation) | Node + Edge + Property | | **Schema** | Rigid, predefined, ALTER TABLE heavy | Flexible, schema-optional, property-driven | | **Relationship Storage** | Foreign key (one-way by default) | Bidirectional edge (native) | | **Relationship Cardinality** | 1:1, 1:N, M:N via junction table | Native M:N; no junction needed | | **Relationship Attributes** | Stored in junction table | Stored directly on edge | | **Query Language** | SQL (declarative, set-based) | Cypher / GQL / SPARQL (pattern-match) | | **Multi-hop Queries** | Cost grows as **O(N · k)** per JOIN | Cost grows as **O(k · d)** per traversal | | **JOIN Performance** | Degrades with depth & table size | Near-constant per hop (index-free adjacency) | | **Indexing** | B-Tree, Hash, Bitmap | Label + Property indexes, BTREE in Neo4j | | **ACID Compliance** | Native & mature | Supported (Neo4j 4.0+, JanusGraph with plugins) | | **Horizontal Scaling** | Sharding, replication (mature) | Native (Neo4j Fabric, JanusGraph + Cassandra) | | **Best Fit Domains** | OLTP, OLAP, financial systems | Social, fraud, knowledge graphs, IoT | | **Traversal Depth Limit** | Practical: 5–7 JOINs (deteriorates) | Practical: 10+ hops (graceful) | | **Schema Evolution Cost** | High (migrations) | Low (add property or label) | | **Referential Integrity** | Enforced via FK constraints | Enforced by edge existence | | **Data Volume Sweet Spot** | Billions of rows (columnar) | Billions of nodes & edges | | **Relationship Count Threshold** | Performance cliff at millions of M:N | Linear scaling into billions of edges | --- ## 2.3 The Index-Free Adjacency Principle > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the most important single concept in graph database theory.** **Index-free adjacency** means that each node maintains a **direct physical pointer (reference)** to its adjacent nodes and edges. When a query needs to traverse a relationship, the database engine does *not* perform a global lookup. It simply follows the pointer — an **O(1)** operation per hop. By contrast, in a relational system, every JOIN requires the engine to: 1. Look up the foreign-key value in the index. 2. Locate matching rows in the joined table. 3. Construct a result set (often via hash or nested-loop join). This is the reason multi-hop queries in graphs remain performant while multi-JOIN queries in RDBMSs **exponentially degrade** beyond a few levels. ### Mathematical Expression of the Cost Difference For a query traversing $h$ hops across a domain of $N$ entities, with average degree $d$ per node: $$T_{\text{graph}}(h, d) = \mathcal{O}(h \cdot d)$$ $$T_{\text{relational}}(h, N) = \mathcal{O}(N^{h})$$ > [!NOTE] > The relational expression assumes each JOIN can in the worst case scan the full relation. With proper indexes this is mitigated, but in practice the cardinality explosion of multi-JOIN queries is the dominant cost. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Relational Modelling** powers **core banking systems, airline reservation systems, ERP platforms** (SAP R/3), and **data warehouses** (Snowflake, Teradata) — anywhere transactional integrity, fixed schema, and decades of optimization matter most. - **Graph Modelling** powers **fraud detection** (PayPal, Mastercard), **recommendation engines** (Amazon, Netflix knowledge graphs), **cybersecurity threat analysis** (MITRE ATT&CK mapping), **drug interaction discovery** (Bio4j), and **master data management** in large enterprises. --- ## 2.5 Limitations & Trade-offs (Exam-Favorite Section) ### Relational Weaknesses that Graphs Address 1. **Recursive query inefficiency** — finding "managers of managers of managers" needs CTEs and degrades fast. 2. **Schema rigidity** — adding a new attribute requires ALTER TABLE on production. 3. **Junction-table clutter** — M:N relationships need a third table, which doubles JOIN cost. 4. **Sparse data** — NULL values bloat tables; graph property absence is free. ### Graph Weaknesses that RDBMS Addresses 1. **Mature tooling & ecosystem** — decades of ORMs, BI tools, DBA expertise. 2. **Bulk analytics** — columnar RDBMS outperforms graph engines on aggregations over millions of rows. 3. **Distributed transactions** — RDBMS distributed transactions are more battle-tested. 4. **Standardization** — SQL is ISO-standard; GQL only standardized in **2023**. > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** GQL (Graph Query Language) was published as an **ISO/IEC 39075** standard in **2023** — a frequently asked fact in viva voce. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — Modelling a Social Network Domain To make the comparison concrete, let us derive how the **same business domain** — a simple *Friendship + Posts + Likes* social network — is represented in each paradigm. ### 3.1.1 Relational Modelling of the Domain **Step 1 — Identify Entities and Convert to Tables.** Entities detected: `User`, `Post`, `Friendship` (M:N between User and User), `Like` (M:N between User and Post). **Step 2 — Define Schemas (DDL).** **Table: User** - `user_id` (PK, INT, AUTO_INCREMENT) - `username` (VARCHAR, UNIQUE, NOT NULL) - `email` (VARCHAR, UNIQUE, NOT NULL) - `created_at` (TIMESTAMP) **Table: Post** - `post_id` (PK, INT, AUTO_INCREMENT) - `author_id` (FK → User.user_id, NOT NULL) - `content` (TEXT, NOT NULL) - `created_at` (TIMESTAMP) **Table: Friendship** (junction for M:N self-relationship) - `user_id_1` (FK → User.user_id, PK part 1) - `user_id_2` (FK → User.user_id, PK part 2) - `since` (DATE) - *(Composite PK: user_id_1, user_id_2)* **Table: PostLike** (junction for M:N User↔Post) - `user_id` (FK → User.user_id, PK part 1) - `post_id` (FK → Post.post_id, PK part 2) - `liked_at` (TIMESTAMP) **Step 3 — Sample Data Insertion (conceptual).** User table after 3 inserts: | user_id | username | email | |---|---|---| | 1 | alice | a@x.com | | 2 | bob | b@x.com | | 3 | carol | c@x.com | Friendship table: | user_id_1 | user_id_2 | since | |---|---|---| | 1 | 2 | 2023-01-15 | | 2 | 3 | 2023-02-20 | **Step 4 — Express "Friends of Friends of Alice" Query.** $$Q_{\text{Rel}} = \text{User } a \;\Join\; \text{Friendship } f_1 \;\Join\; \text{User } u_1 \;\Join\; \text{Friendship } f_2 \;\Join\; \text{User } u_2$$ **Step 5 — Translate to SQL (5-table JOIN for 2-hop query).** ```sql SELECT DISTINCT u2.username FROM User a JOIN Friendship f1 ON a.user_id = f1.user_id_1 JOIN User u1 ON f1.user_id_2 = u1.user_id JOIN Friendship f2 ON u1.user_id = f2.user_id_1 JOIN User u2 ON f2.user_id_2 = u2.user_id WHERE a.username = 'alice' AND u2.user_id <> a.user_id; ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Count the JOINs:** **2 hops = 5 joined tables = 4 JOIN operations**. Each additional hop appends ~2 more joins. This is the *quadratic/exponential cost explosion* the graph model eliminates. --- ### 3.1.2 Graph Modelling of the Same Domain **Step 1 — Identify Nodes and Edges.** - **Nodes:** `(:User)`, `(:Post)` - **Node Properties:** `User {user_id, username, email, created_at}`, `Post {post_id, content, created_at}` - **Edges:** `(:User)-[:FRIEND_WITH {since}]->(:User)`, `(:User)-[:LIKED {liked_at}]->(:Post)`, `(:User)-[:POSTED]->(:Post)` **Step 2 — Sample Cypher Insertion (conceptual).** ```cypher CREATE (a:User {user_id:1, username:'alice', email:'a@x.com'}), (b:User {user_id:2, username:'bob', email:'b@x.com'}), (c:User {user_id:3, username:'carol', email:'c@x.com'}), (a)-[:FRIEND_WITH {since:date('2023-01-15')}]->(b), (b)-[:FRIEND_WITH {since:date('2023-02-20')}]->(c); ``` **Step 3 — Same "Friends of Friends of Alice" Query in Cypher.** ```cypher MATCH (a:User {username:'alice'})-[:FRIEND_WITH*1..2]->(fof:User) WHERE fof <> a RETURN DISTINCT fof.username; ``` > [!NOTE] > **Observe:** A *single* pattern-match expression replaces the entire 4-JOIN SQL statement. The `*1..2` is a **variable-length path operator** — a 1-to-2-hop traversal. To extend to N hops, only the integer changes: `*1..N`. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Same Query, Both Models | Aspect | Relational (SQL) | Graph (Cypher) | |---|---|---| | Lines of code | ~7 | 3 | | JOINs / Traversals | 4 JOINs | 1 traversal, 2 hops | | Edge-type constraints | Implicit (FK column names) | Native (`:FRIEND_WITH` label) | | Relationship attributes | Separate `since` column in junction | On-edge `{since: ...}` property | | Cyclic traversal risk | Cartesian explosion possible | Bounded by `*1..N` upper limit | | Adding a new relationship type | New table + FK + migration | One new edge label | | Schema migration cost | High (DDL, locks, downtime) | Zero (just start using) | --- ## 3.3 Performance & Cost Derivation Suppose our social graph has: - $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ users - Average degree $d = 50$ friends per user **Graph traversal for 3-hop friends-of-friends-of-friends:** $$T_{\text{graph}} = \mathcal{O}(3 \cdot 50) = 150 \text{ pointer-hops}$$ **Relational JOIN cost for 3-hop (estimated, with proper indexes):** $$T_{\text{rel}} \approx 50 \times 50 \times 50 = 125{,}000 \text{ index lookups}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The graph traversal is **~830× faster** in this scenario — a difference that scales super-linearly as the network grows. This is the central *engineering justification* for graph adoption in relationship-heavy domains. --- ## 3.4 Decision Framework Derivation A useful decision rule for the KTU exam: $$ \text{Choose Graph} \iff \begin{cases} \text{Relationship density} \geq 3 \text{ edges/node}, \\ \text{Avg traversal depth} \geq 3 \text{ hops}, \\ \text{Schema evolves} \geq 1 \text{ time/quarter}. \end{cases} $$ Otherwise, prefer relational. > [!TIP] > This is a **rule-of-thumb heuristic**, not a theorem. Real systems often run *both* (polyglot persistence): RDBMS for transactional core + Graph for analytical/relationship workloads. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Domain Representation Comparison The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the *same* Friendship-Post-Like domain is structured under both modelling paradigms. The relational side is shown as a normalized entity-relationship layout; the graph side is shown as a literal property graph. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph REL["RELATIONAL MODEL (Tables + FKs)"] U1[("User Table
user_id PK
username
email")] P1[("Post Table
post_id PK
author_id FK")] F1[("Friendship Junction
user_id_1 PK,FK
user_id_2 PK,FK
since")] L1[("PostLike Junction
user_id PK,FK
post_id PK,FK
liked_at")] U1 -. "1..N posts" .-> P1 U1 -. "M..N self" .-> F1 F1 -. "M..N self" .-> U1 U1 -. "M..N likes" .-> L1 L1 -. "M..N posts" .-> P1 end subgraph GRA["GRAPH MODEL (Nodes + Edges)"] UA(("User:alice
id=1")) UB(("User:bob
id=2")) UC(("User:carol
id=3")) PA(("Post:hello
id=10")) UA -- "FRIEND_WITH
since=2023-01" --> UB UB -- "FRIEND_WITH
since=2023-02" --> UC UA -- "POSTED" --> PA UB -- "LIKED
at=2023-03" --> PA end classDef relNode fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef graNode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class U1,P1,F1,L1 relNode class UA,UB,UC,PA graNode ``` --- ## 4.2 Conceptual Block Architecture — Query Evaluation Pipelines ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph QREL["RELATIONAL QUERY PIPELINE"] Q1[User SQL Query] --> P1a[Parser + AST] P1a --> O1[Optimizer
Cost-Based] O1 --> E1[Execution Engine] E1 --> J1[Hash Join /
Nested Loop Join] J1 --> R1[Result Set
Materialized] end subgraph QGRA["GRAPH QUERY PIPELINE"] Q2[User Cypher Query] --> P2a[Parser + AST] P2a --> P2b[Pattern Matcher
Cypher Planner] P2b --> E2[Traversal Engine] E2 --> T1[Index-Free
Adjacency Walks] T1 --> R2[Streaming
Result Paths] end classDef relBox fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef graBox fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class Q1,P1a,O1,E1,J1,R1 relBox class Q2,P2a,P2b,E2,T1,R2 graBox ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The relational pipeline ends in a **materialized result set** (rows), whereas the graph pipeline ends in **streaming result paths** (a sequence of nodes and edges). This is why graph queries feel "navigational" — you are walking the graph, not copying it into a temporary table. --- ## 4.3 Performance Cost Growth Comparison Plot (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Hops = 1] -->|Graph: linear| A2[Hops = 2] A2 -->|Graph: linear| A3[Hops = 3] A3 -->|Graph: linear| A4[Hops = 4] B1[JOIN = 1] -->|Relational: linear| B2[JOIN = 2] B2 -->|Relational: super-linear| B3[JOIN = 3] B3 -->|Relational: exponential| B4[JOIN = 4] A4 --- NOTE["Graph remains near-constant
per additional hop due to
index-free adjacency"] B4 --- NOTE2["Relational cost grows
exponentially per JOIN
as relation cardinality multiplies"] classDef hopNode fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef joinNode fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A1,A2,A3,A4 hopNode class B1,B2,B3,B4 joinNode ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Expected Pattern)] **Differentiate between a foreign key in the relational model and an edge in the graph model. Why does this distinction matter for query performance?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Foreign Key (1 mark):** A foreign key is an **attribute (column) value** in one table that references the primary key of another table. It is a *logical* pointer and stores only the referenced ID. A relationship between two tables is therefore not a stored object — it is *implied* and must be *resolved* at query time via a JOIN. - **Edge (1 mark):** An edge in a graph model is a **first-class persisted object** with its own identity, type (label), direction, and the ability to carry properties (e.g., `since`, `weight`). It is a *physical* pointer — both endpoints and the relationship itself exist as discrete entities in storage. - **Why it matters (1 mark):** Because an edge is a direct physical reference (index-free adjacency), traversal is an **O(1) pointer hop**. A foreign key, by contrast, requires an **index lookup + table scan + materialization** of the joined tuple. Hence multi-hop queries on graphs scale near-linearly, while multi-JOIN relational queries scale super-linearly. --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define "Index-Free Adjacency". Why is it considered the defining performance advantage of graph databases over relational databases?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1.5 marks):** Index-free adjacency is the storage principle in which each node maintains a **direct disk/memory pointer** to its adjacent nodes and outgoing/incoming edges. When a query needs to traverse a relationship, the engine does not consult a global index; it simply **follows the pointer** to the next node, which is an **O(1)** operation. - **Why defining (1.5 marks):** It eliminates the need for a costly index lookup at every hop. In a relational JOIN, each step typically requires re-reading an index, scanning a table, and materializing an intermediate result. Index-free adjacency thus allows graph databases to handle **deep traversals (5–10+ hops) at near-constant per-hop cost**, a regime in which relational databases suffer exponential performance degradation. It is what makes graph analytics on relationship-heavy data practical. --- ## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — [14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** With a suitable example, explain how a domain with **deeply recursive relationships** (e.g., organizational hierarchy of a multinational company) is modelled in **(i)** the relational model and **(ii)** the graph model. Compare the two approaches. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **schema evolution** differences between relational and graph databases. Why does this matter for agile, evolving domains such as IoT or social platforms? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** We model the hierarchy: `CEO → VP-Engineering → Director-Platform → Manager-API → Developer-A → Intern-X`. **(i) Relational model (3.5 marks):** A single table is used with a self-referential foreign key: | emp_id (PK) | name | role | manager_id (FK→emp_id) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Asha | CEO | NULL | | 2 | Vivek | VP-Eng | 1 | | 3 | Diya | Director | 2 | | 4 | Mohan | Manager | 3 | | 5 | Riya | Developer | 4 | | 6 | Karan | Intern | 5 | To find the *reporting chain* (all superiors) of Karan, we use a **recursive CTE**: ```sql WITH RECURSIVE chain AS ( SELECT e.emp_id, e.name, e.manager_id, 1 AS depth FROM Employee e WHERE e.name = 'Karan' UNION ALL SELECT m.emp_id, m.name, m.manager_id, c.depth + 1 FROM Employee m JOIN chain c ON m.emp_id = c.manager_id ) SELECT * FROM chain; ``` This requires **iterative self-JOINing**, and the cost grows with hierarchy depth. Each level adds an index lookup + join. **(ii) Graph model (3.5 marks):** ```cypher CREATE (ceo:Employee {name:'Asha', role:'CEO'}), (vp :Employee {name:'Vivek', role:'VP-Eng'}), (d :Employee {name:'Diya', role:'Director'}), (m :Employee {name:'Mohan', role:'Manager'}), (r :Employee {name:'Riya', role:'Developer'}), (k :Employee {name:'Karan', role:'Intern'}), (ceo)-[:REPORTS_TO]->(vp), (vp) -[:REPORTS_TO]->(d), (d) -[:REPORTS_TO]->(m), (m) -[:REPORTS_TO]->(r), (r) -[:REPORTS_TO]->(k); ``` Reporting chain of Karan: ```cypher MATCH (k:Employee {name:'Karan'})-[:REPORTS_TO*]->(s:Employee) RETURN s.name, s.role; ``` The `*` operator traverses the chain in a single expression. The query cost is **O(h)** where $h$ is the chain length, regardless of total employees. Adding a new hierarchy level requires no schema change — just one more edge. > **Conclusion:** Relational requires recursive CTEs and pays per-level cost; graph handles the same query with a single variable-length pattern and constant per-hop cost. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Schema Evolution in Relational (3.5 marks):** - Adding a new attribute to the `Employee` table requires an `ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN` statement, often with a default value, and may lock the table during migration. - Adding a new relationship type (e.g., "mentor of") requires creating a new junction table with foreign keys and a new index. - Production migrations need careful planning: backups, downtime windows, rollback scripts. - Existing rows gain `NULL` values for the new column — wasted storage if the attribute is sparse. **Schema Evolution in Graph (3.5 marks):** - Adding a new property requires no DDL — simply start setting it on nodes. Nodes without the property are unaffected. - Adding a new relationship type requires no schema change — just use a new label in MATCH/CREATE. - Old data is untouched; new data carries new properties/edges. This is **schema-on-read** flexibility. - Migration is therefore *online* and *non-disruptive*. **Why it matters for IoT / social platforms:** - IoT devices evolve constantly: new sensor types, new telemetry fields, new device classes. A rigid relational schema would require constant migrations, causing operational pain. - Social platforms add new interaction types (reactions, stories, threads) frequently. Graph naturally absorbs these as new edge labels with no downtime. > **Conclusion:** Graph's flexible schema is a *competitive advantage* in fast-evolving domains, while relational's rigid schema is an *integrity advantage* in stable, regulated domains. --- ### **Question B — [14 Marks]** *(Alternative Choice)* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** Compare the **storage, query language, and ACID compliance** aspects of relational and graph databases. Mention at least **three concrete advantages** of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A fraud detection system needs to find "all accounts that have transacted with blacklisted entities within 4 hops, in the last 30 days". Explain how you would model and query this in both paradigms. Which is better, and why? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** | Aspect | Relational | Graph | |---|---|---| | **Storage** | Tables of tuples, normalized, FKs as columns | Nodes, edges, properties persisted as native graph objects | | **Query Language** | SQL (ISO since 1986) | Cypher / GQL (ISO 2023), SPARQL | | **ACID** | Native and mature in all major RDBMS | Supported in Neo4j (4.0+), partially in others | **Three advantages of Relational (3 marks):** 1. **Mature ecosystem** — decades of tooling, ORM frameworks, DBA expertise, and optimization. 2. **Standardized language** — SQL is ISO-standard and portable across vendors. 3. **Bulk aggregations** — columnar RDBMSs outperform graph stores on `SUM`, `AVG`, `GROUP BY` over millions of rows. **Three advantages of Graph (3 marks):** 1. **Index-free adjacency** — multi-hop queries remain performant. 2. **Schema flexibility** — schema-on-read, no migration overhead. 3. **Native M:N relationships** — no junction tables; relationship attributes live on edges. *(1 mark for crisp tabular presentation.)* --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Relational modelling (3 marks):** Tables: `Account (acc_id, ...)`, `Transaction (txn_id, from_acc, to_acc, amount, ts)`, `Blacklist (acc_id, flagged_at)`. Query (4-hop, 30-day window): ```sql WITH RECURSIVE hops AS ( SELECT ba.acc_id AS start_id, bl.acc_id AS blacklisted_id, 1 AS depth, ba.ts FROM Blacklist bl JOIN Transaction ba ON ba.from_acc = bl.acc_id WHERE ba.ts >= NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days' UNION ALL SELECT h.start_id, t.to_acc, h.depth + 1, t.ts FROM hops h JOIN Transaction t ON t.from_acc = h.blacklisted_id WHERE h.depth < 4 AND t.ts >= NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days' ) SELECT DISTINCT start_id FROM hops; ``` This is a 4-level recursive CTE — costly, hard to read, and degrades as `Transaction` table grows. **Graph modelling (3 marks):** ```cypher MATCH (bl:Blacklist)<-[:TRANSACTED_WITH*1..4]-(a:Account) WHERE bl.flagged_at >= date() - duration({days:30}) RETURN DISTINCT a.acc_id; ``` Single pattern. The `*1..4` cap prevents runaway traversal. Index on `TRANSACTED_WITH` and on `flagged_at` makes this near-instantaneous. **Which is better? (1 mark):** **Graph is decisively better** for this workload. The query is a *4-hop path traversal with a time predicate* — the exact sweet spot of graph engines. The relational version requires recursive CTEs with self-JOINs and time-window filters, and will not scale to billions of transactions. In production fraud systems (e.g., Mastercard's *Brighterion*, PayPal's *Neo4j-based graph*), graph models are the de-facto choice for similar ring-detection queries. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make — and where you lose marks:** > 1. **Confusing "Graph model = NoSQL"** — Graph stores *can* be NoSQL, but the distinction is structural, not marketing. A graph model can run on top of a relational engine (e.g., SQL Server graph features). Don't get trapped by buzzwords. > 2. **Saying "Graph is always faster"** — Graphs are faster *for relationship-heavy traversals*. For bulk aggregations or simple `SELECT` lookups, RDBMS still wins. Examiners *will* deduct marks for over-generalization. > 3. **Forgetting property-on-edge** — A frequent viva trap: "Can an edge have a `weight` or `since`?" Yes — this is precisely what distinguishes the **property graph model** from a basic labeled graph. Always mention this when asked about edge capabilities. > 4. **Mixing up GQL/SQL/Cypher/SPARQL** — They are *not* the same. SQL is relational, Cypher is Neo4j's property-graph language, SPARQL is for RDF, GQL is the 2023 ISO graph standard. > 5. **Not labelling the relationship direction** — In Cypher, `-[r:KNOWS]->` is directed, while `-[r:KNOWS]-` is undirected. Examiners expect you to know the difference. > 6. **Skipping the cost-formula justification** — When asked "why is graph faster", students often say "because of pointers" without the $\mathcal{O}(h \cdot d)$ vs $\mathcal{O}(N^h)$ reasoning. Always pair intuition with the asymptotic claim. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Top 12 high-yield points — memorize before the exam:** - [x] **Relational = tables + FKs + JOINs.** **Graph = nodes + edges + properties.** - [x] **Codd (1970)** formalized the relational model; **property graph** formalized for modern engines ~**2010**. - [x] **Foreign key** is a *logical* pointer; **edge** is a *physical, persisted* object. - [x] **Index-free adjacency** = the defining performance feature of graph DBs. - [x] **Cost of traversal:** $\mathcal{O}(h \cdot d)$ for graphs vs $\mathcal{O}(N^h)$ for multi-JOIN relational. - [x] **Junction tables** are needed in RDBMS for M:N — graphs handle M:N natively. - [x] **Schema evolution:** RDBMS = DDL + migration; Graph = no DDL, just start using. - [x] **Use RDBMS** for: OLTP, financial, fixed-schema, bulk aggregation workloads. - [x] **Use Graph** for: social, fraud, knowledge graphs, recommendation, deeply recursive hierarchies. - [x] **Cypher** uses `*1..N` for variable-length paths; **SQL** uses recursive CTEs. - [x] **GQL = ISO/IEC 39075 (2023)**; **SQL = ISO 1986+**. - [x] **Polyglot persistence** — real systems often run *both* (RDBMS for transactions + Graph for relationships). > [!TIP] > **Last-mile exam tip:** When a long-answer question asks you to "compare" the two models, always close with a **single-line conclusion** that names the *use-case boundary* (e.g., "Relational for transactional cores, Graph for relationship-heavy analytics"). Examiners reward a structured, opinionated ending.

topics.title: Cross Domain Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Cross Domain Models # 📘 MODULE 2: CROSS DOMAIN MODELS — SECTION 1 ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cross-Domain Models** in graph databases refer to *vendor-neutral, domain-agnostic graph data models* that can uniformly represent, integrate, and query knowledge spanning multiple application domains (e.g., bioinformatics, social networks, library science, geospatial systems, e-commerce, and IoT) using a single, semantically rich graph representation. The two principal **cross-domain graph models** standardized and adopted in industry and academia are: 1. **Labeled Property Graph (LPG) Model** — also called the **Property Graph Model (PGM)**. 2. **Resource Description Framework (RDF)** — a W3C standard for the Semantic Web, often extended with **RDF Schema (RDFS)** and the **Web Ontology Language (OWL)**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course explicitly studies *Cross-Domain Models* as the bridge between traditional domain-specific databases and the modern knowledge-graph paradigm. Mastery of LPG vs. RDF trade-offs is a frequently tested concept. ### 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Standard Terminology) **Property Graph Model (PGM):** A directed, labeled, attributed multigraph defined as a 7-tuple: $$G_{PG} = (V, E, L_V, L_E, P_V, P_E, \lambda)$$ where: - $V$ = finite set of **Vertices (Nodes)** - $E \subseteq V \times V$ = finite set of **Edges (Relationships)** - $L_V$ = set of **Vertex Labels** - $L_E$ = set of **Edge Types** - $P_V, P_E$ = sets of **Property Keys** - $\lambda$ = function mapping vertices/edges to labels/types and properties. **Resource Description Framework (RDF):** An RDF graph is a finite set of **triples** of the form: $$T = (s, p, o) \in (I \cup B) \times I \times (I \cup B \cup L)$$ where: - $I$ = set of **Internationalized Resource Identifiers (IRIs)** - $B$ = set of **Blank Nodes** - $L$ = set of **Literals** (typed values) > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic — "SPO":** Every RDF statement is **S**ubject → **P**redicate → **O**bject. Think *"The Sky → hasColor → Blue"*. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Universal Translator 📚🌐** Imagine an international airport where passengers speak hundreds of different languages. Without a **Universal Translator**, communication is impossible. **Cross-Domain Models** are exactly that — a universal linguistic framework for data. - **Domain 1 (Medicine):** *"Patient has disease Diabetes"* — uses its own vocabulary (HL7, ICD codes). - **Domain 2 (Pharmacy):** *"Drug Metformin treats Diabetes"* — uses another vocabulary (RxNorm, ATC codes). - **Domain 3 (Insurance):** *"Policy covers treatment for Diabetes"* — yet another vocabulary. A **Cross-Domain Graph Model** (like RDF) acts as the *lingua franca* — it lets all three domains describe their knowledge using one shared, machine-readable format, so a query like *"Find all diabetic patients covered for Metformin across our hospital network"* can be answered in a single graph traversal. The **Property Graph Model (LPG)** is like an *efficient native database language* (Neo4j, JanusGraph), while **RDF + OWL** is like an *academic standard with formal grammar* (used by DBpedia, Wikidata, Google Knowledge Graph). ### 1.4 Why Cross-Domain Models Matter in Industry | Application Domain | Cross-Domain Model Usage | Real-World System | |---|---|---| | **Life Sciences** | RDF + OWL for gene/protein networks | UniProt, Bio2RDF | | **Libraries / Cultural Heritage** | RDF for bibliographic metadata | Europeana, Library of Congress BIBFRAME | | **E-Commerce** | LPG for product/recommendation graphs | Amazon, Walmart Knowledge Graph | | **Social Networks** | LPG for follower/friendship graphs | Facebook TAO, LinkedIn | | **Geospatial Systems** | RDF with GeoSPARQL ontology | OpenStreetMap RDF | | **IoT / Smart Cities** | LPG + RDF hybrid | Siemens MindSphere | | **Cybersecurity** | LPG for attack-graph analytics | MITRE ATT&CK (Neo4j) | ### 1.5 The Two Pillars — LPG vs RDF at a Glance > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Favourite:** Expect direct 3-mark and 14-mark questions comparing LPG and RDF. Memorize the following distinguishing axes: | Axis | LPG (Property Graph) | RDF (Triple Store) | |---|---|---| | **Foundational Unit** | Node/Edge with key-value properties | Triple `(s, p, o)` | | **Schema** | Optional, schema-on-read | Recommended: RDFS / OWL (schema-on-write) | | **Edge Properties** | ✅ First-class citizens | ⚠️ Reification needed or RDF* | | **Inference / Reasoning** | Application-level | Native via OWL reasoners (HermiT, Pellet) | | **Standard Query Language** | Cypher (openCypher), GQL | SPARQL 1.1, SPARQL* | | **Identity** | Native internal IDs + optional URIs | Mandates globally unique IRIs | | **Multi-domain Federation** | Application-level joins | Native via owl:sameAs, federated SPARQL | | **Performance for Traversals** | Highly optimized (index-free adjacency) | Slower for deep traversals | | **Typical Engines** | Neo4j, Memgraph, JanusGraph | Apache Jena, Blazegraph, Stardog, GraphDB | | **Origin / Body** | Industry (Neo4j 2011) | W3C Standard (1999, RDF 1.1 in 2014) | # ⚙️ SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Property Graph (LPG) — Operational Walkthrough A **Labeled Property Graph** stores data as a network of typed nodes connected by typed, directed relationships, with arbitrary key–value properties attached to both. ### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown 1. **Vertex Creation:** Each real-world entity (e.g., *Alice*, *MovieInception*) is a node. 2. **Label Assignment:** Labels classify nodes (*Person*, *Movie*, *City*) — equivalent to a "type tag." 3. **Edge Construction:** Relationships are *directed* and *typed* (*Alice → ACTED_IN → Inception*). 4. **Property Attachment:** Both nodes and edges can carry properties (e.g., `born: 1985`, `role: "Cobb"`). 5. **Multi-Label Support:** A node can simultaneously bear multiple labels (`Person`, `Actor`, `Director`). ### Why Each Step Matters (The "Why & How") - **Step 1 — Vertices:** Allow sparse representation; only existing entities occupy storage. - **Step 2 — Labels:** Enable *indexed scans* (e.g., "find all nodes with label `Person`") without needing a separate schema table. - **Step 3 — Edges:** A typed relationship removes ambiguity — `KNOWS` ≠ `MARRIED_TO`. - **Step 4 — Properties:** Avoid creating extra nodes for attributes (a classic relational anti-pattern). - **Step 5 — Multi-Labeling:** Models polymorphism naturally — a *Person* who is also an *Employee* and a *Customer*. ## 2.2 Anatomy of RDF — Triple by Triple ### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown 1. **Subject (`s`)** is always a resource identified by an **IRI** (e.g., ``). 2. **Predicate (`p`)** is an IRI denoting a property or relation (e.g., `foaf:knows`). 3. **Object (`o`)** is either: - an **IRI** (linking to another resource), - a **Literal** (a string, number, date), or - a **Blank Node** (an anonymous resource `_:`). 4. **Triples are aggregated into a *Directed Labeled Graph*** where subjects and objects are nodes, and predicates are edge labels. > [!NOTE] > **RDF is fundamentally "edge-centric" — every fact is a single labeled edge.** ### 2.2.1 Worked Example — Building an RDF Graph Suppose we want to represent: *"Alice knows Bob since 2010."* ```turtle @prefix ex: . @prefix foaf: . @prefix xsd: . ex:Alice foaf:knows ex:Bob ; ex:since "2010"^^xsd:year . ``` This produces **two triples**: $$T_1 = (\text{ex:Alice},\ \text{foaf:knows},\ \text{ex:Bob})$$ $$T_2 = (\text{ex:Alice},\ \text{ex:since},\ "2010"^\wedge\text{xsd:year})$$ ## 2.3 RDFS — The Schema Layer of RDF > [!IMPORTANT] > **RDF Schema (RDFS)** is the *minimal vocabulary* for describing classes, properties, and hierarchies in RDF. It is the first step toward **ontology**. RDFS introduces five core terms: | RDFS Term | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---| | `rdfs:Class` | A category of resources | `foaf:Person rdf:type rdfs:Class` | | `rdfs:subClassOf` | Hierarchical class relation | `ex:Student rdfs:subClassOf foaf:Person` | | `rdf:Property` | A predicate | `foaf:knows rdf:type rdf:Property` | | `rdfs:subPropertyOf` | Hierarchical property relation | `ex:closeFriend rdfs:subPropertyOf foaf:knows` | | `rdfs:domain` / `rdfs:range` | Restrict the subject/object types | `foaf:knows rdfs:domain foaf:Person` | ## 2.4 OWL — The Ontology Layer The **Web Ontology Language (OWL)** extends RDFS with expressivity needed for true cross-domain knowledge representation: - **Equality & Inequality:** `owl:sameAs`, `owl:differentFrom` - **Property Characteristics:** `owl:TransitiveProperty`, `owl:SymmetricProperty`, `owl:FunctionalProperty`, `owl:InverseFunctionalProperty` - **Class Constructors:** `owl:intersectionOf`, `owl:unionOf`, `owl:complementOf` - **Cardinality Restrictions:** `owl:cardinality`, `owl:minCardinality`, `owl:maxCardinality` - **Enumerations:** `owl:oneOf` > [!TIP] > **Real-World Utility:** OWL powers the **Google Knowledge Graph**, **Wikidata**, **DBpedia**, and the **Gene Ontology** — all of which integrate thousands of cross-domain datasets. ## 2.5 SPARQL — The Query Language of RDF **SPARQL 1.1** is the W3C standard query language for RDF graphs. Its core construct is the **basic graph pattern (BGP)**. ### SPARQL Query Template ```sparql PREFIX foaf: SELECT ?person ?friend WHERE { ?person foaf:knows ?friend . ?person foaf:name "Alice" . } ``` ### Key SPARQL Features for Cross-Domain Use | Feature | Purpose | |---|---| | `OPTIONAL` | Tolerate missing data (essential for federated datasets) | | `UNION` | Merge heterogeneous schemas | | `FILTER` | Constrain results by literal value | | `SERVICE` | Federated query across remote SPARQL endpoints | | `CONSTRUCT` | Output a new RDF subgraph | | `ASK` / `DESCRIBE` | Boolean / descriptive single-result queries | | **Property Paths** (`foaf:knows/foaf:knows`) | Recursive traversal without recursion in code | ## 2.6 🔢 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Cross-Domain Models | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | LPG definition | $G = (V, E, L_V, L_E, P_V, P_E, \lambda)$ | Vertices, Edges, Labels, Properties | | 2 | RDF triple count | $\vert T \vert = \sum_{r \in R} \vert t_r \vert$ | Total triples = sum of triples per resource | | 3 | Graph density (LPG) | $d = \dfrac{2\vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert (\vert V \vert - 1)}$ | Fraction of possible edges present | | 4 | Average degree | $\bar{k} = \dfrac{2\vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert}$ | Avg edges per node | | 5 | Hop distance bound | $h_{\max}(u, v) \le \vert V \vert - 1$ | BFS upper bound | | 6 | RDFS entailment | $(C,\ \text{rdfs:subClassOf},\ D) \wedge (x,\ \text{rdf:type},\ C) \Rightarrow (x,\ \text{rdf:type},\ D)$ | Class inheritance | | 7 | OWL transitivity | $(x,\ P,\ y) \wedge (y,\ P,\ z) \wedge P \in \text{Transitive} \Rightarrow (x,\ P,\ z)$ | Inference rule | | 8 | SPARQL BGP matching | $\mu : V_P \to V_D$ s.t. $\forall (s,p,o) \in P: (\mu(s), p, \mu(o)) \in G_D$ | Pattern homomorphism | | 9 | LPG → RDF mapping | Node → IRI; Label → `rdf:type` triple; Property → $(node,\ pred,\ val)$ | Standardized translation | | 10 | RDF* reification edge | $\langle (s, p, o), q, v \rangle$ | Quoted triple for edge properties | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Do **NOT** confuse `rdfs:domain` (subject type) with `rdfs:range` (object type). This is a 1-mark trap in MCQs. ## 2.7 Mapping Between Property Graph and RDF — Engineering Reality > [!IMPORTANT] > Many enterprise deployments require **PG ↔ RDF interoperability** (e.g., Neo4j ↔ GraphDB via neosemantics plugin, or LPG → RDF via R2RML mapping language). ### Canonical Mapping Rules | LPG Construct | RDF Equivalent | Mechanism | |---|---|---| | Vertex `v` with label `L` | IRI `ex:v` with triple `(ex:v, rdf:type, ex:L)` | IRI minting rule | | Edge `(u, T, v)` | Triple `(ex:u, ex:T, ex:v)` | Edge → predicate triple | | Edge property `k = val` | **RDF*** quoted triple or reified node | `<< ex:u ex:T ex:v >> ex:k val` | | Node property `k = val` | Triple `(ex:v, ex:k, "val")` | Datatype property | | Multi-label node | Multiple `rdf:type` triples | Disjoint union OK | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** LPG is preferred for *transactional graph workloads* (recommendation, fraud detection). RDF is preferred for *semantic integration* (knowledge graphs, life sciences, government open data). The W3C has standardized **RDF 1.2 / RDF*** to close the expressivity gap with LPG. # 🧮 SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Computing Graph Density for a Cross-Domain Knowledge Graph > [!NOTE] > This derivation is **highly likely (≈70%)** to appear as a 7-mark sub-part in the 14-mark question. ### Problem Statement A university knowledge graph contains: - **Vertices:** $V = \{$ *Student, Course, Professor, Department, Project, Publication, Grant, Lab, Building, Event* $\}$, i.e., $\vert V \vert = 10$. - **Edges** (relationships): $E = \{$ *enrolledIn, teaches, belongsTo, advises, partOf, cites, fundedBy, locatedIn, hosts, attendedBy* $\}$, i.e., $\vert E \vert = 10$ edge **types**. - For a *concrete instance*, suppose we load a snapshot with $\vert V \vert = 1{,}000$ nodes and $\vert E \vert = 4{,}500$ edges. **Task:** Compute the *graph density* $d$, the *average degree* $\bar{k}$, and infer whether the graph is **sparse** (typical for cross-domain graphs). ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Recall the density formula:** $$ d = \frac{2 \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert (\vert V \vert - 1)} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute $\vert V \vert = 1{,}000$ and $\vert E \vert = 4{,}500$:** $$ d = \frac{2 \times 4{,}500}{1{,}000 \times (1{,}000 - 1)} $$ **Step 3 — Compute numerator and denominator:** $$ d = \frac{9{,}000}{1{,}000 \times 999} $$ $$ d = \frac{9{,}000}{999{,}000} $$ **Step 4 — Reduce the fraction:** $$ d = \frac{9}{999} = \frac{1}{111} $$ $$ d \approx 0.009009 \approx 9.0 \times 10^{-3} $$ **Step 5 — Average degree computation:** $$ \bar{k} = \frac{2 \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert} = \frac{2 \times 4{,}500}{1{,}000} = \frac{9{,}000}{1{,}000} = 9.0 $$ **Step 6 — Interpret the result:** $$ d \ll 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \bar{k} = 9 $$ A density below **0.01** and an average degree in single digits confirm the graph is **sparse**. Cross-domain knowledge graphs are *almost always sparse* — this justifies **adjacency-list storage** rather than dense adjacency matrices. > [!TIP] > **Threshold Heuristic:** For a graph to be considered *dense*, typically $d > 0.5$. Most real-world graph datasets, including RDF knowledge graphs, satisfy $d < 0.01$. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — RDF Triple Explosion from Edge Properties ### Problem Statement Consider a *Social Network Knowledge Graph* with $\vert V \vert = 1{,}000{,}000$ users. Each user has on average $\bar{k} = 50$ `friend` edges. In the LPG model, each edge may carry **3 properties**: `since`, `strength`, `lastInteraction`. **Compute how many RDF triples are required to represent this graph using classical reification vs. using RDF\*.** ### Step 3.2.1 — Classical Reification (4 auxiliary triples per property) For each edge with $p$ properties, classical RDF reification requires **1 + 4p triples**: - 1 edge triple - 4 reification triples (`rdf:subject`, `rdf:predicate`, `rdf:object`, `rdf:type rdf:Statement`) **per property**. $$ T_{\text{reified}} = \vert E \vert \cdot (1 + 4 \cdot p) $$ With $\vert E \vert = 50 \cdot 10^{6}$ and $p = 3$: $$ T_{\text{reified}} = 50 \times 10^{6} \times (1 + 12) = 50 \times 10^{6} \times 13 = 650 \times 10^{6} \text{ triples} $$ ### Step 3.2.2 — RDF\* Quoted Triple (1 triple per property) RDF\* treats a triple itself as a subject. Each edge property requires **only 1 additional triple**: $$ T_{\text{RDF*}} = \vert E \vert \cdot (1 + p) $$ $$ T_{\text{RDF*}} = 50 \times 10^{6} \times (1 + 3) = 50 \times 10^{6} \times 4 = 200 \times 10^{6} \text{ triples} $$ ### Step 3.2.3 — Storage Reduction Ratio $$ \text{Ratio} = \frac{T_{\text{RDF*}}}{T_{\text{reified}}} = \frac{200}{650} = \frac{4}{13} \approx 0.3077 $$ Therefore RDF\* saves: $$ \Delta = 1 - 0.3077 = 0.6923 = 69.23\% \text{ storage reduction} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** RDF\* reduces storage by **≈69%** compared to classical reification — a critical engineering reason for adopting **RDF 1.2 / SPARQL\*** in modern cross-domain knowledge graphs. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use LPG vs RDF | Criterion | Choose LPG ✅ | Choose RDF ✅ | |---|---|---| | Primary goal is **transactional speed** | ✅ | | | Need **native edge properties** | ✅ | (only with RDF*) | | Schema is **frequently changing** | ✅ (schema-on-read) | | | Domain requires **formal ontology / inference** | | ✅ | | Data sources are **heterogeneous and federated** | | ✅ | | Compliance with **W3C / open data standards** | | ✅ | | Workload is **deep graph traversal** (≥5 hops) | ✅ | | | Workload is **semantic search** with reasoning | | ✅ | | Need to publish as **Linked Open Data (LOD)** | | ✅ | | Project size < 10 nodes, prototype only | ✅ | | ## 3.4 Worked Example — SPARQL Property Path Query (Federated Cross-Domain) **Problem:** In a *university + library + hospital* federated knowledge graph, find all professors who have co-authored a publication with a doctor from a partner hospital. ```sparql PREFIX ex: PREFIX foaf: PREFIX bib: PREFIX hosp: SELECT ?professor ?doctor ?paper WHERE { SERVICE { ?professor a ex:Professor ; foaf:name ?profName . ?paper a bib:Publication ; bib:author ?professor . ?paper bib:author ?doctor . } SERVICE { ?doctor a hosp:Doctor ; foaf:name ?docName . ?doctor hosp:affiliatedWith ?partnerHosp . FILTER(?partnerHosp = ) } } ``` This query exploits **federated SPARQL** (`SERVICE` clause) — a *defining capability* of RDF for cross-domain integration that LPG cannot natively match. # 🗺️ SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Architecture of a Cross-Domain RDF Knowledge Graph Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph srcA["Source Domain A: Library"] A1["LIBRARY
Catalogue RDF"] A2["Bibliographic
Ontology BIBFRAME"] end subgraph srcB["Source Domain B: Life Sciences"] B1["MEDICAL
Patient Records"] B2["Gene Ontology
GO / UniProt"] end subgraph srcC["Source Domain C: Geospatial"] C1["IoT Sensor
Streams"] C2["GeoSPARQL
