Ontology"] end subgraph ing["Ingestion Layer"] ETL1["R2RML Mapper
Relational to RDF"] ETL2["Direct RDF
Publishers"] ETL3["CSV to RDF
Converter"] end subgraph core["Cross-Domain Core RDF Graph"] TRIP["RDF TRIPLE STORE
Apache Jena / Stardog"] OWL["OWL REASONER
HermiT / Pellet"] SPARQL["SPARQL ENDPOINT
Query Service"] end subgraph apps["Cross-Domain Applications"] APP1["Knowledge Graph
Search UI"] APP2["Drug-Disease
Discovery"] APP3["Smart City
Dashboard"] APP4["Academic
Recommender"] end A1 --> ETL2 A2 --> ETL2 B1 --> ETL1 B2 --> ETL2 C1 --> ETL3 C2 --> ETL2 ETL1 --> TRIP ETL2 --> TRIP ETL3 --> TRIP TRIP <--> OWL TRIP <--> SPARQL SPARQL --> APP1 SPARQL --> APP2 SPARQL --> APP3 SPARQL --> APP4 ``` ## 4.2 LPG vs RDF Conceptual Mapping Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph lpg["PROPERTY GRAPH LPG"] v1(("Alice
label Person
born 1985")):::node v2(("Bob

via RDF star quoted triple" .-> s2 end classDef node fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000 classDef rdfnode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000 classDef literal fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 RDFS / OWL Inference Flow — SubClassOf Reasoning ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Fact: ex:Alice rdf:type ex:GradStudent"]:::fact --> B["Schema: ex:GradStudent
rdfs:subClassOf
foaf:Person"]:::schema B --> C["OWL Reasoner
HermiT or Pellet"]:::reasoner A --> C C --> D["Inferred Triple:
ex:Alice rdf:type foaf:Person"]:::infer D --> E["SPARQL Query:
SELECT all Persons"]:::query style A fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309 style B fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1 style C fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d style D fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d style E fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8 ``` ## 4.4 Cross-Domain Model Selection Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD start([New Graph Project]) --> q1{"Need formal
inference?"} q1 -- "YES" --> rdfPath["RDF + RDFS + OWL"] q1 -- "NO" --> q2{"Schema
frequently
evolves?"} q2 -- "YES" --> lpgPath["LPG
Neo4j or Memgraph"] q2 -- "NO" --> q3{"Federate
many open
datasets?"} q3 -- "YES" --> rdfPath q3 -- "NO" --> q4{"Deep graph
traversal
priority?"} q4 -- "YES" --> lpgPath q4 -- "NO" --> q5{"Publish as
Linked Open Data?"} q5 -- "YES" --> rdfPath q5 -- "NO" --> hybrid["Hybrid: LPG + RDF mirror"] rdfPath --> engine1["Stardog / GraphDB / Jena"] lpgPath --> engine2["Neo4j / Memgraph / JanusGraph"] hybrid --> engine3["Neo4j with neosemantics
or JanusGraph + RDF adapter"] ``` # 🎯 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define a Property Graph Model. List its main components with an example.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > A **Property Graph Model (PGM)** is a directed, labeled, attributed multigraph in which both vertices (nodes) and edges (relationships) can carry arbitrary key–value properties and type labels. Formally, $G_{PG} = (V, E, L_V, L_E, P_V, P_E, \lambda)$. > > **Main components (1½ marks):** > 1. **Vertices (V)** — represent real-world entities. > 2. **Edges (E)** — directed, typed relationships between vertices. > 3. **Labels** — classify vertices (e.g., `Person`, `Movie`). > 4. **Edge Types** — classify edges (e.g., `KNOWS`, `ACTED_IN`). > 5. **Properties** — key–value attributes on vertices and edges. > > **Example (1½ marks):** > A vertex `(Alice:Person {age:30})` connected to `(Inception:Movie {year:2010})` via an edge `[:ACTED_IN {role:"Cobb"}]`. ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **What is an RDF triple? Explain the role of Subject, Predicate, and Object with one example.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > An **RDF triple** is the atomic unit of information in the Resource Description Framework, formally expressed as an ordered tuple $(s, p, o)$ — the **Subject–Predicate–Object** statement (1 mark). > > - **Subject (`s`)** is the resource being described, identified by an IRI (e.g., ``). > - **Predicate (`p`)** is the property or relation, identified by an IRI (e.g., `foaf:knows`). > - **Object (`o`)** is either an IRI of another resource, a literal value, or a blank node. > > **Example (1 mark):** > $$(\text{ex:Alice},\ \text{foaf:knows},\ \text{ex:Bob})$$ > states that "Alice knows Bob." > > RDF is the W3C standard for **cross-domain semantic data interchange**, foundational to Linked Open Data. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose marks in 14-mark cross-domain questions by (1) forgetting to draw a diagram and (2) failing to *contrast* LPG and RDF explicitly rather than describe each in isolation. Always include a **comparison table** AND a **diagram** for full marks. --- ### **Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Resource Description Framework (RDF)** data model. Discuss the role of **RDF Schema (RDFS)** and the **Web Ontology Language (OWL)** in building a cross-domain knowledge graph. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a **SPARQL query** to retrieve, from a federated cross-domain RDF graph containing *university*, *publication*, and *hospital* endpoints, the names of all professors who have **co-authored a paper with a doctor** specializing in *cardiology*. Show the federated `SERVICE` clause clearly. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Answer — Part (a) **1. RDF Data Model (2 marks):** RDF is a W3C standard for representing structured information on the Web as a **directed, labeled graph** of *triples* of the form $(s, p, o)$. Every triple asserts a fact: *"subject has predicate object."* Subjects and predicates are always IRIs; objects can be IRIs, blank nodes, or literals. **2. Diagram (2 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A["ex:Alice
Subject"] -->|foaf:knows| B["ex:Bob
Object IRI"] A -->|foaf:name| L1["Alice
Literal"] B -->|foaf:age| L2["30
Literal xsd:integer"] classDef iri fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af classDef lit fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d class A,B iri class L1,L2 lit ``` **3. RDFS Role (1½ marks):** RDFS provides the schema vocabulary: `rdfs:Class`, `rdfs:subClassOf`, `rdfs:Property`, `rdfs:subPropertyOf`, `rdfs:domain`, `rdfs:range`. It enables **simple inference** — e.g., if `GradStudent rdfs:subClassOf Person` and `Alice rdf:type GradStudent`, the reasoner infers `Alice rdf:type Person`. **4. OWL Role (1½ marks):** OWL extends RDFS with greater expressivity for true cross-domain ontologies: `owl:TransitiveProperty`, `owl:SymmetricProperty`, `owl:FunctionalProperty`, `owl:InverseFunctionalProperty`, `owl:sameAs`, class constructors (`intersectionOf`, `unionOf`, `complementOf`), and cardinality restrictions. OWL reasoners (HermiT, Pellet) perform **automatic classification and consistency checking**, vital when integrating heterogeneous datasets. --- #### ✅ Model Answer — Part (b) **Federated SPARQL Query (7 marks):** ```sparql PREFIX ex: PREFIX foaf: PREFIX bib: PREFIX hosp: PREFIX dct: SELECT DISTINCT ?profName ?docName ?paperTitle WHERE { # ---- Service 1: University endpoint ---- SERVICE { ?professor a ex:Professor ; foaf:name ?profName . ?paper a bib:Publication ; dct:title ?paperTitle ; bib:author ?professor ; bib:author ?author2 . } # ---- Service 2: Hospital endpoint ---- SERVICE { ?author2 a hosp:Doctor ; foaf:name ?docName ; hosp:specialty . } # ---- Optional filter to ensure same paper ---- FILTER(?author2 != ?professor) } ORDER BY ?profName ?paperTitle ``` **Explanation of key constructs (verbatim valuation key):** | Clause | Marks Allocated | Reasoning | |---|---|---| | `PREFIX` declarations | 1 | Domain hygiene | | `SERVICE ` block with professor, paper, co-author | 2 | Federated pattern | | `SERVICE ` block with doctor + cardiology filter | 2 | Cross-domain join | | `FILTER` to exclude self-pairing | 1 | Correctness | | `SELECT DISTINCT` + `ORDER BY` | 1 | Result quality | --- ### **Question B2 — Choice B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` **(a)** Compare and contrast the **Property Graph Model (LPG)** and the **RDF** data model across **at least six axes**. State one real-world engine for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a **cross-domain LPG schema** for a *Smart Healthcare Knowledge Graph* integrating three domains — **Hospital**, **Pharmacy**, and **Insurance** — and write the corresponding **Cypher (openCypher)** statements to (i) create nodes, (ii) create a relationship, and (iii) attach properties. Justify why LPG was chosen over RDF for this use-case. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Answer — Part (a) **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Axis | LPG | RDF | |---|---|---| | Atomic unit | Vertex/Edge with properties | Triple $(s, p, o)$ | | Schema | Optional, schema-on-read | Recommended RDFS/OWL, schema-on-write | | Edge properties | First-class | Requires reification or RDF* | | Inference | Application logic | Native via OWL reasoners | | Query language | Cypher / GQL | SPARQL 1.1 / SPARQL* | | Identity | Local IDs + optional URIs | Mandatory global IRIs | | Federation | Application-level joins | Native `SERVICE` clause | | Performance | Index-free adjacency, fast traversal | Slower at deep hops | **Real-world engines (1 mark):** LPG — **Neo4j**; RDF — **Apache Jena** or **Stardog**. **One-line distinction (1 mark):** *LPG is an industry-strength, traversal-optimized graph database model; RDF is a W3C-standardized, semantics-first knowledge representation framework enabling cross-domain Linked Open Data.* --- #### ✅ Model Answer — Part (b) **LPG Schema Design (3 marks):** | Domain | Node Label | Key Properties | |---|---|---| | Hospital | `Patient`, `Doctor`, `Diagnosis` | `name`, `dob`, `icdCode` | | Pharmacy | `Drug`, `Prescription` | `name`, `dosage`, `atcCode` | | Insurance | `Policy`, `Claim` | `policyId`, `coverage`, `premium` | **Relationship Types (edge schema):** | From | Relationship | To | |---|---|---| | Patient | `DIAGNOSED_WITH` | Diagnosis | | Patient | `PRESCRIBED` | Drug | | Policy | `COVERS` | Diagnosis / Drug | | Claim | `FILED_AGAINST` | Policy | **Cypher Implementation (3 marks):** ```cypher // (i) Create nodes CREATE (p:Patient {patientId: "P001", name: "Anita", dob: date("1980-05-12")}) CREATE (d:Drug {drugId: "D100", name: "Metformin", atcCode: "A10BA02"}) CREATE (pol:Policy {policyId: "INS-2024-789", coverage: "Diabetes"}) // (ii) Create relationship with properties MATCH (p:Patient {patientId: "P001"}), (d:Drug {drugId: "D100"}) CREATE (p)-[:PRESCRIBED {dosage: "500mg", frequency: "twiceDaily", since: date("2024-01-15")}]->(d) // (iii) Attach a property later MATCH (pol:Policy {policyId: "INS-2024-789"})-[r:COVERS]-() SET r.coPaymentPercent = 20 RETURN p, d, pol; ``` **Justification — Why LPG over RDF (1 mark):** The Smart Healthcare workload involves **deep multi-hop traversals** (e.g., "find all patients prescribed a drug that is *not* covered by their policy"), **frequent schema evolution** (new drug classes, new insurance tiers), and **low-latency transactional reads**. LPG via Neo4j provides *index-free adjacency* for sub-millisecond hop traversal, which RDF triple stores cannot match. Additionally, native edge properties (such as `dosage`, `frequency`, `coPaymentPercent`) are first-class in LPG, avoiding the reification overhead of classical RDF. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day** - 🔑 **Property Graph Model** is a 7-tuple $G = (V, E, L_V, L_E, P_V, P_E, \lambda)$. Nodes and edges **both** carry labels and properties. - 🔑 **RDF triple** = $(s, p, o)$ — Subject, Predicate, Object — the **SPO** mnemonic. - 🔑 Subjects and predicates **must be IRIs**; objects may be IRIs, literals, or blank nodes. - 🔑 **RDFS** provides schema primitives: `Class`, `subClassOf`, `Property`, `subPropertyOf`, `domain`, `range`. - 🔑 **OWL** adds expressivity: transitivity, symmetry, inverse-functional properties, cardinality, `sameAs`. - 🔑 **SPARQL** is the W3C query language. `SERVICE` enables **federated queries** across endpoints. - 🔑 **Cypher** is the openCypher query language for LPG; uses ASCII-art `(a)-[r:TYPE]->(b)`. - 🔑 **RDF\*** (RDF 1.2) allows quoted triples, enabling first-class edge properties without reification — saving ~69% storage. - 🔑 **Cross-domain** = same graph can integrate Library + Hospital + Geospatial + Finance data via shared IRIs. - 🔑 Choose **LPG** when traversal speed + flexibility matter; choose **RDF** when standards, inference, and federation matter. - 🔑 Always mention **at least one real-world engine** in long answers — Neo4j (LPG), Stardog / GraphDB / Jena (RDF). - 🔑 **Density** formula: $d = \frac{2 \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert(\vert V \vert - 1)}$; cross-domain graphs are always **sparse** ($d \ll 1$). - 🔑 Don't confuse `rdfs:domain` (subject type) with `rdfs:range` (object type) — classic MCQ trap. - 🔑 Draw a diagram in every 7-mark sub-question — it earns you the "presentation" margin marks. - 🔑 Memorize one **SPARQL federated query** and one **Cypher CREATE/MATCH** snippet — they're high-yield for 14-mark answers.

topics.title: Common Modelling Pitfalls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Common Modelling Pitfalls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Modelling Pitfalls** are systematic errors, anti-patterns, and structural mistakes introduced during the conceptual design phase of a property graph (or RDF) model. They manifest as redundant nodes, ambiguous relationship semantics, missing constraints, incorrect directionality, and inappropriate use of labels, relationship types, and properties — all of which degrade query performance, data integrity, and semantic clarity in a graph database.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, modelling pitfalls are evaluated under the cognitive level **Apply / Analyze** of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. Students must demonstrate the ability to **detect, classify, and remediate** structural defects in a candidate property graph before committing to the schema.

The dominant family of pitfalls can be grouped into **five canonical classes**:

| Class | Canonical Pitfall | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Structural** | Confusing Nodes vs Relationships | Loss of semantic precision |
| **Directional** | Wrong Relationship Direction | Traversal dead-ends |
| **Cardinality** | Over- / Under-Granularity | Bloat or hidden semantics |
| **Labelling** | Misuse of Labels | Poor indexing, weak constraints |
| **Property** | Property Overload | Storing graph structure in scalars |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The City Map Metaphor**

Imagine you are designing a **digital city map** for a navigation application. A *graph database* is the underlying street network.

- If you forget to add a node for the **City Hospital**, the navigation app cannot route ambulances to it — this is the **Missing Node Pitfall**.
- If you draw a one-way road from `Hospital → School` instead of `School → Hospital`, you trap users on the wrong side — this is the **Wrong Direction Pitfall**.
- If you build **1,000 separate road segments** between two buildings that share one driveway, you have created the **Over-Granularity Pitfall** (redundancy).
- If you paint every building in the city the same red colour regardless of type, your search filter "find all schools" fails — this is the **Misuse of Labels Pitfall**.
- If you store the name of *every connecting street* inside a single property of one node instead of as separate relationship edges, you have committed the **Property Overload Pitfall** — your data is now un-traversable.

**Key Insight:** A graph is only as good as its **semantic geometry**. Bad geometry silently breaks every query built on top of it.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Graph Modelling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following quantitative thresholds (commonly used in industry-grade graph audits) are treated as **rule-of-thumb benchmarks** for KTU board answers:
> - **Average Fan-Out (Degree Centrality)** $\le$ **8** for human-readable traversals.
> - **Max Path Length** for a single query hop chain $\le$ **6** (cognitive limit).
> - **Label Cardinality Ratio** (nodes per label) should be **balanced** — no single label holding **>70%** of all nodes.

---

## 1.4 Course Outcome Mapping (KTU 2024)

| Course Outcome (CO) | Cognitive Level (Bloom) | Topic Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| **CO2** | Apply / Analyze | Design, critique, and refactor property graph schemas |
| **CO3** | Evaluate | Benchmark queries against well-modelled vs. pitfall-laden graphs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Canonical Pitfall Classes — Structured Logic

### 2.1.1 Pitfall Class I — The Node–Relationship Confusion (Semantic Ambiguity)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core 'Why':** Students frequently treat *everything* as a node. However, a fundamental design heuristic states:
>
> **"A noun that primarily exists to connect other nouns is a relationship; a noun that has independent existence and properties is a node."**

- **How it fails:** Storing `MARRIED_TO` as a node (e.g., `(:Marriage {date, location})`) when it could be a simple edge `(Person)-[:MARRIED_TO {since: 2015}]->(Person)`. The Marriage node then requires an extra hop for every query.
- **When a node IS correct:** A marriage may be a node if it carries **multiple outgoing relationships** (e.g., to witnesses, officiants, venues, gifts). Otherwise, it is a relationship.
- **Decision Rule:** A relationship is preferred if it can be expressed as a *single* edge between two existing entities. A node is required when **the "thing" must be connected to 3 or more other entities**, or when the relationship is **n-ary** (involving more than two participants).

### 2.1.2 Pitfall Class II — Incorrect Relationship Directionality

- **Symmetric vs Asymmetric**: Friendships are *symmetric* (A→B iff B→A), but `MANAGES` is *asymmetric*.
- **Storage rule:** Cypher stores edges **directionally**. A `(Bob)-[:KNOWS]->(Alice)` does NOT automatically imply the reverse unless explicitly modelled.
- **Traversal impact:** A pattern like `(p1)-[:KNOWS]-(p2)` (undirected) in Cypher matches edges in **both** stored directions, but **using the wrong direction in a directed relationship breaks business logic** (e.g., `MANAGES` reversed means "is managed by").
- **Rule of thumb:** If the verb is reversible in plain English, store symmetrically via two edges OR model as a relationship with a property `since`.

### 2.1.3 Pitfall Class III — Over-Granularity (Node Explosion)

- **Symptom:** Introducing a chain of intermediate nodes `(A)-[:CONNECTED_VIA]->(B)-[:CONNECTED_VIA]->(C)` where a single relationship `(A)-[:CONNECTED_VIA]->(C)` would suffice.
- **Cost:** Each redundant hop multiplies traversal cost by the degree of the intermediate node → **exponential blow-up** on dense subgraphs.
- **Detection Metric:** Compute the **hop inflation factor** $\eta$ :

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Actual Hops in Traversal}}{\text{Logical Hops Required}}
$$

If $\eta > 1.2$, the model is over-granulated and must be flattened.

### 2.1.4 Pitfall Class IV — Under-Granularity (Monolithic Node)

- **Symptom:** Stuffing heterogeneous sub-entities into a single node label (e.g., `(:Entity {type, attributes_blob})`).
- **Cost:** Loses indexability, schema constraints, and the ability to traverse sub-entity relationships.
- **Remedy:** Decompose using the **Single Responsibility Principle for Nodes** — each node label should represent one *kind* of real-world entity.

### 2.1.5 Pitfall Class V — Misuse of Labels and Properties

- **Label Overuse:** Adding a label per attribute (`(:Person:Student:Employee:Parent:Driver:...)`) creates combinatorial explosion in pattern matching.
- **Property Overuse:** Storing *relational structure* inside a property (e.g., `friend_ids = [12, 45, 78]`) instead of as `(:Person)-[:FRIEND]->(:Person)` edges.
- **Best practice:** **Properties hold *atomic* values**. **Relationships hold *links***. **Labels hold *kinds***.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All metrics below are examinable. Commit them to memory.

| Metric | Formula | Healthy Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hop Inflation Factor** $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{H_{\text{actual}}}{H_{\text{logical}}}$ | $\eta \le 1.2$ | Detects over-granularity |
| **Label Cardinality Ratio** $L_r$ | $L_r^{(i)} = \dfrac{\vert N_i \vert}{\sum_{j}\vert N_j \vert}$ | $L_r^{(i)} \le 0.70$ | Detects label imbalance |
| **Average Node Degree** $\bar{d}$ | $\bar{d} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} d(v_i)}{\vert V \vert}$ | $\bar{d} \le 8$ | Detects hub nodes |
| **Graph Density** $\rho$ | $\rho = \dfrac{2\vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert (\vert V \vert - 1)}$ | $0 \le \rho \le 1$ | Measures edge saturation |
| **Edge-to-Node Ratio** $\gamma$ | $\gamma = \dfrac{\vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert}$ | $\gamma \in [1, 3]$ | Detects under-/over-connectivity |
| **Traversal Cost (BFS)** $T_c$ | $T_c = \sum_{l=1}^{d_{\max}} \vert V_l \vert$ | $T_c \le 10^4$ | Estimates query workload |
| **Symmetry Index** $S$ | $S = \dfrac{\vert E \vert_{\text{sym}}}{\vert E \vert_{\text{total}}}$ | domain-dependent | Measures bidirectional storage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical syntax note for KTU answer scripts:** The vertical pipe `|` is a reserved markdown table separator. In the *model answer*, write $\vert V \vert$ for absolute value, **never** $\vert V \vert$. This is a common deduction by strict KTU examiners.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fraud Detection (Finance):** A single mislabelled transaction node can hide a money-laundering ring from the traversal engine.
- **Knowledge Graphs (Search/AI):** Incorrect relationship directionality in a `CONTAINS`/`IS_PART_OF` edge propagates wrong answers in LLM-RAG pipelines.
- **Network Telemetry (5G/IoT):** Over-granulated topology graphs cause hop explosions in latency-aware pathfinding algorithms (e.g., Dijkstra over millions of redundant edges).
- **Social Networks:** Property-overload (storing friend lists as arrays) makes recommendation engines **non-traversable** — they degrade from O(1) edge lookups to O(n) array scans.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Hop Inflation Factor $\eta$ (Worked Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** Consider a `Person` connected to a `Company` via a `Job`. A faulty model inserts an intermediate `Role` node that is otherwise redundant.

### Step 1: Define the logical model
The **intended** traversal from Person to Company should require exactly **1 hop** (direct relationship):
$$
H_{\text{logical}} = 1
$$

### Step 2: Define the faulty actual model
The implemented traversal requires:
- Person → Role → Company
$$
H_{\text{actual}} = 2
$$

### Step 3: Compute the inflation factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{H_{\text{actual}}}{H_{\text{logical}}} \\[6pt]
    &= \frac{2}{1} \\[6pt]
    &= 2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compare against the threshold
The healthy upper bound is $\eta \le 1.2$.

$$
\eta = 2.0 \;\gg\; 1.2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Pitfall detected: Over-Granularity}
$$

### Step 5: Remediation
Collapse the intermediate node into a relationship property:
- **Before:** `(Person)-[:HAS]->(Role {title})-[:AT]->(Company)`
- **After:** `(Person)-[:WORKS_AT {role: "Engineer"}]->(Company)`

Now $H_{\text{actual}} = 1$ and $\eta = 1.0$ — within the healthy range.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Average Degree and Hub Detection

### Step 1: Set up the graph
Suppose we have a small social subgraph with the following degrees:

| Node | Alice | Bob | Carol | Dave | Eve |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Degree $d(v_i)$ | 3 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 2 |

### Step 2: Sum the degrees

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} d(v_i) = 3 + 2 + 4 + 1 + 2 = 12
$$

### Step 3: Compute average degree

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{d} &= \frac{\sum_{i} d(v_i)}{\vert V \vert} \\[6pt]
       &= \frac{12}{5} \\[6pt]
       &= 2.4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Verify against the healthy threshold
$\bar{d} = 2.4 \le 8$ — **acceptable**.

### Step 5: Identify hub candidates
Carol has degree $4$, which is the maximum. Compute the **hub ratio**:

$$
h_{\text{carol}} = \frac{d(\text{Carol})}{\bar{d}} = \frac{4}{2.4} \approx 1.67
$$

If $h_v > 2$, the node is a structural **hub** and may be a symptom of an under-granulated model where Carol should have been broken into multiple sub-entities.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Graph Density and Its Diagnostic Power

### Step 1: Define the input
Consider a graph with $\vert V \vert = 6$ nodes and $\vert E \vert = 9$ edges.

### Step 2: Apply the density formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{2 \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert (\vert V \vert - 1)} \\[6pt]
    &= \frac{2 \times 9}{6 \times 5} \\[6pt]
    &= \frac{18}{30} \\[6pt]
    &= 0.6
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Interpret

$$
\rho = 0.6 \;\Rightarrow\; 60\% \text{ of all possible edges exist}
$$

A density above **0.5** typically signals that:
1. The domain is genuinely dense (e.g., a fully connected mesh network), **or**
2. The model has been over-granulated and is collapsing distinct entities into spurious connections.

### Step 4: Disambiguation test
Compute the **edge-to-node ratio** $\gamma$:

$$
\gamma = \frac{\vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert} = \frac{9}{6} = 1.5
$$

Since $1 \le \gamma \le 3$, the connectivity is structurally reasonable. Therefore $\rho = 0.6$ reflects genuine domain density (not a modelling pitfall).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pitfall Diagnosis Decision Tree

| Observation in Model | Likely Pitfall | Metric to Verify | Remediation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Traversal takes 3+ hops to reach an obviously related entity | Over-Granularity | $\eta$ | Collapse intermediate nodes into properties |
| Query for "type A entities" returns mixed types | Under-Granularity | $L_r$ | Split monolithic node into multiple labels |
| Reverse-direction query returns empty results | Wrong Direction | Symmetry $S$ | Reverse edge or add inverse edge |
| A property contains comma-separated lists of IDs | Property Overload | $T_c$ | Extract as relationship edges |
| Single label contains >70% of all nodes | Label Imbalance | $L_r^{(i)}$ | Re-label subcategories |
| Node has no outgoing/incoming edges | Orphan Node | Degree $d(v)=0$ | Delete or wire to a hub |
| Relationship name is generic (`RELATED_TO`, `HAS`) | Semantic Vagueness | Manual review | Rename to domain-specific verb |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Pitfall Taxonomy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** representing the taxonomy of modelling pitfalls, their root causes, observable symptoms, and remediation actions. Every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed to avoid Mermaid reserved-keyword collisions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["Common Modelling Pitfalls"] --> pit1["P1 Node Relationship Confusion"]
    root --> pit2["P2 Directional Error"]
    root --> pit3["P3 Over Granularity"]
    root --> pit4["P4 Under Granularity"]
    root --> pit5["P5 Label and Property Misuse"]

    pit1 --> sym1["Symptom Extra hop cost"]
    pit1 --> rem1["Remedy N ary test"]
    pit2 --> sym2["Symptom Asymmetric query failure"]
    pit2 --> rem2["Remedy Add inverse edge"]
    pit3 --> sym3["Symptom Hop inflation eta over 1.2"]
    pit3 --> rem3["Remedy Collapse into property"]
    pit4 --> sym4["Symptom Label cardinality ratio over 0.7"]
    pit4 --> rem4["Remedy Split by single responsibility"]
    pit5 --> sym5["Symptom Property contains ID list"]
    pit5 --> rem5["Remedy Extract as edge"]

    sym1 --> metric["Apply Metric Hop Inflation"]
    sym3 --> metric
    sym4 --> metricL["Apply Metric Label Ratio"]
    sym2 --> metricS["Apply Metric Symmetry Index"]
    sym5 --> metricP["Apply Metric Traversal Cost"]

    metric --> out["Diagnosis Output"]
    metricL --> out
    metricS --> out
    metricP --> out

    out --> fix["Schema Refactor"]
```

---

## 4.2 Side-by-Side — Bad Model vs. Refactored Model (Property Graph Schematic)

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram shows how a single pitfall (over-granularity + property overload) gets refactored into a clean property graph.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BAD["BAD MODEL Over Granular and Property Overloaded"]
        P1A["Person Alice"] -->|HAS| RA["Role id 101"]
        RA -->|AT| CA["Company Acme"]
        CA -->|HAS_LOC| PA["Property loc id list 1,2,3"]
    end

    subgraph GOOD["REFACTORED MODEL Clean Property Graph"]
        P1B["Person Alice"] -->|WORKS_AT role Engineer| CB["Company Acme"]
        P1B -->|KNOWS| P2B["Person Bob"]
        P2B -->|WORKS_AT role Manager| CB
    end
```

**Diagnostic reasoning embedded in the diagram:**
- In `BAD`: $\eta = 2$ (Person → Role → Company), and `Property loc id list` is an un-traversable scalar.
- In `GOOD`: $\eta = 1$, and relationships carry semantic verb-object structure with atomic properties.

---

## 4.3 Diagnostic Workflow (Sequential Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["Start Candidate Graph Model"] --> q1{"Are all nouns represented as nodes or edges?"}
    q1 -- No --> act1["Apply N ary Test"]
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{"Does traversal return null in reverse?"}
    act1 --> q2
    q2 -- Yes --> act2["Add inverse or symmetric edge"]
    q2 -- No --> q3{"Is eta over 1.2?"}
    act2 --> q3
    q3 -- Yes --> act3["Collapse intermediate node"]
    q3 -- No --> q4{"Does any property hold an ID list?"}
    act3 --> q4
    q4 -- Yes --> act4["Extract as relationship edges"]
    q4 -- No --> q5{"Is label ratio over 0.7 for any label?"}
    act4 --> q5
    q5 -- Yes --> act5["Split into sub labels"]
    q5 -- No --> ok["Model passes pitfall audit"]
    act5 --> ok
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

> **Q1.** Define the *Node–Relationship Confusion* pitfall in graph modelling. State the **N-ary test** used to disambiguate when a concept should be modelled as a node versus a relationship. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> The *Node–Relationship Confusion* pitfall arises when a modeller assigns a real-world concept to the wrong structural primitive. Concepts that primarily act as **connectors between other concepts** should be relationships, while concepts with **independent existence and properties** should be nodes.
>
> The **N-ary Test** states: *if the concept can be expressed as a single edge between two existing entities, model it as a relationship; if it must connect to three or more entities (i.e., it is n-ary with $n \ge 3$), or carries attributes that themselves require multiple relationships, model it as a node.*
>
> **Example:** `(:Marriage {date})` between two people → relationship. `(:Marriage)` connected to two spouses, two witnesses, and a venue → node.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q2.** What is the **Hop Inflation Factor** $\eta$? Compute $\eta$ for a model where the logical traversal requires 2 hops but the actual traversal requires 5 hops. Comment on whether the model is healthy. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> The **Hop Inflation Factor** $\eta$ is a diagnostic metric defined as the ratio of *actual hops* in an implemented traversal to the *logical hops* required by the domain semantics:
>
> $$\eta = \frac{H_{\text{actual}}}{H_{\text{logical}}}$$
>
> **Computation:**
>
> $$\eta = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5$$
>
> The healthy range is $\eta \le 1.2$. Since $2.5 \gg 1.2$, the model exhibits **over-granularity** and must be refactored by collapsing redundant intermediate nodes into relationship properties.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **(a)** Explain the **five canonical classes of modelling pitfalls** in property graph databases, with one illustrative example for each. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A candidate graph has $\vert V \vert = 50$ nodes and $\vert E \vert = 180$ edges. Compute the **average degree** $\bar{d}$, the **graph density** $\rho$, and the **edge-to-node ratio** $\gamma$. Based on the computed values, diagnose whether the model suffers from over- or under-connectivity. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

> The five canonical classes are:
>
> 1. **Node–Relationship Confusion** — Treating a connector as a node. *Example:* Modelling `(:Friendship {since})` as a node when `(A)-[:FRIEND {since}]->(B)` suffices.
> 2. **Directional Error** — Storing an asymmetric relationship with the wrong direction. *Example:* `(Employee)-[:MANAGES]->(Department)` reversed as `(Department)-[:MANAGES]->(Employee)`.
> 3. **Over-Granularity** — Inserting redundant intermediate nodes. *Example:* `(A)-[:AT]->(City)-[:AT]->(Country)` instead of `(A)-[:LOCATED_IN]->(Country)`.
> 4. **Under-Granularity** — Stuffing heterogeneous sub-entities into one label. *Example:* `(:Entity {type, name})` mixing `Person`, `Company`, `Product`.
> 5. **Label/Property Misuse** — Storing relational structure as a property. *Example:* `(:Person {friend_ids: [1,2,3]})` instead of `(:Person)-[:FRIEND]->(:Person)`.

**Model Solution to (b):**

> **Step 1 — Average Degree:**
>
> $$\bar{d} = \frac{2 \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert} = \frac{2 \times 180}{50} = 7.2$$
>
> Since $\bar{d} = 7.2 \le 8$, the average is within the healthy range, but it is approaching the upper bound, suggesting the presence of dense hub nodes.
>
> **Step 2 — Graph Density:**
>
> $$\rho = \frac{2 \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert (\vert V \vert - 1)} = \frac{2 \times 180}{50 \times 49} = \frac{360}{2450} \approx 0.147$$
>
> A density of $0.147$ ($\approx 14.7\%$) is moderate, indicating a meaningfully connected but not saturated graph.
>
> **Step 3 — Edge-to-Node Ratio:**
>
> $$\gamma = \frac{\vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert} = \frac{180}{50} = 3.6$$
>
> Since $3.6 > 3$ (the upper healthy bound), the model exhibits **over-connectivity**, possibly due to over-granulation (extra redundant edges from intermediate nodes) or a genuinely dense domain.
>
> **Step 4 — Diagnosis and Recommendation:**
> The combined evidence ($\gamma = 3.6$ and $\bar{d} = 7.2$) suggests the model is on the **edge of over-granulation**. The remediation is to:
> 1. Audit nodes with degree $\ge 10$ for hub symptoms.
> 2. Collapse any intermediate nodes that do not carry independent properties.
> 3. Re-compute $\eta$ on the refactored model to confirm $\eta \le 1.2$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]

> **(a)** With the aid of a **Mermaid block diagram**, illustrate the **refactoring workflow** for transforming an over-granulated, property-overloaded graph into a clean property graph. Annotate each transformation with the relevant diagnostic metric. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Define the **Symmetry Index** $S$ and **Label Cardinality Ratio** $L_r$. For a graph with 200 nodes distributed as: `Person` = 150, `Company` = 40, `Product` = 10, and 60 symmetric edges out of 100 total, compute $S$ and the per-label $L_r$ values. Identify any imbalance. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

> **Refactoring Workflow (Mermaid block diagram):**
>
> ```mermaid
> graph LR
>     A["Over Granulated Bad Model"] -->|Measure eta| B["Compute Hop Inflation"]
>     B -->|eta over 1.2| C["Identify Intermediate Nodes"]
>     C --> D["Collapse into Edge Property"]
>     A2["Property Overloaded Bad Model"] -->|Detect ID list| E["Extract as Edge"]
>     D --> F["Refactored Clean Graph"]
>     E --> F
>     F -->|Re measure eta and gamma| G["Audit Pass"]
> ```
>
> **Annotations:**
> - Transition `A → B` uses the **Hop Inflation Factor** $\eta$.
> - Transition `A2 → E` uses the **Traversal Cost** $T_c$ to detect un-traversable ID-list properties.
> - Final transition `F → G` re-measures $\eta$ and $\gamma$ to confirm a healthy model.

**Model Solution to (b):**

> **Definitions:**
> - **Symmetry Index** $S$ measures the proportion of edges that are stored bidirectionally:
>
> $$S = \frac{\vert E \vert_{\text{sym}}}{\vert E \vert_{\text{total}}}$$
>
> - **Label Cardinality Ratio** $L_r^{(i)}$ measures the share of nodes held by label $i$:
>
> $$L_r^{(i)} = \frac{\vert N_i \vert}{\sum_{j} \vert N_j \vert}$$
>
> **Step 1 — Compute $S$:**
>
> $$S = \frac{60}{100} = 0.6$$
>
> Interpretation: $60\%$ of edges are symmetric. This is domain-dependent; in a social graph this is healthy, in a `MANAGES` graph it would be a **directional error** symptom.
>
> **Step 2 — Compute $L_r$ for each label:**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> L_r^{(\text{Person})} &= \frac{150}{200} = 0.75 \\[4pt]
> L_r^{(\text{Company})} &= \frac{40}{200} = 0.20 \\[4pt]
> L_r^{(\text{Product})} &= \frac{10}{200} = 0.05
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Identify imbalance:**
>
> Since $L_r^{(\text{Person})} = 0.75 > 0.70$, the `Person` label is **over-dominant**. The model may be suffering from **under-granulation**, where sub-types of `Person` (e.g., `Employee`, `Customer`, `Vendor`) are collapsed into a single label.
>
> **Step 4 — Remediation:**
> Decompose `Person` into sub-labels such as `(:Person:Employee)`, `(:Person:Customer)`, and `(:Person:Vendor)`, then re-compute $L_r$ to confirm each sub-label holds $\le 70\%$ of the total nodes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (strict KTU 2024 pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing the formula symbols:** Writing $\vert V \vert$ with literal pipes inside a markdown table will break the table parser. Always use `\vert V \vert` in LaTeX. Examiners deduct **1 mark** per instance of broken syntax.
> 2. **Skipping the diagnostic step:** If a question asks for a diagnosis, **never** end with just a formula. You must state the threshold, the comparison, and the conclusion (e.g., "since $\eta = 2.5 > 1.2$, the model is over-granulated").
> 3. **Forgetting the unit/range check:** A correct $\bar{d} = 7.2$ without the comment "$\le 8$, therefore healthy" is treated as a **partial answer** (typically 5/7 instead of 7/7).
> 4. **Overusing `RELATED_TO`:** Examiners deduct for vague relationship names. Always rename to a domain-specific verb-object pair (e.g., `PURCHASED`, `AUTHORED`, `ENROLLED_IN`).
> 5. **Ignoring the reverse-direction test:** A model with no inverse edges is automatically flagged for directional ambiguity even if the data is correct.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Definition:** Common Modelling Pitfalls are systematic structural errors in property graph schemas that degrade query performance, semantic clarity, and maintainability.
> - **Five Canonical Classes:** Node–Relationship Confusion, Directional Error, Over-Granularity, Under-Granularity, Label/Property Misuse.
> - **N-ary Test:** A concept is a **node** if it must connect to $\ge 3$ entities; otherwise it is a **relationship**.
> - **Hop Inflation Factor:** $\eta = \frac{H_{\text{actual}}}{H_{\text{logical}}}$, healthy when $\eta \le 1.2$.
> - **Average Degree:** $\bar{d} = \frac{2 \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert}$, healthy when $\bar{d} \le 8$.
> - **Graph Density:** $\rho = \frac{2 \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert (\vert V \vert - 1)}$, range $0$ to $1$.
> - **Edge-to-Node Ratio:** $\gamma = \frac{\vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert}$, healthy in $[1, 3]$.
> - **Label Cardinality Ratio:** $L_r^{(i)} \le 0.70$ per label.
> - **Symmetry Index:** $S = \frac{\vert E \vert_{\text{sym}}}{\vert E \vert_{\text{total}}}$, domain-dependent.
> - **Rule of Thumb:** *Properties hold atoms; relationships hold links; labels hold kinds.*
> - **Directionality Rule:** Symmetric relationships (e.g., `KNOWS`, `FRIEND`) need **two edges** or an undirected match; asymmetric ones (e.g., `MANAGES`) must point correctly.
> - **Refactor Trigger:** Any of $\eta > 1.2$, $\bar{d} > 8$, $\gamma > 3$, $L_r > 0.70$, or property ID-lists → schema refactor mandatory.
> - **KTU Answer Style:** Always (i) state the metric, (ii) compute it, (iii) compare to the threshold, (iv) state the diagnosis, (v) propose a remediation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building a Graph Database Application: Data Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Building a Graph Database Application: Data Modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Graph Data Modelling** is the disciplined process of translating a real-world domain (business, science, social network, supply chain, etc.) into a structured representation composed of **nodes (vertices)**, **relationships (edges)**, and **properties (attributes)** within a property-graph (or RDF) framework. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the modelling activity spans three levels: **Conceptual**, **Logical**, and **Physical**, with iterative refinement guided by the application's **query patterns** and **use cases**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Data modelling in graph databases is **query-driven** — meaning the shape of the model is dictated primarily by the most frequently executed traversals, *not* by normalization rules (unlike relational modelling).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine planning a city metro map.
- **Stations** = Nodes (passengers, platforms, hubs)
- **Rail lines connecting stations** = Relationships (with a type such as *connects_to*, *transfers_at*)
- **Station details** (name, wheelchair access) = Properties
- **Train attributes** (line color, frequency) = Relationship properties

The metro map is *not* drawn first and then routes discovered — instead, the **most-traveled passenger routes dictate which lines, transfers, and stations deserve their own node or relationship**. That is exactly how graph modelling works: **use cases drive the schema**, not the other way around.

### 1.3 Key Modelling Entities (Quick Reference)

| Entity | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Node (Vertex)** | An entity instance, usually represents a *thing* | `(Person:Alice)`, `(Movie:Inception)` |
| **Label** | A categorical type assigned to a node | `:Person`, `:Movie`, `:City` |
| **Relationship (Edge)** | A directed, typed connection between two nodes | `-[:ACTED_IN]->` |
| **Property** | A key-value attribute on a node or relationship | `{name: "Alice", born: 1985}` |
| **Path** | A sequence of alternating nodes & relationships | `(a)-[:KNOWS*1..3]->(b)` |
| **Graph Pattern** | A declarative description of a subgraph of interest | `(p:Person)-[:FRIEND_OF]-(q:Person)` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Heuristic:** Every relationship in a graph database carries a *single, semantically meaningful type*. Avoid generic edges like `:RELATED_TO` — they destroy query expressiveness and force you back into SQL-style ambiguity.

### 1.4 Why Graph Data Modelling Matters in Industry
- **Recommendation engines** (Netflix, Spotify) — model user-item-context as a tripartite graph.
- **Fraud detection** (PayPal, Mastercard) — model entity-relationship-entity rings.
- **Knowledge graphs** (Google, IBM Watson) — model entities and semantic relations.
- **Network/IT operations** (Cisco, Telcos) — model devices, interfaces, and topology.
- **Life sciences** (Pharma R&D) — model proteins, drugs, pathways.

The **graph industry standard metrics** to remember are:
- **Graph traversal cost:** O(1) per hop (index-free adjacency) — vs. O(log n) per join in RDBMS.
- **Typical relationship-type cardinality ratio:** 1 graph : 5–15 relationship types (varies by domain).
- **Property-to-node ratio:** ~3–8 properties per node is the **healthy model band**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Stage Graph Modelling Pipeline

Graph data modelling is **not a single drawing exercise**. It is a layered engineering process, and KTU 2024 expects students to know each stage.

#### Stage 1 — Conceptual Modelling (Domain Discovery)
- Identify the **primary entities** (people, products, events, documents).
- Identify the **verbs that connect them** → these become relationship types.
- Capture **attributes** of each entity and each relationship.
- Produce a *whiteboard graph* — no database syntax, no performance considerations.

#### Stage 2 — Logical Modelling (Pattern Engineering)
- Translate whiteboard nodes into **labelled node types**.
- Translate verbs into **typed, directed relationships**.
- Identify **labels**, **constraint keys**, and **index candidates**.
- Refactor for **symmetric predicates** (e.g., `FRIEND_OF` may need a canonical direction rule).
- Introduce **intermediate nodes** when a relationship has its own attributes (e.g., a `:Review` node between `:User` and `:Product`).

#### Stage 3 — Physical Modelling (Performance & Storage)
- Choose the **storage engine** (native vs. non-native).
- Decide on **indexes**, **constraints**, **partitioning**, and **replication**.
- Map **query patterns → traversal plans** to validate that hot queries stay within O(k) hops.
- Plan for **write throughput** and **ACID requirements**.

### 2.2 Property Graph Data Model — Formal Definition
A **Property Graph** is a 6-tuple:

$$G = (V, E, L_V, L_E, P_V, P_E)$$

Where:
- $V$ = finite set of vertices (nodes)
- $E \subseteq V \times V$ = finite set of directed edges (relationships)
- $L_V$ = set of vertex labels (types)
- $L_E$ = set of edge labels (types)
- $P_V$ = function mapping a vertex to its property set
- $P_E$ = function mapping an edge to its property set

Each edge $e \in E$ is a tuple $(u, v, \tau, \pi)$ where:
- $u$ = source node
- $v$ = target node
- $\tau \in L_E$ = edge type
- $\pi \in P_E$ = edge property map

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Fan-out cost** | $C_{fan} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d(v_i)$ | Sum of degrees touched in a traversal |
| **Path length** | $L(p) = \vert E_p \vert$ | Number of edges in a path |
| **Graph density** | $D = \dfrac{2 \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert(\vert V \vert - 1)}$ | Edges-to-possible-edges ratio |
| **Traversal cost (index-free adjacency)** | $O(1)$ per hop | Constant time, regardless of $V$ |
| **Hop budget rule** | $h \leq 6$ for OLTP | Most OLTP queries stay within 6 hops |
| **Property-to-node healthy band** | $3 \leq \vert P_V \vert \leq 8$ | Avoid mega-nodes / anemic nodes |
| **Relationship-type optimal count** | $5 \leq \vert L_E \vert \leq 25$ | Sweet spot for query expressiveness |
| **Symmetric predicate rule** | $\forall e(u,v): e(v,u)$ | Store only canonical direction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do *not* confuse graph **density** with relational **normalization**. A dense graph is not a "denormalized" graph — it is simply well-modelled for traversal-heavy workloads.

### 2.4 The Query-Driven Modelling Principle
The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that graph models are **inverted** from relational models: instead of asking *"what joins do I need?"*, you ask:

> *"What are my top 5–10 critical query patterns, and what is the shortest path through the graph that satisfies each one?"*

This is formalized as the **Pattern-First Modelling (PFM)** technique:

1. **List all application queries** (e.g., *find friends-of-friends who liked movie X*).
2. **Convert each query into a graph pattern** (e.g., `(a)-[:FRIEND_OF*2]->(b)-[:LIKED]->(m)`).
3. **Reconcile overlapping patterns** into a single unified schema.
4. **Optimize for the most frequent pattern** — add labels, indexes, or intermediate nodes if needed.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Healthcare EHR systems** — model patient-diagnosis-treatment-outcome as a graph for clinical decision support.
- **Smart grid management** — model substations, transformers, lines, outages for fault tracing.
- **Cybersecurity** — model users, hosts, IPs, sessions for lateral-movement detection.
- **Academic citation networks** — model papers, authors, venues, citations for bibliometric analytics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — From ER Diagram to Graph Schema

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most examination-relevant transformation** in KTU Module 2. Master it thoroughly.

The following table establishes the **deterministic mapping rules** from an Entity-Relationship (ER) conceptual model to a Property Graph logical model. Every row is a proof-grade rule with a worked example.

| # | ER Construct | Graph Construct | Worked Example | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Strong Entity** | `Node` with a `Label` | `ER: STUDENT` → `(:Student)` | Each entity instance becomes a vertex. |
| 2 | **Weak Entity** | `Node` + `Relationship` to owner | `ER: DEPENDENT` → `(:Dependent)-[:BELONGS_TO]->(:Employee)` | Weakness is captured via a *mandatory* relationship, not a separate schema construct. |
| 3 | **1:1 Relationship** | Single directed `Relationship` (one direction is sufficient) | `ER: Person — has — Passport` → `(:Person)-[:HAS_PASSPORT]->(:Passport)` | Bidirectionality handled at query-time. |
| 4 | **1:N Relationship** | Directed `Relationship` from "1" side to "N" side | `ER: Department — employs — Employee` → `(:Department)-[:EMPLOYS]->(:Employee)` | Direction follows cardinality. |
| 5 | **M:N Relationship** | Dedicated `Relationship` (with optional properties) | `ER: Student — enrolls — Course` → `(:Student)-[:ENROLLED_IN {semester:"S1"}]->(:Course)` | Edge itself models the association. |
| 6 | **M:N Relationship with Attributes that need a unique identity** | Intermediate `Node` (hyper-node) | `ER: Actor — acts in — Movie (with role)**` → `(:Actor)-[:ACTED_IN]->(:Role)-[:IN_MOVIE]->(:Movie)` | When the relationship is a first-class entity, promote it. |
| 7 | **Simple Attribute** | `Node Property` | `ER: Person.name` → `(:Person {name:"Alice"})` | Properties live on vertices. |
| 8 | **Composite Attribute** | Multiple `Node Properties` | `ER: address(street, city)` → `(:Person {street:"...", city:"..."})` | Flatten into key-value pairs. |
| 9 | **Multi-valued Attribute** | New `Node` + `Relationship` | `ER: Person(phone*)` → `(:Person)-[:HAS_PHONE]->(:Phone)` | First-class entity rule. |
| 10 | **Derived Attribute** | Not stored; computed at query-time | `ER: Person.age` → derived from `born` via Cypher function | Avoid redundancy. |
| 11 | **Primary Key** | Uniqueness `Constraint` | `ER: SSN` → `CREATE CONSTRAINT ON (p:Person) ASSERT p.ssn IS UNIQUE` | Enforced at engine level. |
| 12 | **Foreign Key** | Implicit (edge endpoint) | `ER: dept_id in Employee` → replaced by `(:Employee)-[:WORKS_IN]->(:Department)` | Edges replace FK joins. |
| 13 | **ISA / Specialization** | `Label` hierarchy or extra `Relationship` | `ER: Employee ⊂ Person` → `(:Person:Employee)` (multi-label) | Use multi-labelling for shared attributes. |
| 14 | **Aggregation (ternary)** | Hub-and-spoke graph pattern | `ER: Supplier — supplies — Part to Project` → 3 edges from a central event node | Every n-ary becomes a star-shaped subgraph. |
| 15 | **Recursive / Self-relationship** | `Relationship` between same `Label` | `ER: Employee manages Employee` → `(:Employee)-[:MANAGES]->(:Employee)` | Allowed and common. |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Building a "Movie Recommendation" Graph

We will now apply rules 1–15 above to a real KTU-style problem.

**Step 1 — Conceptual Layer (Whiteboard)**

> [!NOTE]
> We do not yet use any Cypher / SPARQL syntax. We only identify entities, verbs, and attributes.

Identified entities: **Person, Movie, Genre, Director, Actor, Review**.
Identified verbs: *directed, acted_in, belongs_to, reviewed, follows, friend_of*.

**Step 2 — Logical Layer (Apply Mapping Rules)**

Applying the rules from the matrix:

- `Person` (entity) → `(:Person)` (Rule 1)
- `Movie` (entity) → `(:Movie)` (Rule 1)
- `Genre` (entity) → `(:Genre)` (Rule 1)
- `directed` (verb, 1:N) → `(:Person)-[:DIRECTED]->(:Movie)` (Rule 4)
- `acted_in` (verb, M:N with attribute `role`) → **promote to intermediate node** (Rule 6)
  - `(:Person)-[:ACTED_IN]->(:Role)-[:IN_MOVIE]->(:Movie)`
- `belongs_to` (verb, M:N) → `(:Movie)-[:IN_GENRE]->(:Genre)` (Rule 5)
- `reviewed` (verb, M:N with rating & timestamp) → **intermediate node** (Rule 6)
  - `(:Person)-[:WROTE]->(:Review {rating:5, ts:...})-[:ABOUT]->(:Movie)`
- `follows` (self-relationship) → `(:Person)-[:FOLLOWS]->(:Person)` (Rule 15)
- `friend_of` (self-relationship, symmetric) → `(:Person)-[:FRIEND_OF]->(:Person)` (Rule 15 + symmetric rule)

**Step 3 — Pattern Reconciliation (PFM)**

Consider the KTU typical query:
*"Recommend movies to Alice based on what her friends rated above 4."*

Graph pattern:
```
(alice:Person {name:'Alice'})-[:FRIEND_OF]->(f)-[:WROTE]->(r:Review)
                                            -[:ABOUT]->(m:Movie)
```

This pattern validates that our logical model is **correct and complete**.

**Step 4 — Physical Layer (Decisions)**

- Create **uniqueness constraint** on `Person.email` (Rule 11).
- Create **index** on `Movie.title` for fast lookup.
- Decide **storage engine**: native graph store (e.g., Neo4j) for OLTP, *not* an RDBMS-backed triple store.
- Plan **partitioning**: shard by `Person.country` for horizontal scale.

### 3.3 Algebraic Derivation — Estimating Traversal Cost

Let us derive the **expected number of nodes visited** when answering the recommendation query above.

Let:
- $d_F$ = average number of `FRIEND_OF` edges per person
- $d_R$ = average number of `WROTE` edges per reviewer
- $d_M$ = average number of `ABOUT` edges per review
- $N$ = total person count

The expected breadth of search at hop $i$ is:

$$B_i = d_i \cdot (1 - p_{prune})$$

where $p_{prune}$ is the early-pruning probability from filters (e.g., `rating > 4`).

For the 3-hop query, the total expected node visits are:

$$V_{total} = 1 + d_F + d_F \cdot d_R \cdot (1 - p_{review}) + d_F \cdot d_R \cdot d_M \cdot (1 - p_{rating})$$

**Worked numerical example** (typical social graph):
- $d_F = 150$, $d_R = 5$, $d_M = 1$
- $p_{review} = 0.6$, $p_{rating} = 0.85$

$$V_{total} = 1 + 150 + 150 \cdot 5 \cdot 0.4 + 150 \cdot 5 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.15$$

$$V_{total} = 1 + 150 + 300 + 112.5 = 563.5 \text{ nodes}$$

This is **constant-bounded** by the query pattern — independent of $N$. This is why graph modelling focuses on **local patterns, not global tables**.

### 3.4 Common Modelling Anti-Patterns (With Remedies)

| # | Anti-Pattern | Symptom | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mega-node** | One node with 10⁵+ relationships and dozens of labels | Split into sub-entity nodes. |
| 2 | **Anemic node** | Node with only an ID and no semantic label | Re-evaluate labelling strategy. |
| 3 | **Generic edge** | Edges labelled `:RELATED` or `:HAS` | Replace with domain-specific types. |
| 4 | **Bidirectional duplication** | Storing `(a)-[:FRIEND]->(b)` AND `(b)-[:FRIEND]->(a)` | Store once, query in both directions. |
| 5 | **Using properties as edges** | `MATCH (a)-[]-(b) WHERE r.weight > 5` (treating weight as a pseudo-edge) | Promote to a real intermediate node. |
| 6 | **Over-labelling** | `:Person:Customer:Buyer:User:Member` | Use 1–3 labels max; prefer relationships. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Three-Stage Modelling Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Domain Discovery] --> B[Conceptual Model Whiteboard]
    B --> C[Entity Identification]
    B --> D[Verb Identification]
    B --> E[Attribute Identification]
    C --> F[Logical Model]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Node Type Definition]
    F --> H[Relationship Type Definition]
    F --> I[Property Schema Definition]
    G --> J[Physical Model]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Storage Engine Choice]
    J --> L[Index and Constraint Setup]
    J --> M[Query Pattern Validation]
    K --> N[Live Graph Database]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Performance Tuning Loop]
    O --> J
```

### 4.2 ER-to-Graph Mapping Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[ER Strong Entity] -->|Rule 1| G1[Node with Label]
    E2[ER Weak Entity] -->|Rule 2| G2[Node plus Mandatory Edge]
    E3[ER 1 to N] -->|Rule 4| G3[Single Directed Edge]
    E4[ER M to N] -->|Rule 5| G4[Typed Edge]
    E5[ER M to N with Attributes] -->|Rule 6| G5[Intermediate Hyper Node]
    E6[ER Multivalued Attribute] -->|Rule 9| G6[New Node plus Edge]
    E7[ER Foreign Key] -->|Rule 12| G7[Implicit Edge Endpoint]
```

### 4.3 Movie Recommendation Logical Graph Schema

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Person] -->|FOLLOWS| P
    P -->|FRIEND_OF| P
    P -->|ACTED_IN| R[Role]
    R -->|IN_MOVIE| M[Movie]
    P -->|DIRECTED| M
    P -->|WROTE| Rev[Review]
    Rev -->|ABOUT| M
    M -->|IN_GENRE| Gen[Genre]
    P -->|LIVES_IN| C[City]
    C -->|LOCATED_IN| Cou[Country]
```

### 4.4 Pattern-First Modelling (PFM) Iteration Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Enumerate Application Queries] --> Q2[Translate to Graph Patterns]
    Q2 --> Q3[Identify Pattern Hotspots]
    Q3 --> Q4{All Patterns Satisfiable?}
    Q4 -->|No| Q5[Refine Logical Schema]
    Q5 --> Q2
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q6[Add Indexes and Constraints]
    Q6 --> Q7[Validate with EXPLAIN PROFILE]
    Q7 --> Q8{Performance Acceptable?}
    Q8 -->|No| Q9[Add Labels or Intermediate Nodes]
    Q9 --> Q2
    Q8 -->|Yes| Q10[Deploy to Production]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `Q1`, `E7`, `G5`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) are used as standalone node IDs. No markdown bold/italic markers are embedded inside node labels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2025]**

**Q1. Define "Property Graph Model" with its formal components.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A Property Graph is a directed, labelled, attributed multigraph formally defined as the 6-tuple $G = (V, E, L_V, L_E, P_V, P_E)$, where $V$ is the set of vertices, $E$ is the set of directed edges, $L_V$ and $L_E$ are sets of vertex and edge labels respectively, and $P_V$ and $P_E$ are property-mapping functions. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024]**

**Q2. Differentiate between conceptual and logical graph data modelling.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Conceptual modelling** is technology-agnostic; it identifies domain entities, relationships, and attributes on a whiteboard, with no database syntax. (1.5 marks)
> - **Logical modelling** translates the conceptual model into a database-specific schema — defining labels, relationship types, properties, and constraints. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2025]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full-Module Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Pattern-First Modelling (PFM)** approach in graph database design. List its four steps. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider an **E-Learning platform** domain with entities `Student`, `Course`, `Instructor`, `Lesson`, and `Assignment`. Relationships include *enrolls*, *teaches*, *contains*, *submits*, and *grades*. Design the **logical graph schema** using the property graph model. State any modelling assumptions. *(7 marks)*

---

> **Model Answer — Q(a) [7 marks]**
>
> **Pattern-First Modelling (PFM)** is a query-driven methodology for designing graph schemas. Unlike the relational "table-first" approach, PFM starts with the application's most critical queries and works backward to the schema.
>
> The four steps are:
> 1. **Enumerate application queries** — list the top 5–10 business-critical questions the application must answer. *(1.5 marks)*
> 2. **Translate to graph patterns** — convert each natural-language query into a graph traversal pattern (e.g., `(a)-[:KNOWS*2]->(b)-[:LIKED]->(m)`). *(2 marks)*
> 3. **Reconcile overlapping patterns** — merge patterns that share nodes/edges into a single unified schema. *(1.5 marks)*
> 4. **Optimize the hot pattern** — add labels, indexes, or intermediate nodes so the most frequent pattern executes within the hop budget. *(2 marks)*

---

> **Model Answer — Q(b) [7 marks]**
>
> Applying the ER-to-Graph mapping rules (Rules 1, 4, 5, 6 from the Analytical Matrix):
>
> 1. `Student`, `Course`, `Instructor`, `Lesson`, `Assignment` → **nodes** with corresponding labels. *(1 mark)*
> 2. `Instructor teaches Course` (1:N) → `(:Instructor)-[:TEACHES]->(:Course)` *(Rule 4)*. *(1 mark)*
> 3. `Student enrolls in Course` (M:N with `enrollmentDate` property) → `(:Student)-[:ENROLLED_IN {enrollmentDate:"2024-08-01"}]->(:Course)` *(Rule 5)*. *(1.5 marks)*
> 4. `Course contains Lesson` (1:N composition) → `(:Course)-[:CONTAINS]->(:Lesson)` *(Rule 4)*. *(1 mark)*
> 5. `Student submits Assignment` (M:N with `submissionTime`, `fileURL`) — because it has multiple attributes that warrant a first-class identity, **promote to intermediate node** *(Rule 6)*: `(:Student)-[:SUBMITTED]->(:Submission {ts:..., url:...})-[:OF]->(:Assignment)`. *(1.5 marks)*
> 6. `Instructor grades Submission` (1:N) → `(:Instructor)-[:GRADES]->(:Submission)`. *(1 mark)*

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **six major anti-patterns** in graph data modelling with one-line remedies for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Given a social-network domain with entities `User`, `Post`, `Comment`, `Group`, and `Event`, design the conceptual model (whiteboard) and then the logical property-graph model. State cardinality assumptions. *(7 marks)*

---

> **Model Answer — Q(a) [7 marks]**
>
> 1. **Mega-node** — A single node with thousands of relationships; remedy: split into sub-entity nodes (e.g., `:User` → `:User`, `:Address`, `:Profile`). *(1.2 marks)*
> 2. **Anemic node** — A node with only an ID and no label; remedy: re-evaluate labelling and add semantic types. *(1.2 marks)*
> 3. **Generic edge** — Edges labelled `:RELATED`, `:HAS`; remedy: replace with domain-specific types like `:FOLLOWS`, `:OWNS`. *(1.2 marks)*
> 4. **Bidirectional duplication** — Storing both `(a)-[:FRIEND]->(b)` and `(b)-[:FRIEND]->(a)`; remedy: store once, query in both directions. *(1.2 marks)*
> 5. **Properties as edges** — Using `r.weight` to mimic a relationship; remedy: promote to a real intermediate node. *(1.2 marks)*
> 6. **Over-labelling** — Adding 5+ labels to a single node; remedy: use 1–3 labels max, push semantics into relationships. *(1.0 marks)*

---

> **Model Answer — Q(b) [7 marks]**
>
> **Conceptual (whiteboard):** *(3 marks)*
> - Entities: `User`, `Post`, `Comment`, `Group`, `Event`.
> - Verbs: *posts*, *comments_on*, *belongs_to*, *joins*, *attends*, *likes*.
> - Attributes: `User(name, email)`, `Post(content, ts)`, `Comment(text, ts)`, `Group(name, topic)`, `Event(title, date, venue)`.
>
> **Logical (property graph):** *(4 marks)*
> - `(:User)-[:POSTED]->(:Post)` *(Rule 4)*.
> - `(:User)-[:COMMENTED]->(:Comment)-[:ON_POST]->(:Post)` *(Rule 6 — intermediate node because Comment has its own attributes and identity)*.
> - `(:User)-[:MEMBER_OF]->(:Group)` *(Rule 5, M:N)*.
> - `(:User)-[:JOINED]->(:Event)` and `(:Event)-[:HAS_ATTENDEE]->(:User)` (chosen canonical direction; symmetric rule).
> - `(:User)-[:LIKED]->(:Post)` *(Rule 4)*.
> - Cardinality assumption: a User can join many Events; an Event has many Users (M:N).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Writing Cypher or SQL in the conceptual layer** → loses 1–2 marks per occurrence. Conceptual = English + boxes/circles only.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify edge direction** → in KTU 2024 Scheme, an undirected edge is treated as a *modelling error*. Always state direction explicitly.
> 3. **Failing to promote "M:N with attributes" to an intermediate node** → -2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing labels and node-types** → in property graphs, a *node type* is conceptual; a *label* is physical. Mixing them = 1 mark deduction.
> 5. **Skipping cardinality assumptions in design questions** → examiners expect them. -1 mark if omitted.
> 6. **Writing Python code** — strictly prohibited in this course's theory answers unless the question explicitly asks for an algorithm in pseudocode.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Data Modelling**

- ✅ **Graph data modelling is query-driven**, not table-driven.
- ✅ A **Property Graph** is a 6-tuple $(V, E, L_V, L_E, P_V, P_E)$.
- ✅ Three modelling stages: **Conceptual → Logical → Physical**.
- ✅ The **Pattern-First Modelling (PFM)** approach has exactly **four steps**: enumerate → translate → reconcile → optimize.
- ✅ ER-to-Graph mapping has **15 deterministic rules**; master at least Rules 1, 4, 5, 6, 9, 12, 15.
- ✅ **M:N relationships with attributes** must be **promoted to intermediate nodes** (Rule 6).
- ✅ **Foreign keys** in ER become **implicit edge endpoints** in graph — no FK columns.
- ✅ **Multi-valued attributes** become **new nodes** connected by an edge.
- ✅ **Generic edges** (`:RELATED`, `:HAS`) are anti-patterns — always use domain-specific types.
- ✅ **Bidirectional relationships** (e.g., friendship) are stored once and queried in both directions.
- ✅ **Hop budget rule**: keep OLTP queries within **6 hops** of the starting node.
- ✅ **Traversal cost** is $O(1)$ per hop due to **index-free adjacency** — this is the killer feature.
- ✅ **Healthy property-to-node ratio**: 3–8 properties per node.
- ✅ **Healthy relationship-type count**: 5–25 distinct types per schema.
- ✅ Always state **cardinality assumptions** explicitly in design questions.
- ✅ Always state **edge direction** explicitly in design questions.
- ✅ **Mega-nodes, anemic nodes, and over-labelling** are the three most common model smells.
- ✅ **Storage engine choice** (native vs. non-native) belongs to the *physical* stage, not logical.
- ✅ **Uniqueness constraints** in property graphs replace primary keys from ER.
- ✅ **Indexes** are added in the *physical* stage to accelerate frequent lookup patterns.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Application Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Graph Database Application Architecture** refers to the structured arrangement of software components, communication layers, data access patterns, and runtime services that collectively enable an application to read, write, query, and manage data stored in a graph database. It defines the logical and physical separation of concerns between the client application, the database driver/connector layer, the query processing engine, and the underlying storage/cluster infrastructure.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PECDT751 – Graph Databases and Analysis*, Module 2 expects the learner to articulate the **end-to-end topology** of a graph-based application, classify deployment models, and justify the selection of a given architecture for a target use case (transactional vs. analytical, single-node vs. distributed).

The architecture is typically decomposed into **three logical tiers**:

| Tier | Responsibility | Typical Components |
|------|----------------|--------------------|
| **Presentation / Application Tier** | Business logic, UI, request orchestration | Web servers, microservices, mobile apps |
| **Driver / API Tier** | Query construction, connection pooling, session handling | Bolt driver, JDBC/ODBC, REST/HTTP wrappers |
| **Database / Storage Tier** | Transaction management, query optimisation, persistence | Cypher engine, storage manager, cluster manager |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Post Office Network"**
>
> Imagine a national postal system:
> - The **sender (application)** writes a letter (Cypher query) but does not know which postman will deliver it.
> - The **post office counter (driver layer)** accepts the letter, weighs it, and assigns a delivery route.
> - The **sorting hub (database engine)** reads the address, looks up the index, and decides the best path.
> - The **mailbox cluster (storage + replicas)** physically stores letters, with multiple copies in different cities for safety.
>
> The **architecture** is, therefore, the **complete set of rules, counters, hubs, and backup mailboxes** that work together so the sender never has to worry about *how* the letter reaches its destination — only that it does, accurately and quickly.

This mirrors how a graph database application abstracts away physical storage, replication, and routing from the developer, exposing a **graph-native API** instead.

---

### 1.3 Architectural Classification (High-Level)

> [!TIP]
> **Two orthogonal axes classify every graph database application:**
> 1. **Deployment Axis** → *Embedded* vs. *Standalone/Server-Based*
> 2. **Workload Axis** → *OLTP (Transactional)* vs. *OLAP (Analytical)*

A **native, embedded, OLTP** deployment (e.g., a desktop fraud-detection tool using Neo4j Embedded) and a **distributed, server-based, OLAP** deployment (e.g., a Spark+GraphX cluster) sit at opposite corners of this design space. Most production systems lie somewhere in between.

### 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (Bolded Standards)

- **Query Latency:** typical p99 target for OLTP graph queries is **< 50 ms** for single-hop traversals.
- **Connection Pool Size:** default recommended range is **50 – 200 concurrent connections** per driver instance.
- **Replication Factor:** enterprise defaults use a **factor of 3** with **quorum = 2** for write acknowledgement.
- **Causal Consistency Window:** typically bounded at **< 250 ms** in leader-follower topologies.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Layer Reference Architecture

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognise the canonical **four-layer reference architecture** for a graph database application. Each layer encapsulates a well-defined set of responsibilities.

---

**Layer 1 — Application / Domain Layer**

- Contains the **business logic** of the consumer system.
- Issues **declarative graph queries** (e.g., Cypher, Gremlin, SPARQL).
- Stateless or stateful microservices.
- Implements the **Repository / DAO pattern** so that the storage backend can be swapped without rewriting business code.

**Layer 2 — Driver / Connector Layer**

- A vendor-provided **language-native library** (Java Bolt, Python neo4j-driver, Go driver).
- Responsibilities:
  - **Connection management** and pooling
  - **Session lifecycle** (open, reuse, close)
  - **Transaction demarcation**
  - **Result streaming** and deserialisation
  - **Authentication / TLS** negotiation
- Translates a high-level query (string + parameters) into a **binary wire protocol**.

**Layer 3 — Database Engine Layer**

The heart of the system. Sub-modules:

1. **Query Parser** — lexical + syntactic analysis of Cypher/Gremlin.
2. **Query Optimiser** — cost-based rewriter that pushes predicates, chooses join order, and selects indexes.
3. **Execution Engine** — runs the optimised logical plan (operator pipeline, e.g., `NodeByLabelScan` → `Expand(All)` → `Filter`).
4. **Transaction Manager** — implements **ACID** with concurrency control (e.g., optimistic locking, MVCC).
5. **Cluster Manager** — handles leader election, heartbeats, Raft/Paxos consensus.

**Layer 4 — Storage Layer**

- **Page cache** in RAM (typically 50 % of heap).
- **On-disk store files** (`.store`, `.idstore`).
- **Native graph storage** (linked lists of relationships co-located with node records) vs. **non-native** (relational or columnar projection).
- **Write-Ahead Log (WAL)** for durability.

---

### 2.2 Why Each Layer Exists — The "Why-How" Rationale

| Layer | Why it Exists | How it Achieves its Goal |
|-------|---------------|--------------------------|
| Application | Encapsulate domain rules | Uses repositories, services, and DTOs |
| Driver | Decouple wire protocol from app | Maintains a pool, reuses TCP sockets |
| Engine | Translate intent to physical ops | AST → Logical Plan → Physical Plan |
| Storage | Persist billions of nodes/edges | Fixed-size records + free-list pages |

> [!NOTE]
> **The single most important architectural property is "Location Transparency."** The application issues a logical query (`MATCH (n:Person {id:42})`); the engine decides whether to hit a local store, a follower replica, or a remote cluster member. The application remains unaware.

---

### 2.3 Embedded vs. Server-Based Deployment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Embedded Mode** — the database engine runs **in-process** with the application (same JVM / same OS process).
> - **Pros:** zero network latency, simplified deployment, ideal for unit tests and single-user desktop tools.
> - **Cons:** no horizontal scalability, app crashes can corrupt the store, no concurrent multi-client access.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Server-Based (Standalone / Cluster) Mode** — the engine runs as a **separate process or set of processes**, accessed over TCP/HTTP.
> - **Pros:** independent scaling, fault isolation, multi-tenancy, supports load balancers.
> - **Cons:** network round-trip cost, requires driver-side connection management.

---

### 2.4 Cluster Topology Patterns

A production graph cluster is typically organised as:

1. **Core Cluster (Raft-Replicated)** — accepts reads and writes; uses a Raft consensus ring of 3 or 5 members.
2. **Read Replicas (Causal Consistency)** — asynchronous followers that serve read traffic to scale-out query throughput.
3. **Routing Layer** — a load balancer or driver-side router that directs reads to followers and writes to leaders.

**Consistency Models** the architecture must respect:

- **Strong (Leader-Read)**: reads always served by the leader; lowest latency variance.
- **Causal**: reads from a follower are guaranteed to observe all writes causally preceding them, bounded by a session.
- **Read-Your-Writes**: every read in the same session sees that session's prior writes.
- **Eventual**: lowest latency, weakest guarantees — used in OLAP workloads.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Definition / Formula | Application Layer |
|------------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| $N$ | Number of nodes in the graph | Logical data model |
| $E$ | Number of edges (relationships) | Logical data model |
| $D$ | Average node degree, $D = \frac{2E}{N}$ | Capacity planning |
| $QPS$ | Queries per second the system must serve | Driver pool sizing |
| $C$ | Recommended pool size, $C \approx \frac{QPS \cdot t_{avg}}{1000}$ | Connection pool |
| $t_{avg}$ | Average query latency in **ms** | Driver pool sizing |
| $R$ | Replication factor, typically $R = 3$ | Cluster topology |
| $W$ | Write quorum, $W = \lceil R/2 \rceil + 1$ | Cluster topology |
| $S$ | Storage per node $\approx 15$ bytes (label) + property size | Capacity planning |
| $L_{p99}$ | Tail latency (99th percentile) target, typically $< 50$ ms | SLA engineering |

> [!TIP]
> The sizing heuristic $C \approx \frac{QPS \cdot t_{avg}}{1000}$ (Little's Law analogue) tells you: if you expect **1000 QPS at 20 ms average latency**, configure a pool of about **20 connections** to keep utilisation under 100 %.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Fraud detection** (PayPal, Mastercard) — embedded-mode *in-process* graph traversal for sub-millisecond pattern matching during payment authorisation.
- **Knowledge graphs** (Google, Amazon) — server-based, multi-region cluster serving thousands of microservices via a routing layer.
- **Social networks** (LinkedIn, Facebook TAO) — polyglot persistence: graph database for relationship queries, relational DB for user profiles, key-value store for activity feeds.
- **Bioinformatics** — OLAP cluster (Neo4j + Spark GraphFrames) for genome-scale protein-interaction analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Connection Pool Sizing Using Little's Law

**Problem context:** A retail recommendation engine issues an average of $QPS = 800$ graph queries per second against a remote Neo4j cluster. The measured average query latency is $t_{avg} = 25 \text{ ms}$. Determine the minimum driver-side connection pool size $C$ to keep the system stable (i.e., utilisation $\rho < 1$).

---

**Step 1 — State Little's Law.**

Little's Law, adapted to a connection pool, states that the number of in-flight requests equals the arrival rate multiplied by the average time each request spends in the system:

$$
L = \lambda \cdot W
$$

where
- $L$ = number of concurrent in-flight requests,
- $\lambda$ = arrival rate (requests per second),
- $W$ = average time in system (seconds per request).

**Step 2 — Convert units to a consistent set.**

We are given $t_{avg} = 25 \text{ ms}$, so convert to seconds:

$$
W = \frac{25 \text{ ms}}{1000 \text{ ms/s}} = 0.025 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the required number of concurrent requests.**

$$
L = 800 \text{ req/s} \times 0.025 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ requests}
$$

**Step 4 — Translate to pool size.**

To keep utilisation below 1 (i.e., the pool must hold at least the expected concurrency, with a small safety margin), set:

$$
C = \left\lceil L \cdot 1.25 \right\rceil
$$

where the factor **1.25** adds a 25 % safety margin to absorb bursty traffic.

$$
C = \left\lceil 20 \cdot 1.25 \right\rceil = \left\lceil 25 \right\rceil = 25
$$

**Step 5 — Result and Interpretation.**

$$
\boxed{C_{min} = 25 \text{ connections}}
$$

> The driver must be configured to maintain **at least 25** open connections to the cluster. Below this value, queueing latency will rise sharply and tail latency will breach the 50 ms SLA.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quorum Sizing for a Raft-Replicated Core Cluster

**Problem context:** A graph cluster must tolerate the failure of $f$ cluster members while still accepting writes. The cluster has $R$ members. Derive the minimum value of $W$ (write quorum) and confirm that $R$ must be odd.

---

**Step 1 — Survival condition.**

To tolerate $f$ simultaneous failures, the quorum size $W$ must satisfy:

$$
W \geq f + 1
$$

because at least one surviving member must acknowledge the write.

**Step 2 — Overlap condition for consistency.**

Two majorities must overlap in at least one member to avoid split-brain. That is:

$$
W + W > R \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 2W > R \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; W \geq \left\lfloor \frac{R}{2} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $W$.**

Combining the two conditions:

$$
W = \left\lfloor \frac{R}{2} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

**Step 4 — Example: $R = 3$.**

$$
W = \left\lfloor \frac{3}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2
$$

So with 3 members, the cluster needs **2** confirmations per write and tolerates the loss of **1** member.

**Step 5 — Example: $R = 5$.**

$$
W = \left\lfloor \frac{5}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3
$$
