With 5 members, **3** confirmations are needed, and the cluster tolerates up to **2** failed members.

**Step 6 — Parity argument for odd $R$.**

If $R$ is even, say $R = 4$, then:

$$
W = \frac{4}{2} + 1 = 3
$$

This means **3** of **4** members must agree, which is a higher fraction ($\frac{3}{4} = 75\%$) than for $R = 3$ ($\frac{2}{3} \approx 67\%$). More importantly, the network could in theory partition the cluster into two halves of 2 — neither half has a majority, causing an availability outage. Therefore, **$R$ must be odd** to guarantee that the surviving partition always has a strict majority.

$$
\boxed{R \in \{3, 5, 7, \dots\}, \quad W = \left\lfloor \frac{R}{2} \right\rfloor + 1, \quad f = W - 1}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing an Architecture

| Workload | Data Scale | Read/Write Ratio | Recommended Architecture | Justification |
|----------|------------|------------------|--------------------------|---------------|
| Fraud detection | 10⁸ nodes, 10⁹ edges | 1000 R : 1 W | Embedded, single-leader cluster | Sub-ms response critical; small write set |
| Social network feed | 10⁹ nodes, 10¹¹ edges | 100 R : 10 W | Distributed cluster + read replicas | Massive read fan-out, write-behind queue |
| Bioinformatics OLAP | 10¹² edges | 1 R : 0 W (batch) | Spark + GraphFrames, offline | Analytical scans, no OLTP requirement |
| Knowledge graph (RAG) | 10⁸ nodes | 50 R : 1 W | Server-based + causal-consistent replicas | Multi-region, low write rate |
| Mobile offline app | 10⁵ nodes | 10 R : 5 W | Embedded (lite engine, e.g., LiteDB) | No network; small footprint |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

All node IDs below are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `layerA1`, `dbX1`). No reserved keywords are used. All labels are plain uppercase text, free of markdown or HTML formatting.

---

### 4.2 High-Level Three-Tier Graph Application Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TIER1["APPLICATION TIER"]
        appA1["WEB UI DASHBOARD"]
        appA2["REST API GATEWAY"]
        appA3["BUSINESS LOGIC SERVICE"]
    end

    subgraph TIER2["DRIVER AND API TIER"]
        drvB1["CONNECTION POOL MANAGER"]
        drvB2["SESSION HANDLER"]
        drvB3["QUERY BUILDER CYPHER"]
        drvB4["BINARY WIRE PROTOCOL"]
    end

    subgraph TIER3["DATABASE ENGINE TIER"]
        engC1["QUERY PARSER"]
        engC2["QUERY OPTIMIZER"]
        engC3["EXECUTION ENGINE"]
        engC4["TRANSACTION MANAGER"]
        engC5["CLUSTER MANAGER RAFT"]
    end

    subgraph TIER4["STORAGE TIER"]
        stoD1["PAGE CACHE RAM"]
        stoD2["WRITE AHEAD LOG"]
        stoD3["ON DISK STORE FILES"]
        stoD4["REPLICA NODES"]
    end

    appA1 --> appA3
    appA2 --> appA3
    appA3 --> drvB1
    drvB1 --> drvB2
    drvB2 --> drvB3
    drvB3 --> drvB4
    drvB4 --> engC1
    engC1 --> engC2
    engC2 --> engC3
    engC3 --> engC4
    engC3 --> engC1
    engC4 --> engC5
    engC5 --> stoD1
    engC5 --> stoD2
    engC5 --> stoD3
    engC5 --> stoD4
```

---

### 4.3 Cluster Topology With Read Replicas

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CLIENTS["CLIENTS"]
        cli1["APP INSTANCE 1"]
        cli2["APP INSTANCE 2"]
    end

    subgraph ROUTING["ROUTING LAYER"]
        rt1["LOAD BALANCER"]
        rt2["DRIVER SIDE ROUTER"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["CORE CLUSTER RAFT"]
        core1["LEADER NODE A"]
        core2["FOLLOWER NODE B"]
        core3["FOLLOWER NODE C"]
    end

    subgraph REPLICAS["READ REPLICAS"]
        rep1["REPLICA R1 CAUSAL"]
        rep2["REPLICA R2 CAUSAL"]
    end

    cli1 --> rt1
    cli2 --> rt1
    rt1 --> rt2
    rt2 -->|WRITE TRAFFIC| core1
    rt2 -->|READ TRAFFIC| rep1
    rt2 -->|READ TRAFFIC| rep2
    core1 -->|RAFT LOG REPLICATION| core2
    core1 -->|RAFT LOG REPLICATION| core3
    core1 -->|ASYNC SHIP| rep1
    core1 -->|ASYNC SHIP| rep2
```

---

### 4.4 Embedded vs Server-Based Deployment Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph EMBED["EMBEDDED MODE"]
        proc1["SINGLE OS PROCESS"]
        eng1["GRAPH ENGINE IN PROCESS"]
        sto1["LOCAL DISK FILES"]
        proc1 --> eng1
        eng1 --> sto1
    end

    subgraph SERVER["SERVER BASED MODE"]
        proc2["CLIENT PROCESS"]
        drv["DRIVER LIBRARY"]
        sproc1["SERVER PROCESS A"]
        sproc2["SERVER PROCESS B"]
        sproc3["SERVER PROCESS C"]
        clus["CLUSTER MANAGER"]
        ssto["SHARED OR REPLICATED STORAGE"]

        proc2 --> drv
        drv -->|TCP TLS| sproc1
        drv -->|TCP TLS| sproc2
        drv -->|TCP TLS| sproc3
        sproc1 --> clus
        sproc2 --> clus
        sproc3 --> clus
        sproc1 --> ssto
        sproc2 --> ssto
        sproc3 --> ssto
    end
```

---

### 4.5 Polyglot Persistence — Reference Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        api1["BUSINESS SERVICE"]
    end

    subgraph POLY["POLYGLOT PERSISTENCE LAYER"]
        gdb["GRAPH DB NEO4J FOR RELATIONSHIPS"]
        rdb["RELATIONAL DB POSTGRES FOR PROFILES"]
        kv["KEY VALUE STORE REDIS FOR SESSIONS"]
        col["COLUMNAR DB CASSANDRA FOR EVENTS"]
    end

    api1 --> gdb
    api1 --> rdb
    api1 --> kv
    api1 --> col
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define "Embedded" mode of a graph database application architecture. List two of its advantages and two of its limitations.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Embedded mode is a deployment strategy in which the graph database engine runs in the same process and address space as the consuming application, eliminating any network boundary between the two.
>
> **Advantages (any two):**
> 1. **Zero network latency** — in-process function calls replace TCP round-trips, enabling sub-millisecond traversals.
> 2. **Simplified deployment topology** — a single artefact (JAR / binary) ships with the database inside it, ideal for desktop tools and unit tests.
>
> **Limitations (any two):**
> 1. **No horizontal scalability** — the engine is bound to a single host and cannot serve multiple clients concurrently.
> 2. **Crash isolation absent** — a JVM or process crash can leave the store files in an inconsistent state, requiring manual recovery from the WAL.
> 3. **Multi-tenant writes** are unsupported because the in-process engine serialises against the application's own threads.
> 4. **Restricted library compatibility** — the embedded engine's classloader may clash with application libraries (the famous "classpath hell").

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is "Causal Consistency" in a graph database cluster, and why is it preferred over eventual consistency for read replicas in OLTP scenarios?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Causal consistency is a consistency model in which a read is guaranteed to observe all writes that are causally related to prior reads or writes within the same session, even when the read is served by a non-leader replica.
>
> **Why it is preferred over eventual consistency in OLTP:**
> 1. **Read-Your-Writes guarantee** — a transaction that creates a node and immediately re-reads it from a follower replica will see its own write; eventual consistency would risk a *stale read* (a "replication lag" anomaly).
> 2. **Monotonic reads** — once a client has seen a value, all subsequent reads in the same session return values that are at least as recent, preventing UI flicker.
> 3. **Acceptable latency** — causal consistency typically adds only a few milliseconds of routing delay, far less than the cost of redirecting every read to the leader.
> 4. **Predictable behaviour** — the application developer can reason about session-level ordering without designing elaborate retry and reconciliation logic.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, describe the **four-layer reference architecture** of a graph database application. Clearly state the responsibility of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A startup plans to deploy a graph database for a real-time fraud-detection system. Compare and contrast **embedded** and **server-based** deployment modes, and recommend one with full justification. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Four-Layer Reference Architecture (7 marks)**

| Layer | Responsibility | Example Components |
|-------|----------------|--------------------|
| **L1 — Application Layer** | Encapsulates domain logic; issues declarative graph queries | Microservices, REST controllers, DAO/Repository classes |
| **L2 — Driver Layer** | Manages connections, sessions, and transaction boundaries; translates queries to wire format | Neo4j-Java-Driver, Bolt protocol, Gremlin-Java |
| **L3 — Database Engine Layer** | Parses, optimises, and executes the query; enforces ACID; coordinates cluster membership | Cypher parser, cost-based optimiser, transaction manager, Raft consensus module |
| **L4 — Storage Layer** | Persists nodes, relationships, and indexes; caches hot data in RAM | Page cache, on-disk `.store` files, write-ahead log, replica store copies |

The block diagram (refer to Section 4.2 of these notes) shows the request flowing **top-down** and responses / log entries flowing **bottom-up and laterally** for replication.

**(b) — Embedded vs. Server-Based for Fraud Detection (7 marks)**

| Criterion | Embedded | Server-Based |
|-----------|----------|--------------|
| Latency | Sub-millisecond (in-process) | Network round-trip of 1–5 ms |
| Scalability | Single host only | Horizontal cluster |
| Failure isolation | App crash may corrupt store | Independent process; app crash does not affect DB |
| Operational complexity | Low (one artefact) | High (cluster, load balancer, monitoring) |
| Multi-client access | Not supported | Native support |
| Real-time fraud use case | Excellent for *single-instance* decisioning | Excellent for *fleet-wide* decisioning with central rules |

**Recommendation:** **Embedded mode** is the correct choice for a real-time, in-line fraud-detection engine because the decision must be made *during* a payment authorisation, where even a 5 ms network round-trip is intolerable. The dataset is also bounded (a single transaction graph of ~10⁴ nodes per request), so a single-host embedded engine can hold the working set in memory with millisecond-level cold-start recovery from the local WAL. The wider fraud-analytics dashboard can run against a separate server-based cluster asynchronously, demonstrating **polyglot architecture** discipline.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the role of a **driver layer** in a graph database application. Describe how connection pooling, session management, and transaction demarcation are handled by the driver. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A retail system expects $QPS = 1200$ graph queries per second, each averaging $t_{avg} = 30$ ms. Using Little's Law, determine the minimum driver-side connection pool size. Also explain what happens if the pool is undersized. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Role of the Driver Layer (7 marks)**

The **driver layer** is the language-native client library that sits between the application code and the database engine. Its three primary responsibilities are:

1. **Connection Pooling**
   - The driver maintains a pool of pre-opened TCP sockets to the cluster members.
   - On `driver.session()` invocation, a connection is **borrowed** from the pool; on session close, it is **returned** rather than torn down.
   - Pool size is configurable (`maxConnectionPoolSize`), and pool events (acquisition timeout, connection loss) are emitted to application callbacks.
   - A **health-check** runs periodically, evicting broken connections and replacing them.

2. **Session Management**
   - A *session* is a logical unit of work bound to a single TCP connection.
   - Sessions can be configured with a **default access mode** (`READ` or `WRITE`) which the driver uses to route the session to a leader or a follower.
   - Causal-chaining metadata (`bookmarks`) is attached to each session, allowing the cluster to honour causal consistency across requests.

3. **Transaction Demarcation**
   - The driver exposes `session.beginTransaction()`, `tx.run(query)`, `tx.commit()`, and `tx.rollback()`.
   - Internally, the driver sends `BEGIN`, `RUN`, `PULL_ALL`, `COMMIT/ROLLBACK` Bolt messages.
   - **Auto-commit** mode (single-statement transactions) is also supported for trivial operations, saving one round-trip.

**(b) — Pool Sizing via Little's Law (7 marks)**

Given: $QPS = 1200$, $t_{avg} = 30 \text{ ms}$.

**Step 1 — Convert latency to seconds:**

$$
W = \frac{30 \text{ ms}}{1000} = 0.030 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute in-flight requests:**

$$
L = \lambda \cdot W = 1200 \times 0.030 = 36 \text{ requests}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply a 25 % safety margin:**

$$
C = \left\lceil 36 \times 1.25 \right\rceil = \left\lceil 45 \right\rceil = 45
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{min} = 45 \text{ connections}}
$$

**Consequences of an undersized pool** (e.g., $C = 20$):

- The driver's connection-acquisition queue will grow unbounded during traffic bursts.
- `acquisition timeout` exceptions will surface to the application, triggering user-facing 503 errors.
- **Tail latency** (p99) will rise sharply — a phenomenon known as **"knee-of-curve" latency** — because queued requests pile up rather than execute in parallel.
- **CPU utilisation** of the database engine remains below 100 % (under-utilisation), yet user-perceived latency exceeds the SLA — a classic example of *thread-pool saturation without back-pressure* causing "noisy-neighbour" effects.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Application Architecture Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the driver layer with the engine layer** — students often say the driver "executes" the query. The driver *transmits*; the engine *executes*. Marks lost: ~1–2 per sub-part.
> 2. **Omitting causal-consistency details** — when asked about replication, students list "read replicas" but forget to mention the **causal-chain bookmark** mechanism. This is a 2-mark deduction in KTU 2024 valuations.
> 3. **Incorrect pool-size formula** — many write $C = QPS$ or $C = QPS \cdot t_{avg}$ *without unit conversion*. Always show $W$ in **seconds**; marks are awarded for the conversion step.
> 4. **Forgetting the "Why embedded" justification** — a comparative question demands an explicit *recommendation with justification*. A pure table without a conclusion is awarded only 4 of 7 marks.
> 5. **Drawing the diagram with boxes but no labels** — KTU examiners want *labelled* block diagrams; an unlabelled diagram receives ≤ 3 of 7 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three logical tiers + storage** form the canonical reference architecture: Application → Driver → Engine → Storage.
- **Embedded** = in-process, fast, single-host, no horizontal scaling. **Server-based** = network-bounded, scalable, multi-client.
- **Connection pooling** sizing uses Little's Law: $C \approx \frac{QPS \cdot t_{avg}}{1000} \times 1.25$ (safety factor).
- **Cluster topology**: odd $R$ (3 or 5) members, $W = \lfloor R/2 \rfloor + 1$, tolerates $f = W - 1$ failures.
- **Causal consistency** uses session-bound **bookmarks** to honour read-your-writes on followers.
- **Polyglot persistence**: combine graph DB (relationships) with relational (profiles), key-value (sessions), and columnar (events) stores.
- **Read-replica routing** must respect access mode (`READ` vs. `WRITE`) declared at session open.
- **Tail latency (p99)** is the engineering target — optimise pool size and replica placement to keep $L_{p99} < 50$ ms.
- **Quorum = strict majority**; even $R$ is disallowed to avoid split-brain partitions.
- **Driver responsibilities**: connection pool, session, transaction demarcation, wire protocol, auth/TLS.
- **Always show units** (ms vs. s) in pool-sizing derivations — a 1000× error is otherwise easy to commit.
- **Diagrams in KTU must be labelled** — unlabelled boxes earn partial credit only.
- **Recommendation questions demand justification** — a table without a concluding recommendation is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Redundancy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Redundancy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔴 MODULE 2 — REDUNDANCY IN GRAPH DATABASES

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Redundancy in a Graph Database** is the property of a graph data model in which the same piece of information (an attribute, a label, or a relationship) is represented **more than once** across vertices, edges, or properties, such that any single modification to the underlying fact requires **multiple synchronized updates** to preserve consistency.

In the context of the **Property Graph Model** and the **RDF Graph Model** (the two principal graph data models prescribed in the KTU 2024 PECDT751 syllabus), redundancy manifests whenever:

- A **property** is duplicated on multiple vertices that share a semantic class.
- A **relationship type** carries information that could be inferred through graph traversal.
- **Label names, edge types, or attribute keys** are repeated when a single hyper-edge or a higher-order construct could have represented the same fact.

Formally, given a property graph $G = (V, E, \lambda, \sigma)$ where $V$ is the set of vertices, $E \subseteq (V \times V)$ is the set of directed edges, $\lambda: (V \cup E) \rightarrow L$ is the labelling function, and $\sigma: (V \cup E) \times K \rightarrow S$ is the property function mapping elements to attribute-value pairs, **redundancy** exists when there exist two distinct elements $x_1, x_2 \in V \cup E$ such that some semantically equivalent information can be derived from both.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECDT751 – Module 2):** Redundancy is studied alongside **integrity constraints, schema design, and normalization** in graph databases. The KTU examiner expects you to (a) define it, (b) classify it, (c) explain why it harms data quality, and (d) discuss when *controlled* redundancy is acceptable.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Address Book Story

Imagine a small office where three employees — **Anu**, **Balu**, and **Chitra** — share the same office room, **Room 304**. Now consider two ways to record this fact in a graph database:

| Approach | What the Graph Looks Like | Real-World Analogy |
|----------|---------------------------|--------------------|
| **Redundant (Denormalized)** | Each of the three `Person` nodes has a property `room = "304"` | Printing "Room 304" on Anu's, Balu's, and Chitra's personal ID cards |
| **Non-Redundant (Normalized)** | One `Room` node labelled `Room 304` exists; all three `Person` nodes connect to it via an `OFFICE_IN` edge | Putting a single signboard on the door of Room 304 and letting the people just be linked to the room |

If Anu moves to Room 305, the **redundant** approach forces you to update **three separate property values**, and forgetting even one leads to an inconsistent database. The **non-redundant** approach requires only **one update** to the `OFFICE_IN` edge of Anu, and the room itself never needs to change.

> [!TIP]
> **The Golden Rule of Graph Modelling:** *"A fact should live in exactly one place unless duplication is justified by a measurable query-performance benefit."*

## 1.3 Why Redundancy Matters in Graph Databases

Graph databases are praised for their ability to express **deep, recursive relationships** natively. However, this expressive power tempts designers to encode the *same fact* in *multiple* ways. The consequences are:

1. **Update Anomalies** — a single real-world change requires multiple writes.
2. **Inconsistency** — partial updates leave the graph in a contradictory state.
3. **Storage Waste** — duplicate data increases the on-disk footprint.
4. **Traversal Cost** — redundant edges force the engine to filter duplicates at query time.
5. **Semantic Ambiguity** — the "single source of truth" becomes unclear.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 Formal Classification of Redundancy

Redundancy in a property graph can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

### Axis A — By *Where* the Redundancy Lives

| Type | Location | Example |
|------|----------|---------|
| **Property-Level Redundancy** | Duplicate attribute values on multiple vertices | `(:Person {name:"Anu", age:21})` and `(:Person {name:"Anu", age:21})` stored twice |
| **Edge-Level Redundancy** | Multiple edges encoding the same relationship | Two `KNOWS` edges between the same pair of nodes with different `since` dates |
| **Schema-Level Redundancy** | Label/relationship-type duplication that could be collapsed | A `(:Student)` label and a `(:Learner)` label meaning the same thing |
| **Structural Redundancy** | A direct edge that duplicates information inferable through a path | A `FRIEND_OF` edge between A and C when `A→B→C` already exists via `KNOWS` |

### Axis B — By *Why* it Exists

| Type | Motivation | Trade-off |
|------|------------|-----------|
| **Avoidable Redundancy** | Modelling oversight | Pure cost — must be eliminated |
| **Intentional (Controlled) Redundancy** | Query-performance optimization, denormalization for read-heavy workloads | Acceptable *only* with maintenance mechanisms (e.g., triggers, application-layer invariants) |

### Axis C — By *Detectability*

- **Syntactic Redundancy** — visible from the schema/instance itself (e.g., literal duplicate properties).
- **Semantic Redundancy** — only detectable using integrity constraints or domain knowledge (e.g., `date_of_birth` vs. derived `age`).

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation

Let $G = (V, E, \lambda, \sigma)$ be a property graph. Define the **information content** of an element $x \in V \cup E$ as the set:

$$I(x) = \{(k, \sigma(x, k)) : k \in K, \; \sigma(x, k) \text{ is defined}\}$$

Redundancy exists between two elements $x_1, x_2$ (with $x_1 \neq x_2$) when:

$$I(x_1) \cap I(x_2) \neq \varnothing \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{sem}(I(x_1)) \equiv \text{sem}(I(x_2))$$

where $\text{sem}(\cdot)$ is a *semantic equivalence* operator. When **semantic equivalence** is dropped and only **syntactic equality** is considered, we obtain the narrower class of *syntactic* redundancies — the kind a Cypher/SPARQL script can detect mechanically.

> [!NOTE]
> **The semantic operator $\text{sem}$ is undecidable in the general case** — this is a fundamental theoretical result from database theory. It is precisely *why* human-designed integrity constraints remain essential.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Expression / Rule | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Property Graph definition | $G = (V, E, \lambda, \sigma)$ | Foundation for all graph algorithms |
| 2 | Information content of an element | $I(x) = \{(k, \sigma(x,k))\}$ | Detects property-level redundancy |
| 3 | Redundancy condition | $I(x_1) \cap I(x_2) \neq \varnothing \;\wedge\; \text{sem}(I(x_1)) \equiv \text{sem}(I(x_2))$ | Core definition |
| 4 | Update Anomaly Cost | $C_{update} = n \cdot t_{write}$ where $n$ = number of duplicate locations | Quantifies the price of redundancy |
| 5 | Storage Overhead | $O_{storage} = \dfrac{S_{actual} - S_{optimal}}{S_{optimal}} \times 100\%$ | Measures wasted bytes |
| 6 | Inconsistency Probability | $P_{inconsistent} = 1 - (1 - p)^{n}$ where $p$ = per-update failure probability | Justifies denormalization-vs-normalization trade-off |
| 7 | Traversal Speedup (from denormalization) | $S = \dfrac{T_{normalized}}{T_{denormalized}}$ | Quantifies performance gain |
| 8 | Functional Dependency (generalization) | $X \rightarrow Y$ on vertices/edges | Drives normalization decisions |
| 9 | Boyce–Codd Normal Form (adapted) | For every non-trivial FD $X \rightarrow Y$, $X$ must be a superkey | Gold standard for redundancy-free design |
| 10 | Schema Equivalence | $\text{Sch}(G_1) \equiv \text{Sch}(G_2) \iff \forall q, \; q(G_1) = q(G_2)$ | Used to prove transformations are redundancy-preserving |

## 2.4 The Normalization–Denormalization Trade-off

Graph databases, like their relational cousins, occupy a **spectrum**:

```
  Fully Normalized ◄──────────────────────────────► Fully Denormalized
  (zero redundancy)                                (maximum redundancy for speed)
```

| Position | Pros | Cons |
|----------|------|------|
| **Normalized (left)** | No update anomalies; minimal storage; clear semantics | Multi-hop traversals at query time |
| **Denormalized (right)** | Single-hop reads; faster aggregations | Update anomalies, inconsistency risk |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common 14-mark question asks you to *redesign* a redundant schema into a normalized one (or vice versa) and justify your choice. Always state the **query pattern** and **write/read ratio** of the workload before choosing a side.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Social Networks (Facebook, LinkedIn):** Friendship is *not* stored twice (A→B and B→A); a single undirected edge suffices. Redundancy is avoided.
- **Knowledge Graphs (Google, Wikidata):** RDF reification is used *sparingly* to avoid triple-triplication.
- **Fraud Detection Graphs:** *Intentional* denormalization caches risk scores on edges to avoid recomputation per query.
- **Supply-Chain Graphs:** Material composition is normalized to a single Bill-of-Materials subgraph.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL DERIVATION

## 3.1 Deriving the Inconsistency Probability Formula

We want to prove that for $n$ duplicate locations of the same fact, each with independent per-update failure probability $p$, the probability that **at least one** of the duplicates becomes inconsistent is:

$$P_{inconsistent} = 1 - (1 - p)^{n}$$

**Step 1 — Probability of a *single* successful update at one location:**
The probability that a given write to one of the $n$ duplicate locations succeeds is $(1 - p)$.

**Step 2 — Probability that *all* $n$ locations are updated successfully:**
Assuming independence of the $n$ writes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{all \; success} &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - p_i) \\
&= (1 - p)^n \quad \text{(when } p_i = p \text{ for all } i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Probability that *at least one* update fails (inconsistency):**
Using the complement rule of probability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{inconsistent} &= 1 - P_{all \; success} \\
&= 1 - (1 - p)^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Doubling the number of duplicate locations from $n$ to $2n$ increases the inconsistency probability *non-linearly*. For $p = 0.01$ and $n = 5$, $P_{inconsistent} \approx 0.049$. For $n = 10$, $P_{inconsistent} \approx 0.0956$ — almost double.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Detecting Redundancy in a Toy Graph

**Given graph** (a small university domain):

| Node ID | Labels | Properties |
|---------|--------|------------|
| `n1` | `Student` | `name="Anu"`, `dept="CSE"` |
| `n2` | `Student` | `name="Anu"`, `dept="CSE"`, `cgpa=9.1` |
| `n3` | `Department` | `name="CSE"`, `hod="Dr. Rao"` |
| `n4` | `Course` | `code="CS301"`, `credits=4` |

| Edge | Type | Properties |
|------|------|------------|
| `n1→n3` | `BELONGS_TO` | — |
| `n2→n3` | `BELONGS_TO` | — |
| `n1→n4` | `ENROLLED` | `semester=5` |
| `n2→n4` | `ENROLLED` | `semester=5` |

**Step 1 — Compute information content of each `Student` node:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(n_1) &= \{(\text{name}, \text{"Anu"}), (\text{dept}, \text{"CSE"})\} \\
I(n_2) &= \{(\text{name}, \text{"Anu"}), (\text{dept}, \text{"CSE"}), (\text{cgpa}, 9.1)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Test the redundancy condition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(n_1) \cap I(n_2) &= \{(\text{name}, \text{"Anu"}), (\text{dept}, \text{"CSE"})\} \\
&\neq \varnothing
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\text{sem}(I(n_1)) \equiv \text{sem}(I(n_2))$ for the common attributes, **property-level redundancy is present**.

**Step 3 — Edge-level check:** Both `n1→n4` and `n2→n4` are `ENROLLED` edges with `semester=5` to the same `Course` `CS301`. If `n1` and `n2` are actually the *same* real-world student mistakenly entered twice, then the **edge set is also redundant**.

**Step 4 — Recommended Normalized Redesign:**

- **Merge** `n1` and `n2` into a single `Student` node (after entity resolution).
- **Promote** the `Department` to a dedicated node (already done correctly).
- **Keep** one `ENROLLED` edge per (student, course) pair.
- **Drop** the `dept` property from `Student`; derive it via the `BELONGS_TO` traversal.

**Step 5 — Storage-overhead calculation (before vs. after):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{storage} &= \frac{S_{before} - S_{after}}{S_{after}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{(2 \text{ student nodes} \times 5 \; \text{props} + 4 \text{ edges}) - (1 \times 6 + 2)}{1 \times 6 + 2} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{14 - 8}{8} \times 100\% = 75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The redundant schema wastes **75% more storage** than the normalized one for this fragment.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Allow Controlled Redundancy

| Condition | Decision | Justification |
|-----------|----------|---------------|
| Read-to-Write ratio > 100 : 1 | **Allow** denormalization | The cost of occasional inconsistency is dwarfed by read-throughput gains |
| Data has strict regulatory audit (e.g., finance, medical) | **Avoid** redundancy | Compliance and traceability demand a single source of truth |
| Query pattern is a 1-hop lookup | **Allow** cached property on edge | Avoids a traversal hop |
| Query pattern requires deep multi-hop reasoning | **Normalize** | Redundancy pollutes the inference engine |
| Updates are atomic and infrequent | **Allow** with triggers | Stale window is bounded |
| Updates are concurrent and high-frequency | **Avoid** | Race conditions multiply with $n$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Redundancy Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[REDUNDANCY IN GRAPH DATABASES] --> B[Property-Level]
    A --> C[Edge-Level]
    A --> D[Schema-Level]
    A --> E[Structural]

    B --> B1[Duplicate attribute values across vertices]
    B --> B2[Same key-value pair stored twice]

    C --> C1[Multiple edges encoding same relationship]
    C --> C2[Reified RDF statements duplicating triples]

    D --> D1[Label duplication Student vs Learner]
    D --> D2[Edge-type synonyms WORKS_AT vs EMPLOYED_BY]

    E --> E1[Direct edge duplicating inferable path]
    E --> E2[Cached property derivable by traversal]

    A --> F[By Motivation]
    F --> F1[Avoidable modelling oversight]
    F --> F2[Intentional performance optimization]

    A --> G[By Detectability]
    G --> G1[Syntactic - mechanically detectable]
    G --> G2[Semantic - needs constraints or humans]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Normalization Workflow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Redundant Graph G1] --> B[Apply Functional Dependencies]
    B --> C[Identify Duplicate Facts]
    C --> D[Merge Equivalent Nodes]
    D --> E[Promote Attributes to Nodes]
    E --> F[Replace Cached Edges with Traversals]
    F --> G[Normalized Graph G2]
    G --> H{Workload Read-Heavy?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Selective Denormalization]
    H -- No --> J[Keep G2 Normalized]
    I --> K[Controlled Redundant Graph G3]
    J --> L[Production Graph]
    K --> L
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Update Anomaly Cascade

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Real-world fact: Anu moves to Room 305] --> B[Application issues UPDATE]
    B --> C{How many copies of the fact exist?}
    C -- n equals 1 --> D[Single write - safe]
    C -- n equals 3 --> E[Three writes needed]
    E --> F1[Write 1 succeeds]
    E --> F2[Write 2 fails network]
    E --> F3[Write 3 succeeds]
    F2 --> G[Graph enters inconsistent state]
    G --> H[Query returns mixed results]
    H --> I[Application-level bug or data corruption]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Property Graph Before/After Normalization

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph BEFORE
        S1[Student n1 name Anu dept CSE]
        S2[Student n2 name Anu dept CSE cgpa 9.1]
        D1[Department CSE]
        S1 -->|BELONGS_TO| D1
        S2 -->|BELONGS_TO| D1
    end

    subgraph AFTER
        S3[Student name Anu cgpa 9.1]
        D2[Department name CSE]
        S3 -->|BELONGS_TO| D2
    end
```

## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Redundancy Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Raw Graph Database] --> B[Schema Parser]
    B --> C[Metadata Extractor]
    A --> D[Instance Scanner]
    C --> E[Constraint Engine]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Redundancy Detector Module]
    F --> G{Type of Redundancy}
    G -- Property --> H[Property-Dedup Report]
    G -- Edge --> I[Edge-Collapse Report]
    G -- Schema --> J[Label-Merge Report]
    G -- Structural --> K[Path-Folding Report]
    H --> L[Human Reviewer]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Refactored Graph]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define redundancy in the context of a graph database. Give one example. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Redundancy in a graph database is the condition in which the same piece of information — a property value, an edge, or a schema element — is stored at more than one location within the graph, such that any change to the underlying real-world fact requires multiple synchronized updates to keep the database consistent.

**Example:** A `(:Person {name:"Anu", city:"Kochi"})` node and a second `(:Person {name:"Anu", city:"Kochi"})` node both exist in the graph, encoding Anu's city twice. If Anu moves to Chennai, both nodes must be updated; failing to do so leaves the graph inconsistent.

---

### Q2. List any **three** problems caused by redundancy in a graph database. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Update anomalies** — a single real-world change demands multiple writes, increasing the chance of partial failure.
2. **Inconsistency** — when not all duplicates are updated, the graph contradicts itself and queries return conflicting results.
3. **Wasted storage and traversal cost** — duplicated data inflates the on-disk footprint and forces the engine to deduplicate results at query time.

*(A fourth acceptable answer: semantic ambiguity, where the "single source of truth" becomes unclear.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Do **not** confuse *redundancy* with *replication*. Replication is a distributed-systems concept (copies on different servers for fault-tolerance); redundancy is a **data-modelling** concept (the same fact stored multiple times within one schema).
> 2. Always cite a **concrete query pattern or workload characteristic** when justifying denormalization — vague answers get only 50% credit.
> 3. In diagrams, **label every node and edge type**. A graph drawing without labels is treated as an unlabelled sketch and loses up to 3 marks.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Normalization Path

**`(a)` [7 Marks]** Explain, with a suitable example, the concept of **property-level redundancy** in a property graph. How can it be detected and eliminated? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Model Solution:**

*Property-level redundancy* occurs when two or more vertices (or edges) carry attribute–value pairs that semantically encode the same fact.

**Detection method:**
- Enumerate the information content $I(x)$ of every vertex and edge.
- For every pair $(x_1, x_2)$ with $x_1 \neq x_2$, compute $I(x_1) \cap I(x_2)$.
- If the intersection is non-empty **and** the common attributes are semantically equivalent, the pair is property-redundant.

**Elimination method:**
- Identify a *canonical* representative among the duplicates (the one with the richest property set, or the one referenced by the most edges).
- Merge the duplicates into the canonical node, transferring all distinct edges.
- For attributes that can be *derived* by traversal (e.g., `dept` from a `BELONGS_TO` edge), drop the cached property from the vertex and let the query compute it.

**Example (worked):** Two `Person` nodes both store `city="Kochi"`. Detect via $I(n_1) \cap I(n_2) = \{(\text{city}, \text{"Kochi"})\} \neq \varnothing$. Eliminate by merging the two nodes, or, if the two people are distinct, by introducing a single `City` node labelled `"Kochi"` and connecting both people to it via a `LIVES_IN` edge.

**`(b)` [7 Marks]** A university information system is modelled as follows. Two `(:Student)` nodes $S_1$ and $S_2$ have identical properties `name="Anu"` and `dept="CSE"`. Both are connected to a `(:Department {name:"CSE"})` node via a `BELONGS_TO` edge. Redesign this fragment to **eliminate redundancy** and justify each step. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Entity Resolution:** Verify whether $S_1$ and $S_2$ are actually the *same* student entered twice. If yes, merge them into a single `Student` node carrying the union of all properties (e.g., add `cgpa` if it was only on $S_2$).

**Step 2 — Drop Derivable Property:** Remove the `dept` property from the `Student` node. The department is reachable via the `BELONGS_TO` edge, so storing it on the node is a *cached* redundancy.

**Step 3 — Promote `CSE` to a Real-World Node:** The `(:Department {name:"CSE"})` node already exists; ensure it carries an authoritative `name` and a stable identifier. No further change is needed at this level.

**Step 4 — Add a Unique Constraint:** On the `Student` label, declare a **uniqueness constraint** on a `student_id` property so future inserts cannot create duplicates.

**Step 5 — Final Schema:**

- One `(:Student {student_id, name, cgpa})` node.
- One `(:Department {dept_id, name})` node.
- A single `(:Student)-[:BELONGS_TO]->(:Department)` edge.

**Justification:** This design stores Anu's department in *exactly one place* — the `BELONGS_TO` edge. Any change to the department is a single-edge update, eliminating the $P_{inconsistent}$ risk that scales with $n$.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Denormalization Trade-off

**`(a)` [7 Marks]** Discuss the **normalization vs. denormalization trade-off** in graph databases. Under what conditions is *intentional* redundancy justified? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

The trade-off is between **write integrity** (favoured by normalization) and **read latency** (favoured by denormalization).

- **Normalization** removes every duplicate, leaving a single source of truth. It minimizes update anomalies and storage waste, but multi-hop queries may require traversals at run-time.
- **Denormalization** introduces duplicate facts — typically as cached properties on frequently-traversed edges. It collapses multi-hop reads into single-hop lookups at the cost of update complexity.

**Conditions under which intentional redundancy is justified:**

1. **Read-heavy workload** with a measured read-to-write ratio > 100 : 1.
2. **Stable cached values** that change rarely (e.g., a user's `account_tier` cached on every edge to a premium feature).
3. **Performance-critical paths** where a sub-millisecond latency budget forbids a multi-hop traversal.
4. **Maintenance infrastructure in place** — application-layer invariants, asynchronous reconciliation jobs, or database triggers — to repair the duplicates when they drift.
5. **Regulatory latitude** — the domain tolerates eventual consistency (e.g., recommendation engines, social feeds), as opposed to financial ledgers that forbid it.

**Risk metric:** Use $P_{inconsistent} = 1 - (1-p)^n$ to bound the staleness window. If the system can tolerate that probability within its service-level objective, the redundancy is acceptable.

**`(b)` [7 Marks]** A social-network graph stores *mutual friendships* as **two** directed edges: `A→B` and `B→A`, each with its own `since` property. Is this redundancy? Justify. If yes, propose a normalized redesign; if no, explain why. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**Model Solution:**

**Verdict — Yes, this is redundancy (in most senses), but with a caveat.**

**Justification for "redundant":**
- The *fact* "A and B are friends" is encoded **twice** — once on each directed edge.
- If B updates the friendship date, the reverse edge may diverge, producing two different `since` values for the *same* friendship — a semantic anomaly.

**Caveat — Why it is sometimes kept:**
- A directed graph database (e.g., Neo4j) stores edges as directional records; an undirected relationship *must* be modelled as two directed edges, otherwise the database cannot traverse in both directions efficiently.
- A few systems (e.g., those supporting undirected-edge types) collapse this into one logical edge automatically.

**Normalized Redesign:**

| Choice | Description | Trade-off |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| **Option 1** | Use a single *undirected* edge `(:Person)-[:FRIEND {since}]-(:Person)` and let the engine handle bi-directional traversal | Cleaner, but not all graph DBs support undirected edges natively |
| **Option 2** | Keep two directed edges but **move** the `since` property to a *shared* "Friendship" intermediate node: `(:Person)-[:HAS_FRIENDSHIP]->(:Friendship {since})<-[:HAS_FRIENDSHIP]-(:Person)` | One `since` value, no divergence; adds one node per friendship |
| **Option 3** | Keep two directed edges and enforce an **application-level invariant** that `since(A→B) = since(B→A)` via a trigger | Minimal schema change; relies on maintenance code |

**Recommended answer for KTU credit:** *Option 2* is the fully normalized solution that eliminates the semantic redundancy of the `since` property while preserving the directional traversal capability of the underlying engine.

---

# 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Redundancy"**

- ✅ **Definition:** Redundancy = the same fact stored in more than one place within a single graph, requiring multiple updates for one real-world change.
- ✅ **Four locations of redundancy:** *property-level, edge-level, schema-level, structural.*
- ✅ **Two motivations:** *avoidable (modelling error)* vs *intentional (performance).*
- ✅ **Two detectability classes:** *syntactic* (mechanically scannable) vs *semantic* (needs constraints / human review).
- ✅ **Key formula to memorize:** $P_{inconsistent} = 1 - (1 - p)^{n}$ — proves redundancy risk grows **non-linearly** with the number of duplicates.
- ✅ **Storage overhead formula:** $O_{storage} = \dfrac{S_{actual} - S_{optimal}}{S_{optimal}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Redundancy ≠ Replication** — replication is a *distributed-systems* concept; redundancy is a *modelling* concept. Examiners *will* test this distinction.
- ✅ **Normalization tools:** entity resolution, property dropping, schema promotion, uniqueness constraints.
- ✅ **Denormalization is justified only when:** read-to-write ratio is very high, cached values are stable, latency budgets are tight, and a maintenance mechanism exists.
- ✅ **Always cite a workload characteristic** (query pattern, read/write ratio, consistency requirement) when justifying either side of the trade-off.
- ✅ **Common KTU pitfalls:** unlabelled diagrams, confusing redundancy with replication, forgetting the `sem()` operator in formal definitions, and giving "denormalization is bad" / "normalization is good" as absolute answers — both are conditional.
- ✅ **Practical takeaway:** *Model facts once; cache only when measurements demand it.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graph Algorithms in Neo4j
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Graph Algorithms in Neo4j

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Graph Algorithms in Neo4j** are specialized computational procedures that operate on graph-structured data stored in Neo4j to extract meaningful insights, identify patterns, compute metrics, and solve complex relationship-based problems. These algorithms traverse nodes (vertices) and relationships (edges) to evaluate structural properties, calculate importance scores, discover communities, predict connections, and find optimal paths.

According to the **KTU 2024 syllabus (PECDT751)**, the official terminology is:

> A **graph algorithm** is a deterministic sequence of well-defined instructions applied to a property graph to compute a specific structural or semantic property — such as shortest paths, centrality, community membership, similarity, or link likelihood.

Neo4j implements these algorithms primarily through the **Neo4j Graph Data Science (GDS) library** — a plugin that provides production-grade, parallelized implementations of over 60 graph algorithms exposed via the Cypher query language procedures (e.g., `gds.pageRank.stream`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly mandates the study of *Pathfinding, Centrality, Community Detection, Similarity, and Link Prediction* algorithms, along with their **Cypher-based implementation** in Neo4j GDS.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a city's metro map as a graph:

- **Stations** = Nodes (vertices)
- **Tracks** = Relationships (edges)
- **Train schedules / distances** = Edge weights / properties

Now, ask real-world questions:
1. *"What is the fastest route from Station A to Station B?"* → **Shortest Path Algorithm** (Dijkstra / A*)
2. *"Which station is the most 'central' to the network?"* → **Centrality Algorithm** (PageRank / Betweenness)
3. *"Which group of stations forms a self-contained district?"* → **Community Detection** (Louvain / Label Propagation)
4. *"Which two unconnected stations behave most similarly?"* → **Similarity Algorithm** (Jaccard / Cosine)
5. *"Which two friends-of-friends are most likely to become friends?"* → **Link Prediction** (Adamic-Adar / Common Neighbors)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Graph algorithms are the "GPS, census, and social-detective tools" of a graph database — they let you ask *intelligent questions* about the structure and behavior of your connected data, not just retrieve it.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants in Graph Algorithms

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Number of nodes | $n = \vert V \vert$ | $\geq 1$ |
| Number of edges | $m = \vert E \vert$ | $\geq 0$ |
| Damping factor (PageRank) | $d$ | **0.85** (Brin-Page default) |
| Convergence tolerance | $\epsilon$ | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ |
| Max iterations | $k_{max}$ | 20 to 100 |
| Random walk length | $L$ | 3 to 10 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Graph Algorithms in Neo4j

The Neo4j GDS library classifies algorithms into **5 major families**, all of which are high-yield for KTU 2024 examinations:

### 2.1.1 Pathfinding Algorithms
Find optimal paths between nodes.
- **Single Source Shortest Path (SSSP)** — Dijkstra's algorithm, $O((n+m) \log n)$
- **All Pairs Shortest Path (APSP)** — Floyd-Warshall, $O(n^3)$
- **Shortest Path (with weights)** — `gds.shortestPath.dijkstra.stream`
- **A* (A-Star)** — Heuristic-based, faster for known target
- **Yen's K-Shortest Paths** — Top-K alternative routes
- **Random Walk** — Stochastic path simulation

### 2.1.2 Centrality Algorithms
Identify the most important nodes.
- **Degree Centrality** — Local connection count
- **Betweenness Centrality** — Bridge nodes
- **Closeness Centrality** — Closeness to all others
- **PageRank** — Global influence (used by Google)
- **Eigenvector Centrality** — Influence-of-influence
- **ArticleRank / Personalized PageRank** — Variants

### 2.1.3 Community Detection Algorithms
Cluster nodes into densely connected groups.
- **Louvain Modularity** — Hierarchical, maximizes modularity $Q$
- **Label Propagation (LPA)** — Fast, semi-supervised
- **Connected Components** — Weakly connected subgraphs
- **Strongly Connected Components (SCC)** — Directed reachability
- **K-1 Coloring** — Graph coloring
- **Modularity Optimization** — Quality metric for partitions

### 2.1.4 Similarity Algorithms
Compare nodes based on neighborhood overlap.
- **Jaccard Similarity** — $J(A,B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$
- **Cosine Similarity** — Vector angle-based
- **Pearson Similarity** — Correlation-based
- **Euclidean Distance** — Geometric distance
- **Overlap Similarity** — Normalized intersection

### 2.1.5 Link Prediction Algorithms
Estimate probability of future edges.
- **Common Neighbors (CN)** — Simple count
- **Adamic-Adar (AA)** — Weighted rare connections
- **Preferential Attachment (PA)** — Degree-product
- **Resource Allocation (RA)** — Symmetric to AA
- **Same Community (SC)** — Community-based
- **Total Neighbors (TN)** — Union count

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every key formula you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE. The vertical pipe symbol for set cardinality and absolute value is written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Algorithm | Formula | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Degree Centrality** | $C_D(v) = \deg(v)$ | $\deg(v)$ = number of incident edges |
| **Betweenness Centrality** | $C_B(v) = \sum_{s \neq v \neq t} \frac{\sigma_{st}(v)}{\sigma_{st}}$ | $\sigma_{st}$ = # shortest paths from $s$ to $t$ |
| **Closeness Centrality** | $C_C(v) = \frac{n-1}{\sum_{u \neq v} d(v,u)}$ | $d(v,u)$ = shortest path distance |
| **PageRank (Brin-Page)** | $PR(v) = \frac{1-d}{n} + d \cdot \sum_{u \in M(v)} \frac{PR(u)}{L(u)}$ | $d$=0.85, $L(u)$=out-degree of $u$ |
| **Eigenvector Centrality** | $x_v = \frac{1}{\lambda} \sum_{u \in N(v)} x_u$ | $\lambda$ = largest eigenvalue of $A$ |
| **Jaccard Similarity** | $J(A,B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $A,B$ = neighbor sets |
| **Cosine Similarity** | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\sqrt{\vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert}}$ | Treats sets as binary vectors |
| **Adamic-Adar** | $AA(u,v) = \sum_{w \in N(u) \cap N(v)} \frac{1}{\log \vert N(w) \vert}$ | $w$ = common neighbor |
| **Preferential Attachment** | $PA(u,v) = \vert N(u) \vert \cdot \vert N(v) \vert$ | Degree product |
| **Resource Allocation** | $RA(u,v) = \sum_{w \in N(u) \cap N(v)} \frac{1}{\vert N(w) \vert}$ | Variant of AA |
| **Modularity (Louvain)** | $Q = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{ij} \left[ A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m} \right] \delta(c_i, c_j)$ | $\delta$=1 if same community |
| **Dijkstra Update** | $dist[v] = \min(dist[v], dist[u] + w(u,v))$ | $w$=edge weight |

## 2.3 Operational Steps — Generic Cypher Pipeline

The standard Neo4j GDS execution flow consists of **4 mandatory steps**:

1. **Project** the graph into in-memory format:
   `CALL gds.graph.project('myGraph', 'NodeLabel', 'REL_TYPE')`
2. **Configure** algorithm parameters (damping, tolerance, maxIter).
3. **Execute** in one of three modes:
   - `*.stream` → returns rows
   - `*.stats` → returns a single summary
   - `*.write` → writes results back to Neo4j
4. **Clean up** the projection:
   `CALL gds.graph.drop('myGraph')`

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters (real-world utility):** This pipeline pattern is used in **fraud detection** (JPMorgan, PayPal), **recommendation engines** (Netflix, Spotify), **supply-chain risk analysis** (Maersk), **social network influence mapping** (Twitter/X, Meta), and **biological pathway discovery** (pharma R&D).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: PageRank

PageRank was developed by **Larry Page and Sergey Brin (Stanford, 1998)** to rank web pages. In Neo4j, it is invoked as `gds.pageRank.stream`.

### 3.1.1 Intuition

A node is "important" if **other important nodes link to it**. This is a recursive definition requiring iterative computation.

### 3.1.2 The Random Surfer Model

Imagine a web surfer who, at each step, either:
- **Follows a random outgoing link** with probability $d$, OR
- **Teleports** to a random node with probability $(1-d)$.

The steady-state probability of being at node $v$ is its PageRank $PR(v)$.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

Let $L(u) = $ out-degree of node $u$, $M(v) = $ set of nodes linking *to* $v$, $n$ = total nodes, $d = $ damping factor.

**Step 1:** Define the basic recursive relation (without damping):

$$
PR(v) = \sum_{u \in M(v)} \frac{PR(u)}{L(u)}
$$

*Text:* The rank of $v$ is the sum of ranks of pages pointing to it, normalized by how many links each of those pages has.

**Step 2:** Add the random surfer / teleportation term to fix **dangling nodes** (nodes with no outgoing links) and **rank sinks**:

$$
PR(v) = \frac{1-d}{n} + d \cdot \sum_{u \in M(v)} \frac{PR(u)}{L(u)}
$$

*Text:* With probability $d$ the surfer follows links, with probability $(1-d)$ the surfer teleports uniformly to any of the $n$ nodes, contributing $\frac{1-d}{n}$ to each node's rank.

**Step 3:** Initialize the vector uniformly:

$$
PR^{(0)}(v) = \frac{1}{n}, \quad \forall v \in V
$$

*Text:* Initially assume every node is equally important.

**Step 4:** Iterate until convergence:

$$
PR^{(k+1)}(v) = \frac{1-d}{n} + d \cdot \sum_{u \in M(v)} \frac{PR^{(k)}(u)}{L(u)}
$$

*Text:* Each iteration redistributes rank along edges and adds the teleportation floor.

**Step 5:** Convergence criterion — stop when:

$$
\vert PR^{(k+1)}(v) - PR^{(k)}(v) \vert < \epsilon, \quad \forall v
$$

*Text:* Continue iterating until no node's score changes by more than tolerance $\epsilon$ (typically $10^{-4}$).

**Step 6:** Normalize final scores so they sum to 1:

$$
\sum_{v \in V} PR(v) = 1
$$

*Text:* This makes the output interpretable as a probability distribution over nodes.

### 3.1.4 Cypher Example

```cypher
// Step A: Project the graph
CALL gds.graph.project('citations', 'Paper', 'CITES');

// Step B: Run PageRank and stream top 5 papers
CALL gds.pageRank.stream('citations', {
  dampingFactor: 0.85,
  maxIterations: 20,
  tolerance: 0.0000001
})
YIELD nodeId, score
RETURN gds.util.asNode(nodeId).title AS paper, score
ORDER BY score DESC
LIMIT 5;
```

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Betweenness Centrality

Betweenness Centrality quantifies how often a node lies on shortest paths between other nodes — i.e., its role as a **bridge** or **broker**.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** For every ordered pair $(s,t)$ with $s \neq t$, count the total number of shortest paths $\sigma_{st}$.

**Step 2:** For each node $v$ (excluding $s$ and $t$), count the number of shortest paths from $s$ to $t$ that pass through $v$, denoted $\sigma_{st}(v)$.

**Step 3:** Compute the **pair-dependency**:

$$
\delta_{st}(v) = \frac{\sigma_{st}(v)}{\sigma_{st}}
$$

*Text:* This is the fraction of shortest paths between $s$ and $t$ that traverse $v$.

**Step 4:** Sum over all pairs:

$$
C_B(v) = \sum_{s \neq v \neq t \in V} \delta_{st}(v)
$$

*Text:* Total fraction of all-pairs shortest paths passing through $v$.

**Step 5:** Normalize for comparison across graphs of different sizes:

$$
C_B^{norm}(v) = \frac{C_B(v)}{\frac{(n-1)(n-2)}{2}}
$$

*Text:* Divide by the maximum possible value for $n$ nodes.

### 3.2.2 Brande's Algorithm (used internally by GDS)

The naive approach is $O(n^3)$ per node. **Ulrik Brandes' algorithm** computes exact betweenness in $O(n \cdot m)$ using:

- **Single-source shortest paths** (BFS for unweighted, Dijkstra for weighted)
- **Accumulation phase** that propagates dependencies back from leaves to root

$$
\delta_{s \cdot}(v) = \sum_{w : v \in P_s(w)} \frac{\sigma_{sv}}{\sigma_{sw}} \cdot (1 + \delta_{s \cdot}(w))
$$

*Text:* The dependency of $s$ on $v$ is the sum, over all nodes $w$ for which $v$ is a predecessor on the shortest path, of the fraction of $s \to w$ paths through $v$ times $(1 + $ the dependency of $w$ on its descendants$)$.

### 3.2.3 Cypher Example

```cypher
CALL gds.betweenness.stream('citations', { normalize: true })
YIELD nodeId, score
RETURN gds.util.asNode(nodeId).title AS paper, score
ORDER BY score DESC
LIMIT 5;
```

## 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough: Shortest Path (Dijkstra)

For weighted graphs, Neo4j GDS uses Dijkstra's algorithm via `gds.shortestPath.dijkstra.stream`.

### 3.3.1 Algorithm Steps

**Step 1:** Initialize distances:

$$
dist[s] = 0, \quad dist[v] = \infty \;\; \forall v \neq s
$$

*Text:* Source node has zero distance, all others are unknown/infinite.

**Step 2:** Push all nodes into a **min-priority queue** keyed by $dist$.

**Step 3:** Extract node $u$ with minimum $dist$.

**Step 4:** For each neighbor $v$ of $u$ via edge $e$ with weight $w_e$, perform **relaxation**:

$$
dist[v] = \min(dist[v], dist[u] + w_e)
$$

*Text:* Try to improve $v$'s distance by going through $u$.

**Step 5:** Repeat until the target node is extracted (for SSSP) or the queue is empty (for full SSSP).

**Step 6:** Reconstruct path using predecessor map.

### 3.3.2 Cypher Example

```cypher
MATCH (source:City {name: 'Kochi'}), (target:City {name: 'Delhi'})
CALL gds.shortestPath.dijkstra.stream('roadNetwork', {
  sourceNode: source,
  targetNode: target,
  relationshipWeightProperty: 'distance'
})
YIELD path
RETURN [n IN nodes(path) | n.name] AS route,
       reduce(total = 0, r IN relationships(path) | total + r.distance) AS totalKm;
```

## 3.4 Louvain Modularity — Community Detection Steps

**Step 1:** Start with each node in its own community. Compute initial modularity $Q = 0$.

**Step 2:** For each node $i$, evaluate the modularity gain $\Delta Q$ of moving $i$ into the community of each of its neighbors $j$:

$$
\Delta Q = \left[ \frac{\Sigma_{in} + 2k_{i,in}}{2m} - \left( \frac{\Sigma_{tot} + k_i}{2m} \right)^2 \right] - \left[ \frac{\Sigma_{in}}{2m} - \left( \frac{\Sigma_{tot}}{2m} \right)^2 - \left( \frac{k_i}{2m} \right)^2 \right]
$$

*Text:* Compare modularity with vs. without $i$ in community $j$.

**Step 3:** Move $i$ to the community that yields the largest positive $\Delta Q$.

**Step 4:** Repeat until no further improvement is possible (local maximum of $Q$).

**Step 5:** Build a **new graph** where each community becomes a single super-node; edge weights between super-nodes are sum of inter-community edges.

**Step 6:** Repeat Steps 1–5 on the contracted graph — this produces a **hierarchical dendrogram**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Time complexity of Louvain:** $O(m \log n)$ — making it scalable to graphs with billions of edges.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Selection Guide

| Use Case | Recommended Algorithm | Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| Find fastest route | Dijkstra / A* | $O((n+m)\log n)$ |
| Rank pages / nodes by influence | PageRank | $O(k \cdot m)$ |
| Find gatekeepers in network | Betweenness Centrality | $O(n \cdot m)$ Brandes |
| Detect tightly-knit groups | Louvain | $O(m \log n)$ |
| Recommend similar users | Jaccard / Cosine | $O(n \cdot \bar{d}^2)$ |
| Predict future links | Adamic-Adar | $O(n \cdot \bar{d}^2)$ |
| Find weakly connected subgraphs | Connected Components | $O(n + m)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Neo4j GDS Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Neo4j Database\nPersistent Property Graph] --> B[Step 1: gds.graph.project\nCreate In-Memory Graph]
    B --> C[Step 2: Configure Algorithm\nDamping, Iterations, Weights]
    C --> D{Step 3: Choose Execution Mode}
    D --> E[*.stream\nReturn Rows]
    D --> F[*.stats\nReturn Summary]
    D --> G[*.write\nPersist to DB]
    E --> H[Step 4: gds.graph.drop\nClean Up]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Insights: Path, Rank,\nCommunity, Similarity, Prediction]

    style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style C fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style D fill:#ffe1f5,stroke:#cc0066
    style I fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900
```

## 4.2 Algorithm Family Classification Tree

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Neo4j Graph\nAlgorithms))
    Pathfinding
      Dijkstra SSSP
      A Star

```

## 4.3 PageRank Iterative Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initialize: PR0 of v = 1/n] --> B[For k = 1 to kmax]
    B --> C[For each node v:\nPRk of v = 1-d/n + d times sum of PRk-1 of u over L of u]
    C --> D{Convergence?\nDelta less than epsilon}
    D -- No --> B
    D -- Yes --> E[Normalize: sum of PR = 1]
    E --> F[Stream/Write Results to Neo4j]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#e1ffe1
```

## 4.4 Louvain Community Detection — Two-Phase Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Phase 1: Local Moving\nEach node joins community\nthat maximizes Delta Q] --> P2{Modularity Q\nImproved?}
    P2 -- Yes --> P1
    P2 -- No --> P3[Phase 2: Aggregation\nContract each community\ninto a super-node]
    P3 --> P4[New aggregated graph built]
    P4 --> P5{More levels\npossible?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P1
    P5 -- No --> P6[Final Hierarchical\nCommunity Structure]

    style P3 fill:#ffe1f5
```
