
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. Define a "Healthcare Knowledge Graph" and list any four node types commonly used in it. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Healthcare Knowledge Graph** is a semantic graph database that represents medical entities (patients, diseases, drugs, genes, hospitals) as **nodes** and the clinical or biological connections between them as **typed relationships**, enriched with biomedical ontologies.

Four common node types:
1. **Patient** — `:Patient {id, age, gender, dob}`
2. **Disease/Condition** — `:Disease {icd10_code, name}`
3. **Drug** — `:Drug {rxnorm_id, name, class}`
4. **Hospital/Facility** — `:Hospital {name, location, capacity}`

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always name at least 2 *property keys* alongside the label — full marks require the schema-style answer.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between *relational* and *graph* modelling of patient data, with one example query. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Relational (RDBMS) | Graph (Property Graph) |
|---|---|---|
| **Schema** | Rigid tables with foreign keys | Flexible, schema-less nodes/edges |
| **Joins** | Multi-table JOINs degrade with depth | Index-free adjacency — constant-time hops |
| **Query** | SQL with multiple JOIN clauses | Cypher pattern matching |

**Example — "Find all drugs prescribed to diabetic patients who are allergic to Sulfonylureas":**
- *RDBMS:* Requires 4–5 table JOINs (`Patient → Diagnosis → Prescription → Drug → Allergy`).
- *Graph:* A single pattern `(:Patient)-[:HAS_DIAGNOSIS]->(:Disease)<-[:TREATS]-(:Drug)`, traversed in 3 hops.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

---

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **layered architecture of a healthcare knowledge graph**, clearly identifying the role of nodes, edges, properties, ontologies, and analytics. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The healthcare knowledge graph is organized in **five layers**:

1. **Entity Layer (Nodes):** Discrete medical objects — patients, doctors, diseases, drugs, genes, hospitals, lab tests. Example: `(:Patient {name:"Ravi", age:62})`.

2. **Relationship Layer (Edges):** Directed, typed semantic links — `(:Patient)-[:DIAGNOSED_WITH]->(:Disease)`. Edges encode clinical meaning and directionality.

3. **Context Layer (Edge Properties):** Properties on edges like `dosage`, `start_date`, `severity` add temporal and quantitative context to relationships.

4. **Knowledge Layer (Ontologies):** Standard vocabularies such as **SNOMED-CT** for clinical terms, **ICD-10** for diseases, **RxNorm** for medications, **HPO** for phenotypes. They provide semantic interoperability.

5. **Analytics Layer:** Graph algorithms — **pathfinding** (shortest path between drug and disease), **centrality** (identifying key doctors/hospitals), **community detection** (patient cohorts), **embeddings** (predicting missing links).

**Diagram (textual representation):**

$$
\text{Entities} \rightarrow \text{Relationships} \rightarrow \text{Edge Properties} \rightarrow \text{Ontologies} \rightarrow \text{Analytics}
$$

Each layer builds on the previous, producing a **semantically rich, queryable, and analytically powerful** health-data substrate.

---

**(b)** Compute the **network density**, **average degree**, and **clustering coefficient of node X** for a small healthcare subgraph with 10 nodes and 18 edges, where node X has degree 5 and 4 of its neighbours are mutually connected. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$$
n = 10, \quad m = 18, \quad \deg(X) = 5, \quad e_X = 4
$$

**Step 1 — Network Density $D$:**

$$
D = \dfrac{2m}{n(n-1)} = \dfrac{2 \times 18}{10 \times 9} = \dfrac{36}{90} = 0.4
$$

**Step 2 — Average Degree $\bar{k}$:**

$$
\bar{k} = \dfrac{2m}{n} = \dfrac{36}{10} = 3.6
$$

**Step 3 — Clustering Coefficient of X $C_X$:**

$$
k_X(k_X - 1) = 5 \times 4 = 20
$$

$$
C_X = \dfrac{2 e_X}{k_X(k_X - 1)} = \dfrac{2 \times 4}{20} = \dfrac{8}{20} = 0.4
$$

**Interpretation:** The graph is **moderately dense (40 %)**, the average node is connected to **~3.6** others, and node X's neighbourhood is **40 % closed into triangles** — a strong indicator of a clinical cohort cluster.

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice)

**(a)** Discuss **four major use cases of graph databases in the health industry**, explaining the graph operations (e.g., traversal, centrality) that enable each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Adverse Drug Reaction (ADR) Detection — Multi-hop Traversal**
Detects harmful drug combinations by traversing 4–6 hop paths: Drug → Target Protein → Pathway → Side Effect. Uses **variable-length path queries**.

**2. Precision Medicine / Genomic Analysis — Subgraph Matching**
Matches patient genomic profiles (`:Gene → :Variant → :Phenotype`) against disease knowledge subgraphs to recommend personalized therapies. Uses **pattern matching + similarity search**.

**3. Hospital Readmission Prediction — Community Detection & Centrality**
Identifies high-risk patient cohorts using **Louvain community detection** and **PageRank** to flag central, highly-readmitted individuals.

**4. Clinical Trial Recruitment — Subgraph Isomorphism**
Searches the patient graph for subgraphs matching trial-eligibility criteria (age, diagnosis, biomarkers, prior treatments). Replaces slow multi-table SQL JOINs with a single **Cypher pattern**.

**Bonus — Pandemic Surveillance:** Uses **temporal graph analysis** and **BFS propagation** to model infection spread through contact networks.

---

**(b)** For a patient–doctor–disease triangle pattern `(P)-[:VISITS]->(D)-[:DIAGNOSED_WITH]->(Dis)<-[:DIAGNOSED_WITH]-(D2)-[:VISITS]->(P)`, explain the **clinical insight** it provides, and write the equivalent **Cypher** query to find all such triangles in the database. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Clinical Insight:**
A **triangle** of (Patient ↔ Doctor ↔ Disease) reveals **diagnostic agreement patterns**:
- If two different doctors diagnose the same patient with the same disease → high **clinical confidence**.
- Identifies **over-diagnosis clusters** (false positives) and **misdiagnosis trends**.
- Helps in **referral pattern analysis** and **specialist reputation scoring**.

**Equivalent Cypher Query:**

```cypher
MATCH (p:Patient)-[:VISITS]->(d:Doctor)-[:DIAGNOSED_WITH]->(dis:Disease)<-[:DIAGNOSED_WITH]-(d2:Doctor)-[:VISITS]->(p)
WHERE d <> d2
RETURN p.name AS Patient, dis.name AS Disease,
       collect(DISTINCT d.name) AS Doctors,
       count(DISTINCT d) AS DoctorCount
ORDER BY DoctorCount DESC
```

**Query Logic Walkthrough:**
1. `(p)-[:VISITS]->(d)` — patient visits doctor.
2. `(d)-[:DIAGNOSED_WITH]->(dis)` — that doctor diagnoses a disease.
3. `<-[:DIAGNOSED_WITH]-(d2)` — a *different* doctor also diagnoses the same disease.
4. `(d2)-[:VISITS]->(p)` — the second doctor also visits the same patient.
5. `WHERE d <> d2` — excludes self-loops.
6. `collect(DISTINCT d.name)` — aggregates doctor names per triangle.
7. Results sorted by number of agreeing doctors.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `WHERE d <> d2` filter** — produces a degenerate self-loop and loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Using `MATCH ... MATCH` instead of a single chained pattern** — fails to express the triangle as one graph motif; partial credit only.
> 3. **Not explaining *why* the pattern is clinically meaningful** — clinical insight is worth **2 of the 7 marks**; skipping it caps the answer at 5/7.
> 4. **Forgetting `collect(DISTINCT ...)`** — results in duplicate rows for multi-doctor agreement cases.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Looking at Graphs in the Health Industry"**

- ⭐ **Core idea:** Healthcare data is *natively graph-shaped* — multi-typed entities joined by typed, semantically rich, directional relationships.
- ⭐ **Five layers:** Entity → Relationship → Edge Properties → Ontologies → Analytics.
- ⭐ **Key node types:** Patient, Doctor, Disease, Drug, Gene, Hospital, Lab Test, Trial.
- ⭐ **Key relationship types:** `DIAGNOSED_WITH`, `PRESCRIBED`, `ALLERGIC_TO`, `TARGETS`, `INTERACTS_WITH`, `ADMITS`, `WORKS_AT`, `ENROLLED_IN`.
- ⭐ **Why graphs over RDBMS?** Index-free adjacency + constant-time multi-hop traversal; no expensive JOINs.
- ⭐ **Major use cases:** ADR detection, precision medicine, readmission prediction, trial recruitment, pandemic surveillance, hospital network analysis.
- ⭐ **Standard ontologies:** SNOMED-CT, ICD-10, RxNorm, HPO, UMLS — provide semantic interoperability.
- ⭐ **Key formulas to memorize:** Density $D = 2m/[n(n-1)]$, Degree centrality $C_D = \deg(v)/(n-1)$, Clustering coefficient $C_i = 2e_i/[k_i(k_i-1)]$.
- ⭐ **Cypher pattern syntax:** `(:Label)-[:REL_TYPE]->(:Label)` — use `WHERE` for filters, `collect()` for aggregation.
- ⭐ **Triangle motifs** in clinical graphs reveal diagnostic agreement and referral patterns.
- ⭐ **Graph algorithms frequently tested:** BFS/Dijkstra (pathfinding), PageRank (influence), Louvain (communities), node embeddings (link prediction).
- ⭐ **Time complexity contrast:** RDBMS multi-JOIN ≈ $O(n \log n)$ per join vs. graph traversal ≈ $O(k)$ hops — a critical exam point.
- ⭐ **Industry examples:** Mayo Clinic, Pfizer, FDA, AstraZeneca, and Johns Hopkins have publicly adopted graph databases for biomedical research and clinical analytics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graph Database Internals: Native Graph Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Graph Database Internals: Native Graph Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Native Graph Processing** refers to the internal execution paradigm of a graph database management system (GDBMS) in which graph operations—such as traversals, pattern matching, and analytical queries—are executed directly against a purpose-built graph storage layer, using **index-free adjacency** and **pointer-chasing traversals** rather than translating the workload into relational JOINs.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the term "native" is reserved for systems that satisfy **all three** of the following properties simultaneously:

1. **Native Graph Storage** — Nodes, edges, and properties are stored in a logical/logical-physical form optimised for graphs (e.g., linked lists of fixed-size node/edge records, not normalized tables).
2. **Native Graph Processing** — The query engine walks pointers between records at runtime, achieving **O(1) "hops" per traversal step**, independent of total graph size.
3. **Native Graph Computational Model** — Algorithms operate over the graph structure itself (e.g., fixed-point iteration, BFS/DFS, PageRank) rather than over set/bag algebra of rows.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A database is only considered "native" if storage *and* processing *and* computational model are all graph-first. Systems like Neo4j, Memgraph, and RedisGraph are native; systems built by adding a graph layer on top of PostgreSQL/MySQL (e.g., older Bitnine AgensGraph) are **non-native**, regardless of marketing claims.

The mathematical signature of a native graph operation is given by:

$$T_{\text{traverse}}(k) = \mathcal{O}(k \cdot c_{\text{hop}})$$

where $k$ is the traversal depth and $c_{\text{hop}}$ is the constant cost of resolving a single pointer hop, **independent of** $\vert V \vert$ (the total number of vertices).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Native Graph Processing** as navigating a **city using a local guide who has lived there all his life**:

- **Non-native (relational with JOINs)** is like a tourist who has to consult a *phone book* (index) → look up the *address* → walk there → repeat. Every step requires looking up a global map, even if the destination is the next building.
- **Native graph processing** is like the local guide who simply *points to the next door*, then the next, then the next. There is no "global lookup"; the guide just follows the local connections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** Do **not** confuse *index-free adjacency* with "no index at all." Most native graph DBs still maintain **label and property indexes** for fast *entry points* (e.g., "find a User named 'Alice'"). The "index-free" adjective applies specifically to the *traversal of edges* during query execution, not to overall data access.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Native Graph Processing

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Traversal throughput | $\tau$ | **1M–10M hops/sec** | Pointer resolutions per second per core |
| Pointer chase latency | $L_p$ | **< 100 ns** (in-memory) | Time to dereference one adjacency pointer |
| Cold-cache disk hop | $L_d$ | **~10 μs–1 ms** | Latency when graph must be paged from SSD/HDD |
| Memory footprint per node | $M_n$ | **~32–128 bytes** | RAM cost of storing one node record |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU viva:** If asked "Why are native graph databases faster for deep traversals?", the precise answer is: *because each hop is an O(1) pointer dereference, so a k-hop traversal is O(k) regardless of total graph size, whereas a relational engine must perform k nested-loop JOINs, each of which is at best O(log n) and at worst O(n).*

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of "Native" Graph Processing

### Pillar 1 — Native Graph Storage

- Each **node** is stored as a fixed-size record containing: ID, label list, first-edge-pointer, first-property-pointer.
- Each **edge** is stored as a fixed-size record containing: source-ID, target-ID, type, next-out-pointer, next-in-pointer, first-property-pointer.
- This layout enables **cache-friendly sequential scans** along adjacency lists.

### Pillar 2 — Native Graph Processing (Index-Free Adjacency)

- Given a node $v$, all its outgoing edges are reachable by following the `next-out` pointers starting at $v.\text{first-edge}$.
- **No global edge index** is consulted during traversal. This eliminates the B-tree / hash-lookup cost that dominates relational engines.

### Pillar 3 — Native Computational Model

- Query plans are **algebraic traversals** (e.g., Cypher's `MATCH (a)-[:KNOWS*1..3]->(b)`), compiled into an **iterator pipeline**.
- Each iterator pulls one node/edge at a time from its predecessor, enabling **pipelined, set-oriented** execution without materialising intermediate JOINs.

## 2.2 Traversal Algorithms — The Foundation of Native Processing

Native graph databases implement a standard set of **graph algorithms** as built-in operators. The KTU syllabus emphasises the following four:

### A. Breadth-First Search (BFS)

Used for: shortest path in *unweighted* graphs, level-based community detection.

$$d(v) = \min_{u \in N(v)} \left[ d(u) + 1 \right]$$

### B. Depth-First Search (DFS)

Used for: cycle detection, topological sort, reachability.

$$T_{\text{DFS}}(G) = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$$

### C. Dijkstra's Shortest Path (weighted)

Used for: weighted shortest path, routing.

$$d(v) = \min_{(u,v) \in E} \left[ d(u) + w(u,v) \right]$$

### D. PageRank (iterative, native analytics)

Used for: node importance, recommendation, centrality.

$$PR(v) = \frac{1 - d}{\vert V \vert} + d \sum_{u \in M(v)} \frac{PR(u)}{L(u)}$$

where $d \approx 0.85$ is the **damping factor** and $M(v)$ is the set of in-neighbours of $v$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Complexity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Index-free adjacency hop | $T_{\text{hop}} = \mathcal{O}(1)$ | O(1) | Per-edge traversal cost |
| 2 | k-hop traversal | $T_{\text{traverse}}(k) = \mathcal{O}(k)$ | O(k) | Pattern matching, friends-of-friends |
| 3 | Full BFS | $T = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Linear | Shortest path, connected components |
| 4 | Full DFS | $T = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Linear | Cycle detection, topological order |
| 5 | Dijkstra (binary heap) | $T = \mathcal{O}((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ | Quasi-linear | Weighted shortest path |
| 6 | Bellman–Ford | $T = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ | Cubic-ish | Negative weights, single source |
| 7 | PageRank (per iteration) | $T = \mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert)$ | Linear | Node importance ranking |
| 8 | Relational JOIN (nested loop) | $T = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ per hop | Quadratic+ | Comparative baseline |
| 9 | Relational JOIN (hash) | $T = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Linear | Per hop, but with hashing overhead |
| 10 | Native memory footprint | $M \approx 64 \cdot \vert V \vert + 48 \cdot \vert E \vert$ bytes | — | Capacity planning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Trap:** BFS, DFS, and a single relational hash-join all share the *same* asymptotic complexity $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$. Native processing is faster **in practice** because the **constant factor** $c_{\text{native}} \ll c_{\text{join}}$ (no hash table build, no intermediate result materialisation, cache-friendly pointer walks).

## 2.4 Why Native Graph Processing Matters — Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Why Native Wins |
|---|---|---|
| Social networks | Friend-of-friend, influence spread | Deep traversals (depth 4–6) over billions of edges |
| Fraud detection | Multi-hop ring detection | Path queries of length 5–8, sub-second SLA |
| Knowledge graphs | SPARQL/Cypher pattern matching | Variable-length path patterns |
| Network/IT ops | Root-cause analysis across service graph | Real-time incident triage |
| Recommendation | Collaborative filtering via graph walks | Iterative PageRank-like computations |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From "k-hop JOIN" to "k-hop Native Traversal"

**Goal:** Show mathematically why native graph processing is asymptotically and practically superior to relational JOIN-based processing for traversal queries.

### Step 1 — Formulate the k-hop Traversal Query

Consider a social network where we want to find all users reachable from user $u$ within **exactly** $k$ hops. The Cypher pattern is:

```
MATCH (u:User {name:'Alice'})-[:FRIEND*k]->(v:User) RETURN v
```

### Step 2 — Relational Translation (Non-Native)

In a relational schema with tables `users(id, name)` and `friends(user_id, friend_id)`, this becomes **k nested-loop JOINs**:

$$
T_{\text{rel}}(k) = \prod_{i=1}^{k} \left[ \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert) \right] = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^{k})
$$

Each JOIN scans one full table (in the worst case without indexes).

**Reasoning at each step:** The first JOIN produces $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert)$ intermediate rows. The second JOIN re-scans the friends table once for *each* of those $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert)$ rows, producing $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert^2)$. The pattern continues, yielding $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert^{k-1} \cdot \vert V \vert)$.

### Step 3 — Native Translation (Index-Free Adjacency)

In native processing, the query compiles into an iterator pipeline where each step follows a **single pointer**:

$$
T_{\text{native}}(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} c_{\text{hop}} = k \cdot c_{\text{hop}} = \mathcal{O}(k)
$$

**Reasoning at each step:** Starting at Alice's node record, the engine dereferences `first-edge`, yielding the first neighbour in constant time. From that neighbour, it again dereferences `first-edge`, yielding the second hop. This process is independent of $\vert V \vert$ or $\vert E \vert$.

### Step 4 — Asymptotic Comparison

For $k = 3$ and $\vert V \vert = 10^8$:

$$
T_{\text{rel}}(3) = (10^{8})^{3} = 10^{24} \text{ operations}
$$
$$
T_{\text{native}}(3) = 3 \cdot c_{\text{hop}} \approx 3 \cdot 50 \text{ ns} = 150 \text{ ns}
$$

The native engine is approximately **$10^{16}$ times faster** for this specific query — which is precisely why deep traversals are the *killer use-case* for graph databases.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Dijkstra's Algorithm in Native Graph Processing

We derive the single-source shortest path algorithm as it is typically implemented in a native graph engine's `shortestPath()` operator.

### Step 1 — Initialisation

$$
\forall v \in V : d(v) = \infty, \quad d(s) = 0, \quad Q = V
$$

where $d(v)$ is the tentative distance from source $s$, and $Q$ is a **min-priority queue** keyed by $d(v)$.

### Step 2 — Main Loop (Iterative Relaxation)

At each iteration, extract the vertex $u$ with minimum $d(u)$ from $Q$:

$$
u = \arg\min_{v \in Q} d(v)
$$

### Step 3 — Edge Relaxation

For each outgoing edge $(u, v, w)$ reachable via the **native adjacency pointer** `u.next-out`:

$$
\text{if } d(u) + w < d(v) \text{ then } d(v) \leftarrow d(u) + w
$$

This step is $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per edge in a native engine because the edge $(u, v, w)$ is obtained by pointer chase, not by scanning a global edge table.

### Step 4 — Termination

The algorithm halts when $Q = \emptyset$, i.e., when the shortest path from $s$ to every reachable vertex has been finalised.

### Step 5 — Total Complexity

With a binary heap:

$$
T_{\text{Dijkstra}} = \mathcal{O}\left( (\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \cdot \log \vert V \vert \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is "native":** Each edge relaxation in Step 3 is a single $\mathcal{O}(1)$ pointer dereference. The $\log \vert V \vert$ factor comes **only** from the priority queue, not from edge retrieval — unlike the relational implementation, which would have to perform a B-tree lookup per edge.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Native vs Non-Native

| Dimension | Native Graph Processing | Non-Native (Relational + Graph Layer) |
|---|---|---|
| Storage layout | Pointer-linked fixed-size records | Normalised tables |
| Edge retrieval | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ pointer chase | $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ index seek or full scan |
| k-hop query | $\mathcal{O}(k)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n^{k-1})$ worst case |
| Constant factor | Small (1–2 cache misses/hop) | Large (hash build + materialisation) |
| Memory locality | High (sequential pointer walk) | Low (random B-tree access) |
| Hot-path algorithms | BFS, DFS, Dijkstra, PageRank, A* | Implemented in SQL with recursive CTEs |
| Examples | Neo4j, Memgraph, Amazon Neptune (DGL) | PostgreSQL + Apache AGE, JanusGraph on Cassandra |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Native Graph Processing — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **internal architecture** of a native graph database engine and the data flow for a typical traversal query.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client[Client Application] --> parser[Query Parser and Rewriter]
    parser --> planner[Cost-Based Query Planner]
    planner --> exec[Native Execution Engine]
    exec --> it1[Iterator: GetNode by Index]
    exec --> it2[Iterator: Expand Outgoing Edges]
    exec --> it3[Iterator: Filter by Label or Property]
    exec --> it4[Iterator: Variable-Length Expand k Hops]
    it1 --> store[(Native Graph Store)]
    it2 --> store
    it3 --> store
    it4 --> store
    store --> nodes[Node File Linked List]
    store --> edges[Edge File Linked List]
    store --> props[Property Files]
    store --> idx[Label and Property Indexes]
    exec --> result[Result Stream to Client]
```

### Architecture Explanation (Block-by-Block)

1. **Client Application** — Submits a Cypher/SPARQL/GQL query.
2. **Query Parser & Rewriter** — Converts textual query into a logical operator tree.
3. **Cost-Based Query Planner** — Chooses the optimal execution order (e.g., start traversal from the most selective label).
4. **Native Execution Engine** — The heart of the system. It implements a **pull-based iterator pipeline**, where each operator lazily requests one record at a time from its predecessor.
5. **Iterators** — Specialised operators: `GetNodeByIndex`, `Expand(relationship-type)`, `Filter`, `VarExpand(k)`.
6. **Native Graph Store** — The physical storage layer.
7. **Node / Edge / Property Files** — The raw disk-resident graph data.
8. **Label & Property Indexes** — Auxiliary structures for *entry-point* lookups (not used during traversal).

## 4.2 Traversal Flow — Index-Free Adjacency in Action

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Node A first-edge pointer] --> B[Edge A-B pointer]
    B --> C[Node B first-edge pointer]
    C --> D[Edge B-C pointer]
    D --> E[Node C first-edge pointer]
    E --> F[Edge C-D pointer]
    F --> G[Node D target reached]
    style A fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#577590,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

### Diagram Explanation

- The traversal begins at **Node A** by reading its `first-edge` pointer.
- Each subsequent step is a **single pointer dereference** (one CPU cache line read in the best case).
- Notice that **no global lookup table is consulted** during the traversal — this is the essence of *index-free adjacency*.

## 4.3 Iterator Pipeline for `MATCH (a)-[:KNOWS*1..3]->(b)`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1[AllStart] --> n2[NodeByLabelScan Person]
    n2 --> n3[Expand KNOWS]
    n3 --> n4[VarLengthExpand depth 1 to 3]
    n4 --> n5[NodeByLabelScan Person]
    n5 --> n6[Result]
    style n1 fill:#e0e0e0
    style n2 fill:#bbdefb
    style n3 fill:#90caf9
    style n4 fill:#64b5f6
    style n5 fill:#bbdefb
    style n6 fill:#a5d6a7
```

### Diagram Explanation

Each rounded rectangle is an **iterator (operator)** in the pipeline. Data flows lazily from left to right: `n1` generates a single empty row, `n2` materialises Person nodes (using the label index, *not* during traversal), `n3` performs the first-hop expansion via pointer chase, `n4` repeats this for 1 to 3 hops, `n5` filters the targets, and `n6` returns results to the client.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define "native graph processing" and list the three properties a system must satisfy to be called native. (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**

Native graph processing is a graph database execution paradigm in which queries are executed directly over a graph-optimised storage layer using pointer-chasing traversals, rather than being translated into relational JOINs. A system is called "native" only if it satisfies **all three** of the following properties:

1. **Native graph storage** — nodes, edges, and properties are stored in pointer-linked records, not in normalised relational tables.
2. **Native graph processing** — query execution uses *index-free adjacency*, i.e., $\mathcal{O}(1)$ pointer dereference per traversal step.
3. **Native computational model** — algorithms (BFS, DFS, PageRank, etc.) operate directly on the graph structure rather than on relational set algebra.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for the three properties — ½ mark per property, rounded.)*

### Q2. What is index-free adjacency? Why is it called "index-free"? (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

**Index-free adjacency** is a storage technique in which each node record contains a direct pointer to its first edge (and often to its first in-edge). To traverse from a node $u$ to its neighbour, the engine simply dereferences the `first-edge` pointer of $u$ and follows `next-out` pointers — it does **not** consult any B-tree, hash index, or global lookup table.

It is called "index-free" because, although auxiliary indexes for *labels* and *properties* may still exist for fast entry-point lookups, the *traversal* of edges requires no index at all. Hence, the per-hop cost is $\mathcal{O}(1)$.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for mechanism, 1 for the "index-free" terminology.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** Module internal choice means the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A** (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the internal architecture of a native graph database engine. Draw a block diagram showing the layers from query parsing to physical storage, and describe the role of each layer. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast native graph processing with non-native (relational) processing for the query "find all friends of friends of Alice up to 3 hops deep." Show the complexity of both approaches and discuss the practical implications. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

A native graph database engine has **five logical layers**, top to bottom:

1. **Client / API Layer** — Accepts queries in Cypher, GQL, or SPARQL via Bolt/GraphQL/HTTP.
2. **Parser & Rewriter** — Performs lexical, syntactic, and semantic analysis; produces an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**.
3. **Query Planner** — A cost-based optimiser that selects traversal order, index usage, and join strategies (within the native iterator model).
4. **Execution Engine** — The pull-based **iterator pipeline** that lazily processes records. Key operators: `NodeByLabelScan`, `Expand`, `VarExpand`, `Filter`, `Project`.
5. **Storage Layer** — Native graph store with **node files**, **edge files**, **property files**, and **label/property indexes** (used only at entry points).

*(7 marks: 1 per layer + 2 for a clean labelled diagram. Refer to the Mermaid block in SECTION 4.1.)*

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Query:** Find all friends of friends of Alice, up to depth 3.

**Cypher form:**

```
MATCH (a:User {name:'Alice'})-[:FRIEND*1..3]->(v:User) RETURN DISTINCT v
```

**Relational implementation:**

Translates to **3 nested-loop JOINs** over the `users` and `friends` tables.

$$
T_{\text{rel}} = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert^2) \text{ (worst case)}
$$

Each intermediate join is materialised, hashed, or re-scanned, with high constant cost.

**Native implementation:**

Translates to a single `VarExpand(depth=1..3)` iterator following adjacency pointers.

$$
T_{\text{native}} = \mathcal{O}(k) = \mathcal{O}(3) \text{ per reachable node}
$$

**Practical implications:**

- **Constant factor dominance:** Even though both implementations can be linear in the reachable set, the native one has a 10²–10⁶× smaller constant.
- **Memory:** Native avoids materialising intermediate JOINs.
- **Cache behaviour:** Sequential pointer walks are cache-friendly; hash-join spill is not.

*(7 marks: 2 for query formulation, 2 for relational complexity, 2 for native complexity, 1 for practical comparison.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often claim "native is faster" *only* because of the asymptotic $\mathcal{O}(k)$ vs $\mathcal{O}(n^k)$ argument. **Both** approaches can achieve linear time for *some* operations (e.g., relational hash-join vs BFS). The mark-awarding answer must highlight the **constant-factor** and **materialisation** advantages of native. Omitting this loses 1–2 marks.

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm as it is implemented in a native graph processing engine. Show the step-by-step execution on a small 5-node graph. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** What is PageRank? Derive its update equation and explain how a native graph engine computes it iteratively. Why is PageRank considered a "native" graph algorithm? *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Setup:** Consider the graph $G = (V, E, w)$ with 5 nodes $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ and weighted edges as follows:

- $w(A, B) = 4$, $w(A, C) = 2$
- $w(B, C) = 1$, $w(B, D) = 5$
- $w(C, B) = 1$, $w(C, D) = 8$, $w(C, E) = 10$
- $w(D, E) = 2$, $w(E, D) = 1$

**Step 1 — Initialisation:** $d(A) = 0$, all others $\infty$. Priority queue: $\{A:0, B:\infty, C:\infty, D:\infty, E:\infty\}$.

**Step 2 — Extract A (min=0):** Relax $A \to B$: $d(B) = 4$. Relax $A \to C$: $d(C) = 2$.

**Step 3 — Extract C (min=2):** Relax $C \to B$: $d(B) = \min(4, 2+1) = 3$. Relax $C \to D$: $d(D) = 10$. Relax $C \to E$: $d(E) = 12$.

**Step 4 — Extract B (min=3):** Relax $B \to D$: $d(D) = \min(10, 3+5) = 8$.

**Step 5 — Extract D (min=8):** Relax $D \to E$: $d(E) = \min(12, 8+2) = 10$.

**Step 6 — Extract E (min=10):** No further improvements.

**Final distances from A:** $d(A)=0, d(B)=3, d(C)=2, d(D)=8, d(E)=10$.

**Native implementation note:** Each edge relaxation (Step 3, 4, 5) is a **single pointer dereference** of the form `C.next-out`, eliminating any global edge lookup.

*(7 marks: 1 for graph setup, 1 per major step × 4 = 4, 1 for final result, 1 for native implementation note.)*

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Definition:** PageRank is a link-analysis algorithm that assigns a numerical "importance" score to every node in a graph based on the structure of incoming edges. It is the algorithm that originally powered Google's web search.

**Derivation of the update equation:**

Consider a random surfer who, at each step, either:
- With probability $d$ (the **damping factor**, typically $d = 0.85$), follows a random outgoing edge from the current node.
- With probability $1 - d$, "teleports" to a uniformly random node in the graph.

Let $PR(v)$ be the stationary probability of visiting $v$. The expected number of visits to $v$ via its in-neighbours is:

$$
\text{Contribution from neighbours} = d \sum_{u \in M(v)} \frac{PR(u)}{L(u)}
$$

where $M(v)$ is the set of nodes with an edge into $v$, and $L(u)$ is the out-degree of $u$. Adding the teleport term, the full update equation is:

$$
PR(v) = \frac{1 - d}{\vert V \vert} + d \sum_{u \in M(v)} \frac{PR(u)}{L(u)}
$$

**Iterative native computation:**

1. **Initialisation:** $PR_0(v) = 1 / \vert V \vert$ for all $v$.
2. **Iteration $t$:** For each vertex $v$, scan its **incoming** edges (via `first-in` pointer), sum the contributions, and update.
3. **Convergence:** Repeat until $\max_v \vert PR_{t+1}(v) - PR_t(v) \vert < \epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$).

**Why PageRank is "native":**

- Each iteration touches each edge **once**, in $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert)$ time, using **in-adjacency pointer walks** — no global table scan.
- The algorithm is **embarrassingly parallel** at the edge level, which native engines exploit via SIMD and lock-free data structures.

*(7 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for derivation, 2 for iterative algorithm, 2 for native justification.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** A common mistake is to **omit the teleport term** $(1 - d)/\vert V \vert$ in the PageRank formula. Without it, the iterative computation diverges for graphs that contain **dangling nodes** (nodes with no outgoing edges) or **disconnected components**. Examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for this omission. Always state the full equation with both terms.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:** In Dijkstra's algorithm, students often forget that the priority queue key is `d(v)`, not `v` itself. Always write the queue state explicitly (e.g., "Queue: {A:0, B:3, C:2, D:8, E:10}") at every step. Omitting queue states loses 1–2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Native Graph Processing**

- ☐ **"Native" requires all three** of: storage + processing + computational model. Partial = non-native.
- ☐ **Index-free adjacency** = $\mathcal{O}(1)$ pointer chase per edge; no global lookup during traversal.
- ☐ **k-hop traversal** = $\mathcal{O}(k)$ in native, vs $\mathcal{O}(n^{k-1})$ in worst-case relational.
- ☐ **BFS** = unweighted shortest path; $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.
- ☐ **DFS** = reachability, cycle detection, topological sort; $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.
- ☐ **Dijkstra** = weighted shortest path; $\mathcal{O}((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ with binary heap.
- ☐ **PageRank** = $PR(v) = \frac{1-d}{\vert V \vert} + d \sum_{u \in M(v)} \frac{PR(u)}{L(u)}$; **always include the teleport term**.
- ☐ **Iterator pipeline** = pull-based, lazy, set-oriented execution model.
- ☐ **Entry-point indexes** (label, property) exist, but are *not* used during traversal.
- ☐ **Asymptotic tie-breaking:** When BFS/DFS/hash-join are all $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$, the deciding factor is the **constant factor**, which native engines minimise via cache-friendly pointer walks and zero materialisation.
- ☐ **Examples of native engines:** Neo4j, Memgraph, Amazon Neptune (DGL), TigerGraph. **Examples of non-native:** JanusGraph on Cassandra, PostgreSQL + Apache AGE.
- ☐ **Exam mantra:** *"The deeper the traversal, the bigger the native advantage."* Always quantify this with the $k$-hop complexity equation.
- ☐ **Mermaid diagram must show:** Client → Parser → Planner → Iterator Pipeline → Native Store (Node/Edge/Property/Index files).
- ☐ **Damping factor $d = 0.85$** is a standard value, not a magic number; it is the probability that the random surfer follows a link rather than teleports.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of "Native" Graph Processing

### Pillar 1 — Native Graph Storage

- Each **node** is stored as a fixed-size record containing: ID, label list, first-edge-pointer, first-property-pointer.
- Each **edge** is stored as a fixed-size record containing: source-ID, target-ID, type, next-out-pointer, next-in-pointer, first-property-pointer.
- This layout enables **cache-friendly sequential scans** along adjacency lists.

### Pillar 2 — Native Graph Processing (Index-Free Adjacency)

- Given a node $v$, all its outgoing edges are reachable by following the `next-out` pointers starting at $v.\text{first-edge}$.
- **No global edge index** is consulted during traversal. This eliminates the B-tree / hash-lookup cost that dominates relational engines.

### Pillar 3 — Native Computational Model

- Query plans are **algebraic traversals** (e.g., Cypher's `MATCH (a)-[:KNOWS*1..3]->(b)`), compiled into an **iterator pipeline**.
- Each iterator pulls one node/edge at a time from its predecessor, enabling **pipelined, set-oriented** execution without materialising intermediate JOINs.

## 2.2 Traversal Algorithms — The Foundation of Native Processing

Native graph databases implement a standard set of **graph algorithms** as built-in operators. The KTU syllabus emphasises the following four:

### A. Breadth-First Search (BFS)

Used for: shortest path in *unweighted* graphs, level-based community detection.

$$d(v) = \min_{u \in N(v)} \left[ d(u) + 1 \right]$$

### B. Depth-First Search (DFS)

Used for: cycle detection, topological sort, reachability.

$$T_{\text{DFS}}(G) = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$$

### C. Dijkstra's Shortest Path (weighted)

Used for: weighted shortest path, routing.

$$d(v) = \min_{(u,v) \in E} \left[ d(u) + w(u,v) \right]$$

### D. PageRank (iterative, native analytics)

Used for: node importance, recommendation, centrality.

$$PR(v) = \frac{1 - d}{\vert V \vert} + d \sum_{u \in M(v)} \frac{PR(u)}{L(u)}$$

where $d \approx 0.85$ is the **damping factor** and $M(v)$ is the set of in-neighbours of $v$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Complexity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Index-free adjacency hop | $T_{\text{hop}} = \mathcal{O}(1)$ | O(1) | Per-edge traversal cost |
| 2 | k-hop traversal | $T_{\text{traverse}}(k) = \mathcal{O}(k)$ | O(k) | Pattern matching, friends-of-friends |
| 3 | Full BFS | $T = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Linear | Shortest path, connected components |
| 4 | Full DFS | $T = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Linear | Cycle detection, topological order |
| 5 | Dijkstra (binary heap) | $T = \mathcal{O}((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ | Quasi-linear | Weighted shortest path |
| 6 | Bellman–Ford | $T = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ | Cubic-ish | Negative weights, single source |
| 7 | PageRank (per iteration) | $T = \mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert)$ | Linear | Node importance ranking |
| 8 | Relational JOIN (nested loop) | $T = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ per hop | Quadratic+ | Comparative baseline |
| 9 | Relational JOIN (hash) | $T = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Linear | Per hop, but with hashing overhead |
| 10 | Native memory footprint | $M \approx 64 \cdot \vert V \vert + 48 \cdot \vert E \vert$ bytes | — | Capacity planning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Trap:** BFS, DFS, and a single relational hash-join all share the *same* asymptotic complexity $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$. Native processing is faster **in practice** because the **constant factor** $c_{\text{native}} \ll c_{\text{join}}$ (no hash table build, no intermediate result materialisation, cache-friendly pointer walks).

## 2.4 Why Native Graph Processing Matters — Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Why Native Wins |
|---|---|---|
| Social networks | Friend-of-friend, influence spread | Deep traversals (depth 4–6) over billions of edges |
| Fraud detection | Multi-hop ring detection | Path queries of length 5–8, sub-second SLA |
| Knowledge graphs | SPARQL/Cypher pattern matching | Variable-length path patterns |
| Network/IT ops | Root-cause analysis across service graph | Real-time incident triage |
| Recommendation | Collaborative filtering via graph walks | Iterative PageRank-like computations |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From "k-hop JOIN" to "k-hop Native Traversal"

**Goal:** Show mathematically why native graph processing is asymptotically and practically superior to relational JOIN-based processing for traversal queries.

### Step 1 — Formulate the k-hop Traversal Query

Consider a social network where we want to find all users reachable from user $u$ within **exactly** $k$ hops. The Cypher pattern is:

```
MATCH (u:User {name:'Alice'})-[:FRIEND*k]->(v:User) RETURN v
```

### Step 2 — Relational Translation (Non-Native)

In a relational schema with tables `users(id, name)` and `friends(user_id, friend_id)`, this becomes **k nested-loop JOINs**:

$$
T_{\text{rel}}(k) = \prod_{i=1}^{k} \left[ \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert) \right] = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^{k})
$$

Each JOIN scans one full table (in the worst case without indexes).

**Reasoning at each step:** The first JOIN produces $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert)$ intermediate rows. The second JOIN re-scans the friends table once for *each* of those $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert)$ rows, producing $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert^2)$. The pattern continues, yielding $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert^{k-1} \cdot \vert V \vert)$.

### Step 3 — Native Translation (Index-Free Adjacency)

In native processing, the query compiles into an iterator pipeline where each step follows a **single pointer**:

$$
T_{\text{native}}(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} c_{\text{hop}} = k \cdot c_{\text{hop}} = \mathcal{O}(k)
$$

**Reasoning at each step:** Starting at Alice's node record, the engine dereferences `first-edge`, yielding the first neighbour in constant time. From that neighbour, it again dereferences `first-edge`, yielding the second hop. This process is independent of $\vert V \vert$ or $\vert E \vert$.

### Step 4 — Asymptotic Comparison

For $k = 3$ and $\vert V \vert = 10^8$:

$$
T_{\text{rel}}(3) = (10^{8})^{3} = 10^{24} \text{ operations}
$$
$$
T_{\text{native}}(3) = 3 \cdot c_{\text{hop}} \approx 3 \cdot 50 \text{ ns} = 150 \text{ ns}
$$

The native engine is approximately **$10^{16}$ times faster** for this specific query — which is precisely why deep traversals are the *killer use-case* for graph databases.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Dijkstra's Algorithm in Native Graph Processing

We derive the single-source shortest path algorithm as it is typically implemented in a native graph engine's `shortestPath()` operator.

### Step 1 — Initialisation

$$
\forall v \in V : d(v) = \infty, \quad d(s) = 0, \quad Q = V
$$

where $d(v)$ is the tentative distance from source $s$, and $Q$ is a **min-priority queue** keyed by $d(v)$.

### Step 2 — Main Loop (Iterative Relaxation)

At each iteration, extract the vertex $u$ with minimum $d(u)$ from $Q$:

$$
u = \arg\min_{v \in Q} d(v)
$$

### Step 3 — Edge Relaxation

For each outgoing edge $(u, v, w)$ reachable via the **native adjacency pointer** `u.next-out`:

$$
\text{if } d(u) + w < d(v) \text{ then } d(v) \leftarrow d(u) + w
$$

This step is $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per edge in a native engine because the edge $(u, v, w)$ is obtained by pointer chase, not by scanning a global edge table.

### Step 4 — Termination

The algorithm halts when $Q = \emptyset$, i.e., when the shortest path from $s$ to every reachable vertex has been finalised.

### Step 5 — Total Complexity

With a binary heap:

$$
T_{\text{Dijkstra}} = \mathcal{O}\left( (\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \cdot \log \vert V \vert \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is "native":** Each edge relaxation in Step 3 is a single $\mathcal{O}(1)$ pointer dereference. The $\log \vert V \vert$ factor comes **only** from the priority queue, not from edge retrieval — unlike the relational implementation, which would have to perform a B-tree lookup per edge.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Native vs Non-Native

| Dimension | Native Graph Processing | Non-Native (Relational + Graph Layer) |
|---|---|---|
| Storage layout | Pointer-linked fixed-size records | Normalised tables |
| Edge retrieval | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ pointer chase | $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ index seek or full scan |
| k-hop query | $\mathcal{O}(k)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n^{k-1})$ worst case |
| Constant factor | Small (1–2 cache misses/hop) | Large (hash build + materialisation) |
| Memory locality | High (sequential pointer walk) | Low (random B-tree access) |
| Hot-path algorithms | BFS, DFS, Dijkstra, PageRank, A* | Implemented in SQL with recursive CTEs |
| Examples | Neo4j, Memgraph, Amazon Neptune (DGL) | PostgreSQL + Apache AGE, JanusGraph on Cassandra |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Native Graph Processing — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **internal architecture** of a native graph database engine and the data flow for a typical traversal query.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client[Client Application] --> parser[Query Parser and Rewriter]
    parser --> planner[Cost-Based Query Planner]
    planner --> exec[Native Execution Engine]
    exec --> it1[Iterator GetNode by Index]
    exec --> it2[Iterator Expand Outgoing Edges]
    exec --> it3[Iterator Filter by Label or Property]
    exec --> it4[Iterator Variable-Length Expand k Hops]
    it1 --> store[(Native Graph Store)]
    it2 --> store
    it3 --> store
    it4 --> store
    store --> nodes[Node File Linked List]
    store --> edges[Edge File Linked List]
    store --> props[Property Files]
    store --> idx[Label and Property Indexes]
    exec --> result[Result Stream to Client]
```

### Architecture Explanation (Block-by-Block)

1. **Client Application** — Submits a Cypher/SPARQL/GQL query.
2. **Query Parser & Rewriter** — Converts textual query into a logical operator tree.
3. **Cost-Based Query Planner** — Chooses the optimal execution order (e.g., start traversal from the most selective label).
4. **Native Execution Engine** — The heart of the system. It implements a **pull-based iterator pipeline**, where each operator lazily requests one record at a time from its predecessor.
5. **Iterators** — Specialised operators: `GetNodeByIndex`, `Expand(relationship-type)`, `Filter`, `VarExpand(k)`.
6. **Native Graph Store** — The physical storage layer.
7. **Node / Edge / Property Files** — The raw disk-resident graph data.
8. **Label & Property Indexes** — Auxiliary structures for *entry-point* lookups (not used during traversal).

## 4.2 Traversal Flow — Index-Free Adjacency in Action

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Node A first-edge pointer] --> B[Edge A-B pointer]
    B --> C[Node B first-edge pointer]
    C --> D[Edge B-C pointer]
    D --> E[Node C first-edge pointer]
    E --> F[Edge C-D pointer]
    F --> G[Node D target reached]
    style A fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#577590,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

### Diagram Explanation

- The traversal begins at **Node A** by reading its `first-edge` pointer.
- Each subsequent step is a **single pointer dereference** (one CPU cache line read in the best case).
- Notice that **no global lookup table is consulted** during the traversal — this is the essence of *index-free adjacency*.

## 4.3 Iterator Pipeline for MATCH a- KNOWS star 1 to 3 - b

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1[AllStart] --> n2[NodeByLabelScan Person]
    n2 --> n3[Expand KNOWS]
    n3 --> n4[VarLengthExpand depth 1 to 3]
    n4 --> n5[NodeByLabelScan Person]
    n5 --> n6[Result]
    style n1 fill:#e0e0e0
    style n2 fill:#bbdefb
    style n3 fill:#90caf9
    style n4 fill:#64b5f6
    style n5 fill:#bbdefb
    style n6 fill:#a5d6a7
```

### Diagram Explanation

Each rounded rectangle is an **iterator (operator)** in the pipeline. Data flows lazily from left to right: `n1` generates a single empty row, `n2` materialises Person nodes (using the label index, *not* during traversal), `n3` performs the first-hop expansion via pointer chase, `n4` repeats this for 1 to 3 hops, `n5` filters the targets, and `n6` returns results to the client.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define "native graph processing" and list the three properties a system must satisfy to be called native. (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**

Native graph processing is a graph database execution paradigm in which queries are executed directly over a graph-optimised storage layer using pointer-chasing traversals, rather than being translated into relational JOINs. A system is called "native" only if it satisfies **all three** of the following properties:

1. **Native graph storage** — nodes, edges, and properties are stored in pointer-linked records, not in normalised relational tables.
2. **Native graph processing** — query execution uses *index-free adjacency*, i.e., $\mathcal{O}(1)$ pointer dereference per traversal step.
3. **Native computational model** — algorithms (BFS, DFS, PageRank, etc.) operate directly on the graph structure rather than on relational set algebra.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for the three properties — ½ mark per property, rounded.)*

### Q2. What is index-free adjacency? Why is it called "index-free"? (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

**Index-free adjacency** is a storage technique in which each node record contains a direct pointer to its first edge (and often to its first in-edge). To traverse from a node $u$ to its neighbour, the engine simply dereferences the `first-edge` pointer of $u$ and follows `next-out` pointers — it does **not** consult any B-tree, hash index, or global lookup table.

It is called "index-free" because, although auxiliary indexes for *labels* and *properties* may still exist for fast entry-point lookups, the *traversal* of edges requires no index at all. Hence, the per-hop cost is $\mathcal{O}(1)$.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for mechanism, 1 for the "index-free" terminology.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** Module internal choice means the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A** (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the internal architecture of a native graph database engine. Draw a block diagram showing the layers from query parsing to physical storage, and describe the role of each layer. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast native graph processing with non-native (relational) processing for the query "find all friends of friends of Alice up to 3 hops deep." Show the complexity of both approaches and discuss the practical implications. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

A native graph database engine has **five logical layers**, top to bottom:

1. **Client / API Layer** — Accepts queries in Cypher, GQL, or SPARQL via Bolt/GraphQL/HTTP.
2. **Parser & Rewriter** — Performs lexical, syntactic, and semantic analysis; produces an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**.
3. **Query Planner** — A cost-based optimiser that selects traversal order, index usage, and join strategies (within the native iterator model).
4. **Execution Engine** — The pull-based **iterator pipeline** that lazily processes records. Key operators: `NodeByLabelScan`, `Expand`, `VarExpand`, `Filter`, `Project`.
5. **Storage Layer** — Native graph store with **node files**, **edge files**, **property files**, and **label/property indexes** (used only at entry points).

*(7 marks: 1 per layer + 2 for a clean labelled diagram. Refer to the Mermaid block in SECTION 4.1.)*

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Query:** Find all friends of friends of Alice, up to depth 3.

**Cypher form:**

```
MATCH (a:User {name:'Alice'})-[:FRIEND*1..3]->(v:User) RETURN DISTINCT v
```

**Relational implementation:**

Translates to **3 nested-loop JOINs** over the `users` and `friends` tables.

$$
T_{\text{rel}} = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert^2) \text{ (worst case)}
$$

Each intermediate join is materialised, hashed, or re-scanned, with high constant cost.

**Native implementation:**

Translates to a single `VarExpand(depth=1..3)` iterator following adjacency pointers.

$$
T_{\text{native}} = \mathcal{O}(k) = \mathcal{O}(3) \text{ per reachable node}
$$

**Practical implications:**

- **Constant factor dominance:** Even though both implementations can be linear in the reachable set, the native one has a 10²–10⁶× smaller constant.
- **Memory:** Native avoids materialising intermediate JOINs.
- **Cache behaviour:** Sequential pointer walks are cache-friendly; hash-join spill is not.

*(7 marks: 2 for query formulation, 2 for relational complexity, 2 for native complexity, 1 for practical comparison.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often claim "native is faster" *only* because of the asymptotic $\mathcal{O}(k)$ vs $\mathcal{O}(n^k)$ argument. **Both** approaches can achieve linear time for *some* operations (e.g., relational hash-join vs BFS). The mark-awarding answer must highlight the **constant-factor** and **materialisation** advantages of native. Omitting this loses 1–2 marks.

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm as it is implemented in a native graph processing engine. Show the step-by-step execution on a small 5-node graph. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** What is PageRank? Derive its update equation and explain how a native graph engine computes it iteratively. Why is PageRank considered a "native" graph algorithm? *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Setup:** Consider the graph $G = (V, E, w)$ with 5 nodes $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ and weighted edges as follows:

- $w(A, B) = 4$, $w(A, C) = 2$
- $w(B, C) = 1$, $w(B, D) = 5$
- $w(C, B) = 1$, $w(C, D) = 8$, $w(C, E) = 10$
- $w(D, E) = 2$, $w(E, D) = 1$

**Step 1 — Initialisation:** $d(A) = 0$, all others $\infty$. Priority queue: $\{A:0, B:\infty, C:\infty, D:\infty, E:\infty\}$.

**Step 2 — Extract A (min=0):** Relax $A \to B$: $d(B) = 4$. Relax $A \to C$: $d(C) = 2$.

**Step 3 — Extract C (min=2):** Relax $C \to B$: $d(B) = \min(4, 2+1) = 3$. Relax $C \to D$: $d(D) = 10$. Relax $C \to E$: $d(E) = 12$.

**Step 4 — Extract B (min=3):** Relax $B \to D$: $d(D) = \min(10, 3+5) = 8$.

**Step 5 — Extract D (min=8):** Relax $D \to E$: $d(E) = \min(12, 8+2) = 10$.

**Step 6 — Extract E (min=10):** No further improvements.

**Final distances from A:** $d(A)=0, d(B)=3, d(C)=2, d(D)=8, d(E)=10$.

**Native implementation note:** Each edge relaxation (Step 3, 4, 5) is a **single pointer dereference** of the form `C.next-out`, eliminating any global edge lookup.

*(7 marks: 1 for graph setup, 1 per major step × 4 = 4, 1 for final result, 1 for native implementation note.)*

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Definition:** PageRank is a link-analysis algorithm that assigns a numerical "importance" score to every node in a graph based on the structure of incoming edges. It is the algorithm that originally powered Google's web search.

**Derivation of the update equation:**

Consider a random surfer who, at each step, either:
- With probability $d$ (the **damping factor**, typically $d = 0.85$), follows a random outgoing edge from the current node.
- With probability $1 - d$, "teleports" to a uniformly random node in the graph.

Let $PR(v)$ be the stationary probability of visiting $v$. The expected number of visits to $v$ via its in-neighbours is:

$$
\text{Contribution from neighbours} = d \sum_{u \in M(v)} \frac{PR(u)}{L(u)}
$$

where $M(v)$ is the set of nodes with an edge into $v$, and $L(u)$ is the out-degree of $u$. Adding the teleport term, the full update equation is:

$$
PR(v) = \frac{1 - d}{\vert V \vert} + d \sum_{u \in M(v)} \frac{PR(u)}{L(u)}
$$

**Iterative native computation:**

1. **Initialisation:** $PR_0(v) = 1 / \vert V \vert$ for all $v$.
2. **Iteration $t$:** For each vertex $v$, scan its **incoming** edges (via `first-in` pointer), sum the contributions, and update.
3. **Convergence:** Repeat until $\max_v \vert PR_{t+1}(v) - PR_t(v) \vert < \epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$).

**Why PageRank is "native":**

- Each iteration touches each edge **once**, in $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert)$ time, using **in-adjacency pointer walks** — no global table scan.
- The algorithm is **embarrassingly parallel** at the edge level, which native engines exploit via SIMD and lock-free data structures.

*(7 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for derivation, 2 for iterative algorithm, 2 for native justification.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** A common mistake is to **omit the teleport term** $(1 - d)/\vert V \vert$ in the PageRank formula. Without it, the iterative computation diverges for graphs that contain **dangling nodes** (nodes with no outgoing edges) or **disconnected components**. Examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for this omission. Always state the full equation with both terms.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:** In Dijkstra's algorithm, students often forget that the priority queue key is `d(v)`, not `v` itself. Always write the queue state explicitly (e.g., "Queue: {A:0, B:3, C:2, D:8, E:10}") at every step. Omitting queue states loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Native Graph Processing**

- ☐ **"Native" requires all three** of: storage + processing + computational model. Partial = non-native.
- ☐ **Index-free adjacency** = $\mathcal{O}(1)$ pointer chase per edge; no global lookup during traversal.
- ☐ **k-hop traversal** = $\mathcal{O}(k)$ in native, vs $\mathcal{O}(n^{k-1})$ in worst-case relational.
- ☐ **BFS** = unweighted shortest path; $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.
- ☐ **DFS** = reachability, cycle detection, topological sort; $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.
- ☐ **Dijkstra** = weighted shortest path; $\mathcal{O}((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ with binary heap.
- ☐ **PageRank** = $PR(v) = \frac{1-d}{\vert V \vert} + d \sum_{u \in M(v)} \frac{PR(u)}{L(u)}$; **always include the teleport term**.
- ☐ **Iterator pipeline** = pull-based, lazy, set-oriented execution model.
- ☐ **Entry-point indexes** (label, property) exist, but are *not* used during traversal.
- ☐ **Asymptotic tie-breaking:** When BFS/DFS/hash-join are all $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$, the deciding factor is the **constant factor**, which native engines minimise via cache-friendly pointer walks and zero materialisation.
- ☐ **Examples of native engines:** Neo4j, Memgraph, Amazon Neptune (DGL), TigerGraph. **Examples of non-native:** JanusGraph on Cassandra, PostgreSQL + Apache AGE.
- ☐ **Exam mantra:** *"The deeper the traversal, the bigger the native advantage."* Always quantify this with the $k$-hop complexity equation.
- ☐ **Mermaid diagram must show:** Client → Parser → Planner → Iterator Pipeline → Native Store (Node/Edge/Property/Index files).
- ☐ **Damping factor $d = 0.85$** is a standard value, not a magic number; it is the probability that the random surfer follows a link rather than teleports.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Native Graph Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Native Graph Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Native Graph Storage (Native Graph DBMS):** A graph database management system whose **internal, physical storage layer** is purpose-built and optimized *exclusively* for storing and traversing graph structures (nodes, relationships, and properties). The data resides on disk (and in memory) in formats that are inherently graph-shaped, with **direct physical links** (pointers / disk addresses) connecting nodes to their adjacent relationships.

This is in strict contrast to a **non-native graph database**, which simply bolts a graph-processing layer on top of a foreign engine (e.g., a relational DB, a document store, or a key-value store). A native graph DBMS implements the graph model at the **lowest level of its architectural stack**, meaning the storage engine "thinks" in nodes and relationships — not in tables, documents, or blobs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A graph database is considered *native* only if (1) it stores graph data in a graph-aware format, and (2) it processes graph traversals using **index-free adjacency** with direct, in-memory pointer-based references. If either of these conditions is missing, the system is classified as a non-native graph layer.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "City with Direct Roads" Model

Imagine a city with **10,000 landmarks** (restaurants, hospitals, offices).

| Storage Type | Real-World Analogy | Lookup Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Native Graph Storage** | Every landmark has **direct, physical roads** wired to its immediate neighbors. To find the hospital next door, you simply walk the road. | **O(1)** direct physical pointer hop |
| **Non-Native (e.g., on RDBMS)** | Landmarks are listed in a giant **telephone directory**. To find the hospital, you first look up the index, then search rows, then JOIN three tables. | **O(log n)** + multiple joins |

In a native graph database, a node does not "search" for its neighbors — it *physically points* to them. The relationship is the **first-class citizen** of storage.

### 1.3 The "Property Graph" Foundation

A native graph storage engine is engineered around the **Property Graph Model**, where:

- **Nodes** carry key–value **properties** (labels, attributes).
- **Relationships** are directed, named, and also carry properties.
- **Labels** categorize nodes (e.g., `:Person`, `:Movie`).
- Every entity has a unique internal identifier (often a logical ID, mapped to a **physical disk address**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Property Count Metric:** Modern native graph systems (Neo4j 5.x, Memgraph, TigerGraph) routinely support graphs with **billions of nodes** and **tens of billions of relationships** on commodity hardware, precisely because the storage layer is purpose-built.

---

### 1.4 The Two Defining Hallmarks of Native Graph Storage

1. **Index-Free Adjacency (IFA):** Every node physically stores direct disk/offset references to its first-hop neighbors. This is the **single most important** characteristic. No global index is consulted during traversal.
2. **Graph-Native File Layouts:** On-disk structures (record files, fixed-size pages) are laid out in a way that minimizes pointer-chasing latency. Nodes and relationships are co-located in pages, and memory-mapped I/O is heavily used.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students confuse "graph database" with "native graph database". MySQL with a graph layer (e.g., Graph Engine on MySQL) is **not native**. It is a non-native graph layer because MySQL still stores rows in B+ trees.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Components of Native Graph Storage

A canonical native graph DBMS decomposes its on-disk store into **three logically distinct file groups**:

#### A. Node Store
- Fixed-size node records (typically **9 bytes** for in/out relationship pointers + **1 byte** for label + variable-length property references).
- Each node record contains:
  - A `labelId`
  - Pointer to the **first** outgoing relationship (out-relationship chain head)
  - Pointer to the **first** incoming relationship (in-relationship chain head)
  - Pointer to the **first** property in the property chain
- Nodes are addressed by a `nodeId` that is translated to a file offset via a small `IdGenerator` mapping.

#### B. Relationship Store
- Fixed-size relationship records:
  - `startNodeId` (4 bytes) + `endNodeId` (4 bytes)
  - Pointer to the **next** relationship in the source node's chain
  - Pointer to the **next** relationship in the target node's chain
  - `typeId` + `propertyPointer`
- This is the **core of index-free adjacency** — relationships form doubly-linked lists hanging off their endpoint nodes.

#### C. Property Store
- A separate file (or set of files) holding variable-length key–value pairs.
- Each node/relationship record points to a **chain of property records** for that entity.
- Properties are stored in a **key–value layout**, not in tables.

### 2.2 Index-Free Adjacency (IFA) — Deep Dive

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why IFA matters:** Traversals in graph workloads are *locality-heavy*. A query like *"find all friends-of-friends within 3 hops"* only needs the immediate neighborhood of visited nodes. IFA turns each hop into a **constant-time pointer dereference**, not a search.

**How IFA works internally:**

1. A traversal engine visits Node A. It reads Node A's record from the node store.
2. The node record contains the **disk offset** of A's first outgoing relationship.
3. The engine jumps *directly* to that relationship record.
4. The relationship record contains the **disk offset** of the next relationship in A's chain (linked-list traversal) AND the offset of the target node.
5. The engine jumps *directly* to the target node.
6. **No global index is touched.** No `MATCH` on a label is required during pure traversal.

> [!NOTE]
> This is fundamentally different from RDBMS joins, where the engine must:
> - Look up the indexed key (O(log n) via B+ tree traversal)
> - Read a data page
> - Build a hash or merge join
> - Repeat for every join

### 2.3 Physical Storage Optimizations

| Optimization | Description | Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory-Mapped I/O (mmap)** | The store files are mapped into virtual address space. Pointer dereferences become memory reads. | Eliminates `read()` system call overhead |
| **Fixed-Size Records** | Node and relationship records are fixed-size, so `nodeId` → file offset is a simple multiply. | O(1) record lookup, no fragmentation |
| **Page-Level Locality** | Relationships of a node are often stored on the same page as the node. | Reduces page faults during traversal |
| **Pointer Doubling / Heavyweight Pointer Caching** | Recently followed pointers are kept in an in-memory cache. | Repeated traversals become near-zero-cost |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **verified, mathematically rigorous**, and form the core computation backbone for traversal and storage analysis in graph databases.

$$
T_{\text{traverse}}(k) \;=\; k \cdot C_{\text{hop}}
$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{traverse}}(k)$ = total time to traverse $k$ hops
- $k$ = number of hops (depth of traversal)
- $C_{\text{hop}}$ = cost of a single pointer dereference (≈ memory read latency, **~100 ns** for RAM)

$$
T_{\text{lookup}}^{\text{IFA}} \;=\; O(1) \quad\text{(per hop, with IFA)}
$$

$$
T_{\text{lookup}}^{\text{index}} \;=\; O(\log n) \quad\text{(per hop, with B+ tree index, no IFA)}
$$

$$
\text{Speedup}_{\text{IFA}} \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{lookup}}^{\text{index}}}{T_{\text{lookup}}^{\text{IFA}}} \;=\; \frac{\log_2 n}{1}
$$

For a typical social network with $n = 10^9$ users:

$$
\text{Speedup}_{\text{IFA}} \;=\; \log_2(10^9) \;\approx\; 29.9\times
$$

**Storage sizing formulas:**

$$
S_{\text{node}} \;=\; 15 \text{ bytes} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \text{size}(prop_i)
$$

$$
S_{\text{rel}} \;=\; 33 \text{ bytes} + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \text{size}(relprop_j)
$$

$$
S_{\text{total}} \;=\; N \cdot \bar{S}_{\text{node}} \;+\; M \cdot \bar{S}_{\text{rel}} \;+\; S_{\text{property}}
$$

Where:
- $N$ = number of nodes, $M$ = number of relationships
- $p$ = average number of properties per node, $q$ = average properties per relationship
- $\bar{S}_{\text{node}}$, $\bar{S}_{\text{rel}}$ = average sizes

**Traversal time for BFS / DFS on the entire graph:**

$$
T_{\text{BFS/DFS}} \;=\; O(N + M) \quad\text{(graph-theoretical lower bound)}
$$

**Cache Miss Penalty (critical for native storage):**

$$
T_{\text{miss}} \;=\; T_{\text{latency}}^{\text{RAM}} + T_{\text{latency}}^{\text{disk}} \cdot \text{PF}
$$

Where $\text{PF}$ = page-fault probability. A well-designed native store keeps $\text{PF} \ll 0.01$.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember this for exams:** The *defining* complexity of native graph traversal is **O(1) per hop**, not O(log n). This is the heart of the "native" vs "non-native" debate.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Native graph storage is the backbone of:
- **Social Networks** (Facebook's TAO, LinkedIn's knowledge graph) — billions of edges, multi-hop friend queries.
- **Fraud Detection** (PayPal, Mastercard) — sub-100ms queries traversing 5–8 hops of transaction chains.
- **Recommendation Engines** (Netflix, Uber Eats) — *people who viewed X also viewed Y* type traversals.
- **Knowledge Graphs** (Google Knowledge Graph, Wikidata) — semantic reasoning over heterogeneous entities.
- **Network/IT Operations** (Cisco, Verizon) — dependency-graph queries for root-cause analysis.
- **Life Sciences** (Genomics, drug discovery) — protein–protein interaction networks.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Index-Free Adjacency is O(1) per Hop

**Step 1 — State the goal.**
We want to derive the asymptotic time to follow one edge from a node to its neighbor in a native graph store.

**Step 2 — Define the on-disk addressing scheme.**
In a native graph DBMS, every node is assigned a logical `nodeId` of fixed length (typically 8 bytes, but encoded as a long). The physical record is stored in the node store file at a **fixed offset**:

$$
\text{offset}(v) \;=\; \text{base}_{\text{file}} \;+\; v.\text{nodeId} \cdot R_{\text{node}}
$$

Here $R_{\text{node}}$ is the fixed size of a node record.

**Step 3 — Observe that this is arithmetic, not search.**
Computing the offset requires exactly one multiplication and one addition. Both are **O(1)** operations on any modern CPU.

**Step 4 — Read the relationship pointer.**
Once the node record is fetched, the engine reads two embedded fields:
- `firstOutRelPtr` — the offset of the first outgoing relationship
- `firstInRelPtr` — the offset of the first incoming relationship

These are **direct, absolute disk offsets** — no search is required to interpret them.

**Step 5 — Jump to the relationship record.**
The engine issues a memory read (or `mmap` page access) at `firstOutRelPtr`. Cost:

$$
T_{\text{step5}} \;=\; T_{\text{RAM}} \quad\text{if page is in cache}
$$
$$
T_{\text{step5}} \;=\; T_{\text{disk}} \quad\text{if page fault occurs}
$$

In the amortized case (working set fits in memory), this is **O(1)**.

**Step 6 — Jump to the target node.**
The relationship record contains the `endNodeId`. The engine computes:

$$
\text{offset}(w) \;=\; \text{base}_{\text{file}} \;+\; w.\text{nodeId} \cdot R_{\text{node}}
$$

**Step 7 — Combine the per-step costs.**

$$
T_{\text{hop}} \;=\; T_{\text{offset}}(v) \;+\; T_{\text{RAM}}(\text{read rel}) \;+\; T_{\text{offset}}(w)
$$
$$
\Rightarrow\quad T_{\text{hop}} \;=\; c_1 + c_2 + c_3 \;=\; \Theta(1)
$$

**Step 8 — Conclude.**
Each hop in a native graph database is a **constant-time pointer dereference**, hence:

$$
T_{\text{traverse}}(k) \;=\; k \cdot T_{\text{hop}} \;=\; \Theta(k)
$$

This is **linear in the number of hops**, independent of the graph's total size $N$ or $M$. This is the **theoretical foundation of native graph performance**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Quantifying the IFA Speedup over Indexed Lookups

**Step 1 — Define both lookup mechanisms.**
- **IFA:** $T_{\text{IFA}} = c$ (constant).
- **Indexed lookup** (B+ tree on `nodeId`): $T_{\text{index}} = h \cdot T_{\text{node-compare}}$, where $h$ is the height of the B+ tree.

**Step 2 — Express the B+ tree height.**

$$
h \;=\; \lceil \log_{B}(n) \rceil
$$

Where $B$ is the tree's branching factor (typically $B \approx 100\text{–}200$ for disk-resident B+ trees).

**Step 3 — Compute the per-hop ratio.**

$$
R_{\text{hop}} \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{index}}}{T_{\text{IFA}}} \;=\; \frac{\lceil \log_{B}(n) \rceil \cdot T_{\text{node-compare}}}{c}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the cumulative speedup for a $k$-hop traversal.**

$$
R_{k} \;=\; \left( R_{\text{hop}} \right)^{k} \;=\; \left[ \log_{B}(n) \cdot \frac{T_{\text{node-compare}}}{c} \right]^{k}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute realistic engineering values.**
Let $n = 10^9$, $B = 128$, $T_{\text{node-compare}} \approx 50 \text{ ns}$, $c \approx 100 \text{ ns}$, $k = 3$ (friend-of-friend-of-friend query).

$$
h \;=\; \log_{128}(10^9) \;=\; \frac{\ln(10^9)}{\ln(128)} \;\approx\; \frac{20.72}{4.85} \;\approx\; 4.27
$$

$$
R_{\text{hop}} \;=\; \frac{4.27 \cdot 50 \text{ ns}}{100 \text{ ns}} \;\approx\; 2.14
$$

$$
R_{3} \;=\; (2.14)^{3} \;\approx\; 9.8 \times
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret.**
A 3-hop query is nearly **10× faster** on a native graph store than on an indexed non-native graph layer, holding hardware constant. For deeper traversals ($k = 5, 6, 7$ in fraud detection), the speedup can exceed **40×**.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to compare native and non-native storage, always derive the speedup using the log of the dataset size, not just a vague "native is faster" claim.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Native vs Non-Native Graph Storage

| Dimension | Native Graph Storage | Non-Native (Graph Layer on RDBMS) | Non-Native (Graph Layer on NoSQL) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Storage Format** | Node & relationship records with embedded pointers | Rows in tables; edges in join tables | Documents / wide-column rows |
| **Per-Hop Cost** | **O(1)** (IFA) | **O(log n)** + JOIN overhead | **O(log n)** or scan |
| **Schema** | Schema-optional (property graph) | Rigid SQL schema | Schema-optional |
| **JOIN Performance** | Pointer-based, O(1) | Hash/merge join, O(n log n) | Application-side join |
| **Multi-Hop Queries** | Excellent | Poor (cartesian explosion) | Poor |
| **Aggregation Queries** | Weaker | Excellent (SQL aggregates) | Moderate |
| **ACID Transactions** | Native in most engines | Native (inherited) | Limited / tunable |
| **Examples** | **Neo4j, Memgraph, TigerGraph** | Graph Engine on MySQL, AgensGraph | OrientDB, ArangoDB (hybrid) |
| **Best Use Case** | Deep, multi-hop traversals | Heavy aggregation, reporting | Mixed workloads |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** A system is *truly native* only if it is **"native on storage"** *and* **"native on processing"**. Having only one of the two is not enough.
