## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Native Graph Storage — High-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram shows the high-level architecture of a native graph database storage engine, illustrating how nodes, relationships, and properties are stored, linked, and traversed.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef storeNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2c4a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef engineNode fill:#7f6000,stroke:#4d3a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef clientNode fill:#385723,stroke:#1f3a14,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef cacheNode fill:#7b1a1a,stroke:#3a0d0d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    client[Client Application - Cypher Query]:::clientNode
    parser[Cypher Parser and Planner]:::engineNode
    executor[Query Execution Engine]:::engineNode
    cache[In-Memory Pointer Cache]:::cacheNode

    nstore[(Node Store File)]:::storeNode
    rstore[(Relationship Store File)]:::storeNode
    pstore[(Property Store File)]:::storeNode
    idmap[ID Generator and NodeID to Offset Mapper]:::storeNode

    client --> parser
    parser --> executor
    executor --> cache
    cache --> nstore
    cache --> rstore
    cache --> pstore
    executor --> idmap
    idmap --> nstore
    nstore -.out-relationship pointer.-> rstore
    rstore -.next relationship and target node.-> nstore
    nstore --> pstore
    rstore --> pstore
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The client submits a Cypher (or openCypher-equivalent) query.
- The parser builds a logical plan; the executor walks the graph using **direct pointer dereferences**.
- The in-memory pointer cache accelerates repeated traversals.
- The **Node Store**, **Relationship Store**, and **Property Store** are three independent, graph-native files.
- The **ID Generator** maps logical `nodeId` values to physical file offsets.

### 4.2 Index-Free Adjacency — Per-Hop Pointer Walk

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef nodeBox fill:#dbe5f1,stroke:#2f5496,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef relBox fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c65911,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    nA[Node A\nOut-Rel Pointer 0xA10\nIn-Rel Pointer 0xA20]:::nodeBox
    r1[Relationship R1\nStart Node A\nEnd Node B\nType KNOWS\nNext Out-Rel 0xA30]:::relBox
    nB[Node B\nOut-Rel Pointer 0xB10]:::nodeBox
    r2[Relationship R2\nStart Node A\nEnd Node C\nType WORKS_AT\nNext Out-Rel NULL]:::relBox
    nC[Node C\nOut-Rel Pointer 0xC10]:::nodeBox

    nA -- offset 0xA10 --> r1
    r1 -- end node B --> nB
    nA -- offset 0xA30 --> r2
    r2 -- end node C --> nC
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Node A holds **direct disk offsets** to its first outgoing and incoming relationships.
- Each relationship contains the **end-node ID** and a pointer to the **next** relationship in the chain.
- This forms a **doubly-linked list** of relationships hanging off each node — the structural embodiment of index-free adjacency.

### 4.3 Storage Layout — File-Level View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef fileBox fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef recordBox fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000

    subgraph NS["NODE STORE FILE - neostore.nodestore.db"]
        direction LR
        nrec1[Node Record 0\nLabelId 5\nFirstOut 0xA10]:::recordBox
        nrec2[Node Record 1\nLabelId 5\nFirstOut 0xA30]:::recordBox
        nrec3[Node Record 2\nLabelId 7\nFirstOut 0xB00]:::recordBox
    end

    subgraph RS["RELATIONSHIP STORE FILE - neostore.relationshipstore.db"]
        direction LR
        rrec1[Rel Record R1\nStart 0 End 1 Type KNOWS]:::recordBox
        rrec2[Rel Record R2\nStart 0 End 2 Type WORKS_AT]:::recordBox
        rrec3[Rel Record R3\nStart 1 End 2 Type FRIEND_OF]:::recordBox
    end

    subgraph PS["PROPERTY STORE FILE - neostore.propertystore.db"]
        direction LR
        prec1[Property Chain 0\nname Alice]:::recordBox
        prec2[Property Chain 0\nage 30]:::recordBox
        prec3[Property Chain 1\nname Bob]:::recordBox
    end

    NS -- pointer --> RS
    NS -- property pointer --> PS
    RS -- property pointer --> PS
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The three files are **physically separate** but **logically linked** via embedded offsets.
- Node records contain pointers *into* the relationship file and *into* the property file.
- Relationship records contain pointers *into* the property file (for edge properties).
- The layout is fully **pointer-interlinked**, which is the hallmark of a native graph storage engine.

**Define *Native Graph Storage* and mention any two characteristics that distinguish it from non-native graph layers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*(1 mark)* **Native graph storage** is a graph database storage architecture in which the underlying physical storage engine is purpose-built to store and process graph data using nodes, relationships, and properties as first-class citizens.
*(1 mark)* **Characteristic 1 — Index-Free Adjacency (IFA):** Each node directly stores physical pointers to its adjacent relationships, enabling O(1) per-hop traversal without consulting any global index.
*(1 mark)* **Characteristic 2 — Graph-Native File Layout:** The on-disk files (node store, relationship store, property store) are laid out in graph-aware formats with fixed-size records, allowing O(1) offset computation from node IDs.

> [!NOTE]

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PECDT751, Jul 2024]**
**What is *index-free adjacency*? Why is it critical for multi-hop graph queries?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*(1 mark)* **Index-free adjacency (IFA)** is a storage technique in which every node directly references (via physical disk offsets / pointers) the records of its first-hop neighbors, eliminating the need for a global index lookup during traversal.
*(1 mark)* Each traversal hop becomes a **single pointer dereference** of cost **O(1)**, rather than a B+ tree lookup of cost **O(log n)**.
*(1 mark)* For multi-hop queries (e.g., friend-of-friend-of-friend in a social network), the per-hop efficiency compounds multiplicatively. A 5-hop query with IFA costs $O(5)$, while the same query on an indexed non-native store costs $O(5 \log n)$, which can be **30–100× slower** for large $n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark-split tip:** Examiner awards 1 mark each for: (a) definition, (b) per-hop cost, (c) compounding effect on multi-hop.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Native Graph Storage:**
> Students frequently **conflate "graph database" with "native graph database"**. Writing about a generic graph database without anchoring the answer to **index-free adjacency**, **graph-native file layout**, and **O(1) per-hop cost** will lead to a loss of 3–5 marks. Always name the **three file groups** (node store, relationship store, property store) and explicitly derive the per-hop cost.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — PECDT751, Dec 2024, Module 4 — Internal Choice Option 1]**
**(a)** Explain the architecture of a native graph storage engine with a neat block diagram. Describe the roles of the node store, relationship store, and property store.
**(7 marks)**
**(b)** With a worked example, derive the per-hop time complexity of index-free adjacency and compare it with indexed lookup in a non-native graph layer for a graph with $n = 10^9$ nodes over a 4-hop traversal. Assume B+ tree branching factor $B = 128$, $T_{\text{node-compare}} = 50$ ns, and IFA constant $c = 100$ ns. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
*(1.5 marks)* The native graph storage engine partitions its on-disk data into **three logical file groups**:
- **Node Store:** Holds fixed-size node records. Each record contains the node's `labelId`, pointers to its first outgoing and first incoming relationship, and a pointer to its property chain head.
- **Relationship Store:** Holds fixed-size relationship records. Each record contains the `startNodeId`, `endNodeId`, the relationship `typeId`, pointers to the *next* outgoing and *next* incoming relationships on its endpoint nodes (forming doubly-linked lists), and a property pointer.
- **Property Store:** Holds variable-length key–value pairs as separate records, each chained to the next for a given entity.

*(2 marks)* **Roles and interconnections:**
- The **Node Store** is the entry point for any traversal. Given a `nodeId`, the engine computes the file offset in O(1) using $\text{offset} = \text{base} + \text{nodeId} \cdot R_{\text{node}}$.
- The **Relationship Store** is the **structural backbone of the graph**. Its records implement index-free adjacency by linking each node to its neighbors via direct disk offsets, bypassing any global index.
- The **Property Store** decouples variable-length attributes from the fixed-size structural records, preventing fragmentation of the hot traversal path.

*(1.5 marks)* **Auxiliary components:**
- **ID Generator:** Translates logical `nodeId` values into physical file offsets and reclaims freed IDs.
- **In-Memory Pointer Cache:** Caches recently followed pointers to minimize disk page faults.
- **Memory-Mapped I/O Layer:** Maps the store files into virtual address space, making pointer dereferences into RAM reads.

*(2 marks)* **Block diagram (textual):** A neat block diagram should show the three stores as three vertical layers, with the **Node Store** at the center, the **Relationship Store** to its right (linked via out/in relationship pointers), and the **Property Store** to its left (linked via property pointers from both nodes and relationships). The query execution engine sits on top, fetching and following pointers.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**
*(1.5 marks)* **Per-hop cost in IFA:**
A single hop involves: (i) reading the source node record, (ii) reading the relationship record via direct pointer, (iii) computing the target node offset. Each step is O(1) arithmetic or RAM access. Hence:

$$
T_{\text{hop}}^{\text{IFA}} \;=\; c \;=\; 100 \text{ ns (assumed)}
$$

*(1.5 marks)* **Per-hop cost in indexed lookup (B+ tree):**
Each hop requires traversing a B+ tree to find the node's record:

$$
h \;=\; \lceil \log_{B}(n) \rceil
$$

With $n = 10^9$ and $B = 128$:

$$
h \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{\ln(10^9)}{\ln(128)} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{20.72}{4.85} \right\rceil \;=\; \lceil 4.27 \rceil \;=\; 5
$$

$$
T_{\text{hop}}^{\text{index}} \;=\; h \cdot T_{\text{node-compare}} \;=\; 5 \cdot 50 \text{ ns} \;=\; 250 \text{ ns}
$$

*(1.5 marks)* **Per-hop speedup ratio:**

$$
R_{\text{hop}} \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{hop}}^{\text{index}}}{T_{\text{hop}}^{\text{IFA}}} \;=\; \frac{250}{100} \;=\; 2.5 \times
$$

*(1.5 marks)* **Cumulative speedup over a 4-hop traversal:**

$$
R_{4} \;=\; (R_{\text{hop}})^{4} \;=\; (2.5)^{4} \;=\; 39.0625 \times
$$

*(1 mark)* **Conclusion:** The native graph store is approximately **39× faster** for a 4-hop traversal over an indexed non-native graph layer on identical hardware. This is why native graph storage is the de-facto standard for deep-traversal workloads such as fraud detection, knowledge graph reasoning, and social network analysis.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — PECDT751, Jul 2024, Module 4 — Internal Choice Option 2]**
**(a)** Discuss the three on-disk file groups used in native graph storage. Explain how **index-free adjacency** is physically realized in the relationship store.
**(7 marks)**
**(b)** A social network has $N = 5 \times 10^8$ users and $M = 8 \times 10^9$ friendship relationships. The average node record is 21 bytes and the average relationship record is 33 bytes. Estimate the total on-disk storage size (ignoring property store and indexes).
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
*(2 marks)* **The three file groups:**
1. **Node Store** — fixed-size records, one per node, containing label, in/out relationship pointers, and property pointer.
2. **Relationship Store** — fixed-size records, one per edge, containing start/end node IDs, relationship type, and pointers to the next relationships on the source and target nodes.
3. **Property Store** — variable-length key–value records forming chains attached to nodes and relationships.

*(3 marks)* **Physical realization of IFA in the Relationship Store:**
- Each relationship record contains a field `nextStartRelPtr` that points to the **next** outgoing relationship of its `startNode`, and a field `nextEndRelPtr` that points to the **next** incoming relationship of its `endNode`.
- For a given node $v$, the **Node Store** record contains `firstOutRelPtr` (offset of $v$'s first outgoing relationship) and `firstInRelPtr` (offset of $v$'s first incoming relationship).
- These two pointers, combined with the `nextStartRelPtr` / `nextEndRelPtr` fields in the relationship records, form **two doubly-linked lists per node** — one for outgoing, one for incoming.
- A traversal from $v$ to its first outgoing neighbor follows three **direct disk offsets**: $v \to$ `firstOutRelPtr` $\to$ relationship record $\to$ `endNodeId` $\to$ target node. **No index is consulted.**
- This structural pattern is the physical embodiment of **index-free adjacency** and is the defining feature separating native graph storage from non-native graph layers.

*(2 marks)* **Why this is efficient:**
- Each hop is **O(1)**.
- No global index lookup is required.
- The locality of the relationship chain (often on the same page) keeps cache misses low.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**
*(1 mark)* **Given data:**
- $N = 5 \times 10^8$ nodes
- $M = 8 \times 10^9$ relationships
- Average node record size = 21 bytes
- Average relationship record size = 33 bytes

*(1 mark)* **Formula for total node storage:**

$$
S_{\text{nodes}} \;=\; N \cdot \bar{S}_{\text{node}}
$$

*(1 mark)* **Computation for node storage:**

$$
S_{\text{nodes}} \;=\; 5 \times 10^8 \cdot 21 \text{ bytes} \;=\; 1.05 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes} \;\approx\; 10.5 \text{ GB}
$$

*(1 mark)* **Formula for total relationship storage:**

$$
S_{\text{rels}} \;=\; M \cdot \bar{S}_{\text{rel}}
$$

*(1 mark)* **Computation for relationship storage:**

$$
S_{\text{rels}} \;=\; 8 \times 10^9 \cdot 33 \text{ bytes} \;=\; 2.64 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes} \;\approx\; 264 \text{ GB}
$$

*(1 mark)* **Total on-disk storage (ignoring properties and indexes):**

$$
S_{\text{total}} \;=\; S_{\text{nodes}} + S_{\text{rels}} \;=\; 10.5 \text{ GB} + 264 \text{ GB} \;=\; 274.5 \text{ GB}
$$

*(1 mark)* **Conclusion:** The structural footprint of this social network is approximately **274.5 GB**. In production, after adding the property store (estimated 2–5× the structural size for typical user attributes), total storage often lands in the **0.8–1.5 TB** range for such a graph. This demonstrates why storage efficiency — and not just traversal speed — is a key engineering concern in native graph database design.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for Native Graph Storage:**

- ✅ A **native graph database** stores graph data in a **graph-aware format** at the lowest storage layer — not as tables, documents, or key-value pairs.
- ✅ The **defining trait** of native graph storage is **Index-Free Adjacency (IFA)**: every node holds direct pointers to its adjacent relationships, making each traversal hop **O(1)**.
- ✅ The three canonical file groups are: **Node Store**, **Relationship Store**, **Property Store**.
- ✅ **Node Store records** contain `labelId`, first-out-relationship pointer, first-in-relationship pointer, and a property pointer.
- ✅ **Relationship Store records** contain `startNodeId`, `endNodeId`, `typeId`, next-out pointer, next-in pointer, and a property pointer — forming **doubly-linked lists** off each node.
- ✅ **Property Store** decouples variable-length attributes from fixed-size structural records, preventing fragmentation.
- ✅ Fixed-size records enable **O(1) offset computation** from `nodeId` using $\text{offset} = \text{base} + \text{nodeId} \cdot R_{\text{node}}$.
- ✅ **Memory-mapped I/O (mmap)** is heavily used to turn pointer dereferences into RAM-speed reads.
- ✅ **In-memory pointer caches** accelerate repeated traversals.
- ✅ Per-hop speedup of IFA over indexed lookup grows as $\log_B(n)$ per hop, and **compounds multiplicatively** over $k$ hops: $R_k = (\log_B n / c)^k$.
- ✅ Native graph storage **excels at deep multi-hop traversals** but is **weaker at global aggregation queries** (where RDBMS still wins).
- ✅ Examples of **native** graph DBMS: **Neo4j, Memgraph, TigerGraph**. Examples of **non-native** graph layers: MySQL + Graph Engine, ArangoDB (hybrid).
- ✅ A system must be **native on storage** *and* **native on processing** to be classified as a true native graph database.
- ✅ KTU exam keywords to memorize: **index-free adjacency, O(1) per hop, doubly-linked relationship chains, node/relationship/property store, graph-native file layout, mmap, pointer cache**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programmatic APIs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programmatic APIs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Graph Databases**, a **Programmatic API (Application Programming Interface)** is a software intermediary layer that allows client applications written in general-purpose programming languages (Java, Python, JavaScript, Go, .NET, etc.) to programmatically interact with a graph database engine. It exposes a structured, object-oriented or function-based interface through which developers can perform **CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete)** operations, manage **sessions and transactions**, execute **Cypher / GQL queries**, and process **result records** without relying on the interactive Cypher shell.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus context, programmatic APIs are positioned as the **bridge between the declarative query language and the imperative application code**, enabling the embedding of graph workloads inside enterprise systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> A *Programmatic API for a graph database* is a language-specific driver/library that provides classes, methods, and abstractions for connecting to a graph DBMS, opening sessions, executing queries, binding parameters, traversing results, and committing or rolling back transactions — all under strict ACID guarantees.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **graph database** as a giant, intelligent **post-office** where every letterbox (node) is connected to others by labeled routes (relationships). The **Cypher query language** is like speaking the postmaster's native language fluently over a public counter — quick, but you must stand at the counter each time.

A **Programmatic API**, in contrast, is like getting a **personal clerk** who lives inside your own office. You don't need to walk to the post-office; you just hand the clerk a written request (a function call) — *"Please register this parcel (node) and link it to that other parcel (relationship)."* The clerk knows the postmaster's language, handles the queue, signs the receipts (transactions), and brings back the proof (result set). If something goes wrong, the clerk tears up the receipt and undoes the work (rollback). The clerk also keeps the office key safe (connection pooling), so you don't waste time unlocking the door every time.

## 1.3 Why Programmatic APIs are Essential

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Embeddability** | Applications can call graph logic as plain methods, enabling graph features inside web apps, microservices, and ETL jobs. |
| **Type Safety** | Strongly-typed drivers (Java, .NET) catch errors at compile time, reducing runtime Cypher syntax errors. |
| **Parameter Binding** | APIs accept map/object parameters, preventing Cypher injection vulnerabilities. |
| **Transaction Control** | Programmatic APIs expose explicit `beginTransaction()`, `commit()`, `rollback()` methods. |
| **Streaming Results** | Large result sets can be streamed, reducing memory pressure. |
| **Driver-Level Resilience** | Built-in connection pooling, retries, TLS encryption, and clustering routing. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus places Programmatic APIs **after** declarative query languages (Cypher/SPARQL/GQL) and **before** advanced analytics — meaning the board expects you to understand them as the *integration layer* between the two.

## 1.4 Categories of Programmatic APIs

1. **Native (Embedded) APIs** — e.g., Neo4j's Java Embedded API, where the database runs inside the same JVM as the application. Latency is **near-zero** because no network round-trip occurs.
2. **Bolt / Binary Protocol Drivers** — e.g., Neo4j Java/Python/.NET/Go/JS drivers using the **Bolt protocol** (a custom high-efficiency binary protocol over TCP/TLS, defined on **port 7687**).
3. **HTTP/REST Drivers** — e.g., Neo4j's legacy HTTP API on **port 7474**, which speaks JSON over HTTPS.
4. **Language-Specific ORMs / Graph Mappers** — e.g., **Spring Data Neo4j (SDN)**, **OGM (Object-Graph Mapper)**, **Py2neo**, **Gremlin (OLTP)**. These wrap the raw driver in object-oriented abstractions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of a Programmatic API

A typical driver follows a **four-layer architecture**, moving from the user's code down to the wire protocol:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Layer 4: Application Code (User Logic)     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 3: High-Level Mapper (Optional)      │
│           e.g., SDN, OGM, Py2neo            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 2: Driver Core / Session             │
│           (Session, Transaction, Result)     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 1: Bolt/HTTP Protocol Client         │
│           (Connection Pool, TLS)            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 0: Database Engine (Neo4j/TigerGraph)│
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## 2.2 Core Components (Step-by-Step Logic)

### Step 1 — The Driver Object
- Represents the **top-level handle** to the database.
- Configured with a **URI** (`bolt://localhost:7687`), credentials, and pooling parameters.
- Heavy-weight, **thread-safe**, and meant to be a **singleton**.

### Step 2 — The Session
- A **lightweight, stateful conversation** with the server, scoped to a single user/context.
- Two principal flavors in Neo4j:
  * `session()` — auto-commit (used for one-shot read queries).
  * `session(defaultAccessMode = AccessMode.READ)` — read-only session that can be routed to a **follower** in a Causal Cluster.

### Step 3 — The Transaction
- A **unit of work** that satisfies **ACID** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability).
- Inside one transaction, multiple Cypher statements can be chained.
- A transaction returns a `Result` object, which is an **iterator over Records**.

### Step 4 — The Result and Record
- A `Result` is a **cursor-style stream**.
- A `Record` is a list of **fields** (named or indexed), each mapped to a graph value (Node, Relationship, Path, or scalar).

### Step 5 — Resource Cleanup
- Sessions and transactions implement `AutoCloseable` (Java) / `with` semantics (Python).
- Best practice: **always close** in a `finally` block to avoid socket leaks.

## 2.3 Transaction Lifecycle (The 'Why' Behind Each Step)

| State | Action | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **BEGIN** | `tx = session.beginTransaction()` | Allocates a transaction ID on the server; pins a logical clock. |
| **EXECUTE** | `tx.run("MATCH …", params)` | Sends a Cypher statement + parameters over Bolt; server parses, plans, executes. |
| **CONSUME** | `result.consume()` or stream | Acknowledges the result so the server can free buffers. **Not consuming** a large result blocks the connection. |
| **COMMIT** | `tx.commit()` | Persists all writes with WAL flush; makes changes visible to other transactions. |
| **ROLLBACK** | `tx.rollback()` | Discards all writes if a `RuntimeException` occurs — protects data integrity. |

## 2.4 The Bolt Protocol — The 'Why' of Wire Efficiency

- **Bolt** is a **stateful, binary, request-response protocol** designed for high-throughput, low-latency graph workloads.
- Each message has a fixed **2-byte signature** (e.g., `0xB1` for `HELLO`, `0x10` for `BEGIN`), minimizing parser overhead.
- Supports **pipelining** — multiple statements sent on a single socket round-trip, reducing latency by up to **30–40 %** compared to HTTP/REST.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Bolt's pipelining is what allows Neo4j to achieve **>100k transactions/sec** on commodity hardware — a key reason programmatic APIs are preferred over REST in production.

## 2.5 Parameter Binding & Cypher Injection Defense

Instead of string concatenation, APIs use **named parameters**:

```java
// Java (Neo4j Java Driver 5.x)
session.run("CREATE (p:Person {name: $name, age: $age})",
            Map.of("name", "Anu", "age", 21));
```

The `$name` and `$age` tokens are **placeholders** that the driver serializes as **Bolt structures (Node, List, Map)** — they never become part of the Cypher string. This is **analogous to PreparedStatements** in JDBC.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Syntax | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connection URI | `bolt://host:7687` | Default Bolt port | **7687** |
| HTTP URI | `http://host:7474` | Legacy REST port | **7474** |
| HTTPS URI | `https://host:7473` | Secure REST | **7473** |
| Driver singleton | `GraphDatabase.driver(uri, auth)` | Process-wide client | One per JVM |
| Session scope | `try (Session s = driver.session())` | Logical conversation | Per request |
| Transaction ACID | `A + C + I + D` | Atomic, Consistent, Isolated, Durable | Always on |
| Bolt message size | `<= 2^31-1 bytes` | Max chunk per PULL | **2 GB** |
| Connection pool | `maxConnectionPoolSize` | Concurrent Bolt sockets | **100** |
| Connection timeout | `connectionTimeout` | TCP connect wait | **30 s** |
| Result fetch size | `FetchSize = n` | Records per pull chunk | **1000** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** If a question asks *"List any four configuration parameters of a graph DB driver,"* the four above marked in bold are the safest answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Code Walkthrough

## 3.1 The End-to-End Flow of a Programmatic API Call (Analytical Walkthrough)

We will trace what happens when a single Java statement `session.run(query, params)` is executed — from Java source to disk commit on a Neo4j server.

### Step 1 — Application Invocation

The user calls the method in their application code. The driver method is **overloaded**: the second argument may be a `Map<String,Object>`, a `Record`, or a `Result` of a previous statement.

### Step 2 — Parameter Serialization to PackStream

The driver converts every Java object in the parameter map into a **PackStream structure**:

- `String "Anu"` → PackStream `STRING ('A','n','u')`
- `Integer 21` → PackStream `INT_8 (0xC8, 0x15)`
- `LocalDate` → PackStream `DATE (0xB1, …)`

### Step 3 — Message Construction

The driver composes a **Bolt `RUN` message** with signature `0x10`:

```
[ 0x00 0x00 0x00 0x01 ]   // message tag
[ 0xB3 0x10 ]            // RUN signature (0x10) with 0xB3 chunk header
[ STRING(query) ]        // the Cypher string
[ MAP(parameters) ]      // bound parameters
[ MAP(mode) ]            // {"mode": "r"} (read)
[ LIST(bookmarks) ]      // for causal consistency
```

### Step 4 — TCP Send and Pipelining

The bytes are pushed into a **non-blocking socket** (Netty-based on the server side). The driver may **pipeline** further `PULL`/`DISCARD` messages to avoid waiting for a synchronous response.

### Step 5 — Server-Side Parsing and Planning

The Neo4j server's **Bolt protocol layer** parses the binary, hands the Cypher to the **Cypher parser**, builds an **AST**, then optimizes it via the **rule-based + cost-based planner** to produce a physical plan.

### Step 6 — Execution and Streaming

The execution engine runs the plan, producing `Record` messages (`0x71` signature). These are chunked into batches of `FetchSize` (default **1000**) and pushed back through the socket.

### Step 7 — Application De-serialization

The driver reads the `Record` chunks, decodes PackStream structures back into Java `Node`, `Relationship`, `Path`, or scalar types, and exposes them via the `Result` iterator.

### Step 8 — Commit / Rollback

When the transaction is closed, a `SUCCESS` or `FAILURE` message is sent. On success, the server flushes the **Write-Ahead Log (WAL)** to disk. On failure, the transaction is rolled back and no WAL flush occurs.

## 3.2 Closed-Form Cost Model for an API Call

For a KTU-style analytical question, the **end-to-end latency** of a programmatic API call can be modeled as:

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{serialize}} + T_{\text{network}} + T_{\text{parse}} + T_{\text{plan}} + T_{\text{execute}} + T_{\text{stream}} + T_{\text{deserialize}}$$

Expanding each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{serialize}}   &= k_1 \cdot s_{\text{params}} \quad \text{(linear in parameter size } s_{\text{params}}\text{)} \\
T_{\text{network}}     &= \frac{p}{B} + 2 \cdot \text{RTT} \quad \text{(payload } p \text{ over bandwidth } B\text{)} \\
T_{\text{parse}}       &= k_2 \cdot \vert q \vert \quad \text{(linear in Cypher length } \vert q \vert\text{)} \\
T_{\text{plan}}        &= k_3 \cdot n_{\text{patterns}} \cdot \log n_{\text{patterns}} \\
T_{\text{execute}}     &= k_4 \cdot \vert V \vert + k_5 \cdot \vert E \vert \quad \text{(graph scan cost)} \\
T_{\text{stream}}      &= \frac{r}{F} \quad \text{(result rows } r \text{, fetch size } F\text{)} \\
T_{\text{deserialize}} &= k_6 \cdot r
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_6$ = machine- and driver-specific constants
- $n_{\text{patterns}}$ = number of pattern nodes in the Cypher query
- $\vert V \vert, \vert E \vert$ = number of vertices and edges scanned
- $r$ = number of result rows
- $F$ = fetch size (default **1000**)
- $\text{RTT}$ = round-trip time

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For interactive workloads, $T_{\text{plan}}$ dominates short queries; for analytical scans, $T_{\text{execute}}$ dominates. Hence the design tradeoff: **prepared statements** cache $T_{\text{parse}} + T_{\text{plan}}$ across calls, while **batched `UNWIND`** queries amortize $T_{\text{network}}$ across many rows.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** An analytics dashboard issues a query returning 50,000 records. The driver uses a fetch size of 1,000. The RTT to the server is 2 ms, and the deserialization constant is $k_6 = 0.5 \,\mu s/\text{row}$. How many network round-trips are required, and what is the deserialization time?

**Solution — Step 1: Compute number of fetches**

$$
n_{\text{fetches}} = \left\lceil \frac{r}{F} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{50{,}000}{1{,}000} \right\rceil = 50
$$

**Step 2: Compute streaming time**

$$
T_{\text{stream}} = 50 \times \text{RTT} = 50 \times 2 \,\text{ms} = 100 \,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 3: Compute deserialization time**

$$
T_{\text{deserialize}} = k_6 \cdot r = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 50{,}000 = 0.025 \,\text{s} = 25 \,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 4: Total time for the receiving phase**

$$
T_{\text{receive}} = T_{\text{stream}} + T_{\text{deserialize}} = 100 + 25 = 125 \,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 5: Optimization — increase fetch size to 5,000**

$$
n_{\text{fetches}}' = \left\lceil \frac{50{,}000}{5{,}000} \right\rceil = 10
$$

$$
T_{\text{stream}}' = 10 \times 2 \,\text{ms} = 20 \,\text{ms}
$$

$$
T_{\text{receive}}' = 20 + 25 = 45 \,\text{ms}
$$

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{125}{45} \approx 2.78 \times
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Tuning `FetchSize` from **1,000 → 5,000** yields a **2.78× speedup** with no code changes — a classic KTU-level "engineering trade-off" takeaway.

## 3.4 Sample Code — Complete Java Driver Lifecycle

```java
import org.neo4j.driver.*;
import static org.neo4j.driver.Values.parameters;

public class KTUGraphDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 1: Driver is a singleton
        Driver driver = GraphDatabase.driver(
            "bolt://localhost:7687",
            AuthTokens.basic("neo4j", "password")
        );

        // Step 2: Open a session in try-with-resources
        try (Session session = driver.session()) {

            // Step 3: Begin a transaction
            try (Transaction tx = session.beginTransaction()) {

                // Step 4: Parameterized CREATE
                Result result = tx.run(
                    "CREATE (p:Person {name: $name, age: $age}) RETURN p",
                    parameters("name", "Anu", "age", 21)
                );

                // Step 5: Stream results
                while (result.hasNext()) {
                    Record r = result.next();
                    Node person = r.get("p").asNode();
                    System.out.println(person.get("name").asString());
                }

                // Step 6: Commit
                tx.commit();
            }
        } finally {
            // Step 7: Always close the driver
            driver.close();
        }
    }
}
```

**Line-by-line explanation:**

| Line | What happens |
|---|---|
| `GraphDatabase.driver(...)` | Creates a **singleton** Bolt driver, opens 1+ sockets from a pool. |
| `try (Session session = ...)` | Establishes a **logical session**; auto-closed on exit. |
| `session.beginTransaction()` | Sends a Bolt `BEGIN` message; starts ACID transaction. |
| `tx.run(query, params)` | Sends `RUN` + `PULL` messages; receives first `RECORD` chunks. |
| `result.hasNext() / .next()` | Cursor-style iteration over streamed records. |
| `tx.commit()` | Sends `SUCCESS`; server flushes WAL to disk. |
| `driver.close()` | Sends Bolt `GOODBYE`; closes all pool sockets. |

## 3.5 Python Equivalent (Py2neo / Official Driver)

```python
from neo4j import GraphDatabase

driver = GraphDatabase.driver("bolt://localhost:7687",
                              auth=("neo4j", "password"))

def create_person(tx, name, age):
    tx.run("CREATE (p:Person {name: $name, age: $age})",
           name=name, age=age)

with driver.session() as session:
    session.execute_write(create_person, "Anu", 21)

driver.close()
```

> The `execute_write` helper **automatically retries** on transient errors, demonstrating the resilience built into modern APIs.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End API Call Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant AppCode as Application Code
    participant DriverAPI as Driver API Layer
    participant PackStream as PackStream Serializer
    participant BoltSocket as Bolt TCP Socket
    participant CypherEngine as Cypher Execution Engine
    participant Storage as Storage Engine

    AppCode->>DriverAPI: tx.run(query, params)
    DriverAPI->>PackStream: serialize parameters
    PackStream->>BoltSocket: encode RUN message
    BoltSocket->>CypherEngine: transmit bytes
    CypherEngine->>CypherEngine: parse + plan
    CypherEngine->>Storage: read/write nodes/edges
    Storage-->>CypherEngine: result stream
    CypherEngine-->>BoltSocket: RECORD messages
    BoltSocket-->>PackStream: receive bytes
    PackStream-->>DriverAPI: deserialize Record objects
    DriverAPI-->>AppCode: yield Record iterator
    AppCode->>DriverAPI: tx.commit()
    DriverAPI->>BoltSocket: SUCCESS message
    BoltSocket->>Storage: flush WAL
    Storage-->>AppCode: ACK commit
```

## 4.2 Driver Component Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph clientSide["CLIENT SIDE (Application JVM)"]
        appCode["Application Code"]
        mapper["High-Level Mapper SDN OGM"]
        driverCore["Driver Core"]
        session["Session Factory"]
        tx["Transaction Manager"]
        pool["Connection Pool"]
    end

    subgraph wire["WIRE"]
        bolt["Bolt Binary Protocol over TCP TLS port 7687"]
    end

    subgraph serverSide["SERVER SIDE (Neo4j Process)"]
        boltServer["Bolt Server Module"]
        sessionServer["Server Session"]
        txServer["Server Transaction"]
        engine["Cypher Engine Planner Executor"]
        store["Storage Engine WAL"]
    end

    appCode --> mapper
    mapper --> driverCore
    driverCore --> session
    session --> tx
    tx --> pool
    pool --> bolt
    bolt --> boltServer
    boltServer --> sessionServer
    sessionServer --> txServer
    txServer --> engine
    engine --> store
```

## 4.3 Transaction Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NoTx
    NoTx --> Active: beginTransaction()
    Active --> Executing: tx.run query params
    Executing --> Executing: stream result
    Executing --> Committing: commit success
    Executing --> RollingBack: rollback or exception
    Committing --> Closed: tx.commit ACK
    RollingBack --> Closed: tx.rollback ACK
    Closed --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Mapping Between Bolt Messages and API Calls

```mermaid
graph LR
    apiCall1["session.run query"] --> msg1["RUN message 0x10"]
    apiCall2["result.next()"]     --> msg2["PULL message 0x3F"]
    apiCall3["tx.commit()"]       --> msg3["SUCCESS 0x2F then COMMIT 0x12"]
    apiCall4["tx.rollback()"]     --> msg4["FAILURE 0x7F"]
    apiCall5["session.close()"]   --> msg5["GOODBYE 0x02"]
    apiCall6["driver.close()"]    --> msg6["GOODBYE 0x02 on all sockets"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a Programmatic API in the context of graph databases. Mention any two language-specific drivers for Neo4j.**

**Model Answer:**

A *Programmatic API* is a software layer that exposes graph database operations (CRUD, transaction management, query execution) as methods callable from a host programming language. It abstracts the network protocol, parameter binding, and result streaming, allowing developers to integrate graph operations into enterprise applications without writing raw Cypher over a CLI.

**Two language-specific drivers:**
1. **Neo4j Java Driver** (official, Bolt-based, Maven artifact `org.neo4j.driver:neo4j-java-driver`).
2. **Neo4j Python Driver** (official, Bolt-based, `pip install neo4j`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Full marks require *both* the formal definition **and** at least two named drivers with package names.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the Bolt protocol? Why is it preferred over HTTP/REST for graph database APIs?**

**Model Answer:**

**Bolt** is a **stateful, binary, application-layer protocol** used by Neo4j drivers to communicate with the database server over TCP/TLS on **port 7687**.

**Why preferred over HTTP/REST:**
1. **Lower latency** — binary encoding is smaller and faster to parse than JSON.
2. **Pipelining** — multiple statements can be sent in one round-trip.
3. **Type fidelity** — native support for graph types (Node, Relationship, Path) without JSON marshaling.
4. **Stateful sessions** — connection reuse avoids re-handshaking per query.
5. **Streaming results** — chunked PULL messages scale to large result sets.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Bank (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **architectural layers** of a Neo4j programmatic API driver. With a neat diagram, describe how a parameterized Cypher query travels from the application code to the storage engine and back.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Java program using the Neo4j Java Driver to: (i) create a node `Person` with properties `name` and `age`, (ii) create a `KNOWS` relationship between two persons, and (iii) print the names of all persons known by `"Anu"`. Use **parameterized queries** throughout.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

The Neo4j Java driver has **four logical layers**:

1. **Application Layer** — user code that calls `session.run(...)`.
2. **Driver Core / Session Layer** — manages sessions, transactions, and result cursors.
3. **Bolt Protocol Client** — serializes calls to **PackStream** and transmits over TCP/TLS.
4. **Server-Side Bolt Module** — accepts connections, parses Bolt, and dispatches to the **Cypher Execution Engine**.

**Flow of a parameterized query:**

```
User Code → Session → Transaction → PackStream encode
        → Bolt socket (RUN msg 0x10) → Server Bolt module
        → Cypher parser → Planner → Executor
        → Storage engine (read/write) → result stream
        → PackStream decode → Record iterator → User Code
```

The accompanying **sequence diagram** in Section 4.1 of these notes is the expected illustration.

**Key concepts to mention (for full 7 marks):**
- PackStream serialization (binary, type-safe).
- Bolt message signatures (`RUN = 0x10`, `PULL = 0x3F`, `SUCCESS = 0x2F`).
- Connection pool reuse.
- Server-side parsing, planning, and execution.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

```java
import org.neo4j.driver.*;
import static org.neo4j.driver.Values.parameters;

public class PersonGraph {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Driver driver = GraphDatabase.driver(
            "bolt://localhost:7687",
            AuthTokens.basic("neo4j", "password")
        );

        // (i) Create two Person nodes
        try (Session s = driver.session()) {
            s.run("CREATE (a:Person {name:$n1, age:$a1})",
                  parameters("n1","Anu","a1",21));
            s.run("CREATE (b:Person {name:$n2, age:$a2})",
                  parameters("n2","Manu","a2",22));
        }

        // (ii) Create KNOWS relationship
        try (Session s = driver.session()) {
            s.run("MATCH (a:Person {name:$n1}), (b:Person {name:$n2}) " +
                  "CREATE (a)-[:KNOWS]->(b)",
                  parameters("n1","Anu","n2","Manu"));
        }

        // (iii) Retrieve friends of Anu
        try (Session s = driver.session();
             Result r = s.run("MATCH (a:Person {name:$n})-[:KNOWS]->(f) " +
                              "RETURN f.name AS friendName",
                              parameters("n","Anu"))) {
            while (r.hasNext()) {
                System.out.println(r.next().get("friendName").asString());
            }
        }

        driver.close();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of marks distribution (7):**
- Correct Maven dependency / driver import — **1 mark**
- Driver initialization with URI + auth — **1 mark**
- Parameterized CREATE statements — **2 marks**
- Parameterized MATCH + relationship creation — **1 mark**
- Result iteration with `.hasNext()/.next()` — **1 mark**
- Closing driver/session properly — **1 mark**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **transaction management model** exposed by a typical graph database programmatic API. Include the lifecycle states, the role of ACID properties, and the difference between **auto-commit sessions** and **explicit transactions**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Bolt** and **HTTP/REST** as transport mechanisms for graph database APIs across the dimensions: latency, type fidelity, pipelining, security, and result streaming.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Transaction Lifecycle States (per the diagram in Section 4.3):**

1. **No Transaction** — initial state.
2. **Active** — `beginTransaction()` allocates server resources.
3. **Executing** — one or more `tx.run(query, params)` calls.
4. **Committing** / **Rolling Back** — terminal decision.
5. **Closed** — resources released.

**ACID Properties and their enforcement in APIs:**

| Property | How the API enforces it |
|---|---|
| **Atomicity** | `tx.commit()` flushes all writes; `rollback()` discards all. |
| **Consistency** | Constraint validation (uniqueness, existence) at commit time. |
| **Isolation** | Read-committed by default; `Bookmarks` provide causal consistency. |
| **Durability** | Server flushes **WAL** to disk on commit ACK. |

**Auto-commit vs Explicit Transaction:**

| Feature | Auto-commit session | Explicit transaction |
|---|---|---|
| Syntax | `session.run(query)` | `session.beginTransaction(); tx.run(); tx.commit();` |
| Use case | One-shot reads/writes | Multi-statement workflows |
| Atomicity | Per-statement | Per-transaction (multiple statements) |
| Rollback | Not applicable | Available via `tx.rollback()` |
| Performance | Higher network overhead per statement | Lower overhead (pipelined) |

**For full marks mention:** the role of **bookmarks** in causal consistency across cluster members.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

| Dimension | **Bolt** | **HTTP / REST** |
|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | ~1–5 ms typical (binary, persistent) | ~10–50 ms (JSON, stateless) |
| **Type Fidelity** | Native Node, Relationship, Path, temporal, spatial types | JSON-only; temporal types are strings |
| **Pipelining** | Yes — multiple `RUN/PULL` per RTT | No — one HTTP request per query |
| **Security** | TLS-encrypted TCP, native auth tokens | HTTPS with Basic/Bearer/JWT |
| **Result Streaming** | Chunked `RECORD` messages with `FetchSize` | Full JSON body returned (no native streaming) |
| **Port** | **7687** | **7474 / 7473** |
| **State** | Stateful session | Stateless per request |
| **Throughput** | High (100k+ txn/s in benchmarks) | Low–Moderate |

**Verdict (for 1 extra mark):** Bolt is the **production-grade** default; REST is suitable for low-frequency integrations and quick scripting.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing API with Query Language:** Writing Cypher snippets without showing the API *wrapping* (e.g., `session.run(...)`, `tx.commit()`). Always show the **driver/session/transaction** scaffolding.
> 2. **String Concatenation Trap:** Using `+` to build Cypher strings loses **1–2 marks** for missing parameter binding.
> 3. **Forgetting Resource Cleanup:** No `try-with-resources` or `driver.close()` → lose the 1 mark allocated to cleanup.
> 4. **Wrong Port Number:** Writing `7474` when the question asks for Bolt (correct: **7687**).
> 5. **Treating Bolt as HTTP:** Saying "Bolt uses JSON" — Bolt is **binary** PackStream.
> 6. **No diagram in 7-mark theory questions:** KTU examiners explicitly allocate **1–2 marks for diagrams** in 7-mark sub-parts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must-memorize for KTU 2024):**

- [x] **Programmatic API** = language-specific driver that wraps the graph DBMS.
- [x] Three pillars: **Driver → Session → Transaction**.
- [x] **Bolt protocol** = binary, stateful, on **port 7687**.
- [x] **HTTP/REST** = legacy, on **port 7474 (HTTP)** / **7473 (HTTPS)**.
- [x] **PackStream** = binary serialization format used by Bolt.
- [x] **Default fetch size = 1000** records per PULL chunk.
- [x] **Default connection pool size = 100** sockets.
- [x] Always use **parameter binding** (`$param`) to prevent Cypher injection.
- [x] **Driver is a singleton**; sessions and transactions are **per-request**.
- [x] **Commit** flushes WAL; **Rollback** discards all writes.
- [x] **Bookmarks** provide causal consistency in a clustered setup.
- [x] **Auto-commit** = `session.run`; **Explicit** = `beginTransaction/commit/rollback`.
- [x] Pipelining + larger `FetchSize` → **2.78× speedup** on large result sets.
- [x] **Spring Data Neo4j (SDN)** and **OGM** are high-level mappers, *not* drivers.
- [x] Modern APIs expose **retry helpers** (`execute_write` / `execute_read`) for transient failures.
- [x] Always close resources in `finally` / `try-with-resources` to avoid socket leaks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Takeaway:** Programmatic APIs transform a *declarative* graph query language into a *callable service* for applications — and the **Bolt protocol** is the high-performance backbone that makes this possible in production systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Functional Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Non-Functional Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Graph Databases**, *Non-Functional Characteristics* (also known as **Quality Attributes** or **Non-Functional Requirements — NFRs**) refer to the **qualitative, operational, and architectural criteria** that dictate *how well* a graph database system performs its storage, retrieval, and traversal tasks, rather than *what specific features* it offers.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECDT751**, these characteristics collectively determine the **production-readiness**, **scalability envelope**, and **enterprise suitability** of a graph DBMS (such as **Neo4j**, **JanusGraph**, **TigerGraph**, or **Amazon Neptune**). They are commonly catalogued under the **ISO/IEC 25010 Software Product Quality Model**, adapted for data-intensive systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard Wording):**
> *"Non-functional characteristics of a graph database are the measurable, qualitative attributes describing the system's performance, reliability, scalability, security, maintainability, and portability — independent of any specific functional workload."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen**
> Imagine a graph database is a **high-end restaurant kitchen**.
> - **Functional characteristics** = the *menu items* it can cook (Cypher queries, traversals, pattern matching).
> - **Non-functional characteristics** = the *kitchen's operational excellence*: How fast are the dishes served? (**Performance**), Can it cook for 10,000 guests at once? (**Scalability**), Is the gas supply guaranteed? (**Availability**), Is the kitchen fire-safe? (**Security**), Can a new chef be trained quickly on the system? (**Maintainability**).
>
> Two kitchens may cook the *same dishes* (functional parity), but the one with superior non-functional traits wins customer loyalty.

The principal non-functional characteristics evaluated by the KTU Board for this module are summarized in the table below.

| # | Non-Functional Characteristic | One-Line Board Definition |
|---|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Performance** | Speed of query execution and traversal throughput |
| 2 | **Scalability** | Ability to handle growing data and user load |
| 3 | **Availability** | Uptime guarantee under fault conditions |
| 4 | **Reliability** | Consistency and correctness of operations |
| 5 | **Security** | Protection against unauthorized access and threats |
| 6 | **Maintainability** | Ease of updates, debugging, and schema evolution |
| 7 | **Portability** | Ability to migrate across platforms / cloud vendors |
| 8 | **Interoperability** | Seamless data exchange with other systems |
| 9 | **Extensibility** | Capacity to add new features without redesign |
| 10 | **Data Integrity** | Preservation of accuracy and consistency of graph data |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Characteristic

#### A. **Performance** *(Most heavily tested in KTU ESE)*
- **What it measures:** Latency, throughput, and response time of graph traversals.
- **How it works in graph DBs:** Performance is heavily dependent on **index-free adjacency** — each node maintains direct physical references (pointers) to its neighbours, making traversals run in **O(1)** time per hop rather than O(log n) as in RDBMS joins.
- **Key Metrics:** **Latency (ms)**, **Throughput (ops/sec)**, **P95 / P99 response time**.

#### B. **Scalability**
- **Vertical Scaling (Scale-Up):** Adding more CPU/RAM to a single node. Limited by hardware ceiling.
- **Horizontal Scaling (Scale-Out):** Adding more nodes to the cluster. Achieved in graph DBs via **sharding** (partitioning) and **replication**.
- **Graph-specific challenge:** *Hotspots* — supernodes (e.g., a celebrity node in a social graph) may receive disproportionate traffic.

#### C. **Availability**
- Measured as a **percentage of uptime** over a year.
- The "**five nines**" benchmark (**99.999%**) allows only **~5.26 minutes** of downtime per year.
- Graph DBs achieve this through **replication** and **failover clustering**.

#### D. **Reliability & Consistency**
- Governed by the **CAP Theorem** (see Section 3 for full derivation).
- Distributed graph systems often trade strict **Consistency (C)** for **Availability (A)** and **Partition Tolerance (P)** — categorized as **AP systems** (e.g., Cassandra, JanusGraph in some modes).

#### E. **Security**
- Encompasses **Authentication (AuthN)**, **Authorization (AuthZ)**, **Encryption (at-rest & in-transit)**, and **Audit Logging**.
- Graph DBs implement **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** with fine-grained **property-level** permissions.

#### F. **Maintainability**
- Refers to schema evolution, query optimization, and operational patching.
- Schema-free / schema-optional nature of property graphs aids **evolvability**.

#### G. **Portability & Interoperability**
- Achieved through standard query languages like **Cypher** (openCypher), **GQL** (ISO/IEC 39075:2024), and **Gremlin**.
- Supports export to **RDF**, **JSON-LD**, **CSV** for cross-system exchange.

#### H. **Extensibility**
- Enabled by **user-defined procedures (UDPs)**, **stored procedures**, and **plugin architectures** (e.g., Neo4j's APOC library).

#### I. **Data Integrity**
- Maintained through **ACID transactions** (in Neo4j) or **BASE** semantics (in distributed graph DBs).
- Enforced via **uniqueness constraints**, **existence constraints**, and **graph-level invariants**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Property Graph databases like **Neo4j** are **ACID-compliant** (full Consistency), whereas **distributed graph DBs** like **JanusGraph / Titan** are typically **Eventually Consistent (BASE)**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Expression | Unit | Use Case |
|---|------------------|---------------------|------|----------|
| 1 | **Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{N_{ops}}{\Delta t}$ | ops/sec | Measures query execution rate |
| 2 | **Latency** | $L = \dfrac{\Delta t}{N_{ops}}$ | seconds | Measures per-query response time |
| 3 | **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | % | Uptime guarantee calculation |
| 4 | **Storage Overhead** | $O_{s} = \dfrac{S_{total} - S_{raw}}{S_{raw}} \times 100\%$ | % | Index, replication, pointer cost |
| 5 | **Replication Factor** | $RF = \dfrac{N_{copies}}{N_{unique}}$ | dimensionless | Redundancy measure |
| 6 | **Scalability Index** | $S_{idx} = \dfrac{\text{Perf}(2n)}{\text{Perf}(n)}$ | ratio | Linear = 1.0, Sub-linear < 1.0 |
| 7 | **Cache Hit Ratio** | $CHR = \dfrac{H_{hits}}{H_{hits} + H_{misses}} \times 100\%$ | % | Memory-layer effectiveness |
| 8 | **Concurrent Users** | $CU = \dfrac{T \times L_{avg}}{1}$ | users | Little's Law application |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All table cells above use **LaTeX `\dfrac` and `\vert`-free notation** to preserve KTU-style markdown table integrity. When writing `|x|` in equations, always use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid`.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Social Networks (Facebook, LinkedIn):** Demand *high scalability* and *availability* — use **TAO** and graph sharding.
- **Fraud Detection (PayPal, Mastercard):** Demand *low latency* and *high reliability* — use **Neo4j** with sub-second traversals.
- **Knowledge Graphs (Google, Wolfram):** Demand *extensibility* and *interoperability* — use **RDF/SPARQL** with **GQL** compatibility.
- **Network/IT Operations (Cisco, Verizon):** Demand *high performance* and *maintainability* — use **TigerGraph** for deep-link analytics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Availability Percentage from MTBF and MTTR

**Given:**
- **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures (in hours)
- **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair (in hours)
- **Total Operating Time** = MTBF + MTTR (per failure cycle)

**Step 1 — Write the basic definition of availability.**
Availability is the *fraction of time* the system is operational.

$$
A = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Total Time}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Uptime = MTBF and Total Time = MTBF + MTTR.**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage (multiplier = 100).**

$$
A_{\%} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4 — Apply numerical example** (a typical enterprise graph DB cluster):
- MTBF = 8,760 hours (1 year)
- MTTR = 1 hour

$$
A_{\%} = \frac{8760}{8760 + 1} \times 100\% = \frac{8760}{8761} \times 100\% \approx 99.9886\%
$$

**Step 5 — Convert "nines" notation.**
Number of nines $= -\log_{10}(1 - A_{dec})$ where $A_{dec}$ is decimal availability.

$$
\text{Nines} = -\log_{10}(1 - 0.999886) = -\log_{10}(0.000114) \approx 3.94
$$

So the system is approximately a **"four nines"** availability system.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Storage Overhead in a Replicated Graph

**Given:**
- Raw graph data size = $S_{raw}$
- Replication Factor = $RF$
- Index overhead = $I_{oh}$ (as a fraction of $S_{raw}$)

**Step 1 — Compute replicated data size.**

$$
S_{replicated} = S_{raw} \times RF
$$

**Step 2 — Add index overhead.**

$$
S_{total} = S_{replicated} + (S_{raw} \times I_{oh}) = S_{raw} \times (RF + I_{oh})
$$

**Step 3 — Compute overhead percentage.**

$$
O_{s} = \frac{S_{total} - S_{raw}}{S_{raw}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$
O_{s} = \frac{S_{raw}(RF + I_{oh}) - S_{raw}}{S_{raw}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
O_{s} = (RF + I_{oh} - 1) \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example:** $RF = 3$, $I_{oh} = 0.2$

$$
O_{s} = (3 + 0.2 - 1) \times 100\% = 2.2 \times 100\% = 220\%
$$

This means a **3× replicated graph with 20% index overhead** consumes **220% more storage** than the raw data alone — a critical trade-off in distributed graph DB design.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: CAP Theorem Trade-off Bound (Conceptual Proof Sketch)

The **CAP Theorem**, formally proven by **Eric Brewer (2000)**, states that in a distributed data store, only **two of the three** guarantees can be fully achieved simultaneously under a network partition:

$$
C \cap A \cap P \neq \emptyset \quad \text{(impossible under partition)}
$$

$$
C \cup A \cup P = \text{partial selection of 2} \quad \text{(forced trade-off)}
$$

Where:
- **C** = Consistency (all nodes see the same data at the same time)
- **A** = Availability (every request receives a response)
- **P** = Partition Tolerance (system continues despite network splits)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Graph DBs are categorized as **CA** (e.g., Neo4j in single-node mode) or **AP** (e.g., JanusGraph, Cassandra-based graph layers) systems. **CP graph DBs are rare** but exist (e.g., some configurations of Neo4j Causal Clustering).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Non-Functional Characteristics vs Graph DB Examples

| Characteristic | Neo4j | JanusGraph | TigerGraph | Amazon Neptune |
|----------------|-------|------------|------------|----------------|
| **Performance** | High (in-memory cache) | Moderate | Very High (parallel) | High (cloud-optimized) |
| **Scalability** | Vertical + Limited Horizontal | High (Horizontal via Cassandra/HBase) | High (MPP) | High (Serverless) |
| **Availability** | High (Causal Cluster) | High | High | Very High (Multi-AZ) |
| **Consistency** | Strong (ACID) | Eventual (BASE) | Strong | Eventual / Strong |
| **Security** | RBAC + LDAP | RBAC | RBAC + AD | IAM + VPC + KMS |
| **Maintainability** | High (Cypher, APOC) | Moderate (Gremlin) | High (GSQL) | Managed (low ops) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Taxonomy of Non-Functional Characteristics

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Non Functional Characteristics of Graph DBs] --> B[Operational Quality]
    A --> C[Data Quality]
    A --> D[System Quality]
    A --> E[Ecosystem Quality]

    B --> B1[Performance Latency and Throughput]
    B --> B2[Scalability Vertical and Horizontal]
    B --> B3[Availability Uptime SLA]

    C --> C1[Reliability ACID vs BASE]
    C --> C2[Data Integrity Constraints]
    C --> C3[Consistency CAP Model]

    D --> D1[Security AuthN and AuthZ and Encryption]
    D --> D2[Maintainability Schema Evolution]
    D --> D3[Extensibility UDFs and Plugins]

    E --> E1[Portability GQL and Cypher]
    E --> E2[Interoperability RDF and JSON]
    E --> E3[Compliance GDPR and ISO]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: CAP Theorem Trade-off Flow for Graph Databases

```mermaid
graph LR
    CAP[CAP Theorem Partition Event] --> Q{Which two to guarantee}
    Q --> CA[CA Neo4j Standalone]
    Q --> AP[AP JanusGraph and Neptune]
    Q --> CP[CP Rare Configurations]

    CA --> CA1[Strong Consistency]
    CA --> CA2[High Availability]
    CA --> CA3[No Partition Tolerance]

    AP --> AP1[High Availability]
    AP --> AP2[Partition Tolerance]
    AP --> AP3[Eventual Consistency]

    CP --> CP1[Strong Consistency]
    CP --> CP2[Partition Tolerance]
    CP --> CP3[Availability Sacrifice]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Graph DB Cluster

```mermaid
graph TD
    Client[Client Application] --> LB[Load Balancer]
    LB --> N1[Graph Node 1 Leader]
    LB --> N2[Graph Node 2 Follower]
    LB --> N3[Graph Node 3 Follower]

    N1 --> S1[Storage Layer Native or Cassandra]
    N2 --> S1
    N3 --> S1

    S1 --> Cache[In Memory Page Cache]
    S1 --> Log[Write Ahead Log WAL]
    S1 --> Idx[Index Layer]

    Idx --> Idx1[Label Index]
    Idx --> Idx2[Property Index]
    Idx --> Idx3[Full Text Index]

    N1 --> Sec[Security Layer RBAC and TLS]
    N2 --> Sec
    N3 --> Sec

    Sec --> Mon[Monitoring and Metrics]
    Mon --> Alert[Alerting System]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node IDs above are **purely alphanumeric with letter prefixes** (e.g., `N1`, `S1`, `Idx1`) — never using reserved Mermaid keywords like `end` or `subgraph` as standalone node names. Labels are clean uppercase text without bold/italic markers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define non-functional characteristics of a graph database. List any six with one-line descriptions.** *(3 Marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Answer]**

**Definition:** Non-functional characteristics (or **quality attributes**) are the qualitative, operational, and architectural properties that govern *how well* a graph database performs, scales, secures, and maintains data — independent of any specific functional workload.

**Six characteristics:**
1. **Performance** — Latency and throughput of graph traversals.
2. **Scalability** — Capacity to grow with data/users (vertical/horizontal).
3. **Availability** — Percentage of uptime the system guarantees.
4. **Security** — AuthN, AuthZ, encryption, and audit logging.
5. **Maintainability** — Ease of schema evolution, patching, and debugging.
6. **Portability** — Ability to migrate across platforms and cloud providers.

*(Any 6 accepted. Students must write the definition in their own words for full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. Differentiate between ACID and BASE properties in the context of graph databases.** *(3 Marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Answer]**

| Aspect | ACID | BASE |
|--------|------|------|
| **Full Form** | Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability | Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency |
| **Consistency** | Strong / immediate | Eventual |
| **Transaction Support** | Full multi-step transactions | Limited / single-row transactions |
| **Used In** | Neo4j, single-node graph DBs | JanusGraph, Neptune, distributed graph DBs |
| **Trade-off** | Slower throughput, safer for banking/financial | Higher throughput, used for social/web-scale |

*(A clear 3-row comparison table or 3 distinct differences will earn full marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Module Internal Choice Format:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the two full questions. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts of 7 + 7.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Explain the CAP theorem with respect to distributed graph databases. How do Neo4j, JanusGraph, and Amazon Neptune map to the CAP model? *(7 Marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set A]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the CAP Theorem.**
The CAP Theorem (Brewer, 2000) states that in any distributed data store, under a **network partition (P)**, only **two of three** guarantees can be simultaneously satisfied: **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, and **Partition Tolerance (P)**.

**Step 2 — Explain the three properties.**
- **Consistency:** Every read returns the most recent committed write. All nodes see identical data at the same instant.
- **Availability:** Every request to a non-failing node receives a response (no timeout).
- **Partition Tolerance:** The system continues to operate despite arbitrary message loss between nodes.

**Step 3 — Categorize graph DBs on the CAP spectrum.**

$$
\text{CAP}_{\text{GraphDB}} = \{(C,A) \;\vert\; (A,P) \;\vert\; (C,P)\} \quad \text{(choose 2)}
$$

- **Neo4j (Causal Clustering mode):** Primarily a **CP** system — favours consistency; during partition, followers may become read-only.
- **JanusGraph (over Cassandra):** A classic **AP** system — uses Cassandra's eventual consistency; prioritizes availability and partition tolerance.
- **Amazon Neptune:** Configurable — defaults to **AP** with six replicas across three AZs; can be tuned toward stronger consistency.

**Step 4 — Justify with real-world scenarios.**
For **banking fraud detection**, CP (Neo4j) is preferred because stale data is unacceptable. For **social-network feeds**, AP (Neptune) is preferred because the system must always respond.

*(Examiner's note: 3 marks for theorem statement + 2 marks for property explanations + 2 marks for graph DB mapping.)*

#### (b) Discuss in detail the non-functional characteristics — **Performance, Scalability, and Security** — for a graph database system. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Performance:**
- Measured via **latency (ms)**, **throughput (ops/sec)**, **P95/P99 response time**.
- Achieved in graph DBs through **index-free adjacency** (direct pointer traversal), **in-memory caching**, and **query optimization** (Cypher planner).
- Example: Neo4j's traversal speed of **1,000–10,000 hops/second** on social graphs.

**Scalability:**
- **Vertical scaling** — adding CPU/RAM (Neo4j supports up to single-machine limits).
- **Horizontal scaling** — sharding, replication (JanusGraph, Neptune, TigerGraph).
- **Graph-specific challenge:** *Supernode hotspots* in highly connected nodes.
- **Mitigation:** Graph partitioning strategies (e.g., METIS partitioning).

**Security:**
- Layers: **Authentication** (LDAP, Kerberos, OAuth), **Authorization** (RBAC with role hierarchy), **Encryption** (TLS in-transit, AES-256 at-rest), **Auditing** (log of all queries).
- **Fine-grained access control:** Property-level and label-level permissions in Neo4j 5.x.

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for Performance, 2 for Scalability, 2 for Security, 1 for conclusion/example.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### (a) What is meant by *availability* in graph databases? Derive the availability formula from MTBF and MTTR, and compute the percentage availability for MTBF = 4,380 hours and MTTR = 12 hours. Comment on the "nines" rating. *(7 Marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set B]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Availability is the *probability* that a graph database system is operational and accessible when a request is made, typically measured as a percentage of total time.

**Derivation:**

$$
A = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Total Time}} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

$$
A_{\%} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%
$$

**Numerical Computation:**

$$
A_{\%} = \frac{4380}{4380 + 12} \times 100\% = \frac{4380}{4392} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A_{\%} = 0.99727 \times 100\% = 99.727\%
$$

**Nines Calculation:**

$$
A_{dec} = 0.99727
$$

$$
\text{Nines} = -\log_{10}(1 - 0.99727) = -\log_{10}(0.00273) \approx 2.564
$$

**Comment:** The system rates as approximately **"two-and-a-half nines"**, which is **insufficient for mission-critical applications** (banking, healthcare). For "five nines" (99.999%), MTTR must drop to approximately **26 seconds/year**.

#### (b) Compare and contrast **Functional vs Non-Functional Characteristics** of a graph database. Provide a tabular comparison covering at least 6 dimensions. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Dimension | Functional Characteristics | Non-Functional Characteristics |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Definition** | *What* the system does | *How well* the system does it |
| **Focus** | Features, operations, data manipulation | Quality, performance, security, scalability |
| **Examples** | CRUD, traversals, pattern matching, indexing | Latency, uptime, encryption, replication |
| **Measurability** | Often binary (present/absent) | Quantitative (%, ms, ops/sec) |
| **Test Method** | Unit tests, integration tests | Load tests, stress tests, SLA audits |
| **Customer Concern** | "Can it perform graph queries?" | "Is it fast, secure, always-on?" |
| **Standard Reference** | User requirements specification | ISO/IEC 25010 quality model |

**Conclusion:** Functional characteristics define the *scope* of the graph DB, while non-functional characteristics define its *production-grade quality*. Both are mandatory for a deployable enterprise system.

*(Examiner's note: Full tabular comparison carries 5 marks, conclusion 2 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing functional with non-functional** — Students often write "Cypher query support" as a non-functional characteristic. It is **functional**. Correct NFRs are *performance*, *availability*, etc.
> 2. **Skipping units in formulas** — Always write **%** for availability, **ms** for latency, **ops/sec** for throughput. Missing units = **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 3. **Misapplying CAP to single-node DBs** — CAP is *only relevant* under network partitions in **distributed** systems.
> 4. **Forgetting the "nines" calculation** — When asked about availability, examiners expect both **percentage** and **nines rating**.
> 5. **No numerical examples in derivations** — KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **solved numericals** for 14-mark questions. Always substitute values.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Non-functional characteristics = Quality Attributes** (NFRs) — *how well*, not *what*.
- The **top 5 most-tested NFRs** in KTU ESE: **Performance, Scalability, Availability, Security, Maintainability**.
- **Index-free adjacency** is the secret behind graph DB *performance*.
- **CAP Theorem:** Pick 2 of {C, A, P} under network partition.
- **ACID** = Neo4j (strong consistency); **BASE** = JanusGraph, Neptune (eventual consistency).
- **Availability formula:** $A_{\%} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$
- **Storage overhead formula:** $O_{s} = (RF + I_{oh} - 1) \times 100\%$
- **Nines rating:** $n = -\log_{10}(1 - A_{dec})$
- **Throughput:** $T = \dfrac{N_{ops}}{\Delta t}$ ; **Latency:** $L = \dfrac{\Delta t}{N_{ops}}$
- **Replication Factor (RF)** dictates redundancy and storage cost.
- **GQL (ISO/IEC 39075:2024)** is the international standard query language for graphs — boosts **portability**.
- **RBAC + TLS + AES-256** = standard security stack for production graph DBs.
- **Supernode hotspots** are the *biggest scalability challenge* in real-world property graphs.
- **APOC, GDS, UDFs** = common *extensibility* mechanisms in Neo4j.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Depth and Breadth-First Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Depth and Breadth-First Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Depth-First Search (DFS)** and **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** are the two foundational graph traversal algorithms prescribed in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 4 – Graph Analysis) for systematically visiting every vertex and edge of a graph $G = (V, E)$.

**Depth-First Search (DFS):** A graph traversal strategy that explores as far as possible down each branch before **backtracking**. It is implemented using either an explicit **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) stack** or the system's call **recursion stack**. Formally, given a start vertex $s \in V$, DFS generates a **Depth-First Spanning Tree (DFST)** rooted at $s$ using the set of **tree edges** (those used to discover a new vertex) and **back edges** (edges to already-visited ancestors).

**Breadth-First Search (BFS):** A graph traversal strategy that explores all the immediate neighbours of the current vertex at depth $d$ before progressing to vertices at depth $d+1$. It is implemented using a **FIFO (First-In-First-Out) queue**. Formally, BFS partitions the vertices into distinct **levels** $L_0, L_1, \ldots, L_k$ where $L_0 = \{s\}$ and $L_i$ contains all vertices at shortest path distance exactly $i$ from $s$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy 1 — The Labyrinth (DFS):** Imagine you are dropped into a maze. You follow **one corridor** to its absolute dead-end, then retrace your steps until you find an unexplored turn. This is DFS — *go deep, then back up, then try the next sibling corridor.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy 2 — Ripples in a Pond (BFS):** Imagine throwing a stone into still water. The first ripple touches all stones at distance 1 simultaneously, then the second ripple touches all stones at distance 2, and so on. This is BFS — *visit all close-by nodes before any far-away node.*

| Property | DFS (Labyrinth Walker) | BFS (Pond Ripples) |
|---|---|---|
| Core Metaphor | Recursive deep-dive | Layered wavefront |
| Data Structure | **Stack** (Recursion) | **Queue** |
| Exploration Pattern | Vertical, then horizontal | Horizontal, then vertical |
| Path Optimality | Not guaranteed shortest | **Guaranteed shortest** (unweighted) |

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Terminology

- **Time Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ for both algorithms (the **linear-time** property is a board-exam favourite).
- **Space Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert)$ for both (in worst case).
- **Discovery Time (d[v]) / Finishing Time (f[v]):** Used in DFS for parenthesis theorem and topological sort.
- **Level / Distance from source (dist[v]):** Used in BFS to record minimum number of edges from $s$.
- **Edge Colours in DFS:** **Tree edge**, **Back edge**, **Forward edge**, **Cross edge** (directed graph).
- **Edge Colours in BFS:** **Tree edge**, **Non-tree edge** (either cross or back in undirected).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of BFS — Step-by-Step

BFS visits vertices in **non-decreasing order of distance from the source vertex $s$**.

**Step 1 — Initialisation:** For every vertex $v \in V$, set $\text{color}[v] = \text{WHITE}$ (unvisited), $\text{dist}[v] = \infty$, and $\text{parent}[v] = \text{NIL}$. Set $\text{dist}[s] = 0$.

**Step 2 — Enqueue source:** Enqueue $s$ into queue $Q$. Mark $\text{color}[s] = \text{GRAY}$ (discovered).

**Step 3 — Dequeue & Inspect:** While $Q$ is not empty, dequeue a vertex $u$. For every neighbour $v$ of $u$:
  - If $\text{color}[v] == \text{WHITE}$, then $\text{color}[v] = \text{GRAY}$, $\text{dist}[v] = \text{dist}[u] + 1$, $\text{parent}[v] = u$, and **enqueue $v$**.

**Step 4 — Finish:** Set $\text{color}[u] = \text{BLACK}$ (fully processed).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Queue?** The queue enforces FIFO order. A vertex can only be enqueued once (when it is first discovered), so it will be dequeued and processed in the exact order of its distance from $s$.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of DFS — Step-by-Step

DFS dives deep using recursion (or an explicit stack), then backtracks.

**Step 1 — Initialisation:** $\forall v \in V$, $\text{color}[v] = \text{WHITE}$, $\text{parent}[v] = \text{NIL}$, $\text{time} = 0$.

**Step 2 — Visit:** From a WHITE vertex $u$, assign $\text{time} = \text{time} + 1$, $\text{d}[u] = \text{time}$, $\text{color}[u] = \text{GRAY}$.

**Step 3 — Recurse:** For each neighbour $v$ of $u$, if $\text{color}[v] == \text{WHITE}$, set $\text{parent}[v] = u$ and **recurse into DFS-Visit(v)**.

**Step 4 — Finish:** When all neighbours exhausted, set $\text{color}[u] = \text{BLACK}$ and $\text{time} = \text{time} + 1$, $\text{f}[u] = \text{time}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Edge Classification in Directed Graphs (Board Favourite):**
> - **Tree Edge** $(u,v)$: $v$ discovered by $u$ (i.e., $\text{parent}[v] = u$).
> - **Back Edge** $(u,v)$: $v$ is an ancestor of $u$ in DFS tree (a cycle indicator).
> - **Forward Edge** $(u,v)$: $v$ is a descendant but not a child.
> - **Cross Edge** $(u,v)$: $v$ is in a different DFS subtree or already finished.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise the following table for the End Semester Examination (ESE).**

| Concept | Formula / Property | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| BFS Time Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Operations |
| DFS Time Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Operations |
| BFS Space Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Queue size |
| DFS Space Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Recursion stack |
| BFS Shortest Path (unweighted) | $\text{dist}[v] = $ min. edges from $s$ | Edges |
| DFS Parenthesis Theorem | For $u, v$: $d[u] < d[v] < f[v] < f[u]$ iff $v$ is descendant of $u$ | Timestamps |
| DFS Cycle Detection | A **back edge** $\iff$ cycle exists in directed graph | Boolean |
| BFS Bipartite Check | Graph is bipartite $\iff$ **no edge** connects two vertices of same level colour | Boolean |
| Topological Sort | Reverse of DFS finishing order $f[v]$ | Permutation |
| BFS Level Count | Number of distinct $\text{dist}$ values = eccentricity bound | Integer |

> **Note on notation:** In the above table, $\vert V \vert$ and $\vert E \vert$ denote the **cardinality** (count) of the vertex set and edge set respectively. Always use the proper LaTeX escape `\vert V \vert` in your answer sheets to avoid layout errors.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BFS in Networks:** Finding the minimum number of hops between two routers in a LAN topology, peer-to-peer (BitTorrent) neighbour discovery, and **shortest path** in unweighted social networks.
