- **DFS in Compilers:** Detecting circular dependencies in module imports, **topological sorting** of task graphs in build systems (Make, Maven), and **garbage collection** in JVMs (mark-and-sweep).
- **BFS in AI / Robotics:** Breadth-first search is foundational to the **Breadth-First Search planner** for robot navigation on a uniform-cost grid.
- **DFS in AI:** Used in depth-limited search and IDA* as a subroutine.
- **Both in Graph Databases (Neo4j-style):** The Cypher query engine uses BFS/DFS variants to find all reachable nodes from a starting entity (the *expansion phase* of a pattern match).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Pseudocode & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Reference Graph $G$ for Worked Example

We will trace BFS and DFS on the following **undirected graph** with 6 vertices and 6 edges:

$$V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$$
$$E = \{(1,2),\ (1,3),\ (2,4),\ (2,5),\ (3,6),\ (5,6)\}$$

The adjacency list representation is:

| Vertex | Adjacency List |
|---|---|
| 1 | 2, 3 |
| 2 | 1, 4, 5 |
| 3 | 1, 6 |
| 4 | 2 |
| 5 | 2, 6 |
| 6 | 3, 5 |

## 3.2 BFS Execution Trace (Starting at Source $s = 1$)

**Algorithm (Cormen et al. style):**

```
BFS(G, s):
  for each v in G.V - {s}: color[v] = WHITE; dist[v] = ∞; parent[v] = NIL
  color[s] = GRAY; dist[s] = 0; parent[s] = NIL
  Q = empty queue
  ENQUEUE(Q, s)
  while Q is not empty:
      u = DEQUEUE(Q)
      for each v in G.Adj[u]:
          if color[v] == WHITE:
              color[v] = GRAY
              dist[v] = dist[u] + 1
              parent[v] = u
              ENQUEUE(Q, v)
      color[u] = BLACK
```

**Detailed Step-by-Step Trace:**

| Step | Action | Queue State (left→right) | Vertex Processed | Newly Discovered (dist, parent) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initialise & ENQUEUE(1) | `[1]` | — | dist[1]=0, parent[1]=NIL |
| 2 | DEQUEUE→u=1, inspect 2,3 | `[2, 3]` | 1 → BLACK | dist[2]=1, p=1 ; dist[3]=1, p=1 |
| 3 | DEQUEUE→u=2, inspect 1,4,5 | `[3, 4, 5]` | 2 → BLACK | dist[4]=2, p=2 ; dist[5]=2, p=2 |
| 4 | DEQUEUE→u=3, inspect 1,6 | `[4, 5, 6]` | 3 → BLACK | dist[6]=2, p=3 |
| 5 | DEQUEUE→u=4, inspect 2 (skip) | `[5, 6]` | 4 → BLACK | none |
| 6 | DEQUEUE→u=5, inspect 2,6 (skip) | `[6]` | 5 → BLACK | none |
| 7 | DEQUEUE→u=6, inspect 3,5 (skip) | `[]` | 6 → BLACK | none |
| 8 | Q empty → **terminate** | — | — | — |

> **Final BFS Visit Order:** $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 6$
>
> **Level partition:** $L_0 = \{1\},\ L_1 = \{2, 3\},\ L_2 = \{4, 5, 6\}$
>
> **BFS Spanning Tree edges** (parent pointers): $(1,2),\ (1,3),\ (2,4),\ (2,5),\ (3,6)$
>
> **Cross edge** (undirected, not in tree): $(5,6)$ — a level-edge between $L_2$ and $L_2$.

---

## 3.3 DFS Execution Trace (Starting at Source $s = 1$) — Recursive

**Algorithm (Cormen et al. style):**

```
DFS(G):
  for each v in G.V: color[v] = WHITE; parent[v] = NIL
  time = 0
  for each v in G.V:
      if color[v] == WHITE: DFS-Visit(v)

DFS-Visit(u):
  time = time + 1
  d[u] = time
  color[u] = GRAY
  for each v in G.Adj[u]:
      if color[v] == WHITE:
          parent[v] = u
          DFS-Visit(v)
  color[u] = BLACK
  time = time + 1
  f[u] = time
```

**Detailed Step-by-Step Trace** (using adjacency order = list order):

| Call / Return | Vertex | Action | time | d[·] / f[·] updates | Stack (calls) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DFS-Visit(1) | enter 1 | 1 | d[1]=1, color=GRAY | [1] |
| 2 | → 1's nbr 2 is WHITE | recurse | — | parent[2]=1 | [1, 2] |
| 3 | DFS-Visit(2) | enter 2 | 2 | d[2]=2, color=GRAY | [1, 2] |
| 4 | → 2's nbr 1 is GRAY | skip | — | — | [1, 2] |
| 5 | → 2's nbr 4 is WHITE | recurse | — | parent[4]=2 | [1, 2, 4] |
| 6 | DFS-Visit(4) | enter 4 | 3 | d[4]=3, color=GRAY | [1, 2, 4] |
| 7 | → 4's nbr 2 is GRAY | skip | — | — | [1, 2, 4] |
| 8 | finish 4 | exit | 4 | f[4]=4, color=BLACK | [1, 2] |
| 9 | → 2's nbr 5 is WHITE | recurse | — | parent[5]=2 | [1, 2, 5] |
| 10 | DFS-Visit(5) | enter 5 | 5 | d[5]=5, color=GRAY | [1, 2, 5] |
| 11 | → 5's nbr 2 is BLACK | skip | — | — | [1, 2, 5] |
| 12 | → 5's nbr 6 is WHITE | recurse | — | parent[6]=5 | [1, 2, 5, 6] |
| 13 | DFS-Visit(6) | enter 6 | 6 | d[6]=6, color=GRAY | [1, 2, 5, 6] |
| 14 | → 6's nbr 3 is WHITE | recurse | — | parent[3]=6 | [1, 2, 5, 6, 3] |
| 15 | DFS-Visit(3) | enter 3 | 7 | d[3]=7, color=GRAY | [1, 2, 5, 6, 3] |
| 16 | → 3's nbr 1 is GRAY | skip | — | — | [1, 2, 5, 6, 3] |
| 17 | → 3's nbr 6 is GRAY | skip | — | — | [1, 2, 5, 6, 3] |
| 18 | finish 3 | exit | 8 | f[3]=8, color=BLACK | [1, 2, 5, 6] |
| 19 | → 6's nbr 5 is GRAY | skip | — | — | [1, 2, 5, 6] |
| 20 | finish 6 | exit | 9 | f[6]=9, color=BLACK | [1, 2, 5] |
| 21 | finish 5 | exit | 10 | f[5]=10, color=BLACK | [1, 2] |
| 22 | finish 2 | exit | 11 | f[2]=11, color=BLACK | [1] |
| 23 | → 1's nbr 3 is BLACK | skip | — | — | [1] |
| 24 | finish 1 | exit | 12 | f[1]=12, color=BLACK | [ ] |

> **Final DFS Visit Order:** $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 6 \rightarrow 3$
>
> **Discovery/Finish timestamps:** $d[\cdot] = (1,2,3,5,6,7)$, $f[\cdot] = (12,11,8,4,10,9)$
>
> **DFS Spanning Tree edges:** $(1,2),\ (2,4),\ (2,5),\ (5,6),\ (6,3)$
>
> **Back edges** (undirected — any edge to a GRAY ancestor): $(1,3)$ via path $3 \to 1$ ancestor (cycle $1\text{-}2\text{-}5\text{-}6\text{-}3$).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the KTU-style comparison table examiners love to ask as a 7-mark question.**

| Parameter | BFS | DFS |
|---|---|---|
| Data Structure | **Queue** (FIFO) | **Stack** / Recursion (LIFO) |
| Order of visit | **Level-by-level** (broad) | **Branch-by-branch** (deep) |
| Visit Order (on example graph, from 1) | $1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$ | $1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 3$ |
| Time Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ |
| Space Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ |
| Shortest Path (unweighted) | **Yes**, optimal | **No**, not guaranteed |
| Cycle Detection (Undirected) | Possible (any visited cross-edge) | Possible (back edge) |
| Cycle Detection (Directed) | Difficult | **Yes**, via back edge |
| Topological Sort | Not directly | **Yes**, reverse of $f[\cdot]$ |
| Bipartite Test | **Yes**, by 2-colouring levels | Yes, by 2-colouring DFS tree |
| Memory on wide shallow graphs | **High** (queue holds all level) | **Low** (one path at a time) |
| Memory on deep narrow graphs | **Low** | **High** (stack depth = path length) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Reference Graph (Logical Topology)

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1((1)) --- node2((2))
    node1((1)) --- node3((3))
    node2((2)) --- node4((4))
    node2((2)) --- node5((5))
    node3((3)) --- node6((6))
    node5((5)) --- node6((6))
```

## 4.2 BFS Traversal Block Diagram (Wavefront Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Source Vertex 1"] --> B["Enqueue 1, dist 0"]
    B --> C["Dequeue u"]
    C --> D{"Neighbour v WHITE?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Mark GRAY, set dist and parent"]
    E --> F["Enqueue v"]
    F --> G
    D -- No --> G["More neighbours?"]
    G -- Yes --> C
    G -- No --> H["Mark u BLACK"]
    H --> I{"Queue empty?"}
    I -- No --> C
    I -- Yes --> J["TERMINATE: BFS Spanning Tree built"]
```

## 4.3 DFS Traversal Block Diagram (Recursive Descent Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Source Vertex 1"] --> B["DFS-Visit u: assign d u and set GRAY"]
    B --> C["Pick neighbour v of u"]
    C --> D{"v WHITE?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Set parent v = u"]
    E --> F["Recursive DFS-Visit v"]
    F --> G
    D -- No --> G{"More neighbours?"}
    G -- Yes --> C
    G -- No --> H["Assign f u and set BLACK"]
    H --> I{"Backtrack to parent or new source?"}
    I -- Backtrack to parent --> B
    I -- Unvisited vertex remains --> J["Pick next WHITE vertex in V"]
    J --> B
    I -- All visited --> K["TERMINATE: DFS Forest built"]
```

## 4.4 BFS Level-Wise Spanning Tree (Visual Output)

```mermaid
graph TB
    L0["LEVEL 0: dist 0"] --- L1A["LEVEL 1: dist 1"]
    L0 --- L1B["LEVEL 1: dist 1"]
    L1A --- L2A["LEVEL 2: dist 2"]
    L1A --- L2B["LEVEL 2: dist 2"]
    L1B --- L2C["LEVEL 2: dist 2"]
```

Mapped to our example: L0 = {1}, L1 = {2, 3}, L2 = {4, 5, 6}.

## 4.5 DFS Spanning Tree (Visual Output)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root1(("1")) --> child2(("2"))
    child2(("2")) --> child4(("4"))
    child2(("2")) --> child5(("5"))
    child5(("5")) --> child6(("6"))
    child6(("6")) --> child3(("3"))
```

This DFS tree corresponds to the trace: $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4$ (backtrack) $\rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 6 \rightarrow 3$ (backtrack to root, terminate).

## 4.6 BFS vs DFS — Conceptual Control Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BFS_Block["BFS: QUEUE LAYER BY LAYER"]
        BFS1["1 visited"] --> BFS2["2 3 visited"] --> BFS3["4 5 6 visited"]
    end
    subgraph DFS_Block["DFS: STACK RECURSIVE DEEP"]
        DFS1["1 visited"] --> DFS2["2 visited"] --> DFS3["4 visited"]
        DFS3 --> DFS4["Backtrack, visit 5"] --> DFS5["Visit 6"] --> DFS6["Visit 3, backtrack out"]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** `dist[v]` (BFS shortest distance in edges) with the **edge weight**. BFS shortest-path guarantee holds **only for unweighted graphs**. In weighted graphs, BFS does *not* give shortest path — use **Dijkstra's** instead.
> 2. **Do NOT forget** to specify the **edge classification** asked in the question. Writing "DFS finds cycles" alone is incomplete; explicitly state **"a back edge in DFS indicates a cycle in a directed graph."**
> 3. **Many students mark the same vertex as BLACK twice** when tracing by hand. Use a clean **timestamp table** $(d, f)$ with time monotonically increasing — KTU examiners deduct $\frac{1}{2}$ mark per wrong timestamp.
> 4. **Adjacency list order matters** for DFS visit order. If the question does not specify, **state your assumption** ("assuming adjacency lists are scanned in increasing vertex order").

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*With the help of a suitable example, distinguish between Depth-First Search (DFS) and Breadth-First Search (BFS) in terms of their data structure and order of traversal.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | BFS | DFS |
|---|---|---|
| Data structure | Queue (FIFO) | Stack / Recursion (LIFO) |
| Traversal order | Level-by-level (all neighbours first) | Branch-by-branch (deepest first) |

**Example:** On the graph with edges $(1,2), (1,3), (2,4)$ starting from vertex 1:
- **BFS** visits in order: $1, 2, 3, 4$ (queue-based, neighbours of 1 are processed before children of 2).
- **DFS** visits in order: $1, 2, 4, 3$ (recursion dives into 2, then 4, then backtracks to visit 3).

Both algorithms run in $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ time but differ in the visit order due to their underlying data structure.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Explain how DFS can be used to detect a cycle in a directed graph. Mention the role of edge classification.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

DFS classifies each edge of a directed graph as one of: **tree edge, back edge, forward edge, or cross edge**. A **back edge** $(u, v)$ is an edge that points from a descendant $u$ to an ancestor $v$ in the DFS tree. A directed graph contains a cycle **if and only if** the DFS traversal produces **at least one back edge**.

**Procedure:** Run DFS on the graph. During DFS-Visit$(u)$, if a neighbour $v$ is found with $\text{color}[v] = \text{GRAY}$ (i.e., $v$ is currently on the recursion stack), then $(u, v)$ is a back edge and hence a cycle exists. Conversely, if DFS finishes without any back edge, the graph is a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice, 2 × 14 = 28 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**
> **(a)** Explain the Breadth-First Search algorithm with its pseudocode. Show step-by-step the BFS traversal on the graph $G$ defined by $V = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ and $E = \{(A,B), (A,C), (B,D), (B,E), (C,F), (E,F)\}$ starting from vertex $A$. Construct the BFS spanning tree. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the Depth-First Search algorithm with its pseudocode. List all four edge classifications in DFS for directed graphs and state the role of DFS in **topological sorting**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — BFS Algorithm and Trace (7 Marks)

**Pseudocode:**
```
BFS(G, s):
  for each v in G.V - {s}:
      color[v] = WHITE
      dist[v] = infinity
      parent[v] = NIL
  color[s] = GRAY
  dist[s] = 0
  parent[s] = NIL
  Q = empty queue
  ENQUEUE(Q, s)
  while Q is not empty:
      u = DEQUEUE(Q)
      for each v in G.Adj[u]:
          if color[v] == WHITE:
              color[v] = GRAY
              dist[v] = dist[u] + 1
              parent[v] = u
              ENQUEUE(Q, v)
      color[u] = BLACK
```

**Step-by-step BFS trace from $s = A$:**

| Step | Dequeue (u) | Queue After | Discovered (v, dist[v], parent[v]) | Colour of u |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | [B, C] | (B, 1, A), (C, 1, A) | A → BLACK |
| 2 | B | [C, D, E] | (D, 2, B), (E, 2, B) | B → BLACK |
| 3 | C | [D, E, F] | (F, 2, C) | C → BLACK |
| 4 | D | [E, F] | none | D → BLACK |
| 5 | E | [F] | none (F is GRAY) | E → BLACK |
| 6 | F | [ ] | none | F → BLACK |

**Visit order:** $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow E \rightarrow F$
**BFS Spanning Tree edges:** $(A,B),\ (A,C),\ (B,D),\ (B,E),\ (C,F)$
**Cross edge (non-tree):** $(E,F)$ — both at level 2.

#### Part (b) — DFS Algorithm, Edge Classification & Topological Sort (7 Marks)

**Pseudocode:**
```
DFS(G):
  for each v in G.V:
      color[v] = WHITE
      parent[v] = NIL
  time = 0
  for each v in G.V:
      if color[v] == WHITE:
          DFS-Visit(v)

DFS-Visit(u):
  time = time + 1
  d[u] = time
  color[u] = GRAY
  for each v in G.Adj[u]:
      if color[v] == WHITE:
          parent[v] = u
          DFS-Visit(v)
      else if color[v] == GRAY: record as BACK edge
      else if color[v] == BLACK:
          if d[u] < d[v]: record as FORWARD edge
          else: record as CROSS edge
  color[u] = BLACK
  time = time + 1
  f[u] = time
```

**Edge Classification in Directed Graphs:**
1. **Tree Edge** $(u, v)$: $v$ is discovered by $u$ — present in DFS forest.
2. **Back Edge** $(u, v)$: $v$ is an ancestor of $u$; $v$ is GRAY when edge is examined. Detects cycles.
3. **Forward Edge** $(u, v)$: $v$ is a descendant of $u$ but not a direct child; $v$ is BLACK with $d[v] > d[u]$.
4. **Cross Edge** $(u, v)$: $v$ is in a different DFS tree or subtree; $v$ is BLACK with $d[v] < d[u]$ and $v$ is not an ancestor.

**Topological Sorting using DFS:** For a DAG, run DFS and list the vertices in **decreasing order of finishing time $f[v]$**. This produces a linear ordering such that for every directed edge $(u, v)$, $u$ appears before $v$ in the order. The justification is the **Parenthesis Theorem**: $d[u] < d[v] < f[v] < f[u]$ for any ancestor-descendant pair ensures descendants finish before ancestors when reversed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4]**
> **(a)** Define the terms *discovery time* $d[v]$ and *finishing time* $f[v]$ in DFS. State and prove the **Parenthesis Theorem** for DFS. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss any **four real-world applications** of BFS and DFS, with one specific example for each algorithm. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Parenthesis Theorem (7 Marks)

**Definitions:** During DFS, a global timer `time` is incremented at vertex entry and exit.
- **Discovery time $d[v]$:** The value of `time` when vertex $v$ is first discovered (color set to GRAY).
- **Finishing time $f[v]$:** The value of `time` when DFS-Visit$(v)$ completes (color set to BLACK).
Thus $1 \le d[v] < f[v] \le 2 \vert V \vert$.

**Theorem (Parenthesis Theorem):** For any two vertices $u, v$ in a DFS of a directed or undirected graph, exactly one of the following holds:
1. The intervals $[d[u], f[u]]$ and $[d[v], f[v]]$ are **disjoint** (i.e., $f[u] < d[v]$ or $f[v] < d[u]$).
2. The interval $[d[u], f[u]]$ is **contained within** $[d[v], f[v]]$ (i.e., $d[v] < d[u]$ and $f[u] < f[v]$), meaning $u$ is a descendant of $v$.
3. The interval $[d[v], f[v]]$ is **contained within** $[d[u], f[u]]$, meaning $v$ is a descendant of $u$.

**Proof Sketch:** Consider the first of $u$ and $v$ to be discovered, say $u$ (so $d[u] < d[v]$).
- *Case 1:* $v$ is not a descendant of $u$. Then when $v$ is discovered, the recursion is *not* inside DFS-Visit$(u)$ with respect to $v$'s path. Hence $f[v] < f[u]$, giving disjoint intervals in reverse: $[d[v], f[v]]$ ends before $[d[u], f[u]]$ — contradiction to $d[u] < d[v]$ unless $f[v] < d[u]$… corrected: if $v$ is not a descendant, then $f[v] < d[u]$, but we assumed $d[u] < d[v]$, contradiction. So actually the first case gives $d[v] < d[u]$ and $f[v] < d[u]$, meaning $v$ finished before $u$ started.
- *Case 2:* $v$ is a descendant of $u$. Then $v$ is discovered during the recursive descent from $u$, so $d[u] < d[v]$, and $v$ must finish before returning to $u$, so $f[v] < f[u]$. This gives containment: $[d[v], f[v]] \subset [d[u], f[u]]$.

This proves the theorem. The "parenthesis" interpretation: the discovery and finish times form well-nested intervals, like matched parentheses $(u (\, v \,) u)$.

#### Part (b) — Real-World Applications (7 Marks, 1.75 each)

1. **BFS — Shortest Path in Unweighted Social Network:** BFS on a Facebook friendship graph from a source user $s$ finds the user with minimum hop-distance in $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ — the "people you may know" suggestion engine.

2. **BFS — Web Crawler (Layered Indexing):** Googlebot uses BFS to crawl pages starting from seed URLs; it visits all pages within $k$ hyperlinks before going to depth $k+1$, ensuring broad coverage.

3. **DFS — Topological Sort in Build Systems:** Unix `make` orders compilation of source files (`.o` dependencies) using DFS-based topological sort to avoid circular dependency errors.

4. **DFS — Cycle Detection in Module Imports:** Python's import system uses DFS to detect circular imports (`from a import b` while `a` is being loaded), throwing `ImportError`.

5. *(Bonus)* **DFS — Maze Solving:** A backtracking DFS robot explores a maze; upon hitting a dead-end, it backtracks to the last junction, guaranteeing exit if reachable.

6. *(Bonus)* **BFS — Bipartite Testing for Job Assignment:** BFS 2-colouring a graph of workers and jobs checks if a perfect bipartite matching constraint is satisfiable.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4: DFS & BFS)**

- ✅ BFS uses a **queue**; DFS uses a **stack / recursion**.
- ✅ Both run in $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ time and $O(\vert V \vert)$ space.
- ✅ BFS gives the **shortest path (in edges)** for unweighted graphs; DFS does not.
- ✅ BFS visit order is **level-by-level**; DFS visit order is **branch-by-branch**.
- ✅ DFS timestamps: $d[v]$ (entry) and $f[v]$ (exit) form the **parenthesis structure**.
- ✅ A **back edge** in DFS on a **directed** graph = **cycle exists**.
- ✅ **Topological sort** of a DAG = vertices listed in **decreasing $f[v]$** from DFS.
- ✅ **BFS level** of $v$ equals the **shortest-path edge count** from source.
- ✅ **Bipartite graph** test: 2-colourable by BFS levels (or DFS) without conflict.
- ✅ **BFS Spanning Tree** uses only **tree edges**; non-tree edges are **cross edges** (within a level or between adjacent levels).
- ✅ **DFS Spanning Forest** arises when the graph is disconnected — each tree is one component.
- ✅ Always **state adjacency-list order assumption** in manual traces for full marks.
- ✅ On the same graph, BFS and DFS produce **different visit orders** but the same set of visited vertices.
- ✅ **Kerala KTU examiner tip:** Present BFS and DFS comparison in a **two-column table** for crisp presentation in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Path-Finding with Dijkstra's Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Path-Finding with Dijkstra's Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dijkstra's Algorithm** is a classic **single-source shortest-path (SSSP)** algorithm in graph theory, conceived by the Dutch computer scientist **Edsger W. Dijkstra** in 1956 and published in 1959. In the context of **Graph Databases and Analysis** (PECDT751, KTU 2024 Scheme), it is the foundational path-finding routine that natively powers navigation, recommendation, and dependency-resolution queries in graph stores such as **Neo4j, Memgraph, Amazon Neptune (TinkerPop Gremlin), and JanusGraph**.

Formally, given a **weighted, directed (or undirected) graph** $G = (V, E, w)$ where $V$ is the set of vertices (nodes), $E$ the set of edges (relationships), and $w : E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ the non-negative edge-weight function, Dijkstra's algorithm computes, for every node $v \in V$, the minimum cumulative cost $\delta(s, v)$ from a designated **source node** $s$ to $v$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECDT751 – Module 4):** Path-finding with Dijkstra's Algorithm is studied as the canonical **graph-traversal-based analytic algorithm** that consumes the persisted graph topology (nodes + weighted relationships) exposed by Cypher, Gremlin, or SPARQL Property Paths and returns optimal routes between any two entities.

The algorithm is **greedy** and **label-setting**: at each iteration, it permanently labels ("settles") the unsettled vertex with the globally minimum tentative distance, then "relaxes" every outgoing edge of that vertex.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at **City A** (the source) and want to find the **cheapest flight route** to **City Z** in a network where each flight has a known airfare. You do not know the network topology in advance, but you have a live flight-booking portal that returns adjacent destinations and prices on demand.

1. You write down the cost of every direct flight from A: 50 to B, 100 to C, 40 to D.
2. You always board the **cheapest unresolved flight first** (greedy pick = minimum tentative distance).
3. Whenever a flight takes you to a new city, you ask: *"Is this fare cheaper than what I had noted earlier for that city?"* — this is **edge relaxation**.
4. Once a city is reached via the lowest possible fare, you **permanently commit** to that fare (label-setting) and never reconsider it.
5. You repeat until **City Z** is permanently committed.

Dijkstra's algorithm does exactly this — but with arbitrary positive "fares" (weights) on a generic graph, and it does it with mathematical guarantees because all weights are **non-negative**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bolded for Exam Recall)

- **Source Vertex (s)** — The fixed starting node from which distances are computed.
- **Tentative Distance `dist[v]`** — The best known (possibly unconfirmed) cost from `s` to `v`.
- **Predecessor `prev[v]`** — The vertex that currently gives the best known path to `v` (used for path reconstruction).
- **Settled Set `S`** — The set of vertices whose `dist` is final and optimal.
- **Relaxation** — The operation `dist[v] = min(dist[v], dist[u] + w(u, v))`.
- **Priority Queue (Min-Heap)** — The data structure that returns the unsettled vertex with minimum `dist` in **O(log V)** time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative weights break Dijkstra's correctness.** Once an edge has a negative cost, the "settle-once, never revisit" guarantee fails because a longer path may later become cheaper via the negative edge. For such cases, the **Bellman-Ford algorithm** is the correct substitute.
> 
> **Complexity: $\mathbf{O((V + E) \log V)}$** with a binary heap, or **$\mathbf{O(V^2)}$** with an array — both are the standard Board-exam figures for KTU ESE questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic – Step-by-Step Decomposition

The algorithm maintains three evolving data structures: an **array `dist[1..n]`** initialised to $\infty$ (except `dist[s] = 0`), a **priority queue `Q`** of unsettled vertices keyed by their current `dist`, and a **set `S`** of permanently settled vertices.

1. **Initialisation Phase** — For every vertex $v \in V$, set $\text{dist}[v] \leftarrow \infty$ and $\text{prev}[v] \leftarrow \text{NULL}$. Set $\text{dist}[s] \leftarrow 0$ and insert all vertices into $Q$ with their keys.
2. **Greedy Selection** — While $Q$ is non-empty, extract the vertex $u$ with the minimum `dist` value. This is the **label-setting** step; $u$ is now in $S$.
3. **Early Termination (Optional)** — If $u$ equals the destination $t$, the algorithm may halt and return the optimal path to $t$.
4. **Edge Relaxation Loop** — For every outgoing edge $(u, v)$ with weight $w(u, v)$ such that $v \notin S$, evaluate the candidate cost:
   $$\text{candidate} = \text{dist}[u] + w(u, v)$$
   If $\text{candidate} < \text{dist}[v]$, then update $\text{dist}[v] \leftarrow \text{candidate}$ and $\text{prev}[v] \leftarrow u$, and decrease the key of $v$ in $Q$.
5. **Termination** — When $Q$ becomes empty, `dist[v]` holds the optimal cost $\delta(s, v)$ for all $v \in V$. The optimal path is reconstructed by backtracking from any target $t$ through `prev[]` until `s` is reached.

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why initialise to $\infty$?** Because we have *no information* about the cost to any vertex except the source itself; $\infty$ is the mathematical "unreachable" placeholder.
- **Why is the greedy choice safe?** It follows from the **cut property of shortest-path trees** combined with non-negative edge weights: once a vertex leaves $Q$ with the smallest frontier distance, no path through *unsettled* vertices can ever beat it (because every such detour would add $\geq 0$ to a strictly larger frontier distance).
- **Why "decrease key" on the heap?** Because updating `dist[v]` in step 4 must be reflected in the heap so that the *new* smaller key is correctly bubbled up. A binary min-heap supports this in $\mathbf{O(\log V)}$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Quantity | Definition | Constraints | Typical Value (Board) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V$ | Number of vertices (nodes) | $\vert V \vert \geq 1$ | — |
| $E$ | Number of edges (relationships) | $\vert E \vert \geq 0$ | — |
| $w(u, v)$ | Non-negative weight of edge $u \rightarrow v$ | $w \geq 0$ | Integer / float |
| $\text{dist}[v]$ | Tentative shortest distance $s \rightsquigarrow v$ | $\geq 0$ | Starts at $\infty$ |
| $\delta(s, v)$ | True optimal distance $s \rightsquigarrow v$ | $\delta \leq \text{dist}$ until $v \in S$ | — |
| $T_{\text{array}}$ | Naive complexity (linear scan) | — | $\mathbf{O(V^2 + E)}$ |
| $T_{\text{heap}}$ | Heap-based complexity (Fibonacci / binary) | — | $\mathbf{O((V + E) \log V)}$ |
| Space | Auxiliary storage (dist, prev, Q) | — | $\mathbf{O(V)}$ |
| Cyclic paths | Re-traversal permitted | Allowed | — |
| Negative weights | Disallowed | $w \geq 0$ | Use Bellman-Ford |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Dijkstra is the engine behind Neo4j's `shortestPath()` and `allShortestPaths()` Cypher procedures, Google Maps' road-network layer, OSPF routing in IP networks, and supply-chain "cheapest shipment path" queries in logistics ERP systems.

## 2.3 Variants Encountered in KTU 2024 Graph Database Contexts

| Variant | Data Structure | Complexity | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Naive Array** | Linear scan for min | $\mathbf{O(V^2)}$ | Dense graphs ($E \approx V^2$) |
| **Binary Heap** | Min-heap with decrease-key | $\mathbf{O((V+E) \log V)}$ | General-purpose default |
| **Fibonacci Heap** | Lazy decrease-key | $\mathbf{O(E + V \log V)}$ | Very dense / theoretical optimum |
| **Bidirectional Dijkstra** | Two simultaneous searches | ~Halves explored nodes | Point-to-point queries in road networks |
| **A* (Heuristic)** | Min-heap + heuristic $h(v)$ | Faster in practice | Maps, games, robotics |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example – The Canonical Board Problem

Consider the following **directed weighted graph** with 6 vertices $\{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ and the edge set $E$ given below. Compute the shortest distance from source $A$ to every other vertex using Dijkstra's algorithm.

**Edge List (u → v, weight):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(A, B, 4), \quad (A, C, 2), \quad (C, B, 1), \quad (C, D, 4), \\
(B, D, 5), \quad (D, E, 2), \quad (D, F, 6), \quad (E, F, 3), \quad (B, E, 7)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.1 Initialisation (Step 0)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{dist}[A] &= 0, \quad \text{dist}[B] = \infty, \quad \text{dist}[C] = \infty, \\
\text{dist}[D] &= \infty, \quad \text{dist}[E] = \infty, \quad \text{dist}[F] = \infty \\
S &= \emptyset, \quad Q = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Iteration 1 – Extract Minimum (A)

- **Greedy pick:** $A$ with $\text{dist}[A] = 0$ (global minimum).
- **Settle:** $S \leftarrow \{A\}$.
- **Relax outgoing edges of A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{candidate}(A \to B) &= 0 + 4 = 4 < \infty \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{dist}[B] = 4,\; \text{prev}[B] = A \\
\text{candidate}(A \to C) &= 0 + 2 = 2 < \infty \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{dist}[C] = 2,\; \text{prev}[C] = A
\end{aligned}
$$

**State after Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{dist} &= [A{:}0,\; B{:}4,\; C{:}2,\; D{:}\infty,\; E{:}\infty,\; F{:}\infty] \\
S &= \{A\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Iteration 2 – Extract Minimum (C)

- **Greedy pick:** $C$ with $\text{dist}[C] = 2$ (smallest in $Q$).
- **Settle:** $S \leftarrow \{A, C\}$.
- **Relax outgoing edges of C:** $(C, B, 1)$ and $(C, D, 4)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{candidate}(C \to B) &= 2 + 1 = 3 < \text{dist}[B]=4 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{dist}[B] = 3,\; \text{prev}[B] = C \\
\text{candidate}(C \to D) &= 2 + 4 = 6 < \infty \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{dist}[D] = 6,\; \text{prev}[D] = C
\end{aligned}
$$

**State after Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{dist} &= [A{:}0,\; B{:}3,\; C{:}2,\; D{:}6,\; E{:}\infty,\; F{:}\infty] \\
S &= \{A, C\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Iteration 3 – Extract Minimum (B)

- **Greedy pick:** $B$ with $\text{dist}[B] = 3$.
- **Settle:** $S \leftarrow \{A, C, B\}$.
- **Relax outgoing edges of B:** $(B, D, 5)$ and $(B, E, 7)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{candidate}(B \to D) &= 3 + 5 = 8 > \text{dist}[D]=6 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{No update (relaxation failed).} \\
\text{candidate}(B \to E) &= 3 + 7 = 10 < \infty \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{dist}[E] = 10,\; \text{prev}[E] = B
\end{aligned}
$$

**State after Iteration 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{dist} &= [A{:}0,\; B{:}3,\; C{:}2,\; D{:}6,\; E{:}10,\; F{:}\infty] \\
S &= \{A, C, B\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Iteration 4 – Extract Minimum (D)

- **Greedy pick:** $D$ with $\text{dist}[D] = 6$.
- **Settle:** $S \leftarrow \{A, C, B, D\}$.
- **Relax outgoing edges of D:** $(D, E, 2)$ and $(D, F, 6)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{candidate}(D \to E) &= 6 + 2 = 8 < \text{dist}[E]=10 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{dist}[E] = 8,\; \text{prev}[E] = D \\
\text{candidate}(D \to F) &= 6 + 6 = 12 < \infty \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{dist}[F] = 12,\; \text{prev}[F] = D
\end{aligned}
$$

**State after Iteration 4:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{dist} &= [A{:}0,\; B{:}3,\; C{:}2,\; D{:}6,\; E{:}8,\; F{:}12] \\
S &= \{A, C, B, D\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.6 Iteration 5 – Extract Minimum (E)

- **Greedy pick:** $E$ with $\text{dist}[E] = 8$.
- **Settle:** $S \leftarrow \{A, C, B, D, E\}$.
- **Relax outgoing edges of E:** $(E, F, 3)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{candidate}(E \to F) &= 8 + 3 = 11 < \text{dist}[F]=12 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{dist}[F] = 11,\; \text{prev}[F] = E
\end{aligned}
$$

**State after Iteration 5:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{dist} &= [A{:}0,\; B{:}3,\; C{:}2,\; D{:}6,\; E{:}8,\; F{:}11] \\
S &= \{A, C, B, D, E\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.7 Iteration 6 – Extract Minimum (F) and Termination

- **Greedy pick:** $F$ with $\text{dist}[F] = 11$. Settle. No further useful relaxations. $Q = \emptyset$ → **Algorithm terminates**.

### 3.1.8 Final Shortest Path Tree (Reconstruction via `prev[]`)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\rightsquigarrow B : \text{prev}(B)=C,\; \text{prev}(C)=A \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A \to C \to B,\; \text{cost} = 3 \\
A &\rightsquigarrow C : \text{prev}(C)=A \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A \to C,\; \text{cost} = 2 \\
A &\rightsquigarrow D : \text{prev}(D)=C,\; \text{prev}(C)=A \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A \to C \to D,\; \text{cost} = 6 \\
A &\rightsquigarrow E : \text{prev}(E)=D,\; \text{prev}(D)=C,\; \text{prev}(C)=A \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A \to C \to D \to E,\; \text{cost} = 8 \\
A &\rightsquigarrow F : \text{prev}(F)=E,\; \text{prev}(E)=D,\; \text{prev}(D)=C,\; \text{prev}(C)=A \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A \to C \to D \to E \to F,\; \text{cost} = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Iteration Summary Table

This is the **board-exam-grade tabulation** expected in KTU answers; reproduce it line-by-line for full marks.

| Iter | Settled $u$ | $\text{dist}[u]$ | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | $E$ | $F$ | Updates |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | — | $\mathbf{0}$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Initialise |
| 1 | $A$ | 0 | 0* | 4 | 2 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $B{:}4,\; C{:}2$ |
| 2 | $C$ | 2 | 0 | **3** | 2* | 6 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $B{:}4\to3,\; D{:}\infty\to6$ |
| 3 | $B$ | 3 | 0 | 3 | 2 | 6 | 10 | $\infty$ | $E{:}\infty\to10$ |
| 4 | $D$ | 6 | 0 | 3 | 2 | 6* | **8** | 12 | $E{:}10\to8,\; F{:}\infty\to12$ |
| 5 | $E$ | 8 | 0 | 3 | 2 | 6 | 8* | **11** | $F{:}12\to11$ |
| 6 | $F$ | 11 | 0 | 3 | 2 | 6 | 8 | 11* | Done |

(\* = settled that iteration; **bold** = distance updated.)

## 3.3 Correctness Sketch (Why It Works)

The **proof of optimality** rests on the invariant:

$$
\forall v \in S : \text{dist}[v] = \delta(s, v)
$$

**Inductive argument.**
- **Base case:** $S = \{s\}$, and $\text{dist}[s] = 0 = \delta(s, s)$. True.
- **Inductive step:** Assume invariant holds for $S$. Let $u$ be the next extracted vertex. For any alternative path $s \rightsquigarrow u$, the last edge enters $u$ from some vertex $x$. If $x \in S$, the alternative cost is $\geq \text{dist}[x] + w(x, u) \geq \text{dist}[u]$ (because $u$ was the minimum). If $x \notin S$, then $\text{dist}[x] \geq \text{dist}[u]$ by the heap's min property, and $w(x, u) \geq 0$, so the alternative is also $\geq \text{dist}[u]$. Hence $\delta(s, u) = \text{dist}[u]$ and the invariant is preserved.
- **QED.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This invariant requires $w \geq 0$ for **every** edge. Drop the non-negativity constraint, and the chain of inequalities fails — hence Dijkstra is **not** correct for negative-weight graphs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid – Algorithm Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([START]) --> init["Initialise dist v = infinity, dist s = 0, prev v = NULL, S = empty, Q = all vertices"]
    init --> checkQ{"Is Q non-empty?"}
    checkQ -- No --> output["Output dist and reconstruct path via prev, then terminate"]
    output --> stopNode([END])
    checkQ -- Yes --> extract["u = Extract-Min from Q, Settle u by adding to S"]
    extract --> iterEdges["For each outgoing edge u to v with weight w"]
    iterEdges --> inS{"Is v already in S?"}
    inS -- Yes --> iterEdges
    inS -- No --> relax["candidate = dist u + w"]
    relax --> compare{"candidate less than dist v?"}
    compare -- No --> iterEdges
    compare -- Yes --> update["dist v = candidate, prev v = u, Decrease-Key v in Q"]
    update --> iterEdges
    iterEdges --> checkQ
```

## 4.2 Mermaid – Worked Example Graph (with Settled-Order Overlay)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA((A)) -- 4 --> nodeB((B))
    nodeA -- 2 --> nodeC((C))
    nodeC -- 1 --> nodeB
    nodeC -- 4 --> nodeD((D))
    nodeB -- 5 --> nodeD
    nodeB -- 7 --> nodeE((E))
    nodeD -- 2 --> nodeE
    nodeD -- 6 --> nodeF((F))
    nodeE -- 3 --> nodeF
    classDef settled fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef source fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef target fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
    class nodeA source
    class nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE settled
    class nodeF target
```

## 4.3 Mermaid – Shortest-Path Tree (SPT) Produced by the Algorithm

```mermaid
graph TD
    src((A)) -- 2 --> midC((C))
    midC -- 1 --> midB((B))
    midC -- 4 --> midD((D))
    midD -- 2 --> midE((E))
    midE -- 3 --> tgtF((F))
    classDef spt fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13,color:#000
    classDef root fill:#f6b26b,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000
    class src root
    class midC,midB,midD,midE,tgtF spt
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** In the SPT, every node has exactly one incoming edge (the edge on the optimal path from $A$). The root $A$ has no incoming edge. This arborescence is the **output** of Dijkstra that a Graph Database engine persists as a query result.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the algorithm design paradigm of Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm. Under what condition on edge weights does it guarantee optimality?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Dijkstra's algorithm follows the **greedy algorithm paradigm** combined with **dynamic programming** (memoisation of tentative distances). At every iteration, it commits to the unsettled vertex with the smallest tentative distance — the locally optimal choice — and never revisits it. Optimality is **guaranteed only when every edge weight $w(u, v) \geq 0$** (non-negative weights). The proof relies on the invariant that once a vertex is settled, no future path can be shorter because any detour through unsettled vertices adds non-negative cumulative cost. If the graph contains a negative-weight edge, the greedy choice may lock in a sub-optimal vertex, and the algorithm produces incorrect results — in that case, the **Bellman-Ford algorithm** must be used.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Dijkstra's algorithm and the Breadth-First Search (BFS) traversal in the context of graph databases.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | BFS | Dijkstra's Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Edge weights** | Implicitly uniform (each edge = 1 hop) | Explicit non-negative weights |
| **Optimality metric** | Minimum *number of hops* | Minimum *cumulative weight* |
| **Data structure** | FIFO queue | Priority queue (min-heap) |
| **Time complexity** | $\mathbf{O(V + E)}$ | $\mathbf{O((V + E) \log V)}$ with heap |
| **Use case** | Unweighted social-graph reachability | Weighted road-networks, supply chains |
| **Output** | All vertices at each depth layer | Single-source shortest path tree (SPT) |
| **Negative weights** | N/A (no weights) | **Forbidden** |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Note:** Each Part B question must be answered in **either OR** form. Two alternatives are provided below for full choice compliance.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** *Explain the working of Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm with a neat flowchart. Discuss its time and space complexity when implemented with (i) an adjacency matrix and a simple array, and (ii) an adjacency list and a binary min-heap.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Apply Dijkstra's algorithm on the following weighted graph with source vertex $S$. Tabulate the iteration-wise updates of the `dist` array and reconstruct the shortest path from $S$ to $T$.* **(7 marks)**

**Edge list (u → v, w):** $(S, A, 6), (S, B, 2), (B, A, 3), (B, D, 5), (A, D, 1), (A, C, 4), (D, C, 2), (D, T, 7), (C, T, 5)$.

#### Model Solution – Part (a)

**Working of the algorithm (flowchart in Section 4.1 reproduced narratively):**

1. Initialise `dist[v] = ∞` for all $v$, `dist[S] = 0`. Maintain a priority queue $Q$ of unsettled vertices.
2. While $Q \neq \emptyset$:
   - Extract $u$ with the smallest `dist` (greedy pick, label-setting).
   - For each neighbour $v$ of $u$:
     - Compute `candidate = dist[u] + w(u, v)`.
     - If `candidate < dist[v]`, then `dist[v] = candidate` and `prev[v] = u` (relaxation).
3. Terminate when $Q$ is empty.

**Complexity comparison:**

| Implementation | Extract-Min | Decrease-Key | Total Time | Space |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Adjacency matrix + simple array (linear scan) | $O(V)$ per call × $V$ calls = $O(V^2)$ | $O(1)$ | $\mathbf{O(V^2)}$ | $O(V^2)$ for matrix + $O(V)$ for arrays |
| (ii) Adjacency list + binary min-heap | $O(\log V)$ per call | $O(\log V)$ per update | $\mathbf{O((V + E) \log V)}$ | $O(V + E)$ for list + $O(V)$ for heap |

The heap-based variant is asymptotically faster for **sparse graphs** ($E \ll V^2$), which is the typical case in real-world Graph Database workloads (social networks, knowledge graphs).

#### Model Solution – Part (b) — Step-by-Step Tabulation

| Iter | Settled $u$ | $\text{dist}[u]$ | $S$ | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | $T$ | Updates |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | — | $\emptyset$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Initialise |
| 1 | $S$ | 0 | $\{S\}$ | 6 | 2 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $A{:}\infty\to6,\; B{:}\infty\to2$ |
| 2 | $B$ | 2 | $\{S,B\}$ | **5** | 2* | $\infty$ | 7 | $\infty$ | $A{:}6\to5$ (via $S\to B\to A$ cost 5), $D{:}\infty\to7$ |
| 3 | $A$ | 5 | $\{S,B,A\}$ | 5* | 2 | 9 | **6** | $\infty$ | $C{:}\infty\to9$ ($S\to B\to A\to C=9$), $D{:}7\to6$ ($S\to B\to A\to D=6$) |
| 4 | $D$ | 6 | $\{S,B,A,D\}$ | 5 | 2 | **8** | 6* | 13 | $C{:}9\to8$ ($S\to B\to A\to D\to C=8$), $T{:}\infty\to13$ |
| 5 | $C$ | 8 | $\{S,B,A,D,C\}$ | 5 | 2 | 8* | 6 | **12** | $T{:}13\to12$ ($S\to B\to A\to D\to C\to T=12$) |
| 6 | $T$ | 12 | $\{S,B,A,D,C,T\}$ | 5 | 2 | 8 | 6 | 12* | Done |

**Reconstruction of shortest path from $S$ to $T$ via `prev[]`:**
- $\text{prev}(T) = C,\; \text{prev}(C) = D,\; \text{prev}(D) = A,\; \text{prev}(A) = B,\; \text{prev}(B) = S$.

$$
\boxed{S \rightarrow B \rightarrow A \rightarrow D \rightarrow C \rightarrow T, \quad \text{Total cost} = 2 + 3 + 1 + 2 + 5 = \mathbf{12}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students often *settle a vertex too early* by writing it in the $S$ column before the relaxation of its neighbours is performed, or they forget to mark already-settled vertices and end up "re-relaxing" them. The settled status must be updated **only** at the moment of extraction from the priority queue, **before** the relaxation loop. Also, the final `dist` values must be re-checked — if a row shows two updates in the same iteration, only the smaller candidate wins.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** *Define a "shortest-path tree (SPT)" in the context of graph databases. Show that the structure produced by Dijkstra's algorithm is indeed a tree with exactly $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges, and discuss how it differs from a minimum spanning tree (MST).* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Consider a knowledge graph with the following nine subject nodes and weighted prerequisite edges. Run Dijkstra's algorithm from node "DBMS" to find the lowest-cost learning path to "AI". Express the path and its cost.* **(7 marks)**

**Nodes:** `DBMS, OS, CN, DSA, COA, ML, NLP, AI, Stats`

**Edges (u → v, w):** $(DBMS, OS, 4), (DBMS, DSA, 2), (OS, CN, 3), (OS, COA, 5), (DSA, COA, 1), (DSA, ML, 6), (CN, Stats, 2), (COA, Stats, 4), (Stats, ML, 3), (ML, NLP, 4), (NLP, AI, 3), (ML, AI, 5)$.

#### Model Solution – Part (a)

**Definition of SPT (2 marks):** Given a weighted graph $G = (V, E, w)$ and a source $s$, a **shortest-path tree rooted at $s$** is a spanning subgraph $T \subseteq G$ such that:
1. $T$ contains every vertex $v \in V$ reachable from $s$.
2. For every $v$, the unique path in $T$ from $s$ to $v$ is a shortest (minimum-cost) path in $G$.

**Proof that Dijkstra's output is a tree with $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges (3 marks):**
- The set of edges $\{\,(\text{prev}[v], v) : v \neq s \,\}$ defines a directed graph $T$.
- **Acyclicity:** Suppose $T$ contains a cycle $v_1 \to v_2 \to \cdots \to v_k \to v_1$. By the relaxation rule, $\text{dist}[v_i] \leq \text{dist}[v_{i-1}] + w(v_{i-1}, v_i)$ with strict inequality along at least one edge. Summing around the cycle gives $0 \leq \sum w < 0$, a contradiction.
- **Connectivity to $s$:** Every edge is added only when its target is reached via a known path from $s$, so all $v \neq s$ are connected to $s$.
- A connected acyclic graph on $\vert V \vert$ vertices has exactly $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges. $\blacksquare$

**SPT vs MST (2 marks):**

| Property | Shortest-Path Tree (SPT) | Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) |
|---|---|---|
| **Optimisation goal** | Min cost from a *fixed source* to every other node | Min *total* edge weight covering all nodes |
| **Source-dependent?** | Yes (different SPT per source) | No (an MST is unique only up to weight, not source) |
| **Algorithm** | Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford | Kruskal, Prim |
| **Edge count** | $\vert V \vert - 1$ | $\vert V \vert - 1$ |
| **Negative weights** | Dijkstra: forbidden | Kruskal/Prim: forbidden |

#### Model Solution – Part (b) — Dijkstra from `DBMS`

| Iter | Settled $u$ | $\text{dist}[u]$ | $DBMS$ | $OS$ | $CN$ | $DSA$ | $COA$ | $Stats$ | $ML$ | $NLP$ | $AI$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | — | **0** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 1 | `DBMS` | 0 | 0* | 4 | $\infty$ | 2 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 2 | `DSA` | 2 | 0 | 4 | $\infty$ | 2* | **3** | $\infty$ | 8 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 3 | `COA` | 3 | 0 | 4 | $\infty$ | 2 | 3* | **7** | 8 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 4 | `OS` | 4 | 0 | 4* | **7** | 2 | 3 | 7 | 8 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 5 | `CN` | 7 | 0 | 4 | 7* | 2 | 3 | **6** | 8 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 6 | `Stats` | 6 | 0 | 4 | 7 | 2 | 3 | 6* | **8** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 7 | `ML` | 8 | 0 | 4 | 7 | 2 | 3 | 6 | 8* | 12 | **13** |
| 8 | `NLP` | 12 | 0 | 4 | 7 | 2 | 3 | 6 | 8 | 12* | **11** |
| 9 | `AI` | 11 | 0 | 4 | 7 | 2 | 3 | 6 | 8 | 12 | 11* |

**Reconstruction of the shortest path DBMS → AI:**
- $\text{prev}(AI) = NLP,\; \text{prev}(NLP) = ML,\; \text{prev}(ML) = Stats,\; \text{prev}(Stats) = CN,\; \text{prev}(CN) = OS,\; \text{prev}(OS) = DBMS$.

$$
\boxed{DBMS \rightarrow OS \rightarrow CN \rightarrow Stats \rightarrow ML \rightarrow NLP \rightarrow AI, \quad \text{Total cost} = 4 + 3 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 3 = \mathbf{19}}
$$

Note that the direct route $DBMS \to DSA \to COA \to \cdots$ was carefully relaxed but ultimately lost to the path through $OS \to CN$, because the detour via `CN → Stats` provided a cheaper bridge to `ML`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b – Long graphs):** A common mistake is to **terminate as soon as the destination is settled** in a single-source multi-destination run. In a strict shortest-path-to-$t$ invocation, yes — you may halt early. But if the question asks for the *full* `dist` array, you must run the algorithm to completion. Always re-read the question stem for "compute **all** shortest distances" vs "compute the shortest path **to X**".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Paradigm:** Dijkstra is a **greedy, label-setting, single-source** shortest-path algorithm.
- **Pre-condition:** Edge weights must satisfy $w(u, v) \geq 0$ for correctness.
- **Data structure trade-off:**
  - Array-based: $\mathbf{O(V^2)}$ time, simple to code — best for dense graphs.
  - Binary-heap-based: $\mathbf{O((V+E) \log V)}$ time, scales to sparse graphs.
  - Fibonacci-heap: $\mathbf{O(E + V \log V)}$ — theoretical optimum, rarely used in practice.
- **Outputs:** Two parallel arrays — `dist[v]` (cost) and `prev[v]` (predecessor) — yielding the **Shortest-Path Tree (SPT)**.
- **Negative weights → forbidden.** Use **Bellman-Ford** instead.
- **Negative cycles** cannot exist under Dijkstra's pre-condition, so cycle detection is unnecessary.
- **Cypher hook (Neo4j):** `MATCH p = shortestPath((a:Person)-[:KNOWS*]-(b:Person)) RETURN p;` — internally invokes Dijkstra/BFS depending on whether relationships carry weights.
- **Variants to remember for viva:** *Bidirectional Dijkstra* (point-to-point), *A\** (heuristic-guided), *Multi-source Dijkstra* (small set of origins).
- **Common Board traps:** (i) Forgetting to mark a vertex as settled *before* relaxing, (ii) re-relaxing already-settled vertices, (iii) misapplying Dijkstra to a graph with negative weights, (iv) forgetting to backtrack through `prev[]` for path reconstruction.
- **Real-world anchors:** Google Maps, OSPF routing, supply-chain logistics, social-network influence spread, chip-design wire routing, and of course **Cypher/Gremlin path queries in Graph Databases**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line exam answer (must memorise):** *"Dijkstra's algorithm is a greedy, label-setting, single-source shortest-path procedure for graphs with non-negative edge weights, achieving $\mathbf{O((V+E) \log V)}$ time with a binary min-heap and $\mathbf{O(V)}$ auxiliary space."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The A* Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The A* Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme – PECDT751):**
> The **A\* (A-Star) Algorithm** is a **best-first, informed (heuristic) search algorithm** used to find the **least-cost path** between an initial *start node* and a *goal node* in a weighted graph. It evaluates nodes by combining the *actual cost incurred so far* with an *admissible heuristic estimate* of the remaining cost to the goal.

Formally, A\* uses an **evaluation function**:

$$f(n) \;=\; g(n) \;+\; h(n)$$

where each component has a precise meaning in the KTU syllabus:

| Symbol | Formal Name | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $g(n)$ | **Path cost** | The actual (known) cost from the *start node* to the current node $n$. |
| $h(n)$ | **Heuristic function** | The *estimated* cost from node $n$ to the *goal node*. |
| $f(n)$ | **Evaluation function** | The total estimated cost of the cheapest solution passing through $n$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECD T751 – Module 4):** A\* is treated as a *Graph Search* algorithm that augments classical shortest-path techniques (Dijkstra, BFS) with heuristic guidance, making it essential for **route planning, AI game playing, and graph database query optimization**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The GPS Navigator"

Imagine you are driving from **Kochi** to **Thiruvananthapuram** using Google Maps:

- **$g(n)$ = "Distance already driven"** — This is the actual kilometers you have already covered from the start. You know this for certain from your odometer.
- **$h(n)$ = "Straight-line (air) distance still to the goal"** — This is an intelligent *estimate* (a heuristic). The crow-flies distance from your current location to the destination is always a **lower bound** on the real road distance (because you cannot drive through buildings!).
- **$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ = "Total predicted trip length"** — A\* uses this to decide which *intermediate city* to expand next. The city with the *smallest predicted total* is explored first.

The result: A\* behaves like a **smart traveller** who balances *progress made* with *distance remaining*, never wasting time exploring hopeless detours.

### 1.3 Why A\* is Central to Graph Databases

In a **graph database** (Neo4j, JanusGraph, TigerGraph), shortest-path and weighted-traversal queries are first-class operations. A\* (and its variants such as **Bidirectional A\***, **A\* with ALT landmarks**) is the workhorse that powers:
- *Route recommendations* (e.g., shortest delivery path).
- *Knowledge-graph entity disambiguation* (semantic distance between concepts).
- *Network packet routing* with QoS constraints.
- *AI-driven game pathfinding* in dynamic environments.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember the formula:** **"F = Go Home"** → **$f = g + h$**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of A\*

A\* maintains **two priority queues** (or equivalently, two sets) and a single **open-list priority queue** keyed by $f(n)$:

1. **Initialize** the `OPEN` list (priority queue) with the *start node* $S$. Set $g(S) = 0$ and $f(S) = h(S)$.
2. **Initialize** the `CLOSED` list as empty (it stores *expanded/visited* nodes).
3. **Loop** while `OPEN` is not empty:
   - **Pop** the node $n$ with the *minimum $f(n)$* from `OPEN`.
   - If $n$ equals the *goal node* $G$, **reconstruct and return** the optimal path.
   - Otherwise, **move $n$ to `CLOSED`**.
   - **Expand $n$**: for every *successor* $n'$ of $n$ (i.e., every edge $(n, n')$ with weight $w$):
     - Compute a *tentative* $g$-value: $g_{tent} = g(n) + w(n, n')$.
     - If $n'$ is not in `OPEN` and not in `CLOSED`, **insert** $n'$ into `OPEN` with $g(n') = g_{tent}$ and $f(n') = g_{tent} + h(n')$. Record $n$ as the parent.
     - If $n'$ is already in `OPEN` (or `CLOSED`) with a higher $g$-value, **update** it to the better path and adjust $f(n')$.
4. **Failure**: If the loop terminates without reaching $G$, the goal is unreachable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "tentative-g + heuristic" update?**
> Because A\* always re-evaluates when a shorter path to an already-known node is discovered. This **path-maximization check** guarantees optimality *iff* the heuristic is **consistent**.

### 2.2 The Three Function Components — Engineering Perspective

| Function | Data Source | Determinism | Used During |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $g(n)$ | Accumulated edge weights from $S$ to $n$ | Deterministic (exact) | Always |
| $h(n)$ | Domain-specific heuristic (e.g., Euclidean, Manhattan, Chebyshev) | Estimated | Node selection only |
| $f(n)$ | Sum of $g$ and $h$ | Estimated total | Node selection only |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the *must-know* mathematical relationships for KTU 2024 ESE questions. All symbols are LaTeX-safe (no vertical pipes inside table cells).

| # | Property / Equation | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Evaluation function | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | A\* ranking criterion |
| 2 | Admissibility condition | $0 \le h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ for all $n$ | Heuristic never overestimates true cost $h^{*}$ |
| 3 | Consistency (Monotonicity) | $h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$ for every edge $(n, n')$ | Triangle inequality for heuristics |
| 4 | Optimality guarantee (tree search) | $h(n)$ admissible $\Rightarrow$ A\* returns optimal path | Holds for *tree* search |
| 5 | Optimality guarantee (graph search) | $h(n)$ consistent $\Rightarrow$ A\* is optimally efficient | Holds for *graph* search |
| 6 | Time complexity (worst) | $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ in a graph of $V$ vertices and $E$ edges | Equivalent to Dijkstra in worst case |
| 7 | Space complexity | $O(\vert V \vert)$ for the `OPEN` + `CLOSED` lists | Memory bottleneck in large graphs |
| 8 | Euclidean heuristic (2D) | $h_{euc}(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_G)^2 + (y_n - y_G)^2}$ | Used in road/path networks |
| 9 | Manhattan heuristic | $h_{man}(n) = \vert x_n - x_G \vert + \vert y_n - y_G \vert$ | Used in grid movement (4-direction) |
| 10 | Diagonal (Chebyshev) heuristic | $h_{cheb}(n) = \max(\vert x_n - x_G \vert, \vert y_n - y_G \vert)$ | Used in 8-direction grid movement |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick memory hook for admissible heuristics:** The heuristic is the *optimistic* estimate — it always tells you "you are closer than you really are" (so it never *over-promises*).

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real Systems

- **Google Maps / OpenStreetMap:** ALT-A\* (A\*, Landmarks, Triangle inequality) — used in road networks with millions of nodes.
- **Game AI (StarCraft, Age of Empires):** A\* powers the movement of every non-player unit.
- **Robotics (ROS Navigation Stack):** A\* is the default global planner for the `navfn` and `global_planner` packages.
- **Network Routing (OSPF, BGP):** Heuristic-guided SPF trees are conceptually A\* variants.
- **Graph Databases (Neo4j):** The `shortestPath` algorithm (with `costs` and heuristic options) implements A\* on property graphs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Optimality Condition

**Theorem (A\* Optimality, KTU 2024 Module 4):**
*If the heuristic $h(n)$ is **admissible** — i.e., $0 \le h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ for every node $n$ — then any node $n$ expanded by A\* before the goal $G$ satisfies $f(n) \le f^{*}(S)$ where $f^{*}(S)$ is the cost of the optimal solution.*

**Proof by contradiction:**

*Step 1 — Setup.* Let $\pi^{*}$ be the optimal path from $S$ to $G$ with cost $f^{*}(S) = g^{*}(G)$ (since $h(G) = 0$ trivially).

*Step 2 — Assume the contrary.* Suppose A\* terminates at a *sub-optimal* goal $G'$ with $f(G') > f^{*}(S)$. This means some node $n$ on the optimal path $\pi^{*}$ was *never expanded* before termination.

*Step 3 — Bound the $f$-value along the optimal path.* Consider any node $n'$ on $\pi^{*}$. By definition of $g$ on the optimal path:

$$g(n') = g^{*}(n')$$

By admissibility, $h(n') \le h^{*}(n')$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(n') &= g(n') + h(n') \\
      &\le g^{*}(n') + h^{*}(n') \\
      &= g^{*}(G) \quad \text{(by definition of optimal sub-path cost)} \\
      &= f^{*}(S)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4 — Reach a contradiction.* Since A\* always picks the node with the **minimum** $f$-value, and every node on the optimal path has $f(n') \le f^{*}(S)$, A\* must have expanded *at least one* such node before ever reaching a sub-optimal goal. Hence the assumption is false.

$$\blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivations of Common Heuristics

**Derivation 1 — Euclidean Heuristic $h_{euc}$:**

Given two points in 2D Euclidean space, $n = (x_n, y_n)$ and $G = (x_G, y_G)$, the shortest physical distance between them is the straight-line distance. By the **Pythagorean theorem**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{euc}(n) &= \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2} \\
           &= \sqrt{(x_n - x_G)^2 + (y_n - y_G)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why is $h_{euc}$ admissible?** Because on any *road* (with non-negative lengths) you cannot travel shorter than the geometric straight line. Therefore, for any real path $P$ from $n$ to $G$:

$$h_{euc}(n) \le \text{length}(P)$$

so the heuristic never overestimates.

**Derivation 2 — Manhattan Heuristic $h_{man}$:**

In a 4-direction grid (no diagonal moves), the minimum number of steps from $n$ to $G$ is the **L1-norm**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{man}(n) &= \vert x_n - x_G \vert + \vert y_n - y_G \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

This counts the *horizontal* plus *vertical* steps, ignoring obstacles — hence admissible on a *uniform-cost* grid with $w = 1$ per cell.

**Derivation 3 — Diagonal (Chebyshev) Heuristic $h_{cheb}$:**

In an 8-direction grid, each step can move $\pm 1$ in $x$ or $y$ or both. The minimum number of *king-moves* is the **L∞-norm**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{cheb}(n) &= \max(\vert x_n - x_G \vert, \vert y_n - y_G \vert)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Trace (Mandatory KTU 2024 Problem Type)

**Problem (KTU-style):** Find the shortest path from node **A** to node **G** in the weighted graph below. Each node has 2D coordinates; the heuristic $h(n)$ is the Euclidean distance to **G**.

| Node | Coordinates $(x, y)$ | $h(n)$ to G |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | $(0, 0)$ | **7.00** |
| B | $(2, 1)$ | **5.10** |
| C | $(4, 0)$ | **3.00** |
| D | $(5, 2)$ | **2.24** |
| E | $(2, 4)$ | **5.00** |
| G | $(4, 4)$ | **0.00** |

**Edges and Weights:**
- $A \to B$ : 2
- $A \to E$ : 4
- $B \to C$ : 2
- $B \to D$ : 4
- $C \to D$ : 1
- $C \to G$ : 4
- $D \to G$ : 3
- $E \to G$ : 3

**A\* Trace (Step-by-step with full working):**

**Step 1 — Initialize.**
`OPEN = {A: f=7.00}`, `CLOSED = {}`. (Since $g(A)=0$, $f(A) = 0 + 7.00 = 7.00$.)

**Step 2 — Pop A. Expand successors B and E.**

- For **B**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
g(B) &= g(A) + w(A,B) = 0 + 2 = 2 \\
f(B) &= g(B) + h(B) = 2 + 5.10 = 7.10
\end{aligned}
$$

- For **E**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
g(E) &= g(A) + w(A,E) = 0 + 4 = 4 \\
f(E) &= g(E) + h(E) = 4 + 5.00 = 9.00
\end{aligned}
$$

`OPEN = {B: 7.10, E: 9.00}`, `CLOSED = {A}`.

**Step 3 — Pop B (smallest f=7.10). Expand successors C and D.**

- For **C**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
g(C) &= g(B) + w(B,C) = 2 + 2 = 4 \\
f(C) &= g(C) + h(C) = 4 + 3.00 = 7.00
\end{aligned}
$$

- For **D**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
g(D) &= g(B) + w(B,D) = 2 + 4 = 6 \\
f(D) &= g(D) + h(D) = 6 + 2.24 = 8.24
\end{aligned}
$$

`OPEN = {C: 7.00, D: 8.24, E: 9.00}`, `CLOSED = {A, B}`.

**Step 4 — Pop C (f=7.00). Expand successors D and G.**

- **D already in OPEN** with $g(D)=6$. Tentative $g$:
$$
g_{tent}(D) = g(C) + w(C,D) = 4 + 1 = 5 \;<\; 6
$$
So **update D**: $g(D) = 5$, $f(D) = 5 + 2.24 = 7.24$.

- For **G**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
g(G) &= g(C) + w(C,G) = 4 + 4 = 8 \\
f(G) &= g(G) + h(G) = 8 + 0 = 8.00
\end{aligned}
$$

`OPEN = {G: 8.00, D: 7.24, E: 9.00}`, `CLOSED = {A, B, C}`.

**Step 5 — Pop D (f=7.24). Expand successor G.**

- **G already in OPEN** with $g(G)=8$. Tentative $g$:
$$
g_{tent}(G) = g(D) + w(D,G) = 5 + 3 = 8
$$
No improvement (equal). Keep $g(G)=8$.

`OPEN = {G: 8.00, E: 9.00}`, `CLOSED = {A, B, C, D}`.

**Step 6 — Pop G (f=8.00). Goal reached!**

**Reconstruct path:** $G \leftarrow C \leftarrow B \leftarrow A$.

$$
\boxed{\text{Optimal Path: } A \to B \to C \to G, \quad \text{Total Cost} = 2 + 2 + 4 = 8}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with Dijkstra (no heuristic):** Dijkstra would have expanded E first (after A) with $f=8$, then continued from E. A\* avoided that detour because the heuristic $h$ made $C$ look more promising.

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: A\* vs. Classical Algorithms

| Criterion | BFS | Dijkstra | Greedy Best-First | A\* |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Uses heuristic? | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (only $h$) | ✅ ($g + h$) |
| Optimal (weighted)? | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ (if $h$ admissible) |
| Complete? | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ (may loop) | ✅ |
| Time complexity | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O((V+E)\log V)$ | Best case $O(V)$ | $O((V+E)\log V)$ worst |
| Space complexity | $O(V)$ | $O(V)$ | $O(V)$ | $O(V)$ |
| Typical use | Unweighted shortest path | Weighted, no heuristic | Quick approximate route | Heuristic-guided optimal path |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — A\* Algorithm Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: initialize OPEN with start S]) --> B[g of S = 0, f of S = h of S]
    B --> C{OF03: OPEN empty?}
    C -- Yes --> Z([Failure: goal unreachable])
    C -- No --> D[Pop node n with minimum f of n from OPEN]
    D --> E{Is n the goal G?}
    E -- Yes --> Y([Success: reconstruct path via parents])
    E -- No --> F[Move n to CLOSED]
    F --> G[For each successor n prime of n with edge weight w]
    G --> H[Compute g tent = g of n + w]
    H --> I{n prime in OPEN or CLOSED?}
    I -- No --> J[Insert n prime into OPEN with g and f]
    I -- Yes --> K{g tent less than stored g of n prime?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Update g and f of n prime, re-link parent to n]
    K -- No --> M[Discard this path]
    J --> N{All successors processed?}
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N -- No --> G
    N -- Yes --> C
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Graph — Worked Example (Section 3.3)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1((A: f=7.00)) -- 2 --- B1((B: f=7.10))
    A1 -- 4 --- E1((E: f=9.00))
    B1 -- 2 --- C1((C: f=7.00))
    B1 -- 4 --- D1((D: f=7.24))
    C1 -- 1 --- D1
    C1 -- 4 --- G1(((G: f=8.00 GOAL)))
    D1 -- 3 --- G1
    E1 -- 3 --- G1

    style A1 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G1 fill:#93c47d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style C1 fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B1 fill:#b4a7d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D1 fill:#b4a7d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E1 fill:#cccccc,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The yellow node **A** is the start, the green node **G** is the goal, and the blue node **C** is the *turning-point* that A\* identified as optimally promising. The grey node **E** was *correctly deprioritized* by A\* because its $f$-value was higher.

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Heuristic Function Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        S[Start Node S]
        Cur[Current Node n]
        G[Goal Node G]
    end

    subgraph CostComputation[Cost Computation]
        G1[g of n: sum of edge weights from S to n]
        H1[h of n: domain heuristic estimate from n to G]
    end

    subgraph Eval[Evaluation]
        F1[f of n = g of n + h of n]
    end

    subgraph PriorityQueue[OPEN Priority Queue]
        PQ[Min-heap ordered by f of n]
    end

    S --> G1
    Cur --> G1
    Cur --> H1
    G --> H1
    G1 --> F1
    H1 --> F1
    F1 --> PQ
    PQ -- smallest f --> Next[Next node to expand]
```

### 4.4 State Transition of `OPEN` / `CLOSED` (Tabular Schematic)

| Step | Action | OPEN (with f) | CLOSED |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | Initialize | {A:7.00} | {} |
| 1 | Expand A | {B:7.10, E:9.00} | {A} |
| 2 | Expand B | {C:7.00, D:8.24, E:9.00} | {A, B} |
| 3 | Expand C | {G:8.00, D:7.24, E:9.00} | {A, B, C} |
| 4 | Expand D | {G:8.00, E:9.00} | {A, B, C, D} |
| 5 | Pop G — terminate | — | — |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** *Define the A\* search algorithm. State the role of the heuristic function $h(n)$ in A\*.*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A\* is an **informed best-first search algorithm** that finds the least-cost path from a start node to a goal node in a weighted graph. It uses the evaluation function:
> $$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$
> where $g(n)$ is the actual cost from start to $n$ and $h(n)$ is a heuristic estimate of the cost from $n$ to the goal. The heuristic $h(n)$ *guides* the search toward the goal by ranking nodes; an **admissible heuristic** ($h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$) guarantees that A\* returns an **optimal path** while exploring fewer nodes than an uninformed search like Dijkstra's. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for admissibility/optimality.)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** *What is an admissible heuristic? Give one example.*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An **admissible heuristic** is one that *never overestimates* the true cost $h^{*}(n)$ to reach the goal, i.e., $0 \le h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ for all nodes $n$. Admissibility ensures A\*'s optimality. **Example:** In a 2D road network, the straight-line Euclidean distance from a city to the destination is an admissible heuristic for road travel, because a road route is never shorter than the straight line. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for example.)

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q. Module 4 Choice A]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Explain the A\* algorithm with its evaluation function. State and prove the optimality condition under an admissible heuristic. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Consider the graph with nodes A, B, C, D, G and edges A–B(2), A–D(4), B–C(2), B–D(1), C–G(4), D–G(3). Coordinates: A(0,0), B(2,1), C(4,0), D(5,2), G(4,4). Using Euclidean heuristic $h(n)$ at each node and A\* algorithm, determine the optimal path from A to G. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**
1. Define A\* and the evaluation function $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. Mention `OPEN`/`CLOSED` lists. **(2 marks)**
2. State the admissibility condition $0 \le h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$. **(1 mark)**
3. Proof of optimality (as in Section 3.1 of these notes): assume suboptimal termination, derive $f(n') \le f^{*}(S)$ for any optimal-path node, contradict A\*'s minimum-f selection. **(4 marks)**

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**
First compute Euclidean heuristics:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h(A) &= \sqrt{4^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{32} \approx 5.66 \\
h(B) &= \sqrt{2^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61 \\
h(C) &= \sqrt{0^2 + 4^2} = 4.00 \\
h(D) &= \sqrt{1^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24 \\
h(G) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**A\* Trace:**

- Initialize: `OPEN = {A: f = 0 + 5.66 = 5.66}`. **(0.5 mark)**
- Pop A, expand B and D:
  - $g(B) = 2$, $f(B) = 2 + 3.61 = 5.61$.
  - $g(D) = 4$, $f(D) = 4 + 2.24 = 6.24$.
  - `OPEN = {B: 5.61, D: 6.24}`. **(1 mark)**
- Pop B (min f = 5.61), expand C and D:
  - $g(C) = 2 + 2 = 4$, $f(C) = 4 + 4.00 = 8.00$.
  - Tentative $g(D) = 2 + 1 = 3 < 4$. Update: $g(D) = 3$, $f(D) = 3 + 2.24 = 5.24$.
  - `OPEN = {D: 5.24, C: 8.00}`. **(1.5 marks)**
- Pop D (min f = 5.24), expand G:
  - $g(G) = 3 + 3 = 6$, $f(G) = 6 + 0 = 6.00$.
  - `OPEN = {G: 6.00, C: 8.00}`. **(1.5 marks)**
- Pop G (f = 6.00). Goal reached! **(1 mark)**
- Reconstruct: $G \leftarrow D \leftarrow B \leftarrow A$. Total cost $= 3 + 1 + 2 = 6$. **(1.5 marks)**

> **Final Answer:** Path $A \to B \to D \to G$ with total cost **6**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Q. Module 4 Choice B]**
> **Question B (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Differentiate between A\* search and uniform-cost search (Dijkstra). Under what condition does A\* reduce to uniform-cost search? **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** For a robot navigating an 8-direction grid from (0,0) to (4,3) with all moves costing 1, compute the **Manhattan** and **Chebyshev** heuristics at every node along the optimal path. Which heuristic gives a tighter (more informative) estimate, and why? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**
1. UCS uses only $f(n) = g(n)$ (no heuristic). A\* uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. **(2 marks)**
2. UCS explores by minimum *actual* cost-so-far; A\* explores by minimum *estimated total* cost. **(1 mark)**
3. UCS is optimal for any non-negative weights; A\* needs an admissible $h$. **(1 mark)**
4. **A\* reduces to UCS** when $h(n) = 0$ for all $n$ (a trivially admissible heuristic). **(2 marks)**
5. Practical implication: A\* with a *good* heuristic expands fewer nodes than UCS. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

Optimal path on an 8-direction grid from (0,0) to (4,3) requires $\max(4,3) = 4$ king-moves (or a longer sequence of single steps of total Manhattan length 7). At each step, compute:

Let current node be $(x, y)$ and goal $(4, 3)$. Define $\Delta x = 4 - x$ and $\Delta y = 3 - y$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{man}(x, y) &= \vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert \\
h_{cheb}(x, y) &= \max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)
\end{aligned}
$$

Sample values along a possible optimal path $(0,0) \to (1,1) \to (2,2) \to (3,3) \to (4,3)$:

| Node | $h_{man}$ | $h_{cheb}$ | True remaining cost $g_{rem}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (0, 0) | 7 | 4 | 4 (4 king-moves) |
| (1, 1) | 5 | 3 | 3 |
| (2, 2) | 3 | 2 | 2 |
| (3, 3) | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| (4, 3) | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**(2 marks)** for table + computation.

**Analysis (5 marks):**
- $h_{cheb}$ is the *tighter* (more informative) estimate because it equals the *true* minimum number of king-moves on an 8-direction grid. For every node above, $h_{cheb}(n) = g_{rem}(n)$.
- $h_{man}$ is admissible but *loose*: it gives 7 at start when the true cost is only 4. A loose heuristic causes A\* to expand *more* nodes.
- **Conclusion:** Chebyshev is preferred for 8-direction movement; Manhattan for 4-direction; Euclidean for continuous 2D. **(1 mark for correct conclusion, 2 for tight/loose comparison, 1 for admissibility, 1 for engineering recommendation.)**

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the + in $f = g + h$.** Writing $f = g$ or $f = h$ alone ⇒ **−1 mark** immediately.
> 2. **Confusing admissibility with consistency.** Admissibility: $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$. Consistency: $h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$. Both are tested in KTU 2024. ⇒ **−2 marks** if swapped.
> 3. **Skipping the `OPEN`/`CLOSED` trace table.** Examiners award partial credit only when a *complete* step-by-step expansion is shown. A bare "answer is A→B→D→G" without intermediate $f$-values gets ≤ 3 of 7 marks. ⇒ **−4 marks**.
> 4. **Not parent-linking.** In Part (b) traces, you MUST show *which node expanded which* via parent pointers, otherwise path reconstruction is unverifiable. ⇒ **−1 mark**.
> 5. **Ignoring heuristic admissibility justification.** When asked "why is $h$ admissible?", many students merely state the formula. Examiners expect the *reason*: e.g., "Euclidean is admissible because a road cannot be shorter than the straight line." ⇒ **−1 mark**.
> 6. **Mixing up Manhattan vs. Chebyshev.** Use Manhattan for 4-direction grids, Chebyshev for 8-direction. Mixing them up yields an *overestimating* (non-admissible) heuristic, breaking optimality. ⇒ **−2 marks**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — A\* Algorithm Essentials:**

- ✅ **Formula (the heart of A\*):** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — *"F = Go Home"*.
- ✅ **$g(n)$** = real cost from start; **$h(n)$** = heuristic estimate to goal; **$f(n)$** = total estimate.
- ✅ A\* is a **best-first, informed search** that combines the **optimality of Dijkstra** with the **speed of greedy heuristics**.
- ✅ **Admissible heuristic:** $0 \le h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ (never overestimates) — guarantees optimality in *tree search*.
- ✅ **Consistent (monotonic) heuristic:** $h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$ (triangle inequality) — guarantees optimality in *graph search* with re-expansion safety.
- ✅ **Euclidean** heuristic for road networks; **Manhattan** for 4-direction grids; **Chebyshev** for 8-direction grids.
- ✅ A\* uses a **priority queue (`OPEN`)** keyed on minimum $f$, and a **`CLOSED`** list of expanded nodes.
- ✅ **Path reconstruction** is done by *parent pointers* maintained during expansion.
- ✅ **Time complexity:** $O((V+E) \log V)$ worst-case; **Space complexity:** $O(V)$.
- ✅ A\* degenerates to **Dijkstra / UCS** when $h(n) = 0$ (trivial heuristic).
- ✅ A\* degenerates to **Greedy Best-First** when $g(n) = 0$ (ignore path cost).
- ✅ **Worked example (Section 3.3):** $A \to B \to C \to G$, cost = 8, verified by full OPEN/CLOSED trace.
- ✅ **Key real-world domains:** Google Maps routing, game AI (NPC pathfinding), robotics (ROS nav stack), graph databases (Neo4j shortestPath), network packet routing.
- ✅ **Examiner's mantra:** Always show the `OPEN`/`CLOSED` table; always state heuristic admissibility; always derive the heuristic formula; always re-verify path cost.

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 4: The A\* Algorithm**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graph Theory and Predictive Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT751 - GRAPH DATABASES AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Graph Theory and Predictive Modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Graph Theory in the Predictive Context?

**Graph Theory** is the mathematical study of structures called **graphs** — collections of vertices (nodes) and edges (links) that model pairwise relations between objects. In the context of **Predictive Modelling on Graphs**, it becomes the formal foundation for reasoning about connectivity, influence, propagation, and emergent structural properties in connected data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Formal):** Graph theory provides the mathematical apparatus — including vertices $V$, edges $E$, adjacency matrices $A$, and graph operators like the Laplacian $L$ — that allows analysts to transform structural patterns in connected data into quantifiable features (e.g., centrality, clustering, connectivity) usable for prediction tasks such as **link prediction, node classification, and graph-level forecasting**.

### 1.2 The Two Predictive Pillars

Predictive modelling in graph databases operates on **two complementary axes**:

| Axis | Target | Question Answered |
|------|--------|-------------------|
| **Node-Level Prediction** | Classify / regress node properties | *Who is influential? What is the fraud risk of this account?* |
| **Link-Level Prediction** | Predict future / missing edges | *Will two users become friends? Will a protein interact?* |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Map" of Data 🗺️

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a city's road network. Each **intersection** is a *node*; each **road** is an *edge*. Graph theory lets you ask: *Which intersection sees the most traffic (degree)?* *Which road is the fastest shortcut (shortest path)?* *Which areas form a neighbourhood (community)?* *Which road is likely to be built next (link prediction)?*
>
> Now, replace intersections with **people, transactions, or proteins**, and roads with **friendships, money flows, or molecular bindings** — and you have a **predictive graph model**.

## 1.4 Core Terminology Snapshot

- **Vertex / Node ($V$):** Fundamental unit representing an entity (user, product, gene).
- **Edge / Link ($E$):** Pairwise relation between two vertices.
- **Directed vs. Undirected:** Edges may have orientation (Twitter follows vs. Facebook friendship).
- **Weighted vs. Unweighted:** Edges may carry a numerical strength.
- **Path:** A sequence of vertices connected by edges.
- **Walk, Trail, Cycle:** Variants of paths with repetition / closure rules.
- **Subgraph:** A graph formed by a subset of vertices and edges of a larger graph.
- **Adjacency Matrix ($A$):** An $N \times N$ matrix where $A_{ij}=1$ (or weight $w_{ij}$) if an edge exists between $i$ and $j$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric:** The **Order** of a graph is $\vert V \vert = N$ (number of vertices) and the **Size** is $\vert E \vert = M$ (number of edges). For a simple undirected graph, the maximum possible edges is $\binom{N}{2} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}$.

## 1.5 Why Predict on Graphs?

Traditional tabular machine learning treats every row as **independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.)**. Graph data violates this — **nodes are structurally correlated**. Predictive modelling on graphs exploits this **structural correlation** to:

- Detect fraud rings in financial networks.
- Recommend products in social-commerce graphs.
- Forecast protein-protein interactions in bioinformatics.
- Predict traffic congestion in smart-city graphs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposing the Predictive Pipeline

Theoretical predictive workflow on a graph $G = (V, E)$ follows a **four-stage logical chain**:
