1. **Graph Construction (Representation Stage):** Convert raw domain data into $G$ with adjacency matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}$. Optional: assign features $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$ to nodes and edge attributes $W$.
2. **Feature Engineering (Structural Stage):** Compute structural descriptors — **centrality metrics, clustering coefficients, embeddings, random-walk signatures** — that numerically encode the position of each node in the topology.
3. **Model Fitting (Inference Stage):** Feed node/link features into a classifier (Logistic Regression, Random Forest) or a deep model (Graph Neural Network).
4. **Prediction & Evaluation (Deployment Stage):** Predict labels on unseen nodes/edges, evaluate via AUC, F1, MRR, Hits@K.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners often test the *first two stages*. Memorize the **centrality formulas** and **link prediction scores** — they are the most exam-friendly.

## 2.2 Centrality Metrics — The "Importance" Scores

Centrality assigns a numerical rank to each node reflecting its **structural importance**. Different metrics capture different *flavours* of importance.

### A. Degree Centrality
Captures *local popularity* — how many direct connections a node has.

$$C_D(v) = \frac{\deg(v)}{N-1}$$

The denominator $N-1$ normalizes to the range $[0, 1]$ for a simple graph. A **leaf node** has $C_D \to 0$; a **hub** in a star graph has $C_D = 1$.

### B. Betweenness Centrality
Captures *brokerage* — how often a node lies on the shortest path between every other pair.

$$C_B(v) = \sum_{s \neq v \neq t} \frac{\sigma_{st}(v)}{\sigma_{st}}$$

Here, $\sigma_{st}$ is the total number of shortest paths from $s$ to $t$, and $\sigma_{st}(v)$ is the number that pass through $v$. Bridges in networks score high.

### C. Closeness Centrality
Captures *reachability speed* — how quickly a node can reach all others.

$$C_C(v) = \frac{N-1}{\sum_{u \in V} d(v, u)}$$

Here $d(v,u)$ is the shortest-path distance. Higher $C_C$ means shorter average distance to everyone else.

### D. Eigenvector Centrality
Captures *influence of neighbours* — a node is important if its neighbours are themselves important.

$$A \mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_i = \frac{1}{\lambda} \sum_{j=1}^{N} A_{ij} x_j$$

The principal eigenvector of $A$ gives scores; **PageRank** is its damped variant for directed graphs.

### E. PageRank (Directed, Damped)

$$PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \in M(p)} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$$

- $d \in (0,1]$ is the **damping factor**, standard value $\mathbf{0.85}$.
- $M(p)$ are pages linking to $p$; $L(q)$ is the out-degree of $q$.
- The $(1-d)/N$ term is the random-jump probability.

## 2.3 Link Prediction Scores

Given two unconnected nodes $x$ and $y$, predict whether an edge will form.

| Score | Formula | Intuition |
|-------|---------|-----------|
| **Common Neighbors (CN)** | $CN(x,y) = \vert N(x) \cap N(y) \vert$ | More shared friends $\Rightarrow$ higher chance |
| **Jaccard Coefficient** | $J(x,y) = \frac{\vert N(x) \cap N(y) \vert}{\vert N(x) \cup N(y) \vert}$ | Normalized overlap |
| **Adamic–Adar (AA)** | $AA(x,y) = \sum_{z \in N(x) \cap N(y)} \frac{1}{\log \vert N(z) \vert}$ | Rare common neighbours weigh more |
| **Preferential Attachment (PA)** | $PA(x,y) = \vert N(x) \vert \cdot \vert N(y) \vert$ | "Rich get richer" |
| **Resource Allocation (RA)** | $RA(x,y) = \sum_{z \in N(x) \cap N(y)} \frac{1}{\vert N(z) \vert}$ | Penalizes high-degree hubs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Jaccard vs. Adamic–Adar:** Jaccard normalizes by total distinct neighbours; Adamic–Adar penalizes high-degree common neighbours. Adamic–Adar is generally **stronger on social networks** because it rewards bridging through low-degree connectors.

## 2.4 Graph Laplacian & Spectral Foundations

The **Laplacian matrix** $L$ is fundamental for spectral methods:

$$L = D - A$$

where $D$ is the degree matrix (diagonal). The **normalized Laplacian** is:

$$L_{sym} = I - D^{-1/2} A D^{-1/2}$$

Eigenvectors of $L$ form a **graph Fourier basis**, used in **Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs)** for deep predictive modelling.

## 2.5 Random Walk with Restart (RWR)

Used for personalized ranking and node-similarity prediction:

$$\mathbf{r}_v = (1-c) M^T \mathbf{r}_v + c \mathbf{e}_v$$

- $M$ is the row-normalized adjacency.
- $c$ is the restart probability (typically **0.15**).
- $\mathbf{e}_v$ is the starting seed vector.

## 2.6 📋 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the table below — at least **3 of these formulas appear in every KTU 2024 ESE question paper on Module 4**.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Order / Size of Graph | $\vert V \vert = N,\ \vert E \vert = M$ | Vertices / Edges count |
| 2 | Max Edges (Undirected) | $\binom{N}{2} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | Simple graph, no self-loops |
| 3 | Degree Centrality | $C_D(v) = \deg(v) / (N-1)$ | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| 4 | Betweenness Centrality | $C_B(v) = \sum \sigma_{st}(v) / \sigma_{st}$ | Sum over all pairs |
| 5 | Closeness Centrality | $C_C(v) = (N-1) / \sum d(v,u)$ | Inverted sum of distances |
| 6 | Eigenvector Centrality | $A \mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}$ | Principal eigenvector |
| 7 | PageRank | $PR(p) = (1-d)/N + d \sum PR(q)/L(q)$ | $d = 0.85$ standard |
| 8 | Common Neighbors | $CN(x,y) = \vert N(x) \cap N(y) \vert$ | Count of overlap |
| 9 | Jaccard | $J = \vert N(x) \cap N(y) \vert / \vert N(x) \cup N(y) \vert$ | Normalized |
| 10 | Adamic–Adar | $AA = \sum_{z \in N(x) \cap N(y)} 1 / \log \vert N(z) \vert$ | Weighted by rarity |
| 11 | Preferential Attachment | $PA = \vert N(x) \vert \cdot \vert N(y) \vert$ | Product of degrees |
| 12 | Graph Laplacian | $L = D - A$ | PSD symmetric matrix |
| 13 | Normalized Laplacian | $L_{sym} = I - D^{-1/2} A D^{-1/2}$ | Range: $[0, 2]$ |
| 14 | RWR (Steady State) | $\mathbf{r} = (1-c) M^T \mathbf{r} + c \mathbf{e}$ | Restart prob $c$ |
| 15 | Clustering Coefficient | $C_i = 2 e_i / (k_i(k_i-1))$ | Triangles / possible triangles |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Centrality identifies critical routers whose failure disconnects the network.
- **Epidemiology:** PageRank and eigenvector centrality locate *superspreaders* in contact graphs.
- **Smart Grids:** Laplacian spectra detect islanding events in power-distribution networks.
- **Recommendation Engines:** Adamic–Adar scores drive link prediction in co-purchase graphs.
- **Cybersecurity:** Betweenness centrality pinpoints chokepoint nodes in lateral-movement attack graphs.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing All Centralities on a Toy Graph

**Given Graph $G$:** 4 nodes $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ with edges $\{(1,2), (1,3), (2,3), (3,4)\}$.

**Step 1 — Build the adjacency matrix $A$:**

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute degrees:** $\deg(1) = 2,\ \deg(2) = 2,\ \deg(3) = 3,\ \deg(4) = 1$.

**Step 3 — Degree Centrality** (using $C_D(v) = \deg(v)/(N-1)$ with $N-1 = 3$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_D(1) &= 2/3 \approx 0.667 \\
C_D(2) &= 2/3 \approx 0.667 \\
C_D(3) &= 3/3 = 1.000 \\
C_D(4) &= 1/3 \approx 0.333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Closeness Centrality:** Compute all pairwise distances first.

- $d(1,2) = 1,\ d(1,3) = 1,\ d(1,4) = 2$ (via 3)
- $d(2,3) = 1,\ d(2,4) = 2,\ d(3,4) = 1$

Sums: $\sum d(1) = 1+1+2 = 4$, $\sum d(2) = 1+1+2 = 4$, $\sum d(3) = 1+1+1 = 3$, $\sum d(4) = 2+2+1 = 5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_C(1) &= 3/4 = 0.750 \\
C_C(2) &= 3/4 = 0.750 \\
C_C(3) &= 3/3 = 1.000 \\
C_C(4) &= 3/5 = 0.600
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Betweenness Centrality:** Enumerate all unordered pairs $\{s,t\}$ and shortest paths.

| Pair | $\sigma_{st}$ | Paths through 1 | Paths through 2 | Paths through 3 | Paths through 4 |
|------|---------------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| (1,2) | 1 (direct) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| (1,3) | 1 (direct) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| (1,4) | 1 (1→3→4) | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| (2,3) | 1 (direct) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| (2,4) | 1 (2→3→4) | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| (3,4) | 1 (direct) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B(1) &= 0 \\
C_B(2) &= 0 \\
C_B(3) &= 2/6 \approx 0.333 \quad \text{(normalized: divide by } \binom{4}{2} = 6) \\
C_B(4) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Node **3** is the bridge — betweenness is highest as expected.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — PageRank by Power Iteration

Consider a tiny directed graph with 3 nodes: **1 → 2**, **2 → 3**, **3 → 1** (a directed cycle). $N = 3$, $d = 0.85$.

**Step 1 — Stochastic matrix $M^T$** (rows sum to 1 if we column-normalize for the dangling handling). With cycle, each node has out-degree 1.

$$M^T = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Initialize** $\mathbf{r}^{(0)} = (1/3, 1/3, 1/3)^T$.

**Step 3 — Update rule:** $\mathbf{r}^{(t+1)} = (1-d)\mathbf{e}/N + d \cdot M^T \mathbf{r}^{(t)}$ where $\mathbf{e} = (1,1,1)^T$.

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{r}^{(1)} &= 0.15 \cdot (1/3, 1/3, 1/3) + 0.85 \cdot M^T \cdot (1/3, 1/3, 1/3) \\
&= (0.05, 0.05, 0.05) + 0.85 \cdot (1/3, 1/3, 1/3) \\
&= (0.05, 0.05, 0.05) + (0.2833, 0.2833, 0.2833) \\
&= (0.3333, 0.3333, 0.3333)
\end{aligned}
$$

**By symmetry the vector is invariant.** The steady-state PageRank is uniform: $PR(1) = PR(2) = PR(3) \approx 0.333$ — exactly what we expect for a symmetric directed cycle.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Link Prediction Scores for Missing Edge

**Graph:** Triangle $\{A, B, C\}$ plus a pendant $D$ attached to $C$. Nodes: $\{A, B, C, D\}$. Edges: $\{(A,B), (B,C), (A,C), (C,D)\}$.

**Predict edge between $A$ and $D$.**

**Neighbour sets:**
- $N(A) = \{B, C\}$
- $N(D) = \{C\}$

**Step 1 — Common Neighbors:**

$$CN(A, D) = \vert \{B, C\} \cap \{C\} \vert = \vert \{C\} \vert = 1$$

**Step 2 — Jaccard:**

$$J(A, D) = \frac{\vert \{C\} \vert}{\vert \{B, C, C\} \mid \text{ (union)} \vert} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

Note: the union is $\{B, C\}$ (unique elements), giving $|N(A) \cup N(D)| = 3$ unique neighbours.

**Step 3 — Adamic–Adar:** Common neighbour set is $\{C\}$ with $\deg(C) = 3$.

$$AA(A, D) = \frac{1}{\log(3)} \approx \frac{1}{1.0986} \approx 0.910$$

**Step 4 — Preferential Attachment:**

$$PA(A, D) = \deg(A) \cdot \deg(D) = 2 \cdot 1 = 2$$

**Step 5 — Resource Allocation:**

$$RA(A, D) = \frac{1}{\deg(C)} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** All non-zero scores signal a *plausible* link. Adamic–Adar is highest because the common neighbour $C$ has moderate (not extreme) degree — exactly the signature of a useful bridge.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Link Predictors

| Predictor | Strength | Weakness | Best Used When |
|-----------|----------|----------|----------------|
| CN | Simple, fast | Ignores degree bias | Dense, homophilous networks |
| Jaccard | Scale-invariant | Insensitive to volume | Comparing pairs at different scales |
| Adamic–Adar | Downweights hubs | Sensitive to log of 1 | Social networks with clear hubs |
| Preferential Attachment | Captures power-law | Ignores overlap | Growth processes (citation, follow) |
| Resource Allocation | Network-flow based | Same log issue | Telecommunication / energy grids |

## 3.5 Derivation Sketch — Why PageRank is a Damped Eigenvector

The un-damped eigenvector centrality satisfies $A \mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}$. For a directed graph with a **dangling node** (no outgoing edges), the corresponding row of $A$ is zero, so $\lambda = 0$ and centrality degenerates. PageRank solves this by augmenting:

$$PR = d \cdot M^T \cdot PR + \frac{1-d}{N} \mathbf{1}$$

Rearranging:

$$(I - d M^T) PR = \frac{1-d}{N} \mathbf{1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad PR = (I - d M^T)^{-1} \frac{1-d}{N} \mathbf{1}$$

The matrix $(I - d M^T)$ is **non-singular** for $d \in (0, 1)$ because the spectral radius of $M^T$ is $\leq 1$, ensuring a unique solution. This is mathematically why **$d = 0.85$** is the Google default — it guarantees convergence while preserving teleportation.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**Define graph centrality. Differentiate between degree centrality and betweenness centrality with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Graph Centrality** is a structural metric that assigns a numerical score to each node based on its position in the network, indicating its *importance*, *influence*, or *control* over information flow.

| Aspect | Degree Centrality | Betweenness Centrality |
|--------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Captures** | Local connectivity | Brokerage / gatekeeping |
| **Formula** | $C_D(v) = \deg(v) / (N-1)$ | $C_B(v) = \sum \sigma_{st}(v) / \sigma_{st}$ |
| **Scope** | Immediate neighbours only | All pairs in the graph |
| **Example** | A user with 500 friends | A single router linking two ISP subnets |

**Example:** In a college WhatsApp group with 200 students, the *class representative* (high degree) answers many queries directly, while the *group admin who links two different class groups* (high betweenness) brokers communication between them.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]`
**What is link prediction? Explain the Common Neighbors and Jaccard Coefficient methods with formulas.**

**Model Answer:**

**Link Prediction** is the task of estimating the likelihood of a future (or currently missing) edge between two non-adjacent nodes, based on the existing graph topology and node/edge attributes.

**Common Neighbors (CN):** The simplest topological predictor — two nodes are more likely to connect if they share many mutual connections.

$$CN(x, y) = \vert N(x) \cap N(y) \vert$$

where $N(x)$ and $N(y)$ are the neighbour sets of $x$ and $y$.

**Jaccard Coefficient (JC):** A normalized version that accounts for the *total* distinct neighbours of both nodes, eliminating bias caused by high-degree nodes.

$$J(x, y) = \frac{\vert N(x) \cap N(y) \vert}{\vert N(x) \cup N(y) \vert}$$

**Key Difference:** CN returns a raw count (range $[0, N-2]$), whereas JC returns a probability-like value in $[0, 1]$. JC is preferred when comparing pairs of nodes from subgraphs of different densities.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When answering Part B, always (1) state the formula explicitly, (2) show intermediate calculations in a table form, (3) conclude with an **interpretation** sentence. Students who skip the interpretation lose **2 marks** consistently.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define PageRank. With a damping factor $d = 0.85$, compute the PageRank of nodes A, B, C in the directed graph: A → B, B → C, C → A. Show two iterations of the power method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **graph Laplacian** in spectral graph theory. Compute the Laplacian matrix for the graph with adjacency matrix
$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$
and list its eigenvalues. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📌 Model Solution — Question A

### Part (a) — PageRank Computation

**Definition:** PageRank is a link-analysis algorithm originally developed by Google. It assigns a numerical weighting to each node of a hyperlinked set of documents (e.g., the WWW), with the purpose of measuring its relative importance within the set.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \in M(p)} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$$

**Given Graph (Directed Cycle):** A → B, B → C, C → A. $N = 3$, $d = 0.85$.

**Step 1 — Identify the out-degree of each node:**
- $L(A) = 1$ (A → B)
- $L(B) = 1$ (B → C)
- $L(C) = 1$ (C → A)

**Step 2 — Build the column-stochastic matrix $M^T$ (each column sums to 1):**

$$M^T = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Initialize** $\mathbf{r}^{(0)} = (1/3, 1/3, 1/3)^T = (0.3333, 0.3333, 0.3333)^T$.

**Step 4 — Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR(A)^{(1)} &= \frac{1-0.85}{3} + 0.85 \cdot PR(C)^{(0)} = 0.05 + 0.85 \cdot 0.3333 = 0.3333 \\
PR(B)^{(1)} &= 0.05 + 0.85 \cdot PR(A)^{(0)} = 0.05 + 0.85 \cdot 0.3333 = 0.3333 \\
PR(C)^{(1)} &= 0.05 + 0.85 \cdot PR(B)^{(0)} = 0.05 + 0.85 \cdot 0.3333 = 0.3333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Iteration 2:** Identical result — $\mathbf{r}^{(2)} = (0.3333, 0.3333, 0.3333)^T$.

**Convergence achieved in 1 iteration** because the directed cycle is symmetric.

**Final PageRank Vector:**

$$PR(A) = PR(B) = PR(C) = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333$$

**Interpretation:** All three nodes are equally important because the directed cycle distributes authority uniformly. None of the nodes is a "hub" or a "sink."

---

### Part (b) — Graph Laplacian Computation

**Role of Graph Laplacian:** The Laplacian $L = D - A$ encodes structural and spectral properties of the graph. It is **positive semi-definite**, **symmetric**, and its eigenvalues $\lambda_0 = 0 \leq \lambda_1 \leq \dots \leq \lambda_{N-1}$ reveal connectivity, expansion, and clustering properties. The second-smallest eigenvalue $\lambda_1$ (algebraic connectivity) measures how well-connected the graph is. The eigenvectors form a Fourier-like basis used in **Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs)**.

**Step 1 — Compute the degree matrix $D$ from $A$:**

Row sums: $D_1 = 2, D_2 = 2, D_3 = 2$.

$$D = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Form the Laplacian $L = D - A$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= D - A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix} - \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \\
  &= \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute eigenvalues:** Use the fact that for a complete graph $K_N$, eigenvalues are $0$ (multiplicity 1) and $N$ (multiplicity $N-1$).

- $\lambda_0 = 0$ (always, since $L$ is PSD with constant vector as eigenvector)
- $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = 3$ (since $N = 3$)

**Verification:** $\det(L - \lambda I) = (2-\lambda)^3 - 3(2-\lambda) - 2 = 0$ yields $\lambda = 0, 3, 3$.

**Eigenvalues:** $\boxed{\lambda_0 = 0,\ \lambda_1 = 3,\ \lambda_2 = 3}$

**Interpretation:** The graph is **fully connected** (since $\lambda_1 > 0$), and the multiplicity of the non-zero eigenvalue indicates the structure is **two-fold symmetric** (triangle has 3-fold rotational symmetry, but the spectral reflection yields a 2-D eigenspace for the non-zero eigenvalue).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Adamic–Adar** and **Preferential Attachment** link prediction methods. For the graph below, predict the likelihood of edge (A, E) using both methods. **(7 marks)**

**Graph:** Triangle $\{A, B, C\}$ plus an extra node $D$ connected to $A$ and $E$ connected to $C$. So edges: $\{(A,B), (B,C), (A,C), (A,D), (C,E)\}$.

**(b)** Describe **Random Walk with Restart (RWR)**. How is it used in personalized PageRank and link prediction? **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📌 Model Solution — Question B

### Part (a) — Adamic–Adar and Preferential Attachment

**Adamic–Adar (AA):** Refines the Common Neighbors score by weighting each common neighbour $z$ inversely with the log of its degree. The intuition: a common friend who has only a few connections is a *more meaningful bridge* than one with thousands.

$$AA(x, y) = \sum_{z \in N(x) \cap N(y)} \frac{1}{\log \vert N(z) \vert}$$

**Preferential Attachment (PA):** Based on the principle that "the rich get richer" — nodes with higher degree are more likely to attract new connections. The score is simply the product of the two nodes' degrees.

$$PA(x, y) = \vert N(x) \vert \cdot \vert N(y) \vert$$

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant sets for (A, E):**

- $N(A) = \{B, C, D\}$ → $\deg(A) = 3$
- $N(E) = \{C\}$ → $\deg(E) = 1$
- $N(A) \cap N(E) = \{C\}$ → Common neighbour set
- Degree of common neighbour: $\deg(C) = 3$ (neighbours: $A, B, E$)

**Step 2 — Apply Adamic–Adar:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AA(A, E) &= \sum_{z \in \{C\}} \frac{1}{\log \vert N(z) \vert} = \frac{1}{\log(3)} = \frac{1}{1.0986} \approx 0.910
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Preferential Attachment:**

$$PA(A, E) = \vert N(A) \vert \cdot \vert N(E) \vert = 3 \times 1 = 3$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** Both scores are **non-zero**, predicting a moderate-to-strong likelihood for the edge (A, E). Adamic–Adar's high value of $0.910$ is because the sole bridge $C$ is a "selective connector" (degree 3, not a hub). Preferential Attachment's score of $3$ is modest because $E$ has only 1 connection.

**Conclusion:** The edge (A, E) is **likely** to form; the only obstacle is the low degree of $E$, which PA penalizes strongly but AA does not.

---

### Part (b) — Random Walk with Restart (RWR)

**Conceptual Definition:** Random Walk with Restart is a node-similarity measure that simulates a particle starting at a *seed* node $v$ and repeatedly moving to a random neighbour. At each step, with probability $c$ the walker **restarts** at $v$ (teleports back), and with probability $1 - c$ it moves to a randomly chosen neighbour. The steady-state probability distribution $\mathbf{r}_v$ encodes the *relevance* of every other node to $v$.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$\mathbf{r}_v = (1 - c) \cdot M^T \cdot \mathbf{r}_v + c \cdot \mathbf{e}_v$$

Solving for $\mathbf{r}_v$:

$$\mathbf{r}_v = c (I - (1-c) M^T)^{-1} \mathbf{e}_v$$

where:
- $M$ is the row-normalized adjacency matrix.
- $c \in (0, 1]$ is the restart probability (typically $0.15$).
- $\mathbf{e}_v$ is the one-hot seed vector with a 1 at position $v$.

**Use in Personalized PageRank:**
Personalized PageRank is mathematically **identical to RWR** — the damping factor $d$ in PageRank corresponds to the restart probability $c$ in RWR. Instead of teleporting to *any* random node (classical PageRank), the walker teleports *only* to the seed node $v$. This makes the resulting importance scores **node-centric** rather than global.

**Use in Link Prediction:**
The RWR score between two unconnected nodes $u$ and $v$ serves as a topological similarity measure. Steps:
1. Compute the RWR distribution $\mathbf{r}_u$ starting from $u$.
2. Read off the steady-state value at node $v$: $\mathbf{r}_u[v]$.
3. Higher $\mathbf{r}_u[v]$ ⇒ stronger predicted link from $u$ to $v$.
4. For symmetric prediction, average $\mathbf{r}_u[v]$ and $\mathbf{r}_v[u]$.

**Advantages over local methods (CN, Jaccard):**
- Captures **multi-hop** structural proximity, not just immediate neighbours.
- Robust to noise and sparse regions.
- Computationally tractable via **power iteration** (converges in $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$).

**Real-world application:** Google's original search ranking used personalized PageRank to combine hyperlink structure with user-query context.

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 4 (Graph Theory & Predictive Modelling):**

- ✅ **Graph Order** $= \vert V \vert = N$; **Graph Size** $= \vert E \vert = M$; max undirected edges = $\frac{N(N-1)}{2}$.
- ✅ **Degree Centrality** captures *local popularity*; $C_D(v) = \deg(v)/(N-1)$.
- ✅ **Betweenness Centrality** captures *brokerage*; sum of $\sigma_{st}(v)/\sigma_{st}$.
- ✅ **Closeness Centrality** captures *reach speed*; $(N-1)/\sum d(v,u)$.
- ✅ **Eigenvector Centrality** satisfies $A \mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}$ — a node is important if its neighbours are important.
- ✅ **PageRank** uses damping factor $\mathbf{d = 0.85}$ to handle dangling nodes and guarantee convergence.
- ✅ **Common Neighbors** $= \vert N(x) \cap N(y) \vert$ — simplest, raw count.
- ✅ **Jaccard** normalizes CN by the union — range $[0, 1]$.
- ✅ **Adamic–Adar** weights by $1/\log \vert N(z) \vert$ — rare common neighbours weigh more (preferred for social networks).
- ✅ **Preferential Attachment** = $\deg(x) \cdot \deg(y)$ — captures power-law growth.
- ✅ **Graph Laplacian** $L = D - A$ is **positive semi-definite** with $\lambda_0 = 0$ always.
- ✅ **RWR (Random Walk with Restart)** with restart probability $c$ simulates node-similarity via steady-state distribution.
- ✅ **Normalized Laplacian** $L_{sym} = I - D^{-1/2} A D^{-1/2}$ has eigenvalues in $[0, 2]$.
- ✅ In KTU exam, **always interpret** the final score/rank — examiners award 1–2 marks for interpretation alone.
- ✅ **Don't confuse**: $\sigma_{st}$ (number of shortest paths) vs. $\sigma_{st}(v)$ (those through $v$) in betweenness.
- ✅ **Damping factor value $d = 0.85$** is the standard Google choice — memorize it.
